File.Name: ca08f_ca08-016.jpg
Notes: 8069047231 This is another Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae) in the order Hemiptera perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. You can see its amazing proboscis tucked under it's head, like a switchblade knife blade. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2012)

File.Name: ca08g_ca08-025.jpg
Notes: 8068986206 I believe this is another Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper, one of the the Spur-throated Grasshoppers ( Melanoplinae) in the suborder Acrididae in the order Orthoptera. It's mature, but it has very short net-like wings. This one is perched on a leaf of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus tomentella or R. californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2012)

File.Name: ca08h_ca07-002.jpg
Notes:
8068942559 This is a tiny Leafhopper insect in the family Cicadellidae of the order Homoptera on a leaf in the garden. Look - it's sticking its proboscis into the leaf like a straw! (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2012)

*Sigh* This is what insect photographers do when they can't find new insects - look for really tiny insects! This is an 800x800 crop from my 4288x2848 pixel original photo, taken with my 105 mm macro lens. I got this photo near 1:1 magnification, so I can use my digital camera as an expensive micrometer. The critter is about 565 pixels across on my original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the critter is 23.6x565/4288 = 3.1 mm, a bit less than 1/8 of an inch. Like I said, this critter is tiny!

File.Name: ca09a_ca09-006.jpg
Notes:
8072678404 I'm pretty sure this is another Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly, in the family Libellulidae of the order Odonata. It's perched on a dead plant stem on the dry wash of the river today. I love that neutral background. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 October 2012)

It felt like rain this morning - clouds moving in from the south, a south wind, cloud caps on the mountains - and then it all disappeared into blue skies. There's still a 40% chance of real rain tomorrow night. I hope it happens. The woods really need it.

File.Name: ca09b_ca09-012.jpg
Notes: 8072667921 This is another photo of the beautiful Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly on a still-flowering native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river. This one must be newly emerged as it still has the blue colors on the front edge of its forewings. I got this photo nearly full-frame, so I can't rotate it. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 October 2012)

File.Name: ca09c_ca09-011.jpg
Notes:
8072621836 Here is a beautiful Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly on a still-flowering native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river. This one must be newly emerged as it has the blue colors on the front edge of its forewings. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 October 2012)

Hah - there's an interesting little critter in the very upper-right of my photo! I can't make out what it is.

File.Name: ca09d_ca09-015.jpg
Notes: 8072606995 This is another granite-colored Thalassica grasshopper (Trimerotropis thalassica) grasshopper on the dry desert-like flood plain of the river. It's a Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera) - very beautiful. Uh - do you notice it's missing something, like its right rear leg! I wonder if it always hops to the right? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 October 2012)

File.Name: ca09e_ca09-016.jpg
Notes:
8072558012 I'm not sure what kind of Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera) this is. It sure is very well-camouflaged on the dry desert-like flood plain of the river. It hopped away before I could get a better photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 October 2012)

Alice says it's maybe a Caerulean-winged Grasshopper (Trimerotropis pseudofasciata). I thought it was something different.

File.Name: ca09f_ca09-004.jpg
Notes:
8072531616 This is a handsome little net-winged Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the order Homoptera on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2012)

This photo is a tight crop from my original photo, taken at 1:1 magnification with my 105 mm macro lens. I figure this critter is about 3/16 inch long.

File.Name: ca09g_ca09-033.jpg
Notes: 8072500152 I found this little Wasp in the order Hymenoptera working the remaining flowers of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It has neat curled antennae, like a Spider Wasp (family Pompilidae). It has big eyes and an orange abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 9 October 2012)

File.Name: ca09h_ca09-031.jpg
Notes: 8072457106 I believe this is the burrow of a California Tarantula (Aphonopelma sp.) spider. Either that, or the entrance to a smoky underground jazz club! The hole is quite large, an inch or more across. It doesn't have the cap of a trapdoor spider (Genus Antrodiaetus). I haven't seen any of the spiders yet this year, but now is the time. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 October 2012)

File.Name: ca10a_ca10-010.jpg
Notes:
8075860777 It must be a coincidence that the first native flowers of the new year picked the day of the first winter storm of the new year to flower! Today is our first winter storm of the 2012-2013 rainy season. We've only had 0.15 inches so far, but it's a start, and more might be coming tonight.

These are the first new flowers of a native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family in our mountains. This plant has been growing in our garden for 30 years now. It gets regular summer watering, which may have something to do with its early flowering. But it's doing well, 10 or 12 feet tall, and it seems vigorous. (I had to get my tripod up on a ladder to get this photo!) It might be the longest-flowering native plant in our mountains - at least six months! See my photo from last April 29! (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2012)

I had a Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society board meeting tonight and got home late. Two photos will do just fine tonight. I have another natural history lecture to attend on Thursday night - Jon Keely talking about Fire in Mediterranean Ecosystems. It should be a very interesting talk - 7:00 pm at the Solvang Library.

File.Name: ca10b_ca10-003.jpg
Notes:
8075815374 These are real raindrops falling on native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I got this photo out the back door this morning. I did no photoshop tricks - this is how it came from my camera. (San Marcos Pass, 10 October 2012)

This is our first winter storm of the 2012-2013 rainy season. We've only had 0.15 inches so far, but it's a start, and more might be coming tonight.

File.Name: ca11a_ca10-015.jpg
Notes:
8078978677 This is another Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae) in the order Hemiptera perched on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family - with raindrops. If you compare my [Older] photo, you can see the same brown scar on the leaf. This little bug hasn't moved at all! (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2012)

I got this photo during a moment of sunshine in the rain yesterday. Today, I found four more of the assassin bugs on different leaves of the wild blackberry. They must like the rain! Speaking of rain, we're up to 0.22 inches in our rain gauge. Not much, but it's a start on the new rain year.

The Natural History Society sponsored a very interesting talk tonight by Dr. Jon Keely talking about Fire in Mediterranean Ecosystems. I got home late, so two photos will do.

File.Name: ca11b_ca11-001.jpg
Notes:
8078968297 This is another remarkable Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae) in the order Hemiptera perched on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. You can see its amazing proboscis tucked under it's head. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2012)

If you compare my [Newer] photo taken yesterday, you can see the same brown scar on the leaf. This little bug hasn't moved at all!

File.Name: ca12a_ca12-011.jpg
Notes:
8081524187 This is a new flower of native California Fuschia (Epilobium canum ssp. angustifolium - aka Zauschneria californica) - in the Onagraceae plant family. It has remarkable pollen grains. This one is growing on the dry side of our garden. The flowers are beautiful and surprising in this dry time of year at the end of summer. I reckon their job is to keep the hummingbirds alive. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2012)

I've had my old Nikon D300 camera since June 2008, and it finally died today after 165,738 photos - as counted in the EXIF info. I guess it's fair, as I've heard the shutters are rated for 150,000 photos or so. The mirror started sticking UP after a photo a few days ago, and I would have to take another photo or two to clear it. Finally today, it stayed up.

So I went to Samy's Camera in Santa Barbara to get a new camera. I've really been waiting for the fabled Nikon D400, but I sprung for a D800. It's full-frame with a huge number of pixels - 7360x4912. Of course that comes at the cost huge files - 40 MB raw and 12 MB jpg on my test photo. I guess on my next trip to town, I'll need a new hard drive.

I got home late, and there was little light by the time I got my battery charged and my new camera set up the way I want. This is the first keeper photo from my new Nikon D800 camera. It's really just a test shot, and it seems to work fine.

I'll give it a real test tomorrow!
ScientificName: Epilobium canum; angustifolium; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae
associatedTaxa: Epilobium canum; angustifolium; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae

File.Name: ca12b_ca12-003.jpg
Notes: 8081482202 Here is a mature male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera) sprawled out "butts up" on a leaf of Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden today. These males have wider wings and no ovipositors. Look at that long antenna curled on the plant! Compare my [Older] photo of the female. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2012)

File.Name: ca12c_ca11-008.jpg
Notes: 8081470173 This is a mature female Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera) trying to hide on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The females have narrow wings and long ovipositors on their abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2012)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ca12d_ca11-004.jpg
Notes: 8081431869 This is a tiny "True Bug" in the order Hemiptera sticking its proboscis into a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. It has beautiful sooty colors, and almost looks like a mosquito. I got this photo in the gloom yesterday, and I had to bump up the ISO a notch. It shows. (San Marcos Pass, 11 October 2012)

File.Name: ca12e_ca08-028.jpg
Notes:
8081354631 This looks like a gall with an "exit hole" - but I think it's really a scale insect with an "entrance hole" where some parasite got in or out.

I believe this is not a gall, but the shell of a Gall-like Scale Insect in the family Kermesidae of the superfamily Coccoidea in the Homoptera - a "Kermes Scale". It may well be genus Allokermes. See this photo on the web. I found this critter on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 October 2012)
CommonNames: Gall-like Scale Insect; Kermes Scale; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Kermesidae; Coccoidea; Homoptera; Allokermes; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Kermesidae; Coccoidea; Homoptera; Allokermes; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ca12f_ca07-007.jpg
Notes: 8081312962 These are mostly the dry husks of failed flowers on Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family - though there are a couple of live flowers mixed in. It's been a shock that these late-season shrubs have mostly failed in the mountains this year - no doubt because of the lack of rain. Even if they get a few late flowers like this, the insects have mostly missed them. It's a small calamity. (San Marcos Pass, 7 October 2012)

File.Name: ca12g_ca09-013.jpg
Notes:
8081272978 These are flowers and seed heads of native Tall Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria virgata ssp. pleurocarpa) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's larger than S. exigua, the seeds are not grooved, and the phyllaries are not reflexed. The individual flowers are pretty, like they're wearing purple socks. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 9 October 2012)

I reckon any native plants still flowering at the end of our long hot summer - in a drought year - are interesting!
ScientificName: Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa; Asteraceae; S. exigua
associatedTaxa: Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa; Asteraceae; S. exigua

File.Name: ca12h_ca12-007.jpg
Notes:
8081229403 This is the same Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae) in the order Hemiptera perched on the same leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family as my [Previous] two photos. You can see the same leaf scar behind it. These assassin bugs look fast, but they move very slowly. This one has hardly moved for three days! The wings look golden. (San Marcos Pass, 12 October 2012)

*Sigh* This is almost the last photo of my trusty Nikon D300 camera which I've been using since June 2008. It finally died after 165,738 photos - as counted in the EXIF info. I guess it's fair, as I've heard the shutters are rated for 150,000 photos or so. The mirror started sticking UP after a photo, and I would have to take another photo or two to clear it. Finally today, it stayed up. See my first photo today.

File.Name: ca13a_ca13-031.jpg
Notes:
8084676957 This is a perfect Assassin Bug (family Reduviidae) in the order Hemiptera on a leaf of a native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm intrigued by that tiny turquoise lump at the tip of its scutellum - the little triangle behind the pronotum, the shield-like plate on its back. All my photos seem to show it. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2012)

I've been trying out my new Nikon D800 camera today. It seems to work just fine, as this photo proves. There are a few little annoying differences from my D300, like reversing the + and - buttons to view photos, but I'll get used to it. I haven't tried the video functions yet - I'll have to actually read the manual!

File.Name: ca13b_ca13-017.jpg
Notes: 8084636735 This is a remarkable tiny Leafhopper insect in the family Cicadellidae of the order Homoptera on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It's only about 1/8 inch long. It has good eyes and interesting "eyelashes" which are really antennae. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2012)

File.Name: ca13c_ca13-015.jpg
Notes: 8084599037 This is another handsome little net-winged Planthopper in the family Issidae of the superfamily Fulgoroidea in the order Homoptera on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. Maybe it's genus Neaethus . (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2012)

associatedTaxa: Issidae; Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Neaethus

File.Name: ca13d_ca13-013.jpg
Notes: 8084545864 This is a tiny Fly in the order the order Diptera. It's got bristles on it's abdomen, so I guess it's a little Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae. It's only about 1/8 inch or less long. It's interesting how the tiny flies look just like the large flies, only smaller! (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2012)

File.Name: ca13e_ca13-022.jpg
Notes:
8084509257 This is another mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae guarding her egg sack in a shrub of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2012)

Many of my photos today will look familiar. I've been trying out my new Nikon D800 camera, and not worrying too much about repeating photos.

File.Name: ca13f_ca13-033.jpg
Notes: 8084482194 Here is a large bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae - maybe the Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) or kin - on a leaf of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family growing by the bridge over our summer-dry creek. I spooked it, and it took off up a line into the oak trees - at least 20 feet away! (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2012)
ScientificName: Araneidae; Araneus gemmoides; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Araneus gemmoides; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae

File.Name: ca13g_ca13-028.jpg
Notes: 8084464002 These are fall flowers and seed heads of native California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. The flowers are "diffuse" - it's hard to find a group. You have to admire these autumn native plants that pick the harshest time of our summer-dry climate to flower - even in a drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 13 October 2012)

File.Name: ca13h_ca13-025.jpg
Notes: 8084418394 This looks like a grass, but I believe it's a dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family. They stay green like this all summer. I have a photo of the spring flowers

File.Name: ca14a_ca14-042.jpg
Notes:
8088875002 Do wasps have tongues?

I was already focused close on this gall of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera when this Yellow Jacket-type wasp (family Vespidae, order Hymenoptera) appeared in my viewfinder. It's licking the sweet honey that the galls produce. The action is happening on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. This photo was an accident - a happy one! (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2012)

It was warm today, in the 90s. Our weather has swung the other way after a bit of rain and cool temperatures last week. I've been looking for mushrooms, but the heat is pulling all the water from the ground.
CommonNames: Clasping Twig Gall Wasp; Yellow Jacket; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ca14b_ca14-059.jpg
Notes:
8088782571 I guess this is a Spider Wasp (family Pompilidae) in the order Hymenoptera. It has dark wings, curled antennae, and a muscular body. Maybe it's genus Ageniella? (See BugGuide.) This one is working staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family in the mountains. The adults harvest spiders for their eggs, but they feed on flowers. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 October 2012)

Compare my photo of a mighty Tarantula Hawk:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/7918187178/

File.Name: ca14c_ca14-063.jpg
Notes: 8088720919 This is a Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera) trying to hide in one of the few flowering plants of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family in the mountains. It has bright blue tibia, and appears to have some red on its femur. Alice says that it's a Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes), which seems to favor composites. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 14 October 2012)

File.Name: ca14d_ca14-040.jpg
Notes:
8088610053 This is a tiny Fly in the order Diptera getting sweet honey from the gall of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's a gall that exudes sweet honey. This is on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The fly has remarkable flat antennae. It's quite bristly, so I'm thinking it's some kind of Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae - but I really don't know. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2012)

I've found a similar fly before. See my photo at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6980920593/
CommonNames: Fly; Clasping Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Tachinid Fly

File.Name: ca14e_ca14-004.jpg
Notes: 8088579019 This is a small Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a late-season flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family. Yep, they are still flowering despite the drought conditions this summer. There's an even tinier bee across from the bee's middle-right leg. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2012)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae

File.Name: ca14f_ca14-006.jpg
Notes:
8088532573 This is a tiny Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a late-season flower of composite Bush Groundsel aka Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family today. The new flowers look like pots full of egg yolks! (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2012)

Most of these native plants are higher in our mountains. This is the lowest one I've found, near Knapp's Castle. It's along one of my favorite walks, which I do again and again.
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Bush Groundsel; Douglas' Groundsel

File.Name: ca14g_ca14-026.jpg
Notes: 8088510725 These are pumpkin-like seeds of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. The seeds start with a papery covering which finally tears free. It's pretty to look at, but don't touch if you are sensitive! (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2012)

File.Name: ca14h_ca14-066.jpg
Notes: 8088481882 Some Scrub Oaks (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family are loaded with acorns in this drought year. Others are barren. That's how it is with oaks, both shrubs and trees. They are unpredictable. This made life complicated for the Chumash people who depended on the acorns. (San Marcos Pass, 14 October 2012)

File.Name: ca15a_ca15-037.jpg
Notes:
8091959941 Weren't we just talking about Spider Wasps? (See this photo yesterday.)

This is a mighty Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera). It's peacefully working flowers of native Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's quite large - maybe 2 inches long. I'm a little confused because this one does not have orange antennae. Is it different from the species in this photo? See my [Previous] photo. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 15 October 2012)

In this photo you can see her brilliant blue iridescent colors, but they turn black when the light is wrong. They seem clumsy on the flowers - too big! I can't recall ever seeing one of these wasps in the mountains, nor tarantulas.

I'm posting early - and just two photos - because Julie and I are stepping out tonight to see Dr. John and the Blind Boys of Alabama at the Lobrero Theater in Santa Barbara. I'm actually going to see the blind boys - I saw them do a wonderful Sunday morning show at the Live Oak Music Festival a few years ago.
CommonNames: Spider Wasps; Tarantula Hawk; Ms. Plummer's Baccharis; Smooth Baccharis; Dr. John; Blind Boys of Alabama

Notes:
8091896167 This is a mighty Tarantula Hawk (genus Pepsis, family Pompilidae, order Hymenoptera). It's peacefully working flowers of native Ms. Plummer's Baccharis aka Smooth Baccharis (Baccharis plummerae) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's quite large - maybe 2 inches long. I'm a little confused because this one does not have orange antennae. Is it different from the species in this photo? (Santa Ynez Mountains, 15 October 2012)

This is one of the world's largest wasps - as big as a dragonfly and almost as large as a small hummingbird! I believe this is a female because of the coiled antennae. In this photo you can see her brilliant blue iridescent colors, but they turn black when the light is wrong. They seem clumsy on the flowers - too big! I can't recall ever seeing one of these wasps in the mountains, nor tarantulas.

File.Name: ca16a_ca16-002.jpg
Notes:
8095761894 This is a beautiful Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a new glandular flower of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family. It's our first native flower of the new rain year. I had to stand on a ladder to capture this! (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2012)

The Blind Boys of Alabama stole the show last night at the Lobrero Theater in Santa Barbara. Yes, I listened to the Presidential Debate tonight while posting these photos. ;=)

File.Name: ca16b_ca16-009.jpg
Notes: 8095722464 This is just a Fly in the order Diptera on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. Maybe it's a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae with those red eyes and bristles on its abdomen? (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2012)
CommonNames: Fly; Coast Live Oak; Tachinid Fly

File.Name: ca16c_ca16-004.jpg
Notes: 8095703014 This little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) is getting pollen from a fiery flower of non-native Mexican Sunflower (Tithonia rotundifolia) in the garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2012)

File.Name: ca16d_ca16-007.jpg
Notes: 8095678967 Here is a large bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae - maybe the Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) or kin in the woods. The cat face is down lower on the abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2012)

File.Name: ca16e_ca16-011.jpg
Notes:
8095631262 This little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is waiting for prey on a flower of native composite Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) plant in the Asteraceae plant family - with a seedhead on the right. Good luck little spider - in my experience, these flowers are not very attractive to insects. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2012)

Is that a lost spider leg down below, along with a couple of insect wings?

File.Name: ca16f_ca16-019.jpg
Notes: 8095564349 I guess this is a little Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers. It has a little spider web house (on the left) with an orb-web attached (on the right) an inch or two away. It's "house" is made up from the dead bodies of its prey - look at the bottom-left. (San Marcos Pass, 16 October 2012)

File.Name: ca16g_ca16-013.jpg
Notes: 8095508383 I guess that this is another Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes) - a Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera). File.Name: ca17a_ca17-002.jpg
Notes:
8099039152 This is the perfect ash-cast of a leaf that landed on our porch railing early this morning, when we first became aware of the Lookout Fire by Painted Cave. This ash-leaf traveled a mile in the air, but with one touch it vanished. We had a "snow flurry" of ash this morning, when the wind was right. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2012)

There's some info about the fire on the Incident Report. The fire is below Painted Cave, about a mile from home, and two canyons separate us. I reckon we're fine at home. I was tired of no information, so I walked East Camino Cielo road to a good lookout. I had a great view of the show! See my [Older] photos!

The airplane is number 12 of the CDF (California Department of Forestry and Fire Protection) fleet. I believe it's a Lockheed P-2 Neptune. It's large and beautiful, with a transparent nose cone.

File.Name: ca17h_ca17-005.jpg
Notes:
8098733526 Fire on the mountain...

This is the view from our upstairs porch this morning. We knew instantly that this was a problem, but where is it? The power was out, nothing on the radio, it's tough having no information. In time, the power came back, and there were reports on the radio and web about the Lookout Fire - see the Incident Report. The fire is about a mile from home, and two canyons separate us. No problem. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2012)

It's tough not knowing, so I walked up the road to a good lookout...

Always the naturalist... The trees in the foreground are (left to right) Tanbark Oak (Lithocarpus densiflorus, Fagaceae), California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae), and Madrone (Arbutus menziesii,Ericaceae).

File.Name: ca18a_ca18-001.jpg
Notes:
8101709109 All was quiet at the site of the Lookout Fire by Painted Cave today - a bit of smoke, and nothing else. The wildfire is now officially contained at 44 acres. Really not much of a fire given the massive air support it got, but it had potential. I'm impressed - they really can put out a wildfire when they try! (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2012)

No other new photos today. The clouds moved in and it was windy. It gave me a chance to get my aviation photos together - that's really 4x4=16 photos! There's always tomorrow...

File.Name: ca18b_ca17-airplanes1.jpg
Notes:
8101607829 I'm intrigued by the airplanes and helicopters used to fight wildfires. It's a bit like recognizing dragonflies, though those critters don't have ID numbers! (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2012)

These are the larger air tankers aka "fire bombers" that actually dropped the Phos-Chek fire retardant on the Lookout Fire by Painted Cave yesterday. From the top-left clockwise, here are:

* CDF 05 - a Lockheed P-2H Neptune, beautiful airplane with a clear nose cone.

* CDF 12 - a Lockheed P-2H Neptune, beautiful airplane with a clear nose cone.

* CDF 41 - I'm pretty sure this is a BAe 146 jet airplane, probably operated by Neptune Aviation. See this photo on the web.

* CDF 41 - another photo of the BAe 146. It's remarkable to see a four-engine jet airplane fly so slow and do a water drop, but they are said to have low speed maneuverability. Note that the flaps are fully down.

It must have been a slow fire day in California yesterday, because it was an amazing air show at the Lookout Fire! I got most of my airplane IDs from http://www.ruudleeuw.com/airtanker-id.htm - except for CDF 41, which must be newly approved. I had to do some digging on the web to find it.

There's an interesting article about firefighting jets in the The California Fire Pilots Association newsletter, CFPA News, for October 2011, page 6ff.

By the way, I got all these photos with nothing but my 105 mm macro lens. Some of the airplanes and helicopters are tight 533x400 crops from 7360x4912 originals. My new Nikon D800 camera does have pretty good pixels!

File.Name: ca18f_ca17-040.jpg
Notes:
8101427433 In my photos of the Lookout Fire by Painted Cave yesterday, I didn't show the guide plane. It flies ahead of the fire bombers on the same path, and lays down a smoke trail for them to follow. Once I figured this out, it was easy to capture photos of the bombers dropping their loads. (San Marcos Pass, 17 October 2012)

The bomber pilots get all the glory, but these pilots are just as important!

File.Name: ca19a_ca19-047.jpg
Notes:
8104315835 This is another tiny Western Pygmy Blue (Brephidium exile) butterfly on flower head of native composite Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) in the Asteraceae plant family by the river today. This photo shows the brilliant iridescent spots on its hindwings. This is the smallest butterfly in North America, and one of the smallest in the world. It's about the size of my little finger nail. They are hard to see because they fly close to the ground, but that helps with the wind. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 October 2012)

There are still butterflies in our autumn woods. Every day we have a dozen Painted Ladies (Vanessa cardui) in the well-named Butterfly Bush (Buddleia davidii) in the garden - too high to photograph; a few Monarchs (Danaus plexippus); Mormon Metalmarks (Apodemia mormo); Orange Sulfurs (Colias eurytheme); California Sisters (Adelpha bredowii); and no doubt more. It's not winter yet!

I had no idea that they have such brilliant iridescent colors on their bodies. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 October 2012)

Notes: 8104242299 I guess this large black bee is a Bumble Bee (genus Bombus, family Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera). File.Name: ca19e_ca18-002.jpg
Notes:
8104198037 This is the first mature Bush Katydid (genus Scudderia) in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera that I've found this year. It's a female, as shown by her ovipositor. It's trapped between our sliding glass doors on the upstairs porch, and it took some doing to get her free. I tried to pose it on the porch railing, but it flew away. It has narrow straight wings, unlike the California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum). (San Marcos Pass, 18 October 2012)

This is the adult that comes from the beautiful nymph I was showing earlier this year - like:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/7332100954/
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/7451733804/
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/7469848714/
... and lots more - I really love the nymphs!

File.Name: ca19f_ca19-028.jpg
Notes: 8104185061 These are galled leaves of Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii) in the Salicaceae plant family growing by the river. There is a lumpy gall on the petiole of the each of these leaves that is caused by Poplar Petiole Gall Aphids (Pemphigus populitransversus, Aphididae, Homoptera). Yes, we're getting fall colors early this year. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 October 2012)

Notes:
8104143899 These are burned and standing dead Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) trees in the Platanaceae plant family. They are growing along the Santa Ynez River between the first and second river crossings. There was a wildfire here a few years ago, and these trees didn't recover. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 October 2012)

If you like this photo, please see the original size. Flickr has gotten a bit too aggressive about sharpening uploaded photos. I don't like it.

File.Name: ca20a_ca19-020.jpg
Notes:
8107056618 These new flowers of Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family shouldn't be out at the end of our long, dry summer, but here they are. I found these yesterday on the dry flood plain by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 October 2012)

No new photos today. It was cold and gloomy, and I went to work with film-maker Michael Love who is approaching "final cut" on his impressive video-film about the Paradise area by the Santa Ynez River. It will be a very nice film, hopefully ready for the Santa Barbara Film Festival this year.

File.Name: ca20b_ca19-041.jpg
Notes: 8107032676 Here are new and old flowers of Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) shrubs in the Malvaceae plant family. This late-season native shrub is still producing beautiful flowers at the end of our summer dry season. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 October 2012)

File.Name: ca20c_ca19-029.jpg
Notes: 8107010579 Here's another photo of flowers and seed heads of native California Brickellbush (Brickellia californica) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. It's another of the late-season composite flowers that grows along the river and on rocky banks in the mountains. These late-season composite flowers keep the late-season insects alive. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 October 2012)

File.Name: ca20d_ca19-036.jpg
Notes: 8106984082 These are late-season female pistillate flowers of native Mule Fat (Baccharis salicifolia) in the Asteraceae plant family. It has willow-like leaves, but composite flowers. Honey Bees (Apis mellifera, order Hymenoptera) like the flowers. This shrub is monoecious, with separate male and female flowers on the same plant. I believe the common name reflects it's value as livestock feed. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 October 2012)
ScientificName: Baccharis salicifolia; Asteraceae; Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; monoecious
associatedTaxa: Baccharis salicifolia; Asteraceae; Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; monoecious

File.Name: ca20e_ca19-030.jpg
Notes:
8106939408 Sometimes a flower doesn't look like a flower.

Here are new "flowers" of native Western Ragweed (Ambrosia psilostachya) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a composite related to sunflowers, though you have to study the flowers to see the relationship. It can get weedy. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 October 2012)

File.Name: ca20f_ca19-009.jpg
Notes:
8106887740 These are new flower buds on native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. The buds will wait on the plant for months, until next April or so. On some plants the buds are red, but these are pale. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 October 2012)

It's an interesting strategy to form buds early. Several native plants do it - like Manzanita (Arctostaphylos, Ericaceae), Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii, Garryaceae), and California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae).

Most natives don't form their flower buds early. I wonder if there's an advantage, or is this just how it's always been done?
CommonNames: Sugar Bush; Manzanita; Veatch Silk-Tassel; California Bay
ScientificName: Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; Arctostaphylos; Ericaceae; Garrya veatchii; Garryaceae; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae
associatedTaxa: Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; Arctostaphylos; Ericaceae; Garrya veatchii; Garryaceae; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae

File.Name: ca20g_ca19-016.jpg
Notes: 8106843497 This is the whole shrub of a native composite Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) in the Asteraceae. if you look close, you can see the yellow flowers. These flower heads have ray-flowers, see this photo. It looks pretty scraggly, but you have to admire these late-season plants that flower when most other native shrubs are shriveled at the end of our long dry summer. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 October 2012)

File.Name: ca20h_ca19-011.jpg
Notes: 8106825042 This is a whole shrub of the native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river. There are still a few flowers, but it's mostly gone to seed. I'm sorry to lose the flowers. This is one of the "buggiest" plants we have. The shrubs get 6-8 feet tall, but they are often shorter. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 October 2012)

File.Name: ca21a_ca21-002.jpg
Notes:
8111145016 Here are drops of mist on leaves and tendrils of native Wild Sweet Pea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family, with a filagree of spider webs. It was that kind of day, drippy and wet - with a hint of sun. You can see the woods inverted in the water drops. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2012)

Autumn is the hardest time of the year to get new photos every day, but I'm doing ok so far - and there's a 50% chance of rain tomorrow! I hope that soon there will be mushrooms in the woods.

File.Name: ca21b_ca21-005.jpg
Notes: 8111082854 This is a rather large fly (order Diptera) dipping its proboscis into a staminate flower head of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. It's not pretty, but it is interesting. This fly is visiting flowers, not garbage or dead things. It's big enough to be a Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae, but it doesn't have the typical red eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2012)

File.Name: ca21c_ca21-006.jpg
Notes:
8111047253 A small sign of Autumn...

This is a tiny Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) perched by flower heads of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. It has amazing eyes, and amazing Hopi-like patterns on its wings. This little critter is a sure sign that the seasons are changing. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2012)

File.Name: ca21d_ca21-009.jpg
Notes: 8110997308 These new seedlings in the woods are no doubt weeds - probably annual grasses and Italian Thistle (Carduus pycnocephalus, Asteraceae). But they are new seedlings! We've only had about 1/4 inch of rain this rain year. The natives know better, and wait until we've had an inch or two. These seedlings might not make it. On the other hand, there's a 50% chance of rain tomorrow. If they're lucky they'll get a head start.

File.Name: ca21e_ca21-021.jpg
Notes: 8110970078 This is another "lumpy gall" (my name) on a stem of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. I don't know what insect causes them, and they are not listed in my book on "Field Guide to Plant Galls" by Ron Russo (UC Press, 2006). They always bend the stem around the gall, and often kill the leaves beyond. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2012) Quercus chrysolepis is considered an "Intermediate Oak" - separate from the "Black Oaks" like Coast Live Oak and the "White Oaks" like Scrub Oak. The leaves are sometimes toothed, sometimes not, and they are powdery underneath. For some reason, they are having a good year for galls, but they are scarce in our other oaks.
CommonNames: Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Field Guide to Plant Galls; Coast Live Oak; Scrub Oak

File.Name: ca21f_ca21-029.jpg
Notes:
8110889881 Here are a bunch of "lumpy galls" (my name) on stems of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. I don't know what insect causes them, and they are not listed in my book on "Field Guide to Plant Galls" by Ron Russo (UC Press, 2006). These galls seem to cut off circulation to the leaves beyond them and finally kill them. They always bend the stem around the gall. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2012)

Quercus chrysolepis is considered an "Intermediate Oak" - separate from the "Black Oaks" like Coast Live Oak and the "White Oaks" like Scrub Oak. The leaves are sometimes toothed, sometimes not. For some reason, they are having a good year for galls, but they are scarce in our other oaks.
CommonNames: Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Field Guide to Plant Galls; Coast Live Oak; Scrub Oak

File.Name: ca21g_ca21-013.jpg
Notes:
8110844807 I guess this is the gall of a Yellow Wig Gall Wasp (Andricus fullawayi) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's growing (I think) on a stem of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family in the woods. It's small, with fluffy hairs. I can feel a hard kernel inside with must be the host wasp's chamber. (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2012)

I never see the tiny wasps that make these galls. I think the only way to see them is to "hatch" the galls in an enclosure. I'd rather they went free.
CommonNames: Yellow Wig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Andricus fullawayi; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus fullawayi; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: ca22a_ca22-004.jpg
Notes:
8114775713 Fall colors in the Santa Barbara mountains.

These are autumn leaves of native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family growing in Kinevan Canyon. It seems early for fall colors, but here they are. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2012)

It was gloomy all day, but the gloom was just right to bring out the colors of these leaves. We've had a bit of rain tonight - enough to get the porch wet, but not under the eaves. I hope we get more rain tonight. We really need it.

File.Name: ca22b_ca22-006.jpg
Notes:
8114764676 Fall colors in the Santa Barbara mountains.

These are autumn leaves of native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family growing in Kinevan Canyon. It seems early for fall colors, but here they are. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2012)

File.Name: ca22c_ca22-017.jpg
Notes: 8114747160 Here are new glandular flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family. It's our first native flower of the new rain year. I found this plant in the woods, not the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2012)

File.Name: ca22d_ca22-020.jpg
Notes:
8114648722 "Shelter from the storm"
Well, not much shelter!

I noticed this mature Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) in the columnar cactus (cactaceae) growing in the garden - I believe genus Cereus. I don't know if it can get down! It's already got a few spines stuck in it's foot. (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2012)

I've mostly been seeing little immature lizards out in the open. I think it gets harder and harder for these large, mature lizards to warm up as the sun gets lower.

File.Name: ca22e_ca22-009.jpg
Notes:
8114548831 Here are a pair of uncommon native plants in our mountains, growing on the bank of San Jose Creek today. The broad-leaved plant is native Hemp Dogbane aka Indian Hemp (Apocynum cannabinum) in the Apocynaceae plant family. That's hemp as in fiber. It's an uncommon plant in our mountains - I only know it from two places. The narrow-leaved plant is a Sedge (Carex) in the Cyperaceae plant family - I believe the Santa Barbara Sedge (Carex barbarae). (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2012)

Both native plants were important for the native American Chumash in our mountains - for string, rope, nets, and clothing. For more info, see Jan Timbrook's Chumash Ethnobotany (Heyday Books, 2007)
CommonNames: Hemp Dogbane; Indian Hemp; Sedge; Santa Barbara Sedge
ScientificName: Apocynum cannabinum; Apocynaceae; Carex; Cyperaceae; Carex barbarae; Chumash Ethnobotany
associatedTaxa: Apocynum cannabinum; Apocynaceae; Carex; Cyperaceae; Carex barbarae; Chumash Ethnobotany

File.Name: ca22f_ca22-019.jpg
Notes:
8114526223 These are new red flower buds on native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. The buds will wait on the plant for months, until next April or so. On some plants the buds are pale. (See this photo.) It's an interesting strategy to form buds early, but most natives don't do it. I wonder if there's an advantage, or is this just how it's always been done? (San Marcos Pass, 22 October 2012)

The gloomy day today was just right to bring out the colors of these flower buds.

File.Name: ca22g_ca21-032.jpg
Notes: 8114518000 These are new flower buds of native Basket Bush aka Skunkbush or "Squaw Bush" (Rhus trilobata) shrub in the Anacardiaceae plant family. It's another native shrub that forms buds early to get ready to flower in spring. It's an interesting strategy to form buds early, but most natives don't do it. I wonder if there's an advantage, or is this just how it's always been done? (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2012)

File.Name: ca22h_ca21-031.jpg
Notes: 8114464857 These are new flower buds of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family. Note how furry-tomentose the leaves are underneath. They are another native shrub that forms buds early to get ready to flower in spring. It's an interesting strategy to form buds early, but most natives don't do it. I wonder if there's an advantage, or is this just how it's always been done? (San Marcos Pass, 21 October 2012)

File.Name: ca23a_ca23-007.jpg
Notes:
8117814245 This is a nice "butt shot" of a large Spiny Tachina Fly - species Paradejeania rutiloides, family Tachinidae, order Diptera. This one is perched on a leaf of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family, trying to warm up on a cold morning. My photo shows the remarkable bristles. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2012)

These flies are about 3/4 inch (18 mm) long, bumblebee size. It seems like the ancient "doctrine of signatures" - these bristly flies are apparently predators of the bristly local "woolly bear" caterpillars of tiger moths in the family Arctiidae.

We had a bit of rain last night - just 0.08 inches in the gauge this morning (including a bit of fog-drip from the night before). We need real rain. And it was cold last night - just 40∞ when I got up, and no doubt colder than that last night.
Locality: including a bit of fog-drip from the night before).  We need real rain.  And it was cold last night - just 40∞ when I got up

File.Name: ca23c_ca23-018.jpg
Notes: 8117756550 This is a pretty little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) working pollen-bearing staminate flowers of still-flowering Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family today. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2012)

File.Name: ca23d_ca23-001.jpg
Notes: 8117730762 I found this tiny mushroom growing on debris in the garden this morning after our bit of rain last night. I guess it's a little white-spored Mycena mushroom - the classic "LBM" or "Little Brown Mushroom". We only had 0.08 inches of rain the gauge this morning from the rain last night (and a bit of fog-drip from before), but I'm happy that the mushrooms are trying! (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2012)

File.Name: ca23e_ca23-043.jpg
Notes: 8117686027 This is another near-perfect Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly in the garden. This one is working the flowers of hybrid "Waverly Sage" (Salvia "Waverly" in the Lamiaceae plant family). There are still quite a few of these in the woods, despite the cold weather. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2012)

File.Name: ca23f_ca23-011.jpg
Notes:
8117653282 This is another mature Bush Katydid (genus Scudderia) in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera in the woods on seed heads of native composite Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) plant in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe it's a female - you can make out her ovipositor. She has narrow straight wings, unlike the California Angle-winged Katydid (Microcentrum californicum). (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2012)

This is the adult that comes from the beautiful nymphs I was showing earlier this year - like:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/7332100954/
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/7451733804/
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/7469848714/
... and lots more - I really love the nymphs!

File.Name: ca23g_ca23-028.jpg
Notes: 8117604361 I believe this is another Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper, one of the the Spur-throated Grasshoppers ( Melanoplinae) in the suborder Acrididae in the order Orthoptera. It's mature, but it has very short net-like wings. Its short antennae are mostly hidden by a leaf. See my [Older photo] for a close-up. This one is perched on native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2012)

File.Name: ca23h_ca23-032.jpg
Notes:
8117557145 I believe this is another Oedaleonotus phryneicus grasshopper in the suborder Acrididae in the order Orthoptera - see my [Newer] photo. This one is munching a leaf of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2012)

I know, my photo is not quite sharp - but it's still pretty cool! Grasshoppers have such amazing mouthparts, and they can really take down a leaf fast!

Alice - make an equilateral triangle between the antennae off to the right, and at the apex, I can see a a little hole in its forehead. Any idea what this is?

File.Name: ca24a_ca24-011.jpg
Notes:
8120927568 Here is a Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly, in the family Libellulidae of the order Odonata. This one is perched on a broken dead twig of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It has amazing eyes - like bifocals! This is our most common dragonfly in the fall, with no water to be found. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2012)

It started out breezy today, and got down-right windy. I got these photos pretty early, before the wind kicked up too bad. *Sigh* The wind will suck all the new moisture from the soil. The "Fuel Moisture" as measured down by the river at Los Prietos has already dropped to 8%. It's still fire season.

File.Name: ca24c_ca24-002.jpg
Notes:
8120833241 This is a handsome Shaggy Mane (Coprinus comatus) mushroom that I noticed on the edge of the garden this morning. The mushrooms start white, but they turn black as they "deliquesce" - literally digest themselves into slime! They are edible and delicious - if prepared fresh. This one is already way too old. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2012)

This is growing in a place that we water once and a while over the summer, but here it is after a bit of real rain in the fall. I wonder what the difference is!

File.Name: ca24d_ca24-021.jpg
Notes: 8120824412 Here is another Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working flowers of non-native Fuchsia in the Onagraceae plant family in the garden. It's committing "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering through the sepals rather than through the throat of the flower so that pollination might happen. Many insects know this trick. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2012)

File.Name: ca24e_ca23-004.jpg
Notes:
8120716475 This is a different "rolled oak leaf" from my [Previous] photo. For one thing, the leaf is rolled down, not up. This oak leaf belongs to a native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. They look like galls to me. Note how the leaf structure is changed along the fold. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2012)

There is a "Roll Gall Midge" (Contarinia) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera that is said to cause "roll galls" of leaves of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis). I wonder if this is something similar?

There are also a couple of tiny Whitefly pupae in the photo. I believe they are "Stanfordís Whitefly" (Tetraleurodes stanfordi) in the family Aleyrodidae in the old order Homoptera.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Whitefly
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Contarinia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Quercus chrysolepis; Tetraleurodes stanfordi; Aleyrodidae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Contarinia; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Quercus chrysolepis; Tetraleurodes stanfordi; Aleyrodidae; Homoptera

File.Name: ca24f_ca24-005.jpg
Notes:
8120626349 This is just a Fly in the order Diptera on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. Maybe it's a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae with those red eyes and bristles on its abdomen? (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2012)

I'm more interested in the way the leaf is folded over - or up. Sure, there's a critter in there. But is it a gall, or did some insect larvae roll the leaf over and tie it down? There are lots of insects that make nests on the oak leaves - weevils and moth caterpillars, to name two. Note that this leaf is folded up, and there is no exit. If you know anything about this, please let me know!
CommonNames: Fly; Coast Live Oak; Tachinid Fly

File.Name: ca24g_ca24-004.jpg
Notes:
8120593999 This is a pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched by the spine on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2012)

It's interesting that these Syrphid flies are most active in the fall and spring. It makes sense. The flies lay eggs that hatch into larvae that parasitize the larvae of other insects, and fall and spring are the busy times.
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: ca24h_ca24-003.jpg
Notes:
8120570895 This is another photo of a large bristly Spiny Tachina Fly - species Paradejeania rutiloides, family Tachinidae, order Diptera. It's trying to warm up on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family that's pointed towards the sun. It was cold this morning - 36∞ on the upstairs porch when I went out to have my coffee. (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2012)

These flies are about 3/4 inch (18 mm) long, bumblebee size. It seems like the ancient "doctrine of signatures" - these bristly flies are apparently predators of the bristly local "woolly bear" caterpillars of tiger moths in the family Arctiidae.
ScientificName: Paradejeania rutiloides; Tachinidae; Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Arctiidae

File.Name: ca25a_ca25-019.jpg
Notes:
8123734404 Here is a small harvest of early fruit and nuts of the native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) trees in the Lauraceae plant family growing in the canyon today, with mostly bay leaves in the background. They are related to avocados, and you can see some resemblance. There are green fruit, ripe fruit fruit turning brown, old black skins, and bay nuts in the middle. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2012)

Have you ever accidentally eaten the "Bay Leaf" in a stew? *Yuck* Our native bay trees are like that only more so - and the fruit are even more so than the leaves. But hidden inside the fruit is a real nut which is delicious when dark roasted - rich, almost like bitter chocolate or coffee. They are really good, habit forming. I'll post a recipe here when the season comes. If you can't wait, see this YouTube video!

It was another windy day today, though the wind curiously died down instead of getting stronger as night came. The forecast is for a strong Santa Anna wind event down south. Fortunately, we don't get those winds here, with no direct path to the desert. (We have our own Sundowner Winds to deal with!)

File.Name: ca25b_ca25-021.jpg
Notes:
8123698898 This is strange...

Here is a native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family with flowers - a few months early! Even stranger, the flowers are coming from a thick-stemmed branch that's been galled by a Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth (Periploca ceanothiella) in the order Lepidoptera. Maybe the gall interfered with the plant's internal clock? (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2012)

I just show what I find each day in the woods. Sometimes I can't explain it.
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Periploca ceanothiella; Lepidoptera
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Periploca ceanothiella; Lepidoptera

File.Name: ca25c_ca25-009.jpg
Notes: 8123670600 These are new seeds of a female pistillate Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. Like baby birds and college-age kids, they are leaving home. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2012)

File.Name: ca25d_ca25-001.jpg
Notes:
8123651384 "Who ordered pizza with ink and sand?"

This is all that's left of the Shaggy Mane (Coprinus comatus) mushroom that I found on the edge of the garden yesterday. See my [Older] photo. They turn black as they "deliquesce" - literally digest themselves into black slime! They are edible and delicious - if prepared fresh. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2012)

File.Name: ca25e_ca25-005.jpg
Notes:
8123563322 This is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis or kin) in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera trying to warm up on a cold morning on a grape leaf in the garden. Look how it's holding its petiolate abdomen up on its wings into the sun! (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2012)

This should have been a better photo... It's hard to switch between shooting mushrooms (see my [Newer] photo) and insects. I remembered to change the ISO and the white balance, and totally forgot to change the f/number. I got this photo at f/22 with resulting low shutter speed. I'm sure it won't be the last time I forget to change a setting!
Locality: see my [Newer] photo) and insects.  I remembered to change the ISO and the white balance

Notes:
8123511465 This is a flock of tiny Whitefly pupae on the underside of a leaf of a native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe they are Stanfordís Whitefly (Tetraleurodes stanfordi) in the family Aleyrodidae in the old order Homoptera. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2012)

See p.44-5 of "A Field Guide to Insects and Diseases of California Oaks" at:

File.Name: ca25g_ca25-011.jpg
Notes:
8123426045 This is another "rolled oak leaf" on native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I guess it's the work of a "leaf rolling insect" rather then a gall as UrbanWoodswalker said in a comment yesterday. You can see the lumpy evidence of the critter inside. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2012)

I've been thinking about this, and "leaf rollers" and "gall insects" really have a similar strategy - to have the plant provide food and shelter. The more I think about it, the more similar they seem. Both kinds of insect get shelter and food from the host plant. And how can one folded leaf provide all the sustenance that an insect larvae needs without some help? Now that I think about it, how does the insect fold the leaf UP rather than DOWN in its natural direction? Is it a gall?

*Too many questions...*

File.Name: ca25h_ca24-015.jpg
Notes: 8123423144 Here is one more photo of the beautiful Variegated Meadowhawk (Sympetrum corruptum) dragonfly in the family Libellulidae of the order Odonata that I showed yesterday. This one is perched on a dead twig of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family, and its whole body is in focus. It perches with wings forward. Heh - it's got a klingon on its tail! (San Marcos Pass, 24 October 2012)

File.Name: ca26a_ca26-015.jpg
Notes:
8126645352 This is a mature Bush Katydid (genus Scudderia) in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera sneaking up on a native flower of composite Bush Groundsel aka Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. Pretty cool! (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2012)

It was warmer today, and the winds didn't kick up as expected - a beautiful autumn day. I have a commitment to work tomorrow at the annual Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society Plant Sale - at Ron and Diane Griffin's Manzanita Nursery, 880 Chalk Hill Road, Solvang. We'll see if I can get any photos while working.

File.Name: ca26b_ca26-009.jpg
Notes:
8126593586 Here is a tiny "looper" type caterpillar (Lepidoptera) on a staminate flower head of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe it is a Emerald moth in the genus Synchlora of the Geometrid Moths (Geometridae). They have a way of decorating themselves with bits of the flowers they are feeding on, and they become the green moths on our window screens at night. See this photo for another. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2012)

I was lucky to notice this tiny creature. It is very well camouflaged!
CommonNames: Coyote Bush; Emerald moth; Geometrid Moths

File.Name: ca26c_ca26-040.jpg
Notes:
8126536579 This is a beautiful Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a new pollen-bearing staminate flower head of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family today. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2012)

I didn't think these shrubs would flower at all in this very dry year, but they finally do have some flowers. The flowers should have been out a month ago. They are flowering too late for most of our pollinator insects, but there are a few.

File.Name: ca26d_ca26-043.jpg
Notes: 8126537522 I'm not sure if this is a Carpenter Bee (Xylocopa, Anthophoridae, Hymenoptera) or a Bumble Bee (Bombus, Anthophila, Hymenoptera). Here it is snuggling into a late-morning flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family today. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 October 2012)

Notes: 8126489685 These are new colonial plants surrounding the now-dead flowering stalk of a native Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) in the Liliaceae plant family. I believe this is what makes them Yucca whippleissp. percusa. The plants die after they flower, but they leave offspring. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 October 2012)

File.Name: ca26f_ca26-024.jpg
Notes: 8126475846 Here is a odd galled leaf of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. I don't know what critter causes this. These deformed leaves were quite common last year, but they are scarce this year. You can see how the leaves are covered with "stellate hairs" - hairs that are branched star-like - and they collect golden fuzz underneath. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2012)

File.Name: ca26g_ca26-052.jpg
Notes:
8126418945 These are acorn cups on a different native Scrub Oak - Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens in the Fagaceae plant family. The leaves of this scrub oak are thinner and shiny underneath, quite different from Q. berberidifolia or Q. chrysolepis. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 October 2012)

I was looking for galls on this oak, and didn't notice any. But now I can see a "roll gall" on the top-most leaf. Maybe it's caused by a "Roll Gall Midge" (Contarinia) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera?

File.Name: ca26h_ca26-050.jpg
Notes:
8126378273 I haven't thought much about our native Tanbark Oaks aka Tanoaks (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family since they stopped flowering, but this is interesting. The should have ripening acorns by now, but as you can see, they haven't developed their acorns at all. I guess this is another scene from a drought year. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 26 October 2012)

The acorns don't look much different that they did last May. See my photo from May 30 at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/7317626626/

File.Name: ca27a_ca27-005.jpg
Notes:
8129615144 This is a large bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae - with some of its web. The web doesn't look so neat up close like this! I guess times are hard for these large spiders since there are few pollinator insects out in the woods dry woods right now. I reckon there are fewer of the large spiders too. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2012)

I did my duty at the annual Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society Plant Sale today - at Ron and Diane Griffin's Manzanita Nursery in Solvang. I'm tired. These four photos will do just fine tonight.

File.Name: ca27b_ca27-008.jpg
Notes:
8129522361 These are new male staminate flowers of native California Croton (Croton californicus) in the Euphorbiaceae plant family. It's growing in sandy soil in the parking area of Ron and Diane Griffin's Manzanita Nursery on Chalk Hill Road, outside of Solvang. This perennial is dioecious with separate male and female plants. This one is a gentleman. It's kind of weedy, like some of our other late-season natives. (Chalk Hill Road, Solvang, 27 October 2012)

I was happy to find it, as I've been collecting photos of late summer native flowers. This one doesn't grow in the mountains. It's distribution is pretty much confined to the coastal areas below Solvang.

Always looking for insects... There's a tiny "True Bug" in the order Hemiptera on the small leaf directly below the top-most flower on the right. It's a well-camouflaged gray bug on a gray leaf.
CommonNames: California Croton; Manzanita Nursery; True Bug

File.Name: ca27c_ca23-024.jpg
Notes: 8129496375 This is a perfect male Tree Cricket (genus Oecanthus in the family Gryllidae of the order Orthoptera) hiding in a spent flower head of Bird's Beak (Cordylanthus rigidus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. The males have the large flat wings and the females are more slender - for example this photo. These crickets sleep all day and work the night shift. They are the songsters of our woods in the fall. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2012)
ScientificName: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Cordylanthus rigidus; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Oecanthus; Gryllidae; Orthoptera; Cordylanthus rigidus; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: ca27d_ca26-017.jpg
Notes:
8129465396 This is another photo of the mature Bush Katydid (genus Scudderia) in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera sneaking up on a native flower of composite Bush Groundsel aka Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. See my [Older] photo yesterday. It's picking up some color on its face from the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2012)

These flowers sure are pretty for flowering as late as they do in our summer-dry mountains. We're still waiting for real rain, though we've had a taste of the wet stuff.
CommonNames: Bush Katydid; Bush Groundsel; Douglas' Groundsel
ScientificName: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; Asteraceae

File.Name: ca28a_ca28-030.jpg
Notes:
8133424175 I found this beautiful Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera) perched on a rock by the dry wash near the river today. It has great camouflage colors for posing on the ground, but they are not so good on this bare rock. It's medium size, and seems to have yellow legs. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 October 2012)

Alice says it's a male Pallid-winged Grasshopper (Trimerotropis pallidipennis). That's works for me!

Today was a perfect photo day - warm and not much wind. I hiked my same ol' trail by the river. Insects are getting scarce, but I found a few.
Locality:
Trimerotropis pallidipennis). That's works for me!

Today was a perfect photo day - warm and not much wind. I hiked my same ol' trail by the river. Insects are getting scarce

File.Name: ca28b_ca28-042.jpg
Notes:
8133368444 This is a small Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera) - only about 1/2 inch long. I guess it's a Texas Spotted Range Grasshopper (Psoloessa texana) - a Slant-faced Grasshopper in the subfamily Gomphocerinae . They are small and well-camouflaged. I mostly found them hopping away from me today! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 October 2012)

Aliceinwl1 has been helping me understand local grasshoppers this summer - though of course if I'm wrong about the ID, it's all my fault!
CommonNames: Short-Horned Grasshopper; Texas Spotted Range Grasshopper; Slant-faced Grasshopper

File.Name: ca28c_ca28-016.jpg
Notes:
8133332392 I thought this was a Thalassica grasshopper (Trimerotropis thalassica) grasshopper, but Alice says it's a Pallid-winged Grasshopper (Trimerotropis pallidipennis). At least I was close!

I found it on the dry flood plain of the river today. It's a Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera). It's large, and it has greenish wings when it takes wing. It's well-camouflaged on the dry river wash. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 October 2012)
CommonNames: Thalassica grasshopper; Pallid-winged Grasshopper; Short-Horned Grasshopper
ScientificName: Trimerotropis thalassica; Trimerotropis pallidipennis; Acrididae; Orthoptera
associatedTaxa: Trimerotropis thalassica; Trimerotropis pallidipennis; Acrididae; Orthoptera

File.Name: ca28d_ca28-015.jpg
Notes: 8133266560 This is a small Fly in the order Diptera working one of the last flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river today. Maybe it's a "Root-Maggot Fly" in the Anthomyiidae family? It has remarkable eyes. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 October 2012)

File.Name: ca28e_ca28-014.jpg
Notes:
8133197393 There are still Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterflies in the woods, and there are still a few flowers of native composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash by the river for them to nectar on. Butterflies and flowers are both getting scarce. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 October 2012)

I also saw Mormon Metalmarks (Apodemia mormo); Common Buckeyes (Junonia coenia); a Sulfur (Colias); and a Checkered White (Pontia protodice) butterfly along the river today. That's about it. Their time is ending.
CommonNames: Painted Lady; Scale Broom; Mormon Metalmarks; Common Buckeyes; Sulfur; Checkered White
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Lepidospartum squamatum; Asteraceae; Apodemia mormo; Junonia coenia; Colias; Pontia protodice

File.Name: ca28f_ca28-021.jpg
Notes: 8133138748 I guess this is the recurved mature gall of the Rosette Bud Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia ericameriae) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. It's growing on native composite Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) in the Asteraceae plant family. The galls look like little pine cones. The galls usually kill the branch, so it's unusual to get a gall with flowers in the same photo - but here they are. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 October 2012)
CommonNames: Rosette Bud Gall Midge; Palmer's Goldenbush

File.Name: ca28g_ca28-037.jpg
Notes:
8133089704 This is an astounding wall of non-native and mostly dead White Sweet Clover (Melilotus albus) in the Fabaceae plant family growing along the Santa Ynez River. It's 8+ feet tall. You can get lost in there! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 28 October 2012)

I usually hike alone, but I'll have to get back to this place with Julie as my model to show scale. I don't remember this rampant growth last year.

File.Name: ca28h_ca27-015.jpg
Notes:
8132979315 These are galls of the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the order Homoptera) growing on a Manzanita cultivar (Arctostaphylos) in the Ericaceae plant family. There are many species of manzanita in California, and a zillion cultivars. I have no idea what this one is. (Chalk Hill Road, Solvang, 27 October 2012)

I got this photo at Ron and Diane Griffin's Manzanita Nursery in Solvang during the SYVNHS plant sale yesterday. I guess this photo proves they are organic, as the galls are made by aphids! They have a great selection of interesting native and water-wise plants.
CommonNames: Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid; Manzanita; Manzanita Nursery

associatedTaxa: Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera; Arctostaphylos; Ericaceae

File.Name: ca29a_ca29-014.jpg
Notes:
8137128612 We had a blessed event in the canyon!

These are tiny spiderlings on their nest - mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae is wisely staying a few inches away. There is a family resemblance - look at the chelicerae of that tiny spiderling just below and to the right of center, and compare my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2012)

I've been hoping for rain, but not THAT much! My best wishes for all who have been impacted by Hurricane Sandy,

File.Name: ca29b_ca29-016.jpg
Notes: 8137065759 Here is mama Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae - mother to the clutch of spiderlings in my [Next] photo. She perched in a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. She's wisely staying a couple of inches away from the brood. I'm a grandpa - I can relate! (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2012)
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: ca29c_ca29-007.jpg
Notes:
8137029568 I first thought this was the nymph of a "True Bug" in the order Hemiptera - it seems to have a scutellum. I guess that's part of the reason why the convenient old orders of Hemiptera and Homoptera have been folded together. (I'll keep using the old names - I first learned them first!)

But no, it's a different little Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea in the order Homoptera on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. See my [Previous] photo which shows its face. I'm not sure what kind of planthopper it is. It looks like it's carved from stone. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2012)
ScientificName: Hemiptera; Hemiptera; Homoptera; Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: ca29d_ca29-005.jpg
Notes: 8136989682 I found this different little Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea in the order Homoptera on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family today. I don't know what kind it is, but the flat face and low antennae nail the ID. I guess this is a nymph, with immature wings. See my [Next] (and better) photo. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2012)

File.Name: ca29e_ca29-009.jpg
Notes: 8136906165 This is another little net-winged Planthopper in the family Issidae of the superfamily Fulgoroidea in the order Homoptera on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family today. Maybe it's genus Neaethus. Compare my [Newer] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2012)

File.Name: ca29f_ca26-022.jpg
Notes:
8136865779 This is a different gall on Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's a bud gall, different from the stem galls I've shown before. You can see the exit hole where the critter got out. Or did some other critter get in? (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2012)

I don't know what critter makes this gall, but it looks like the gall of a Cynipid Gall wasp in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It has the same shape but different colors as the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella). Compare this photo.

File.Name: ca29g_ca29-019.jpg
Notes: 8136856772 I believe this is the gall of the Willow Rosette Gall Midge (Rabdophaga salicisbrassicoides) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. It's growing on an Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family by the creek. Willows have long slender leaves, so this is definitely something else. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2012)

File.Name: ca29h_ca28-045.jpg
Notes: 8136795215 The seedpods of the garden milkweed picked yesterday to open and send their seeds to the wind. I guess this is an orange-flowered cultivar of Butterfly Weed (Asclepias tuberosa), but I'm not sure. Bon voyage, little wings! (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2012)

File.Name: ca30a_ca30-007.jpg
Notes:
8140444255 I believe this is an Orange Sulphur (Colias eurytheme) butterfly with the sun behind to show the dark patterns on the upper wings. It's not quite a dog-face, but there is a resemblance. They are said to be common, and I've seen them for a while. This is the first one I've manged to get a photo of. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 October 2012)

Today was another perfect day for photos - warm and little wind. I went for a drive along East Camino Cielo, and down Gibraltar Road to Santa Barbara.

File.Name: ca30b_ca30-024.jpg
Notes: 8140416187 There are still a few Mormon Metalmark (Apodemia mormo) butterflies - probably "Behr's Metalmark" (Apodemia mormo virgulti aka Apodemia virgulti) - in the mountains, but not many. This one is working a new flower of native composite Bush Groundsel aka Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 October 2012)
CommonNames: Mormon Metalmark; Behr's Metalmark; Bush Groundsel; Douglas' Groundsel

File.Name: ca30c_ca30-026.jpg
Notes: 8140402638 Here are flowers and seedheads and empty receptacles of native composite Bush Groundsel aka Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a wonderful plant that can still produce flowers at the end of our dry summer season! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 October 2012)

File.Name: ca30d_ca30-009.jpg
Notes: 8140377624 This is a common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, order Hymenoptera) snuggling into a late-morning flower of native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family today. The pistils of the flower head are remarkable - more worthy of focus than the bee! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 October 2012)
