File.Name: ca30e_ca30-005.jpg
Notes: 8140345052 These are new late-season flowers of Tall Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria virgata ssp. pleurocarpa) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's larger than S. exigua, and the phyllaries are not reflexed. The individual flowers are pretty, like they're wearing purple socks. The pistils are especially pretty - like those old drawings of ovaries, fallopian tubes, and ovaries they used to show us in gym class. Well, I guess that's right! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 October 2012)
ScientificName: Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa; Asteraceae; S. exigua
associatedTaxa: Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa; Asteraceae; S. exigua

File.Name: ca30f_ca26-027.jpg
Notes: 8140328232 This is an immaculate flower of native Small Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria exigua) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's It's a small plant, less than one foot tall. It is rather pretty, with those violet stamens and pistils that look like they've been dipped in sugar - but it looks kind of weedy when you actually see it. The phyllaries are reflexed. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2012)

File.Name: ca30g_ca30-030.jpg
Notes:
8140210319 Here are new flowers of native California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. These are growing near the top of our mountains. (Near La Cumbre Peak, Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 October 2012)

This is a pleasant arrangement. Usually these flowers are diffusely branched, and hard to photograph.

File.Name: ca30h_ca30-002.jpg
Notes: 8140190873 This is another surprisingly beautiful grape-sized gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. Heh - I think there's another petiole gall on the base of the green leaf to the right. Autumn is the time for galls in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 30 October 2012)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: ca31a_ca31-005.jpg
Notes:
8143526805 Here is my creepy photo for Halloween...

This is a large bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae clutching a shrouded Wasp in the order Hymenoptera. Inside the shroud, I can make out the petioled-abdomen which pegs the unfortunate wasp as a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis or kin) in the family Vespidae. It's a tough world - but what's bad for the wasp is good for the spider! (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2012)
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; Wasp; Western Paper Wasp

File.Name: ca31b_ca30-043.jpg
Notes:
8143543836 Update (3 Nov 12): I got copies of these weed photos to David Chang at the County of Santa Barbara Agricultural Commissioner's Office, and he thinks it's a Lanceleaf Nightshade (Solanum lanceolatum) which is listed as a "B"-rated noxious weed in California. David visited the site and got photos to send to CDFA for confirmation. I'll be darned - real action!

Original comment:
There's nothing scarier for Halloween than a new weed in the mountains!

This is a closer photo of the non-native Silver-Leaved Horsenettle (Solanum elaeagnifolium) in the Solanaceae plant family. I found these growing near the bottom of Gibraltar Road yesterday. My only hesitation about the ID is that the Jepson eFlora says it gets a meter tall, and these plants are 5-6 feet. (So I might be wrong about the ID.) See my [Previous] photo for the whole plants. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 30 October 2012)

I'll admit, it is rather pretty for being a weed. It's a small infestation in just one place - new for me, though I don't live in the foothills. I've sent photos to a couple of local restoration people for confirmation - and elimination!

File.Name: ca31c_ca30-041.jpg
Notes:
8143425763 Update (3 Nov 12) I believe this is a Lanceleaf Nightshade (Solanum lanceolatum) which is listed as a "B"-rated noxious weed in California. See mt [Newer] photo for more info.

I guess this is a hedge of non-native Silver-Leaved Horsenettle (Solanum elaeagnifolium) in the Solanaceae plant family. I found these growing near the bottom of Gibraltar Road yesterday. My only hesitation about the ID is that the Jepson eFlora says it gets a meter tall, and these plants are 5-6 feet. (So I might be wrong about the ID.) See my [Next] photo for a closer look. I'll admit, it is rather pretty for being a weed. It's a small infestation in just one place - new for me, though I don't live in the foothills. I've sent photos to a couple of local restoration people for confirmation - and elimination!

File.Name: ca31d_ca31-016.jpg
Notes:
8143408181 These are ripening berries of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family - starting to show some red colors. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2012)

The raw berries are astringent - they dry your mouth out - but they are tasty when cooked and were a native American Chumash staple. I've made a sweet Toyon cider by pouring hot water over the berries and letting it sit, like tea. Delicious!

File.Name: ca31e_ca31-015.jpg
Notes:
8143372529 This is another photo of ripe nuts on native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family growing in our mountains. The nuts are small and taste rather bland, but I'll bet they would be good roasted. The long "tails" on the nut covers are distinctive. They are another deciduous shrub that gives us fall colors as the old leaves turn gold for fall. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2012)

The flowers of this native shrub appear in spring and have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. They seem almost too beautiful to be native chaparral plants. See my photo of the flowers.

File.Name: ca31f_ca31-008.jpg
Notes:
8143318113 I guess these count as galls, thought they are made by a fungus rather than an insect. These are shriveled late-season growths of the Manzanita Leaf Fungus (Exobasidium vacinii) on leaves of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2012)

The galls of the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the order Homoptera) have more obvious pockets. See this photo.
CommonNames: Manzanita Leaf Fungus; Bigberry Manzanita; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid
ScientificName: Exobasidium vacinii; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Exobasidium vacinii; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera

File.Name: ca31g_ca31-022.jpg
Notes:
8143235567 This is a large walnut-sized unisexual summer gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. This one is on a stem of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. These wasps have very different spring bisexual galls - see my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2012)

Male and female wasps emerge from the spring galls, mate, and form these large "oak apple" summer galls, which contain only females. The ladies will emerge next spring to parthenogenetically produce a new generation of new spring galls that hold both males and females. It's complicated, but life is like that!

The more familiar "Oak Apple Gall" that is made by the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) is different. They favor "White Oaks" like Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia). See this photo
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak; California Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Quercus berberidifolia
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Quercus berberidifolia

File.Name: ca31h_ca31-003.jpg
Notes: 8143200829 I believe this is the gall of a Ruptured Twig Gall Wasp (Callirhytis perdens) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera) growing on a stem of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family today. Many of the oak galls I've been showing lately are "detachable" - you can break them off. But this gall is "integral" - part of the stem. There is a hole on the right, which I believe is an exit hole where the critter got out - or is it an entrance hole where some parasitic critter got in? (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2012)
ScientificName: Callirhytis perdens; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis perdens; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: cb01a_cb01-021.jpg
Notes:
8146599066 This common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, order Hymenoptera) is committing the crime of "nectar theft" - robbing the flower by entering through the sepals rather than through the throat of the flower so that pollination might happen. Many insects know this trick. The flower is native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata) in the Labiatae plant family. It's native, but it does quite well in our garden with summer watering - spreading like a groundcover and flowering all summer. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2012)

A now 25 year-old former Dunn Middle School student came by the house to visit today, just as I was editing photos. When is the last time you called on one of your retired middle school teachers?! We had a good visit, but it chewed up some time. Four photos are plenty tonight.
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Stachys bullata; Labiatae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Stachys bullata; Labiatae

File.Name: cb01b_cb01-011.jpg
Notes: 8146578322 Here is another photo of spiderlings of a Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridans) in the family Oxyopidae on their nest in a native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I like that tiny shed skin of a spiderling in the upper left. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2012)
ScientificName: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Peucetia viridans; Oxyopidae; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: cb01c_cb01-003.jpg
Notes: 8146522744 Some insect galls are large - the size of walnuts or tangerines or ping-pong balls. This one is tiny. I believe this is the gall of a "Pumpkin Gall Wasp" (Dryocosmus minusculus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera. It's growing on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The galls are only about 1 mm across, so the wasps inside must be really tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2012)

Notes: 8146458975 This is the almost-full Halloween moon rising through the native Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri) on the ridge above our home. This was a quick photo without checking camera settings. By the time I got them right, the moon was too high. It's a very peaceful photo. (San Marcos Pass, 31 October 2012)

File.Name: cb02a_cb02-014.jpg
Notes:
8149272217 This is a beautiful big-headed Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) moving between flower heads of a pollen-bearing staminate Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. This fly was rather large - not as big as a honey bee, but close. That white face is remarkable. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2012)

It was a chilly morning, so I drove a couple of miles up the road to a Coyote Bush that I knew was still flowering. I found lots of interesting flies on the flowers. When you can't find anything to photograph, just park by one plant and wait!
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Coyote Bush; Coyote Bush

This fly was rather large - not as big as a honey bee, but close. That white face is remarkable. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2012)

8149260834 I believe this fly is the "Secondary Screwworm" (Cochliomyia macellaria). It has the iridescent colors of a common Blow Fly (family Calliphoridae in the order Diptera) - a trash fly - but the bright orange cheeks and beard distinguish it. This one is working new male staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2012)

If I'm right, this fly is in the family Calliphoridae, but it's more interesting than the common trash flies. This fly is native to North America. It's larvae feed on dead tissue, including human tissue. But it is the "secondary" screwworm that only feeds on already dead tissue.

It actually has some medical uses. It has forensic use as the "principal species on which to base postmortem interval estimations". And they have been used "in some cases as surgical maggots" to clean only necrotic tissue from wounds. There's more info at Wikipedia.

File.Name: cb02h_cb02-001.jpg
Notes:
8149086173 This is a pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) trying to warm up on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family that's pointed towards the sun. The rest of my photos today are different flies on staminate flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. When you can't find anything to photograph, just park by one plant and wait!

File.Name: cb03a_cb03-004.jpg
Notes:
8152573671 This is another Bagrada Bug (Bagrada hilaris) in the family Pentatomidae in the order Hemiptera on a leaf native of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family on the dry edge of garden at home. The rumor is that this is a serious pest of crops in the Lompoc Valley. It is a recent introduction - from June, 2008. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2012)

Apparently they are native to northern Africa, and are now munching kale, Brussels sprouts, and broccoli in Lompoc. See this link. There's good info at the Center for Invasive Species Research (CISR) website.

Today's photos are all weeds and flies. It's getting hard to get my photos every day before we get real rain and mushrooms, but I'll keep trying!

File.Name: cb03b_cb02-006.jpg
Notes:
8152547731 This is another new weed in our mountains. I believe it is Skeleton Weed (Chondrilla juncea) in the Asteraceae plant family. The flowers are rather pretty. They close before noon, which reminds me of genus Stephanomeria - and they look like it too. I found a colony of these weeds along East Camino Cielo Road in the mountains. See my [Older] photo. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 2 November 2012)

This is considered a type "A" noxious weed in California - "A pest of known economic or environmental detriment and is either not known to be established in California or it is present in a limited distribution that allows for the possibility of eradication or successful containment." (CDFA Encycloweedia)

I alerted David Chang at the County of Santa Barbara's Agricultural Commissioner's Office about this. He says it's known from Live Oak Campground and from the railroad tracks at Linden in Carpinteria, and this is a new location. He adds "So many weeds, so little time & money.
Locality:

File.Name: cb03d_cb01-018.jpg
Notes: 8152347689 These are the soft seed plumes of non-native Smilo Grass (Piptatherum miliaceum) in the Poaceae plant family. I reckon this has become the worst invasive weed in our mountains. It grows along roads like other weeds, but it's also getting established in the woods and along the river. I've seen it get worse over the years. It's deceptive, because it really does look like a native bunch grass if you don't look too close. It doesn't have the kind of seeds that stick in your socks, but I've been noticing it along the trails I regularly hike. I hope I'm not responsible. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2012)

File.Name: cb03e_ca27-010.jpg
Notes:
8152346308 These are beautiful tubular flowers of Tree Tobacco (Nicotiana glauca) in the Solanaceae plant family. It's a common weedy shrub introduced into California from South America back in Spanish days. This particular shrub near the entrance of Ron and Dianne's Manzanita Nursery is especially beautiful. Hummingbirds like it. (Chalk Hill Road, Solvang, 27 October 2012)

Tree Tobacco is real tobacco, but there are rumors on the web that it doesn't contain nicotine, but anabasine which is something similar. There is a market for it on the "Legal Highs" web sites, and it's being promoted as a smoking herb to help quit smoking. Good, pick them all! But please leave this one plant at Manzanita Nursery that is so beautiful!

Sure, it's a weed, but it's pretty well behaved unlike the weeds in my [Newer] photos. This one sticks to the road sides and waste places in town. It doesn't seem to invade the woods much, at least not in the mountains.
ScientificName: Nicotiana glauca; Solanaceae; Manzanita Nursery; anabasine
associatedTaxa: Nicotiana glauca; Solanaceae; Manzanita Nursery; anabasine

What brilliant patriotic colors it has on its hind legs. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2012)

Don't forget to vote on Tuesday!

Notes: 8156275107 I'll go ahead and guess that this is a Pallid-winged Grasshopper (Trimerotropis pallidipennis). If I'm right, it's a member of the Band-winged Grasshoppers (subfamily Oedipodinae) of the Short-Horned Grasshoppers (family Acrididae) in the order Orthoptera. This one is perfectly camouflaged on the chip seal road in our canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2012)

File.Name: cb04d_cb04-005.jpg
Notes: 8156247537 This is the web of a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae strung between branches of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. You can barely make out the tiny spider sitting in the center. This web is about the size of a CD - and it has interesting radial reflections like a CD too. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2012)

File.Name: cb04e_cb04-027.jpg
Notes:
8156216384 This is an odd spider web for two reasons. First, it has a neat multistory "house" in the center. Second, the web at first looks haphazard, but if you look very close, you can see that there is an orb web right behind it. It might help to view my original photo.

I guess this is the web of a Labyrinth Spider (genus Metepeira) in the family Araneidae of Orb Weavers. It's strung between branches of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family by the creek today. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2012)

File.Name: cb04f_cb04-003.jpg
Notes: 8156180150 This large Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae had a good morning hunting! It had nothing when I went away, but when I came back a 1/2 hour later it had three Vespid Wasps in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera. I guess the two on the outside are Western Paper Wasps (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis or kin) and the one in the center is a Yellow Jacket-type wasp. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2012)
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; Vespid Wasps; Western Paper Wasps; Yellow Jacket

File.Name: cb04g_cb02-004.jpg
Notes:
8156032476 This is a tiny bullet-headed Leafhopper insect in the family Cicadellidae of the order Homoptera on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family on the dry edge of garden at home. I don't know anything about it - but here it is! (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2012)

Warning - if you do a search for "leafhopper" at BugGuide, you get a ridiculous number of photos back! There are lots of these little critters.

File.Name: cb04h_cb03-034.jpg
Notes:
8155937399 Look in the lower-right of my photo - here is the odd cocoon of a Ribbed Casemaker Moth (genus Bucculatrix, family Bucculatricidae) in the order Lepidoptera. The larvae are host-plant specific, and this is on the lower side of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf, so it may well be a Live Oak Ribbed Casemaker (Bucculatrix albertiella) moth. Did the tiny moth get out or did a parasite get in? There are new buds in the upper-left, hope for the new rain year. I got this photo in the last bit of sunlight on our porch. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2012)

The moths are definitely "microlepidoptera" - tiny little critters. The tiny moth larvae "skeletonize" oak leaves by eating the soft tissue between the top and bottom surfaces of the leaves. That gives you a sense of scale.
CommonNames: Ribbed Casemaker Moth; Coast Live Oak; Live Oak Ribbed Casemaker
ScientificName: Bucculatrix; Bucculatricidae; Lepidoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Bucculatrix albertiella
associatedTaxa: Bucculatrix; Bucculatricidae; Lepidoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Bucculatrix albertiella

Notes:
8159883444 I believe this is a rather threadbare Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly on pollen-bearing staminate flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. Threadbare or not, I'm happy to see it. They've been scarce in this drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2012)

Just four photos today - I have to actually study the ballot before I vote tomorrow!

Notes: 8159781867 I haven't see this critter for a while, but here is a small but mighty Ambush Bug (family Phymatidae) in the order Hemiptera, probably the Pacific Ambush Bug (Phymata pacifica). It's resting on pollen-bearing staminate flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. You have to admire its crab claw-like front legs! File.Name: cb05d_cb05-045.jpg
Notes: 8159750631 This is a different view of a beautiful Common Buckeye (Junonia coenia) butterfly on pollen-bearing staminate flowers of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's not the best photo, but it captures the moment. These butterflies have been scarce in our mountains lately. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2012)

File.Name: cb06a_cb06-028.jpg
Notes:
8163111392 I believe this is the Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides) - a large bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae. Like many of these spiders that perch on their webs all day, it seems to have a face on its abdomen - maybe two faces! Those two little white-backed eyes are remarkable. Of course, the spider's real eyes are on its head, down between its legs. It's an amazing critter, but I don't want it on me! (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2012)

The election results seem to be in as I'm writing this. I'm not disappointed!

File.Name: cb06b_cb06-036.jpg
Notes: 8163068375 This is another CD-size web of a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae under the eave of our roof. I think the lens flare adds to the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2012)

File.Name: cb06c_cb06-024.jpg
Notes: 8163032327 I'm pretty sure this is another Grayish Sagebrush Grasshopper (Melanoplus cinereus ssp. cyanipes) - a Short-Horned Grasshopper (family Acrididae, in the order Orthoptera. This one is perched on the twig of native California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family that it hopped up onto, but I think it prefers composites. It's standing up like a puppy dog! (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2012)

File.Name: cb06d_cb06-022.jpg
Notes: 8163005297 One of the most handsome fall color plants in our mountains is native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. It's a deciduous shrub with beautiful white flowers and interesting nuts. It's one of my favorite native plants. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2012)

File.Name: cb06e_cb06-009.jpg
Notes:
8162962527 I don't usually post photos of horticultural flowers from the garden, but I can't resist with this one.

This is Ginger (maybe Zingiber officinale) in the Zingiberaceae plant family. We planted this at least 10 years ago. We saw a few flowers for the first time last year, but this is the first year it has really flowered. Isn't it beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2012)

On the other hand, it's odd to find ginger flowering in the mountains. Is this climate change?

File.Name: cb06f_cb05-036.jpg
Notes: 8162920125 This is looking towards the Santa Barbara backcountry from the ridge above the headwaters of Paradise Creek, looking north. This photo is looking over ranch land towards (from left to right) Zaca Peak, Figueroa Mountain, and Ranger Peak. The low point to the right is Cachuma Saddle. That's the Santa Ynez River at the bottom, with Loma Alta in the middle-right. It was windy, but that doesn't matter so much with landscapes. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2012)

File.Name: cb06h_cb05-032.jpg
Notes:
8162915482 Yesterday was windy - blowing off-shore - but very clear. This is looking towards the Channel Islands - Santa Cruz Island on the left and Santa Rosa Island on the right. I got this photo from the ridge above the headwaters of Paradise Creek, looking south. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2012)

The trees are more complicated. There are Gray Pines (Pinus sabiniana) to the right, and introduced Knobcone Pines (Pinus attenuata) to the left. In the foreground and background are native Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri).
CommonNames: Gray Pines; Knobcone Pines; Coulter Pines
ScientificName: Pinus sabiniana; Pinus attenuata; Pinus coulteri
associatedTaxa: Pinus sabiniana; Pinus attenuata; Pinus coulteri

File.Name: cb06h_cb06-032.jpg
Notes:
8162820961 We had some excitement today - another wildfire in the mountains, but this one was 10-15 miles away. It was the Cold Fire (great name!) because it started near the bottom of Cold Springs trail. This is no threat to us. This is CDF 05 - a Lockheed P-2H Neptune, a beautiful airplane with a clear nose cone, flying home over Fremont Ridge as seen from my upstairs porch. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2012)

There are rumors on the Web that a suspect has been detained on suspicion/reports that he started fire. Damn. The best news by far on local fires has been http://www.edhat.com/.

File.Name: cb07a_cb07-013.jpg
Notes:
8165733114 On the left is a locally uncommon Five-Finger Fern (Adiantum aleuticum) in the in the Polypodiaceae plant family. On the right is a fallen leaf of Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family. Both have a "palmate" arrangement - like the fingers of your hand. I found the leaf just like this. (Tequepis Canyon, Santa Ynez Mountains, 7 November 2012)

I was scouting the trail today for a field trip I'm leading for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society on Saturday. It will be a fine outing.

(Tequepis Canyon, Santa Ynez Mountains, 7 November 2012)

File.Name: cb07d_cb07-009.jpg
Notes:
8165638264 This is the bottom of the small waterfall in Tequepis Canyon. It's more of a "water slide" - with a 4 or 5 inch stream of water falling down through a travertine mineral sluice. The water falls about 20 feet. The wonder is that there is any flowing water in our mountains after a drought year and a drought summer! (Tequepis Canyon, Santa Ynez Mountains, 7 November 2012)

This is a beautiful place. I was scouting the trail today for a field trip I'm leading for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society on Saturday. It will be a fine outing.

File.Name: cb07e_cb07-005.jpg
Notes:
8165600432 You have to squint to see this waterfall in Tequepis Canyon - look to the left of the tree trunk. It's more of a "water slide" - with a 4 or 5 inch stream of water falling down through a travertine mineral sluice. The wonder is that there is any flowing water in our mountains after a drought year and a drought summer! (Tequepis Canyon, Santa Ynez Mountains, 7 November 2012)

It's a beautiful place. I was scouting the trail today for a field trip I'm leading for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society on Saturday. It will be a fine outing.

File.Name: cb07f_cb07-003.jpg
Notes: 8165542261 These are the droopy seed heads of Ocean Spray aka Creambush (Holodiscus discolor) in the in the Rosaceae plant family. It's an uncommon deciduous shrub in our mountains - or maybe "locally common" is the better description. (Tequepis Canyon, Santa Ynez Mountains, 7 November 2012)

File.Name: cb07g_cb07-019.jpg
Notes:
8165505979 I believe these are late-season flowers of Longstem Buckwheat (Eriogonum elongatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family - still flowering in the summer-dry mountains! Flowering now is quite a trick after a drought year and a drought summer, but these are tough natives that can handle it. This specimen is unusual because it still has some leaves - usually they completely dry up. It's growing in shale soil. (Tequepis Canyon, Santa Ynez Mountains, 7 November 2012)

That's native composite Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family in the background. Its gone to seed.

File.Name: cb07h_cb06-011.jpg
Notes: 8165524642 These are new flower clusters of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family. It's our first native flower of the new rain year. The flowers come before the leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2012)

File.Name: cb08a_cb05-024.jpg
Notes:
8170876046 These are the ripening fruit of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. The trees are related to avocados, and it shows in the fruit, which look like little avocado "cukes". (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2012)

Hidden inside the fruit is a real nut which is delicious when dark roasted - rich, almost like bitter chocolate or coffee. I'll post a recipe here when the season comes. If you can't wait, see this YouTube video.

(I had a social engagement this evening, and couldn't post photos. I made it up on Friday.)
Locality: I had a social engagement this evening

File.Name: cb08b_cb05-026.jpg
Notes:
8170823477 This is the last green pair of opposite leaves on a shrub of native Blue Elderberry (Sambucus mexicana) shrub in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. This shrub is deciduous. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2012)

It's now been renamed as Sambucus nigra ssp. caerulea in the Adoxaceae - if you really care. It took me years to get used to the transition from Munz to Jepson, and it will take years again!

File.Name: cb08c_cb05-027.jpg
Notes: 8170808057 These are the papery calyces of native Pitcher Sage (Lepechinia calycina) in the Lamiaceae plant family. Inside each husk, there are usually four seeds. This shrub seems to grow as a fire-follower - coming up in abundance after wildfires or disturbance, and then slowly dying away as other shrubs take their place. (San Marcos Pass, 5 November 2012)

File.Name: cb09a_cb09-004.jpg
Notes:
8171036455 This is the tiny gall of a Gall Wasp (family Cynipidae) in the order Hymenoptera. It's a blister on the vein of a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The white fuzz below the gall is the "furry armpits" of this leaf - a feature of Coast Live Oak. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2012)

This may well be the gall of the "bisexual" generation of wasps - containing both male and female - that form the larger potato-like galls in my [Previous photo - the Gouty Stem Gall Wasp (Callirhtis quercussuttoni).

It's been a tough couple of days for new photos - yesterday was gloomy and drizzly, and today was windy. And tomorrow I'm leading a field trip for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society up Tequepis Canyon.
Locality:
Callirhtis quercussuttoni).

It's been a tough couple of days for new photos - yesterday was gloomy and drizzly

File.Name: cb09b_cb09-001.jpg
Notes:
8170989626 This is an old gall of Gouty Stem Gall Wasps (Callirhtis quercussuttoni) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera riddled with the exit holes of the gall wasps, their inquilines, and parasites. I found it on the ground today under native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the Fagaceae plant family. It's fairly large, like a small potato. It usually kills the branch beyond the gall. I often find them on the ground like this, so I guess it kills the stem behind as well. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2012)

This gall is "polythalamous" - meaning that it contained many wasp larvae. These galls are also "unisexual" - meaning that they contain only females. The ladies lay eggs which make new "monothalamous" galls containing only a single wasp larvae on the leaf veins of oak trees. (See my [Next] photo.) They emerge into male and female wasps which mate and deposit eggs in new oak stems - to bring the cycle full circle. It's complicated, but life is like that!
Locality: See my [Next] photo.)  They emerge into male and female wasps which mate and deposit eggs in new oak stems - to bring the cycle full circle.  It's complicated
CommonNames: Gouty Stem Gall Wasps; Coast Live Oak

associatedTaxa: Callirhtis quercussuttoni; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: cb09c_cb06-014.jpg
Notes:
8170953116 This is bad grass!

It kind of looks like a native bunch grass if you don't look too close, but it's not. This is non-native Smilo Grass aka Rice-Millet or Millet Mountain Rice (Piptatherum miliaceum) in the Poaceae plant family. The stems are tough and wiry. It's impossible to pull by hand from dry ground. I reckon this has become the worst invasive weed in our mountains. It grows along roads like other weeds, but it's also getting established in the woods and along the river. I've watched it get worse over the years. (San Marcos Pass, 6 November 2012)
CommonNames: Smilo Grass; Rice-Millet; Millet Mountain Rice

File.Name: cb09d_cb01-010.jpg
Notes: 8170910523 This is the well-named native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens) in the Poaceae plant family growing in the woods near home. It still has green stems if you look close. You wouldn't want it for a lawn, but it's a real native grass that sometimes forms a grassy understory in our woods. Native grasses are uncommon. I like them a lot. (San Marcos Pass, 1 November 2012)

File.Name: cb09e_cb04-025.jpg
Notes: 8170870075 This is a cluster of Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus) in the Poaceae plant family growing in the canyon. It's not really a giant, though it does grow 3-7 feet tall. It mostly didn't flower in this very dry year, though I have seen some aborted dry flower heads. This is actually an important adaptation - not to flower, but to save their energy for (hopefully) next year. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2012)

File.Name: cb10a_cb10-032.jpg
Notes:
8173814136 This is the beginning of a rave of ladybugs!

These are Convergent Lady Beetles (Hippodamia convergens) in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera. They can have any number of spots, or no spots at all, but they always have those two white lines on the upper-side of their thorax. They are "converging" on a lichen-covered rock in Tequepis Canyon. In some years I find huge masses of these in the mountains near home, but last year I found none. These are the first ones I've seen this year anywhere. I don't understand their schedule. (Tequepis Canyon, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 November 2012)

It really felt like fall today - cold and crisp and clear, We had our first frost last night - not enough to damage plants, but it was just 30∞ when I first checked the thermometer on the porch this morning. I led a hike today for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society (SYVNHS) today to the little waterfall in Tequepis Canyon. We had a great time! I didn't have much chance to take photos, but I got a few with my "normal lens" on a (shaky) monopod. I had to use flash for this photo in low light.
Locality: SYVNHS) today to the little waterfall in Tequepis Canyon.  We had a great time!  I didn't have much chance to take photos

File.Name: cb10f_cb10-012.jpg
Notes:
8173614871 I led a hike today for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society (SYVNHS) today to the little waterfall in Tequepis Canyon. This is our lunch stop at the waterfall after a pretty good hike. It's more of a "water slide" - with a 4 or 5 inch stream of water falling down through a travertine mineral sluice. The water falls about 20 feet. The wonder is that there is any flowing water in our mountains after a drought year and a drought summer! (Tequepis Canyon, Santa Ynez Mountains, 10 November 2012)

The canyon is a jumble of rocks and broken trees. It must be hell in high water!

File.Name: cb11a_cb11-014.jpg

There were several of them in this place that catches morning sunlight. Look how golden her body is! (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2012)

We had another frosty morning today, with temperatures below freezing. I mostly find flies on these cold mornings. This butterfly was a nice surprise.

File.Name: cb12a_cb12-036.jpg
Notes:
8181007678 What's a nice butterfly like you doing on a weed flower like this?

This is a tiny female Western Pygmy Blue (Brephidium exile) butterfly on new "flowers" of Russian Thistle aka Tumbleweed (Salsola tragus) in the Chenopodiaceae plant family. This is the smallest butterfly in North America, and one of the smallest in the world. It's about the size of my little finger nail. What's it doing on this weed flower? We had another cold night, but not quite frosty. We're in a warming turn of our autumn weather - before we turn cold again!

File.Name: cb12h_cb12-029.jpg
Notes:

That lazy, hazy river where we both can dream
Linger in the shade of an old oak tree
Throw away your troubles, dream a dream with me"
- Hoagy Carmichael, 1930

This very lazy river is the dry river bed of the Santa Ynez Valley River just before the 2nd crossing. The cottonwood and sycamore trees are just starting to get their fall colors. That's a burned out Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree in the Platanaceae plant family in the lower-left. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 November 2012)

California rivers flow upside-down at this time of year - rocks on top and water down below!

I took all of today's photos on a single shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis). Most of the photos are flies. Maybe I'm just noticing them more since they are about the only insects on the wing right now, and this is one of the few native plants flowering.

It was 30∞ warmer this morning than it was two days ago - 60's rather than 30's.

File.Name: cb13b_cb13-008.jpg
Notes:
8183999205 This fly has the iridescent blue colors of a common Blow Fly (family Calliphoridae in the order Diptera) - a trash fly - but the bright orange cheeks and beard distinguish it. I believe this fly is the "Secondary Screwworm" (Cochliomyia macellaria). This one is working new male staminate flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2012)

If I'm right, this fly is in the family Calliphoridae, but it's more interesting than the common trash flies. The fly is native to North America. It's larvae feed on dead tissue, including human tissue. But it is the "secondary" screwworm that only feeds on already dead tissue.

It actually has some medical uses. It has forensic use as the "principal species on which to base postmortem interval estimations". And they have been used "in some cases as surgical maggots" to clean only necrotic tissue from wounds. There's more info at Wikipedia.

They fly among the flowers like little helicopters. They seem to be remarkably camera shy. Sometimes they fly away if I so much as glance at them!

File.Name: cb13g_cb13-001.jpg
Notes:
8183817477 This is another bristly Fly in the order Diptera working male staminate pollen-bearing flower heads of a Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2012)

Years ago, I did a proper insect collection and learned to identify flies the real way. But that was decades ago. Now I just enjoy photographing them, and don't worry too much about what kind of fly they are. They have so much structural detail!

(But if you recognize any of my flies, please let me know!)
Locality: But if you recognize any of my flies

File.Name: cb13h_cb13-020.jpg
Notes: 8183832008 Here is a tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on male staminate pollen-bearing flower heads of a Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. It looks like it's doing push-ups. After I got this photo, it jumped away! (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2012)

File.Name: cb14a_cb14-009.jpg
Notes:
8186341007 This is a little "Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae in the order Hemiptera - maybe the Green Stink Bug in the genus Chinavia of the tribe Nezarini. It's feeding *inside* a dry seed pod of the summer-flowering Weed's Mariposa Lily (Calochortus weedii var. vestus) - now renamed as C. fimbriata - in the Liliaceae plant family. It can't get any nutrition from the dry pod, so I assume it's piercing a seed with its sucking mouth-parts. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2012)

Hah - they don't stink. They smell like cilantro!

Just two photos tonight. We had a board meeting of the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society tonight, and I'm tired. And I have to admit I've been a little distracted today. Julie and I are heading up to Oregon in a few days (hopefully on Saturday morning) for wild mushrooms and Thanksgiving dinner with our kids and grandkids. It's become one of my favorite family traditions.

File.Name: cb15c_cb13-028.jpg
Notes: 8189994340 These are male (pollen-bearing) and female (seed-bearing) flowers of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing right next to each other. They are the same species, but it is dioecious with separate male and female plants. You can see dry "failed flowers" on both plants. It's been very dry this year, and these shrubs that only flower at the end of our dry summer have been feeling the drought. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2012)

File.Name: cb15d_cb11-018.jpg
Notes: 8188883063 Here are new flowers and seed heads of a native composite Telegraph Weed (Heterotheca grandiflora) plant in the Asteraceae plant family. It grows like a weed along roads, but it really is a native plant. Insects don't seem to like it much. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2012)

File.Name: cb15e_cb12-039.jpg
Notes:
8189942080 The cottonwood and sycamore trees are just starting to get their fall colors along the river.

Here are Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii) in the Salicaceae plant family on the left, and a Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree in the Platanaceae plant family on the right. I got this photo near the fourth river crossing in Paradise. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 November 2012)
ScientificName: Populus fremontii; Salicaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae
associatedTaxa: Populus fremontii; Salicaceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae

File.Name: cb15f_cb13-022.jpg
Notes:
8189918732 These are more ripening fruit of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. The trees are related to avocados, and it shows in the fruit, which look like little avocado "cukes". Not many insects are interested in these pungent trees, except for Leafcutter Bees (family Megachilidae, Hymenoptera). You can see some evidence of their work in the lower-left leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2012)

Hidden inside the fruit is a real nut which is delicious when dark roasted - rich, almost like bitter chocolate or coffee. I'll post a recipe here when the season comes. If you can't wait, see this YouTube video.

File.Name: cb15g_cb13-024.jpg
Notes:
8189835266 I believe this is the gall of a Split Twig Gall Wasps (Dryocosmus asymmetricus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's growing on Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's an "integral" stem gall (part of the stem) that is "polythalamous" (home to many wasp larvae). It's a characteristic of this gall that it splits open, revealing a nest-like cluster of larval chambers. If you look close, you can see holes drilled by the wasp larvae. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2012)

The oak is interesting. The leaves are usually smooth, but sometimes toothed, like here. They develop powdery rust underneath. They usually grow as shrubs, but there are huge trees in Tequepis Canyon.
CommonNames: Split Twig Gall Wasps; Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak
ScientificName: Dryocosmus asymmetricus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Dryocosmus asymmetricus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae

File.Name: cb15h_cb12-018.jpg
Notes:
8188724077 This is looking inside a open "rolled oak leaf" on native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I think this proves it's not a gall. The leaf tissue within the roll looks normal, not changed like a gall would be. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2012)

See my earlier photos here and here. I still wonder about the folds here which go under the leaf rather than over.

File.Name: cb16a_cb15-001.jpg
Notes:
8192276294 There are almost-ripe berries high up in this beautiful Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. That's a Tanbark Oak aka Tanoak (Lithocarpus densiflorus) tree in the Fagaceae plant family in the background. I got this photo yesterday - a gloomy day, but that helps bring out the color of the berries and smooth manzanita-like bark. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2012)

We've had 1/4 inch of rain so far today - not much, but more is coming. Julie and I are heading off to Oregon tomorrow rain or shine. I'll be back sometime after next week - hopefully with lots of new mushroom photos!
ScientificName: Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Lithocarpus densiflorus; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Lithocarpus densiflorus; Fagaceae

File.Name: cb16b_cb13-034.jpg
Notes: 8191148743 This is looking out over the oak-studded ranch land of the Santa Ynez Valley. I got this photo from the ridge above the headwaters of Paradise Creek, looking north-west on a very clear day. This view is looking more-or-less down the axis of the Los Laureles Syncline. Note how the rock layers are lifted clockwise on the left and counter-clockwise on the right. I believe this syncline is the reason why San Marcos Pass is here. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2012)

File.Name: cb16c_cb13-033.jpg
Notes: 8192209986 This is looking past Knapp's Castle towards the ridge of Little Pine Mountain and Madulce Peak in the background. Much of this country is part of the Dick Smith Wilderness - wild and remote! I got this photo from the ridge above the headwaters of Paradise Creek, looking north-east on a very clear day. "The Castle" is the ruins of a mountain mansion that was destroyed by a wildfire in the 1930s. Only stone walls and chimneys remain. It is privately owned, but the owner is good with visitors. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2012)

File.Name: cb16d_cb13-030.jpg
Notes:
8192162118 This is looking past a stand of pines towards San Miguel Island - the westernmost of the Santa Barbara Channel Islands. I believe that's Castle Rock to the right of the island. I got this photo from the ridge above the headwaters of Paradise Creek, looking south-west on a very clear day. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2012)

The trees are mostly native Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri) in the Pinaceae plant family. There are a few introduced Knobcone Pines (Pinus attenuata) on the left, but they have been dying out. We live in the canyon beyond the trees.

File.Name: cb28a_cb23-028.jpg
Notes:
8227686919 Coral Mushrooms (genus Ramaria) are easy to recognize and hard to identify. I won't try to guess what species it is. Is it the same or different from my [Previous] photos? They are always fantastic to find in the woods. They live up to their name and look like they belong under the ocean! It's about the size of a broccoli stalk. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 November 2012)

We had a family Thanksgiving at our cabin by the North Umpqua River in Oregon. It rained every day except the day we left. We even brought a bit of rain home with us - we've had 1/4 inch so far at home, and more is coming!

Every year I have to re-learn mushroom names and how to photograph them. Photographing mushrooms is very different from insects - long exposure times and large f/numbers. Our Thanksgiving trip always puts in in the mood to photograph local mushrooms - and with this new rain, I may get a chance!

File.Name: cb28b_cb23-031.jpg
Notes: 8227654203 Coral Mushrooms (genus Ramaria) are easy to recognize and hard to identify. I won't try to guess. This pair seem to be different from both the [Next] and [Previous] ones - but I might be wrong. It looks like they belong under the ocean! They are each about the size of a cauliflower head. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 November 2012)

File.Name: cb28c_cb21-015.jpg
Notes: 8227602283 Coral Mushrooms (genus Ramaria) are easy to recognize and hard to identify. I won't try to guess what species it is. They are always fantastic to find in the woods, though they look like they belong under the ocean! This one is growing on the ground from a dead conifer tree in the Oregon woods. It's about the size of a cauliflower head. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2012)

File.Name: cb28d_cb21-002.jpg
Notes: 8228646648 Here are a pair of strange rubbery Toothed Jelly Fungi (Pseudohydnum gelatinosum) growing from buried sticks in the Oregon woods last week. The small teeth resemble those of teeth fungi (Hydnaceae), and the latin name "pseudohydnum" means "false tooth fungus". They are said to be edible with no taste but great texture. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2012)

File.Name: cb28e_cb23-022.jpg
Notes: 8228596698 This appears to be a different little Bird's Nest Fungi in the Nidulariales family from my [Previous] photo. The outside of the nests is quite hairy, so maybe this is Nidula candida. The one open nest contains the tiny "eggs" that are really spore sacs called peridioles. The other caps have their "lids" (epiphragm) still in place, but you can see the epiphragm of the front cap laying by the side. They are growing on a conifer twig. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 November 2012)

File.Name: cb28f_cb23-017.jpg
Notes: 8227460199 These are cute little Bird's Nest Fungi in the Nidulariales family. The nest contains tiny "eggs" that are really spore sacs called peridioles. You can see lots of tiny caps with their "lids" (epiphragm) still in place. The peridioles of these fungi seem to be embedded in muscilage - which places them in the genus Nidula or Nidularia. The outside of the nests is quite smooth. Compare my [Next] photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 November 2012)

File.Name: cb28g_cb21-008.jpg
Notes: 8227417507 I guess these are the tiny fruiting bodies of Yellow Fairy Cups or Lemon Discos (Bisporella citrina or kin) in the Helotiaceae family of fungi. They are only a couple of mm across, but there are lots of them. They are rather pretty if you get close enough to notice them. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2012)

File.Name: cb28h_cb25-010.jpg
Notes:
8228355942 These are strange Candlesnuff Fungi (Xylaria hypoxylon or kin) growing on rotting wood in the Oregon woods. They are very tough - like dark matchsticks with a white tip. I don't think they qualify as "mushrooms" at all, though they are the fruiting bodies of fungi. They are not by any stretch of the imagination fleshy. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2012)

I've also found these odd critters at home, where they are forked like little deer or moose antlers. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/58635266

File.Name: cb29a_cb25-003.jpg
Notes:
8231484916 I believe these are the "Questionable Stropharia" (Stropharia ambigua) mushrooms with purplish-black spores, though the gills look quite white in this photo. They have distinctive cottony white veil fragments on the margin of the cap. They are said to taste like "old leaves". David Arora (author of "Mushrooms Demystified") says it is "one of the most exquisitely beautiful of all mushrooms" - and I think I agree. I've never seen them at home. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2012)

Today, my photos are all Oregon mushrooms with beautiful colors. And that's a real stumper. If mushrooms just release spores, then why are they so beautiful and diverse? As a naturalist, I want to believe there's a reason for everything - or is beauty reason enough?

It rained or drizzled most of the day. We've had an honest 1.6 inches of rain since yesterday, and more is expected through Monday. I did see several new mushrooms in the woods today!

File.Name: cb29b_cb25-018.jpg
Notes:
8231446134 These are surprisingly beautiful Witch's Hat (Hygrocybe conica aka Hygrophorus conicus) mushrooms in the Oregon woods last week. They are in the Hygrophoraceae family of mushrooms. They turn black as you handle them. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2012)

We also have these mushrooms at home. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66261076/

File.Name: cb29c_cb23-025.jpg
Notes: 8231421298 This is looking under a cap of a wonderful last week. I had to under-expose this photo a bit to show the fantastic color. They are such a deep violet that they look almost black in a photo! You can see a bit of the rusty hairs on the stem that peg it as a Cortinarius. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 November 2012)

File.Name: cb29d_cb21-017.jpg
Notes: 8230328761 These are beautiful Amethyst Laccaria (Laccaria amethystina) mushrooms in the Oregon woods last week. It's said to be edible and tasty, though I haven't tried it. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2012)

File.Name: cb29e_cb21-013.jpg
Notes: 8231355466 I believe these are the "Lackluster Laccaria" (Laccaria laccata) mushrooms in the Oregon woods last week. If so, the are uncommonly attractive for this "weedy" mushroom. Compare my [Next] photo of the more colorful Amethyst Laccaria (Laccaria amethystina). They are growing under (mostly) Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii). (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2012)

File.Name: cb29f_cb22-020.jpg
Notes: 8230247807 This is a handsome "Bolete" (family Boletaceae) mushroom in the Oregon woods last week. It has drops under the smooth cap that may be rain drops, but may also be an exudate. (How could drops get under the cap?) It also seems to have a "slime veil" - at least the stem is slimy where it has separated from the cap. Bolete mushrooms all have pores instead of gills to release spores under the cap. This one is growing under (mostly) Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii). (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 22 November 2012)

File.Name: cb29g_cb23-020.jpg
Notes: 8230191157 This is a different Russula mushroom in the Oregon woods last week. The caps are red, but not red enough to be Russula emetica. The bi-colored yellow-red splotched caps suggest Russula bicolor. These are large, hefty mushrooms - pleasing to the eye. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2012)

File.Name: cb29h_cb25-017.jpg
Notes:
8231209270 This is some sort of Russula mushroom in the Oregon woods last week. It has a purplish cap and I can make out some pink color on the stem, so it might be the Shrimp Mushroom (Russula xerampelina) - but I wouldn't bet the farm on that! I was getting photos, not collecting for the table. I didn't taste it to see if it's mild rather than acrid. It is growing under (mostly) Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii). (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2012)

Russula xerampelina is said to be edible and tasty - IF that's what it is.
ScientificName: Russula; Russula xerampelina; Pseudotsuga menziesii; Russula xerampelina

File.Name: cb30a_cb25-023.jpg
Notes:
8234009616 I guess this is a hefty Coccora (Amanita calyptrata) mushroom in the Oregon woods last week. Like all amanitas, it has a volva or cup-like structure at the base, but this one is particularly large and felt-like. This mushroom also has a thick cottony whitish patch on the cap. The cap is not as yellow as the ones we have at home. See my photo from last spring. I found it under conifers, but there was Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family nearby. If I'm right about the ID, it's said to be edible, though I prefer to avoid all amanitas. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2012)

Rain, rain, rain! We're up to 2-1/2 inches since Wednesday with more rain in the forecast through Monday. That puts us above normal for the rain year so far. There are new mushrooms in the woods!

File.Name: cb30b_cb22-004.jpg
Notes: 8232885149 These are the Amanita section Vaginatae mushrooms that used to be known as genus Amanitopsis. They have a membranous "universal veil" at the base and no ring or "partial veil" at all. They may be the Grisette (Amanita vaginata) - or maybe the Western Grisette (Amanita pachycolea), though that one is said to be brown rather than gray. I found them last week in the Oregon woods under (mostly) Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii). They may be edible, but I'd rather avoid all amanitas. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 22 November 2012)

File.Name: cb30c_cb21-022.jpg
Notes: 8232874587 These are beautiful Shaggy-Stalked Parasol (Lepiota clypeolaria or kin) mushrooms growing under conifers in the Oregon woods last week. Lepiota mushroom caps usually have a dark center that breaks into scales as it expands, making them easy to recognize. This one is said to be poisonous. It's best to avoid collecting all slender woodland Lepiotas for the table. But they sure are pretty! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2012)

File.Name: cb30d_cb25-009.jpg
Notes: 8233879552 I believe these are Corn Silk Inocybe (Inocybe sororia or kin) mushrooms in the Oregon woods last week. They have a distinctive "witches hat" shape with umbonate caps. I found small groups of these scattered under mostly Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii). They are NOT edible as they contain high concentrations of muscarine. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2012)

File.Name: cb30e_cb23-019.jpg
Notes:
8233844252 This is the White Chanterelle (Cantherellus subalbidus) mushroom growing in the Oregon woods last week. It's edible and delicious. I think I like it even better than the orange Chantrelles. Unfortunately, all Chantrelles were scarce last week - not enough to collect for our Thanksgiving dinner. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 November 2012)

See my photo from Thanksgiving two years ago - Lobster mushrooms and white, black, and orange Chanterelles - the bounty of the Oregon woods. I think I like the Black Chanterelles aka Horn of Plenty (Craterellus cornucopiodes) mushrooms best of all.


   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65778335/

Notes: 8232740619 This is a strange Truncate Club Coral (Clavariadelphus truncatus) mushroom growing under conifers in the Oregon woods. They are said to be delicious and sweet, but I haven't tried them. It looks and feels like a Chanterelle without gills. I only saw this one. File.Name: cb30g_cb25-004.jpg

They are in the same Cantarellaceae family as delicious chanterelles, but they are not recommended for the table. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2012)

Notes:
8232621317 These are neat little Puffball mushrooms growing in the Oregon woods last week. I guess they are the Common Puffball (Lycoperdon perlatum) - or maybe the Dark Puffball (L. foetidum), but they don't look dark enough. They remind me of full vacuum cleaner bags! These are growing in the woods under mostly Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii). (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 November 2012)

Note the "additional info" to the right. My exposure for this photo was 20 seconds at f/22! It was dark and rainy during most of my Oregon time. I got some of these photos under an umbrella.

File.Name: cc01a_cc01-001.jpg
Notes:
8237016858 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

These are new Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) in the woods today growing from the stump of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. The continuing rainy weather has been good for mushrooms! (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2012)

For gardeners, these honey-mushrooms are said to be the "virulent parasite" of timber, fruit, and garden trees known as "Oak Root Fungus". They seem benign at our house in the woods, only infecting dead stumps. You can see some white spore accumulation on the lower caps, like a natural spore print. The caps are edible and tasty - if you get them before the flies do!

We're up to 3+ inches of rain from the recent storms. It was drizzly all day today. In fact, it's been drizzly since Wednesday, which is unusual weather for us. Usually the storms come in and then they are gone. This is great mushroom weather. I also found Agaricus, Inocybe, Collybia, Mycena, Lepiota, and even a Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) mushrooms in the soggy woods.

It doesn't have dark fibrils on the cap, but it does have pink colors on the stem. Whatever it is, it's rather handsome. If you look on top of the cap on the left, there's a small Drosophilid Fly in the family Drosophilidae of the order Diptera. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 November 2012)

File.Name: cc01c_cb23-016.jpg
Notes: 8236913802 I guess this is the Tiger Tricholoma (Tricholoma pardinum) or kin in the Oregon woods last week. It has dark fibrils on the cap, white notched gills, and the stem is a just a bit flushed pink. It looks a lot like the Russula in my [Next] photo. This one is said to be poisonous - not drop-down-dead poison, but causing severe gastroenteritis that may require a trip to the hospital. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 November 2012)

File.Name: cc01d_cb21-010.jpg
Notes: 8235726849 These are Bolete type mushrooms in the mushroom family Boletaceae. I guess they are genus Suillus - maybe the Western Painted Suillus (Suillus lakei) or kin? It's said to be common under Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii) trees on the West Coast, and here they are. They have non-slimy caps despite the wet weather. They are said to be edible but not choice. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 November 2012)

File.Name: cc01e_cb23-023.jpg
Notes: 8236753474 I guess this is some kind of Polypore (Polyporaceae) or "bracket fungus" growing on the trunk of a fallen Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii) tree in the Oregon woods. I don't know what it is, but it's very beautiful with a furry cap and sculpted layers of pores. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 November 2012)

File.Name: cc01f_cb23-003.jpg
Notes:
8235643597 These look like little puffball fungi, but they are really Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on the well-rotted wood of a fallen Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii) tree. I believe they are the species Lycogala epidendrum. These slime molds are about 1/4 inch (6 mm) across. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 November 2012)

Slime Molds are remarkable critters that seem to be part fungus and part animal. They are Mycetozoa - which literally means "fungus-animals". They are really animals, not fungi. I think these are my favorite organisms, though I don't find them very often - they are tiny, and they don't last long. You have to be in the right place and the right time to get a photo.

Mycetozoa live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in decaying wood. When the weather is right, they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" without cell membranes to make spores. It's a perfect strategy to move from one bit of decaying wood to another.
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Pseudotsuga menziesii; Lycogala epidendrum; Mycetozoa
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Pseudotsuga menziesii; Lycogala epidendrum; Mycetozoa

File.Name: cc02a_cc01-021.jpg
Notes:
8239512143 Trip photos are nice, but photos from today or yesterday at home are always the best!

This is a beautiful and unusual native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) in the woods, maybe genus Helminthoglypta. I interrupted it taking a dump taking a under a board by the mailbox. I don't understand snail anatomy, but it sure looks like it's making poo from it's shell, right behind it's head. The shell looks electric! I wish this photo was sharper, but it was raining and my viewfinder was all steamed up and my camera still had my mushroom photo settings. It will do! (San Marcos Pass, 1 December 2012)

There is an interesting web page on Ventura County Wildlife - Terrestrial Snails and Slugs:
   http://www.magney.org/photofiles/VenturaCountySnails1.htm

Rain, rain, rain! We're approaching four inches of rain since Wednesday. I hope it continues. Last year the rain started good, but dried up in January and February, which should be our wettest months. We'll see what happens this year.
CommonNames: Land Snail; Ventura County Wildlife - Terrestrial Snails and Slugs

File.Name: cc02b_cb21-004.jpg
Notes: 8240496166 This is the Oregon version of a Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus), though it sure looks different and less banana-like than our Southern California slugs - see this photo. (Our slugs might be the California Banana Slug, Ariolimax californicus.) It's munching a mushroom in the soggy Oregon woods, surrounded by fallen leaves of Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree in the Aceraceae plant family. This is the largest terrestrial mollusk along the west coast. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2012)

File.Name: cc02d_cb21-020.jpg
Notes: 8239135607 This is an interesting Lichen growing on the ground in the Oregon woods. I believe it is a Membranous Felt Lichen aka (Peltigera membranacea). The odd orange painted-toenail-like structures are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. This lichen is especially interesting because it is at least partly a Cyanolichen - a fungus and a Cyanobacteria, a blue-green bacteria or blue-green algae. "Blue-green algae" sound like "algae" but they are very different! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 21 November 2012)

File.Name: cc02e_cb25-013.jpg
Notes:
8239099037 The forests of Lilliput...

These are beautiful British Soldier Lichens (Cladonia cristatella or kin - maybe C. deformis?) starting to get their "red hats" in the Oregon woods, growing with moss. I believe the red caps are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. Remember that lichens are dual-organisms - part fungus and part algae - and sometimes the fungal part tries to reproduce on it's own. The mosses (Bryophyta) are starting to "flower" with sporophytes. You can get lost exploring the growth on a single log in the woods! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2012)

File.Name: cc02f_cb19-005.jpg
Notes: 8240145650 This is a different fern that I only know from this one place in the Oregon woods. I guess that it's a Leathery Grape Fern (Botrychium multifidum) in the Opioglossaceae plant family. It has a basal compound leaf and a flowering scape with sporangia rising separately from the ground. It may well be common - I just haven't noticed it. It's leaning over from recent rain. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 19 November 2012)

We have Polypodium californicum at home, but these are probably Polypodium vulgare in the Oregon woods. These are growing from the crook of a mossy Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) tree in the Aceraceae plant family. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 November 2012)

File.Name: cc02h_cb22-005.jpg
Notes: 8238947095 Here are the Oregon mushroom woods that I've been showing for a few days, with some mist in the air. It's a mix of conifers and hardwoods. That's a Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family with all the moss, and the other trees are Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii) and Western Hemlock (Tsuga heterphylla). We also have Grand Fir (?), Incense Cedar, Western Red Cedar, Sugar Pine, Pacific Yew, Madrone, Oregon Ash, Cottonwoods, and Willow. A diversity of trees = a diversity of mushrooms! File.Name: cc03a_cc03-023.jpg
Notes:
8242438579 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

I found this odd colony of Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on an oak leaf along our driveway this morning. They are NOT mushrooms or fungi, thought they look kind of fungus-like. They look like frosting on a bun, and they look a bit like the slime mold I show here. (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2012)

Slime Molds are remarkable critters that seem to be part fungus and part animal. They are Mycetozoa - which literally means "fungus-animals" - but they are really animals, not fungi. Mycetozoa live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in decaying wood and humus. When the weather is right, they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" without cell membranes. They end up as tiny mushroom-like structures like these that release spores that disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one bit of decaying wood to another. I think they might be my favorite creatures.

These slime molds are still in their streaming stage, so I don't know what they will finally look like. I'll get another photo tomorrow.

The rain is done, but I'm a little confused by the rain totals since Wednesday. The Santa Barbara Hydrology website has us at 5.64 inches of rain for the storms, but I only find 4 inches in our rain gauge. Either my rain gauge got emptied at some point or a wood rat widdled in the official gauge! Either way, I'm happy. We're at 150% normal!

File.Name: cc03b_cb25-024.jpg
Notes:
8242380839 I don't know what this mushroom is except that it is genus Lactarius. I sliced the gills and didn't see any latex, though it did stain the gills rusty-brown. It's an attractive mushroom. If you know it, please let me know what it is. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2012)

A nice thing about photographing mushroom in the Oregon woods is that I don't have to know what the are! (I feel different at home in the Santa Barbara mountains.)

File.Name: cc03c_cb25-006.jpg
Notes: 8242355229 I'm not sure what this mushroom is - but it sure is pretty! It's growing with moss from a standing dead Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii) or Western Hemlock (Tsuga heterphylla) tree. It has a tough-looking yellowish stem with a faint ring, with a striate margin and some fibrils on the cap. It's several inches across. If you know it, please let me know what it is. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 22 November 2012)

File.Name: cc03d_cb22-019.jpg
Notes: 8243384770 There were lots of these pale yellow mushrooms growing on a fallen tree in the Oregon woods. They have pale yellow gills, but I think they may be the brown-spored Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Naematoloma fasciculare) or kin mushrooms. I'm not sure. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 22 November 2012)

File.Name: cc03e_cb25-028.jpg
Notes: 8243357726 These handsome Shaggy Mane (Coprinus comatus) mushrooms are trying to eat our gravel driveway in Oregon! When we first got to the cabin, they were all over the road - but every time we drove it, there were fewer of them. This cluster was on the side of the road where we didn't drive. I love their little "rain hats"! They are edible and delicious and unmistakable - a good combination. But gather them young, for they "deliquesce" - turn to black ink - if you wait too long! The caps in the photo are too old. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 25 November 2012)

File.Name: cc03f_cb23-008.jpg
Notes: 8243297390 This is some kind of foliose lichen growing from a twig in the Oregon woods. I figure those white blisters that follow the folds of the "leaf" are reproductive structures - maybe soredia? Lichens do have trouble reproducing since the are composite organisms composed of both of fungi and algae, so both have to reproduce. The easiest way is for tiny bits to break off and disperse, and that's what these small structures provide for. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 23 November 2012)

File.Name: cc03g_cb20-007.jpg
Notes: 8243252524 This is the Oregon Lung Lobaria aka Lettuce Lichen (Lobaria oregana) lichen hanging on trunk of (probably) Douglas Fir (Pseudotsuga menziesii) as an epiphyte. It looks like monster skin! It's a common foliose-type lichen in the Oregon woods. It's especially common on the deciduous trees like Oregon White Oak (Quercus garryana) that drop their leaves in winter and let the light in. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 20 November 2012)

File.Name: cc03h_cb24-003.jpg
Notes: 8243209288 This is looking up the mighty North Umpqua River in the rain. I got this photo from the new footbridge across the river near Susan Creek. It's an impressive bridge which will make a this section of the river more accessible - it used to be a whole day of hiking from either side to get here along the North Umqua Trail from Boulder Creek or Wright Creek. The bridge looks too new, but time wil fix that! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 24 November 2012)

File.Name: cc04a_cc03-013.jpg
Notes:
8246481348 We've been having Banana Slug weather!

This is a large hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus - or is it Ariolimax californicus?) prowling through the woods. They're 6-8 inches long! This is the largest terrestrial mollusk in our mountains, and we only see them after rains. They have two pairs of retractile "feelers" on their heads. The upper pair have eye spots, while the lower air provides a tactile sense and smell. They move surprising fast. Oregon slugs look very different. (San Marcos Pass, 3 December 2012)

File.Name: cc04b_cc04-018.jpg
Notes:
8245395939 This is another beautiful cluster of plasmodia of Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on a fallen leaves and twigs of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They might be Physarella oblongata or kin. You can see them streaming on the central twig. These are quite orange. Compare my [Previous] photo where they are quite yellow. Julie said "They look like sea urchin eggs". See this photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2012)

There are lots of these - dozens of clusters - in the woods right now after our recent rains. But it's too easy to walk right by without noticing!

File.Name: cc04h_cc04-022.jpg
Notes: 8246231478 This is the beautiful shell of a native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) in the woods. It's a perfect spiral. I don't know what kind of snail it is, but it's NOT one of the pesky Garden Snails with a vertical shell that are so common in the lowlands. It's definitely a native. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2012)

File.Name: cc05a_cc05-003.jpg
Notes:
8249047608 This is all that's left of the Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) that I showed a few days ago- a wriggling mass of maggots! I'm sure they are the maggots of Drosophilid Fly in the family Drosophilidae of the order Diptera. This photo isn't quite sharp - maybe for the best. The mushrooms are growing in a place where the sun never shines, and it was quite dark - and the maggots never stop wriggling. I'll try again tomorrow with flash. Mushrooms are so ephemeral - I reckon that's part of their charm. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2012)

It was a nice day today, but windy. Maybe that's why this photo isn't quite sharp!

File.Name: cc05b_cc05-015.jpg
Notes:
8247957611 I can't resist another photo - taken today - of the beautiful plasmodia of Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on a fallen leaves and twigs of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They might be Physarella oblongata or kin. Julie said "They look like sea urchin eggs". See this photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2012)

There were lots of these - dozens of clusters - in the woods yesterday, but today there are just a few. The others have already completed their life cycle of making spores. See my [Previous] photo. If you miss a day in the woods, you'll miss them completely. I reckon that's part of their charm.

File.Name: cc05d_cc05-029.jpg
Notes:
8247875403 Hurdles for Banana Slugs?

This is a rather small Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus - or is it Ariolimax californicus?) crawling over an oak leaf in the woods. They get 6-8 inches long, but this one is only about two inches long. They have two pairs of retractile "feelers" on their heads. The upper pair have eye spots, while the lower air provides a tactile sense and smell. They move surprising fast, slow and steady. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2012)

I know nothing about the life cycle of these wonderful critters. Is this 2 inch slug a baby or a yearling? How do they survive our dry summers? Where are the infants?

File.Name: cc05e_cc05-018.jpg
Notes: 8247854987 This is a strange Candlesnuff Fungus (Xylaria hypoxylon or kin) - with raindrops. It seems to be growing from Oak and Bay leaves, but there is probably wood buried under there. There are many other species, so this ID is really just a placeholder. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2012)

File.Name: cc05f_cc04-021.jpg
Notes:
8247808917 These are Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila or kin) mushrooms coming up in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. They are said to be edible, and they look it. I haven't tried them. They are common in our woods right now. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2012)

You can see that the up-ended mushroom is growing with "glued-together" fallen leaves, with root-like "rhizomorphs" - those thick white root-like things at the base of the stalk.

Of course you shouldn't eat ANY mushroom just because it "looks edible" - some of the deadliest mushrooms look edible too! Nor should you eat any mushroom because someone on Flicker says that it (or something that looks like it) is edible. Know what you're eating. That applies to food from the market as well!

File.Name: cc05g_cc04-017.jpg
Notes: 8247770193 I believe this is a beautiful heart-shaped Lentinellus ursinus mushroom in the Tricholomataceae family of mushrooms on a fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It has ragged or serrated gill edges. It seems to be upside down - maybe the branch fell? I don't know about those brown fibrils of the cap margin however, maybe it's something else? (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2012)
