Notes: 8247753051 I believe these mushrooms are the Rufous Candy Cap (Lactarius rufulus) on the edge of our garden. They don't seen to have any latex when you slit the gills. They don't have the maple-syrup smell of Lactarius fragilis, though they are still said to be good to eat. I haven't tried them. (San Marcos Pass, 4 December 2012)

File.Name: cc06a_cc06-026.jpg
Notes:
8251761646 These are tiny sporangia of a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on the rotting wood of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. I believe they are some kind of genus Physarum. You can see the little lime-encrusted mushroom-like caps opening to spill their cargo of spores. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2012)

They are NOT mushrooms or fungi, though they look kind of fungus-like. Slime Molds are remarkable critters that seem to be part fungus and part animal. They are Mycetozoa - which literally means "fungus-animals" - but they are really animals, not fungi. Mycetozoa live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in decaying wood and humus. When the weather is right, they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" without cell membranes. They end up as tiny mushroom-like structures like these that release spores that disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one bit of decaying wood to another.

We're having a good year for slime molds - or maybe it just that I'm not teaching any more, so I can get out into woods every day to find them. They only last for a day or two. I think they might be my favorite creatures to find and photograph.

File.Name: cc06b_cc06-023.jpg
Notes:
8250650539 This is another Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) in the woods today - maybe two different ones.

I believe these are the neat sporangia of a genus Physarum slime mold fruiting like little mushrooms. They have lime-encrusted caps. I'm not sure about those white plasmodia in the upper-right. They might become the sporangia of genus Stemonitis. See my [Previous] photo. If so, it's interesting to these two growing so close together. See this photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2012)

File.Name: cc06c_cc06-020.jpg
Notes: 8250633345 Here are ripening fruiting bodies of a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) lined up on the rotting wood of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. I believe they are genus Stemonitis. See this photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2012)

File.Name: cc06d_cc06-017.jpg
Notes: 8251672572 These are odd fruiting bodies of a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) lined up on the rotting wood of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. I believe they are genus Stemonitis. They look like little wieners - hot dogs, of course! They also look like fungi, but they are really animals. See this photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2012)

File.Name: cc06e_cc06-027.jpg
Notes:
8251653088 These look like little puffball fungi, but they are really Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on the well-rotted wood of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe they are the species Lycogala epidendrum. These slime molds are about 1/4 inch (6 mm) across. See this photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2012)

I love looking for critters of fallen wood. Can you see those tiny Cup Fungi in the lower-right of my photo?

File.Name: cc06f_cc06-002.jpg
Notes: 8250552931 These are tiny Mycena acicula mushrooms coming up from a bit of oak wood on the edge of our garden. I included my pen as a ruler. My pen has a ball of 0.7 mm, so the caps are only a millimeter or two across. This is the smallest beautiful mushroom I know that I actually have a name for. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2012)

File.Name: cc06g_cc06-012.jpg
Notes:
8250491129 These are medium-size Agaricus mushrooms in the woods growing from a bed of grass-like of (I think) Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family, which does very well in our canyon. I took a bigger bite than I should have, and retched for 5 minutes! It has a strong phenolic taste. It doesn't have a fibrillose cap, but I reckon it is the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) mushroom with that distinctive thick ring that stands out from the stalk. Live and learn! (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2012)

The local woodland Agaricus species are a pretty risky group in my experience.

File.Name: cc06h_cc06-031.jpg
Notes: 8250438383 I found this large, satisfying Cortinarius mushroom in the woods today. It has a rimmed bulb at the base of the stem, but it has dry cap and stem - which places it in subgenus Telamonia or Cortinarius. I ran it through David Arora's 9 page key to the cortinarii, and came to Cortinarius regalis, which sounds right. It does have a an inrolled cap with a large patch of white veil tissue on top. I found these growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and California Bay (Umbellularia californica) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2012)

File.Name: cc07a_cc07-003.jpg
Notes:
8254214516 Here is a micro-world of tiny Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) "mushrooms" growing from oak leaves above the retaining wall on the edge of our garden. There's also a bit of the "electric fungus" I show in my [Previous] photo. If you follow the stems of these "mushrooms" back to the left, you can see that they are growing from the leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2012)

Our canyon on the north-facing slope of the mountains is in the shade this close to the winter solstice, and holds moisture - but even here, it's starting to dry out with the dry winds today. We need more rain to keep the mushrooms coming, and to keep the woods alive. I hope this won't be a repeat of last year's weather pattern, with early rain and then nothing through January and February, which should be our wettest months.

File.Name: cc07b_cc07-004.jpg
Notes: 8254185426 This is the electric white growth of a fungus growing over a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). I once thought they were just the mycelia of a fungus, but I'm pretty sure it is the whole fungus. They are common when you look for them. I have no idea what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2012)

File.Name: cc07c_cc07-009.jpg
Notes: 8254170196 It's always fun to try to spot the smallest mushroom in the woods. I put my pen in the photo for scale, with a 0.7 mm tip - some of the mushroom caps are about the same size! The water drops are a bonus. I have no idea what kind of mushrooms they are. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2012)

File.Name: cc07d_cc07-005.jpg
Notes:
8254134502 Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) have been on my mind lately, and I seem to find them everywhere. I found these tiny lime-encrusted sporangia today on blades of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass above the retaining wall on the dry side of our garden. I guess they are some kind of the genus Physarum. They are just starting to release their spores. See this photo for more info about their interesting biology. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2012)

The blue colors are a "white balance" issue.

File.Name: cc07e_cc07-015.jpg
Notes:
8254102968 I guess these are some kind of Psathyrella mushrooms in the dark-spored Coprinaceae family. I don't know anything about them, except here they are. (San Marcos Pass, 7 December 2012)

I left the canyon today for the first time in days (a beer run) and was surprised by warm winds and crunchy oak leaves. Our canyon on the north-facing slope of the mountains is in the shade this close to the winter solstice, and holds moisture. I went to a favorite mushroom spot and found... only these dry caps and dry leaves. OK rain gods, it's time for more rain!
Locality: a beer run) and was surprised by warm winds and crunchy oak leaves.  Our canyon on the north-facing slope of the mountains is in the shade this close to the winter solstice

File.Name: cc07f_cc06-010.jpg
Notes: 8253004093 I guess these are some kind of white Hygrophorus mushroom in the woods. They are growing from the well-rotted wood of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. You can see a bit of wood at the base of the mushrooms I picked. (San Marcos Pass, 6 December 2012)

File.Name: cc07g_cc05-013.jpg
Notes: 8254036744 I believe these are the part of the Psathyrella longipes group of mushrooms in the dark-spored Coprinaceae family. The caps are adorned with widely spaced, tooth-like veil remnants. They are large but very fragile. I found this beautiful cluster, and managed to brush against them - and two of the hollow stems broke. This photo show the wreckage. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2012)

File.Name: cc07h_cc05-032.jpg
Notes: 8252949601 These are little white-spored Mycena mushrooms - the classic "LBM" or "Little Brown Mushroom". They are small and fragile - quite ephemeral. Usually mushrooms aren't much effected by wind, but it was windy enough on this day to wiggle them a bit. There are thousands of them in our woods right now - so many that I forget to photograph them. (San Marcos Pass, 5 December 2012)

File.Name: cc08a_cc08-034.jpg
Notes:
8255894699 These are the remarkable fruiting bodies of a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on a dead log of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. I guess that's still the gray streaming-plasmodial stage in front. I've never see this before. They look like corals under the ocean. I believe they are Ceratiomyxa fruticulosa in the family Ceratiomyxales. I believe those are the mature fruiting bodies in the upper-right. See my [Previous] photo, and see this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2012)

I reckon the first flush of mushrooms in our woods is about over. All the mushrooms are shriveling with the dry weather. We need another rain - there's a chance next Wednesday or Thursday.
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Ceratiomyxa fruticulosa; Ceratiomyxales
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Ceratiomyxa fruticulosa; Ceratiomyxales

File.Name: cc08b_cc08-037.jpg
Notes:
8256905604 These are the remarkable fruiting bodies of a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on a dead log of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. I believe they are Ceratiomyxa fruticulosa in the family Ceratiomyxales. They look like corals under the ocean. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2012)

When first I found them, I thought they were just a white fungal crust on the wood - but they are quite amazing. See my [Next] photo, and see this photo for more info about slime molds.
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Ceratiomyxa fruticulosa; Ceratiomyxales
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Ceratiomyxa fruticulosa; Ceratiomyxales

File.Name: cc08c_cc08-028.jpg
Notes: 8256876762 This photo shows ripe sporangia of Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on a dead log of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. That lattice-like material is the capillatum of the slime mold. Maybe they are Arcyria denudata or kin in the family Trichiales? I really don't know. I'm better at finding these critters than identifying them! See this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2012)
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; capillatum; Arcyria denudata; Trichiales
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; capillatum; Arcyria denudata; Trichiales

File.Name: cc08d_cc08-038.jpg
Notes: 8255768625 These are still-streaming plasmodia of a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on a log of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. You can see cellular material streaming into the caps in the upper-left. Maybe they are Arcyria denudata or kin in the family Trichiales? I really don't know. I'm better at finding these critters than identifying them! See this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2012)

associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Arcyria denudata; Trichiales

File.Name: cc08e_cc08-018.jpg
Notes: 8255704309 These are ripe sorangia of a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on a log of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. That lattice-like material is the capillatum of the slime mold. Many of them have little caps. Maybe they are Arcyria denudata or kin in the family Trichiales? I really don't know. I'm better at finding these critters than identifying them! See this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2012)

associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; capillatum; Arcyria denudata; Trichiales

File.Name: cc08f_cc08-009.jpg
Notes: 8256739776 These are the tiny sporangia of Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing from dead wood in the woods. I believe they are genus Stemonitis - like hot dogs on a stick. They were hiding in a hollow inside a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. See this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2012)

File.Name: cc08g_cc09-006.jpg
Notes:
8255629787 These are the ripe sporangia of the bright yellow or orange slime molds that I showed a couple of days ago - here, here, and here. The blue sporangia are stalked - look at the upper-left-most one. They might be Physarella oblongata or kin. They look like blue BBs or ripe grapes! See this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2012)

Update, 9 December 2012: I replaced the original photo I took with a better one taken the next day. The colors of the leaves are more subdued,but it bring out the blue colors of the sporangia even more. You can see some of the lime-encrusted sporangia starting to spill their load of precious spores.

File.Name: cc08h_cc08-003.jpg
Notes: 8255611689 It's great fun to try to photograph the smallest mushroom in the woods, and this one is a contender. I put my pen in the photo for scale, with a 0.7 mm tip - the mushroom cap looks about 1 mm across. The water drops are a bonus. I have no idea what kind of mushroom it is. It's growing from a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2012)

File.Name: cc09a_cc08-007.jpg
Notes:
8260148602 I was photographing the pretty fruiting bodies of Yellow Fairy Cups or Lemon Discos (Bisporella citrina or kin) in the Helotiaceae family of fungi growing on the broken end of a fallen branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). But look at that perfectly focused tiny Fly in the order Diptera that I captured in the upper-left! (I didn't notice at the time.) Maybe it's a humpbacked Fungus Gnat in the family Mycetophilidae? I don't see bristle-like hairs on its legs, but I love that name - "mushroom lover"! (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2012)

This was a 1:1 crop - an 800x800 crop from my 4800x3200 original photo. It still looks pretty good, especially with a 5 second exposure. My new Nikon D800 has good pixels. (I mostly use DX mode, which does some cropping for me and saves disk space.)

*Sigh* - busy night and morning. The 6" main water pipe to our canyon broke late last night, and drained our water tank. I had to get up too early to meet with plumbers, and we still have no water in the house. Hopefully it will be fixed tomorrow.
CommonNames: Yellow Fairy Cups; Lemon Discos; Coast Live Oak; Fly
ScientificName: Bisporella citrina; Helotiaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Diptera; Fungus Gnat; Mycetophilidae
associatedTaxa: Bisporella citrina; Helotiaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Diptera; Fungus Gnat; Mycetophilidae

File.Name: cc09b_cc08-002.jpg
Notes: 8260068314 I believe this is a remarkable "Many-Plume Moth" in the family Alucitidae - probably the species Alucita montana. It's remarkable because the wings are divided into individual plumes along the veins. This one was perched on our kitchen window this morning, and I couldn't resist a photo because we just cleaned the windows! I set up my tripod in the kitchen sink. The larvae are said to feed on Snowberry (Symphoricarpos) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family, which is abundant in the woods around our home. (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2012)

File.Name: cc09c_cc08-017.jpg
Notes: 8260034330 These are ripe berries high up in this beautiful native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. The trees have smooth reddish skin like Manzanita. They are surely my favorite local tree. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2012)

File.Name: cc09d_cc08-005.jpg
Notes:
8259973426 These are ripe berries of locally uncommon native Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum) in the Ericaceae plant family, growing in the shady north-slope Madrone and Tanoak woodland near home. These shrubs really belong further north in Northern California and Oregon, but we have a few colonies scattered in our mountains. I've never see berries before here. The shrubs are common in Oregon, though I don't see many berries there either - do the birds get them first? (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2012)

I know this isn't the best photo. I had to increase my ISO and aperture to get any photo in the dark woods, and it's still not sharp. But it is a record photo. Yep, I ate the berries!

File.Name: cc09e_cc08-023.jpg
Notes: 8259921194 I believe this potato-like mushroom is the Dark Puffball (Lycoperdon foetidum). There were a couple of almost-black immature specimens nearby. You can see part of the yellowish spore mass pushing out of the apical pore on top and spilling below. It's growing on my trail yesterday with leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2012)

File.Name: cc09f_cc09-005.jpg
Notes: 8259866286 I didn't study these Agaricus mushrooms closely enough, but maybe they are the Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola) or kin. They are "lutescent" - meaning that they stain yellow when bruised, with a sweet smell. and an enlarged base. They have a ring on the stem and no volval cup at the base. The ring isn't thick and doesn't stand out from the stem. It may well be edible, but I don't trust any of these agaricus species in the woods. See this photo! (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2012)

File.Name: cc09g_cc08-029.jpg
Notes: 8258748895 These are more sporangia of Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on a dead log of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. They look different from any I've shown so far, but maybe it's just the dry weather? See this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2012)

File.Name: cc09h_cc08-031.jpg
Notes:
8259783000 Here are more still-streaming plasmodia of a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on a log of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. You can see cellular material streaming into the caps in the upper-left. Maybe they are Arcyria denudata or kin in the family Trichiales? I really don't know. They sure look like little wieners - hot dogs on a stick, of course! See this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 8 December 2012)

I've been showing lots of slime molds lately. Well, if you got emí, flaunt emí!
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Arcyria denudata; Trichiales
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Arcyria denudata; Trichiales

File.Name: cc10a_cc10-008.jpg
Notes:
8263356742 These are blushing new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. I reckon they are the second shrub to flower in the new rain year, after Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae). They have Thrips (order Thysanoptera)! I guess the tiny thrips are genus Aeolothrips in the family Aeolothripidae. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2012)

The woods are getting dry, but there's a good chance of more rain starting on Wednesday.

*Sigh* Still no water in the house. The six inch line was bent in a curve, so the plumbers couldn't mate the ends, and you can't just bend 6 inch PVC like you can 3/4 inch. Maybe that's why it broke there? Maybe tomorrow morning - I'm ready for a shower!
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae; Thysanoptera; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae; Thysanoptera; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae

File.Name: cc10b_cc10-007.jpg
Notes: 8262241373 Here are tiny Thrips (order Thysanoptera) on a blushing new flower of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. I guess they are genus Aeolothrips in the family Aeolothripidae - see Bug Guide. I watched them for a while - they are like little roosters in the chicken yard! They seem to spend their time chasing each other away. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2012)
ScientificName: Thysanoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae
associatedTaxa: Thysanoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae

File.Name: cc10c_cc10-025.jpg
Notes: 8263275522 These are new flowers of a Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family in the woods. They are always our first native flower of the new rain year. Maybe my white-balance is a bit off in this photo taken in deep shade, but they look about like what I saw today. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2012)

File.Name: cc10d_cc10-021.jpg
Notes: 8262192393 These are ripe berries of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. The raw berries are astringent - they dry your mouth out - but they are tasty when cooked and were a native American Chumash staple. I've made a sweet Toyon cider by pouring hot water over the berries and letting it sit, like tea. Delicious! (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2012)

File.Name: cc10e_cc10-012.jpg
Notes: 8262149667 This is another photo of pear-shaped Dark Puffball (Lycoperdon foetidum) mushrooms in the woods today. They have a narrowed base and small spines that drop away as they mature. Despite the species name, they don't have a bad odor. Rodents like to nibble on them - see my [Previous] photo. And see this photo for a mature cap releasing spores. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2012)

File.Name: cc10f_cc10-016.jpg
Notes: 8263193566 Some rodent has been nibbling on this potato-like Dark Puffball (Lycoperdon foetidum) mushroom in the woods. Despite the species name, it looks edible and probably is, but I haven't tried it. You can see foliose lichens and moss in the background. See my [Next] photo of the mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2012)

File.Name: cc10g_cc10-014.jpg
Notes: 8263136166 Here is a cascade of "Little Brown Mushrooms" (LBMs) growing on well-rotted wood of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). You can see a natural spore print on the third mushroom up from the bottom. They have reddish-brown spores, so they might well be some kind of Galerina in the Cortinariaceae family of mushrooms. Several Galerinas are deadly poison, so don't even think about eating them! They are too small anyway. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2012)

File.Name: cc10h_cc10-009.jpg
Notes:
8263095938 I find the strangest things on my daily walks in the woods!

I suppose this might be a gall, but more likely it's the shell of a Gall-like Scale Insect in the family Kermesidae of the superfamily Coccoidea in the Homoptera - a "Kermes Scale". It may well be genus Allokermes. Did something go in or out? And what gives with those four perfect holes - or six if you count the partial holes at the bottom? Wouldn't one hole be enough for a parasite after the critter inside? I'm glad there are still some mysteries in our woods after so many years! (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2012)

File.Name: cc11a_cc11-010.jpg
Notes:
8265261035 Here are new flower catkins of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family just starting to flower. See my [Previous] photo for a closer photo. The flowers appear as "strings of pearls". These shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. This one is male and staminate. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2012)

There's no sign of it yet, but we have an 80% chance of rain tomorrow. Just what we need to keep the mushrooms going!

File.Name: cc11b_cc11-011.jpg
Notes:
8266281104 These are the odd new flowers of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family. If you look left of center, you can see new stamens emerging from the catkin-like flowers. It's just starting to flower. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2012)

I reckon it's the third flowering shrub of the new rain year, after Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca, Ericaceae) and Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae). The new year is in progress!
ScientificName: Garrya veatchii; Garryaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Garrya veatchii; Garryaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: cc11c_cc11-030.jpg
Notes:
8265153519 In the upper-right is an interesting Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family - with Thrips (order Thysanoptera). I first thought it might be a wasp after the thrips, but you can clearly see the bee working the flower through a hole in the side. It's a very wasp-like bee, but I think I can make out pollen-hairs on its legs. There were quite a few of the visiting the flowers today. They fly with a noisy buzz. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 December 2012)

If you know what wild bee this is, please leave a comment.
ScientificName: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Thysanoptera
associatedTaxa: Anthophila; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Thysanoptera

File.Name: cc11d_cc11-024.jpg
Notes: 8265127943 Here are more new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family - with Thrips (order Thysanoptera)! I guess the tiny thrips are genus Aeolothrips in the family Aeolothripidae. The thrips seem ubiquitous on these flowers. I got this photo higher in the mountains than yesterday, near the FAA radio tower at the high point between Painted Cave and Arroyo Burro saddle. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 December 2012)
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Thysanoptera; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Thysanoptera; Aeolothrips; Aeolothripidae

File.Name: cc11e_cc11-019.jpg
Notes: 8265060903 I believe these are odd Sessile Earthstar (Geastrum saccatum or kin) mushrooms. I moved them into the sunlight for this photo, but they were growing on a shady bank along my trail today under mostly Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They don't have stems, and the apical pore is set off by a circle - see the cap on the right. Like all earthstars, they are modified puffballs with a thick outer skin that splits and unfolds into a star-like shape that lifts up the spore sack within. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2012)

File.Name: cc11f_cc11-005.jpg
Notes: 8266101698 I believe these are dark-spored Psathyrella mushrooms - maybe the Clustered Psathyella (Psathyrella hydrophila)? The caps are "hygrophanous" - changing color as they dry out. There's a fresh-looking dark cap in the upper-right. As mushrooms go, they are kind of weedy, and these are growing with mostly weeds. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2012)

File.Name: cc11g_cc11-016.jpg
Notes: 8264997743 These are squat Agaricus mushrooms coming up with new grass in a sunny place in the woods. They have chocolate-brown spores and a faint ring on the stem. They don't smell like phenol, but I still didn't taste them. (I'm still recovering from tasting a bad agaricus last week!) I won't try to guess what species it is. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2012)

File.Name: cc11h_cc11-001.jpg
Notes: 8266035746 This is part of my ongoing project to photograph tiny mushrooms. I believe this is a tiny Mycena acicula (or kin) mushroom growing from a dark oak wood chip. I included my pen as a ruler. My pen has a ball of 0.7 mm, so the caps are only a couple of millimeters across. This is the smallest beautiful mushroom I know that I actually have a name for. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2012)

File.Name: cc12a_cc11-013.jpg
Notes:
This is a different landscape of Lichens growing on a twig of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. The little cup-like structures are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2012)

It was a dark and gloomy day with rain threatening, and I had a natural history society board meeting at night, so I never got around to posting. Two photos (a day late) will do just fine.
CommonNames: Lichens; Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak

File.Name: cc12b_cc11-014.jpg
Notes: This is looking out over the upper Santa Ynez Valley - turning green from recent rains! I got this photo from the top of the ridge above Paradise Creek, looking north. From left to right in the background is Zaca Peak, Figueroa Mountain, Ranger peak, Cachuma Saddle, Cachuma Peak, and the lower slopes of McKinley Peak. In the middle distance is ranchland and Loma Alta. And at the bottom is the Santa Ynez River. It's fabulous country. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2012)

File.Name: cc13a_cc12-001.jpg
Notes:
8271676592 This is a bubbly mass of Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing from dead wood in the woods. I believe they might be genus Stemonitis. See my [Previous] photo. I found this in woods, not in the ocean. They sure look like the eggs of *something*. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2012)

In my [Previous] photo, I show the same creatures in a photo taken today, a day later. They change so fast! I'll try to get back for another photo tomorrow. See this photo for more info about slime molds.

We had 0.65 inches of rain last night - enough to keep the mushrooms going. It's been very cold today - it never got out of the 30's on our upstairs porch, and it will surely freeze tonight. I brought all the sensitive plants inside.

File.Name: cc13b_cc13-002.jpg
Notes:
8271606992 This is a cellular mass of Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing from dead wood in the woods. They are living up to their name! I guess they are genus Stemonitis - like hot dogs on a stick. You can see the "sticks" lined up around the side, and that might be a failed "hot dog"-like sporangium lying across the top. It rained last night. I wonder if that interfered with its schedule? (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2012)

It really is pretty amazing to think that this is a coin-size mass of material that can reproduce!

In my [Next] photo, I show the same creatures in a photo taken a day earlier. They change so fast! I'll try to get back for another photo tomorrow. See this photo for more info about slime molds.

File.Name: cc13c_cc12-009.jpg
Notes:
8271585156 Compare this photo with my [Previous] one. They show the same little Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on the same fallen leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe they are the species Lycogala epidendrumor kin. These slime molds are about 1/4 inch (6 mm) across. They look like little puffball fungi. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2012)

In my [Previous] photo, I show the same creatures in a photo taken a day later. They change so fast! I'll try to get back for another photo tomorrow. See this photo for more info about slime molds.
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lycogala epidendrum
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lycogala epidendrum

File.Name: cc13d_cc13-008.jpg
Notes:
8271544260 Here are a group of maturing Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on fallen leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family today. I believe they are the species Lycogala epidendrumor kin. These slime molds are about 1/4 inch (6 mm) across. They look like little puffball fungi. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2012)

In my [Next] photo, I show the same creatures in a photo taken yesterday. They change so fast! I'll try to get back for another photo tomorrow. See this photo for more info about slime molds.
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lycogala epidendrum
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Lycogala epidendrum

File.Name: cc13e_cc12-003.jpg
Notes: 8270413697 These are different Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on fallen leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). Maybe they are Badhamia utricularis? These are still in the streaming stage. See this photo for more info about slime molds. I got this photo yesterday, and went back to the same place today - and couldn't find them. We did have some rain last night. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2012)
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Badhamia utricularis
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Badhamia utricularis

File.Name: cc13f_cc12-012.jpg
Notes:
8271437706 Another exotic slime mold or fungus in the woods growing on to an oak leaf?

Nah, it's just a bit of Styrofoam waste along our road, but it sure had me going for a few minutes while I took photos, until I actually touched it! I'm often fooled by these "fungal forgeries" - usually a fallen leaf that looks just like a mushroom. (San Marcos Pass, 12 December 2012)

File.Name: cc13g_cc13-018.jpg
Notes: 8271417968 This is a beautiful dark-spored Inky Cap (genus Coprinus) mushroom growing along our road. The caps are thinner than tissue paper and easier to tear. Mushrooms in this genus "deliquesce" - they literally digest themselves into black slime. The process is starting in the back. They are quite ephemeral. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2012)

File.Name: cc13h_cc13-024.jpg
Notes:
8271371268 These are edible and delicious Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) mushrooms in the woods. Be careful though - the mushroom caps start lavender or blue, but they soon fade to gray or tan like the little cap and become harder to recognize. We also have an inedible blue Cortinarius mushroom in the woods that looks very similar. See this photo. Caveat emptor! (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2012)

This is one mushroom that you can transplant into your garden. The mushrooms always have copious mycelium at the base of the stem, so dig your trimmings into a bank of oak mulch and let them grow.

File.Name: cc14a_cc14-021.jpg
Notes:
8273109615 Here is the plasmodium streaming into new sporangia of a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing from dead wood in the woods today. You can see the multi-nucleate slime coating the wood above. Maybe these will become genus Stemonitis? The white blobs seem stuck on the stems, like marshmallows on a stick. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2012)

It's been a good year for slime molds - or maybe it just that I'm not teaching any more, so I can get out into woods every day to find them. They only last for a day or two. I think they might be my favorite creatures to find and photograph. See this photo for more info about slime molds.

We've had just a sprinkle of rain so far tonight, but I hope more is coming. It's still very cold. It got down to 25∞ last night on our upstairs porch. It's only about 34∞ right now as the rain is starting - maybe it will snow?

File.Name: cc14b_cc14-012.jpg
Notes: 8273078337 I found this streaming plasmodium of a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing from dead wood in the woods today. It's rather large - several inches across. It might be a Badhamia species, maybe Badhamia utricularis, though I don't really know. But I do know where it lives. I'll go back tomorrow and look for sporangia. See this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2012)

File.Name: cc14c_cc14-020.jpg
Notes: 8273048531 These are sporangia of a different species of genus Stemonitis in the Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing from dead wood in the woods. They are not as clustered as the species in my [Previous] photo. They look less dreadlock-like and more wiener-like - hot dogs of course! Maybe those white fibers at the base are the plasmodia still streaming into the sporangia? (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2012)

File.Name: cc14d_cc14-019.jpg
Notes:
8274045310 These are sporangia of genus Stemonitis of the Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing from dead wood in the woods, like a tangle of rasta dreadlocks. You can see them shedding their spores. See this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2012)

I wonder about the shiny dark stems. How does the multi-cellular plasmodium differentiate into stem and sporangia? Is this an example of altruism - that some cells give up their lives for the good of the whole? Or does the multi-nucleate plasmodium migrate all the cell nuclei into the sporangia and form the stem from cytoplasm? See this discussion on The Altruism of Slime - with a comic! (There's a lot more if you Google ["slime mold" altruism].)

File.Name: cc14e_cc14-015.jpg
Notes: 8274015878 This is a small Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plant in the canyon - maybe the California Liverwort (Asterella californica), but I don't really know. They are growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon, and it's neat how they radiate from a central point. Those little tongue-like structures with little bumps on the leaves are probably the flat asexual buds known as gemmae which splash out in the rain. They also produce tiny umbrella-like sporophytes. Liverworts are primitive but complicated! (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2012)

File.Name: cc14f_cc14-002.jpg
Notes: 8272910815 This beautiful Lepiota mushroom sure looks like the Shaggy-Stalked Parasol (Lepiota clypeolaria or kin) mushrooms that I find growing under conifers in the Oregon woods. (See this photo.) I found this specimen growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. Maybe it's the form "nabiscodisca" that mushroom author David Arora says he find under oaks? It has free white gills, a ring on the stem, and the cap dark center breaks into scales as it expands. It looks like a perfectly toasted marshmallow - but it's said to be poisonous. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2012)
ScientificName: Lepiota; Lepiota clypeolaria; Quercus agrifolia; nabiscodisca
associatedTaxa: Lepiota; Lepiota clypeolaria; Quercus agrifolia; nabiscodisca

File.Name: cc14g_cc14-021.jpg
Notes: 8272878947 This is a beautiful cluster of the Psathyrella longipes group of mushrooms in the dark-spored Coprinaceae family. The caps are adorned with widely spaced, tooth-like veil remnants, and grow on debris in the woods. They are large but very fragile. These are growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. I don't usually find them in groups like this. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2012)

File.Name: cc14h_cc13-019.jpg
Notes: 8273934626 This is a beautiful cluster of Oak-Loving Collybia (Collybia dryophila or kin) mushrooms coming up in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. They are said to be edible, but I haven't tried them. The little dark specks on some of the gills are Springtail (order Collembola) insects. These are no doubt the most common mushrooms in the woods right now. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2012)
CommonNames: Oak-Loving Collybia; Coast Live Oak; Springtail

File.Name: cc15a_cc15-015.jpg
Notes:
8275800189 Here is a native terrestrial Land Snail (Gastropoda) in the woods - maybe genus Helminthoglypta. I'm not sure what kind of snail it is, but it's NOT one of the pesky Garden Snails with a vertical shell that are so common in the lowlands. It's definitely a native. I found this snail under the old board I placed out by our mailbox. They like to hide during the day, and I'm pretty sure they're nocturnal. I don't find them very often. You can see a bit of its slime trail behind. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2012)

There is an interesting web page on Ventura County Wildlife - Terrestrial Snails and Slugs: http://www.magney.org/photofiles/VenturaCountySnails1.htm

Tonight is a ringer for last night. It's cold and it's beginning to rain. We only got 0.06 inches of rain last night, but I'm hoping for more tonight.
CommonNames: Land Snail; Garden Snails; Ventura County Wildlife - Terrestrial Snails and Slugs

File.Name: cc15b_cc13-012.jpg
Notes: 8276821326 This is another large hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus - or is it Ariolimax californicus?) prowling through the woods. They're 6-8 inches long! This is the largest terrestrial mollusk in our mountains, and we only see them after rains. They have two pairs of retractile "feelers" on their heads. The upper pair have eye spots, while the lower air provides a tactile sense and smell. They move surprising fast - fast enough to blur a photo. I wonder what the world looks like to a banana slug? (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2012)

File.Name: cc15c_cc15-001.jpg
Notes: 8275732837 These are little Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants in the canyon - maybe the California Liverwort (Asterella californica), but I don't really know. They are growing on a sandstone outcrop. You can see reproductive structures starting to form - probably sporophytes. These are different from the gemmae in yesterday's photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2012)

File.Name: cc15d_cc15-006.jpg
Notes: 8275674613 This is a cute little cluster of Pyxie Cup Lichens (genus Cladonia) growing on sandstone in the woods today. They are a fruticose lichen with a stalk, but they come from a foliose base. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2012)

File.Name: cc15e_cc13-016.jpg
Notes:
8276695320 This is a strange Earthstar (Astraeus hygrometricus or kin) "mushroom" - at least it's the fruiting body of a fungus - growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. The "rays" of the fruiting body fold back to lift up the spore sack inside when it's damp. When it's dry, they fold back up to enclose the spore sack. It's been wet lately! They are modified puffballs with a thick outer skin that splits and unfolds into a star-like shape that lifts up the spore sack within. The old rinds last in the woods for years, but this is a new one with spore sack still attached. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2012)

I keep an old fruiting body above our retaining wall at eye level as a hygrometer. It folds up and down as the humidity changes. It really works!

File.Name: cc15f_cc15-012.jpg
Notes:
8276671238 Here is a troop of "Little Brown Mushrooms" - LBMs - fruiting on wood chips along our road. They have rusty-brown spores and adnate gills. I think these might be Tubaria furfuracea or kin. My mushroom book describes them as the "quintessential LBM". The little mushrooms look robust here, but they dry out to almost nothing. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2012)

No LBMs with rusty spores should be eaten. They are hard to tell apart, and at least one of them - Galerina autumnalis - contains deadly amanita-toxins!

File.Name: cc15g_cc13-011.jpg
Notes: 8275532771 These are pretty little yellow Bolbitius vitellinus mushrooms coming up along our driveway. Despite the white gills, they leave a rusty-brown spore print. They are quite ephemeral - here today and gone tomorrow. I've collected more interesting mushrooms lately, but I need more time to figure out what the heck they are. And most recent mushrooms in the woods are the ephemeral sort - decomposers, not the more interesting "mycorrhizal" kinds that connect with our oak trees in perennial associations. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2012)

File.Name: cc15h_cc14-017.jpg
Notes: 8276579720 This is the streaming plasmodium of a different Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing from dead wood in the woods. Compare my photo yesterday. This one is a different color, and much smaller - only about 1/2" across. We'll see what it becomes. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2012)

File.Name: cc16a_cc14-005.jpg
Notes:
8278926643 I believe these are pink-spored Pluteus petasatus mushrooms. You can see a just a hint of the pink color on the gills and stems. They are growing on dead oak wood in the woods. I actually did a spore print from the mushroom that I picked on the left. The spore print checks out. It's a confusing mushroom with those free gills that look so white on the left! On the other hand, Pluteus was my first thought when I found them. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2012)

The drizzle never stopped today. We only have 1/4+ inch in the rain gauge, but it's been the kind of steady drizzle that is good for Banana Slugs and slime molds and mushrooms! I never did get outside with my camera. There's a good chance for more rain tomorrow, but I hope it holds off until evening.

File.Name: cc16b_cc14-003.jpg
Notes: 8278896911 These mushrooms in the woods had me wondering. They have white gills and gray caps, and they are growing from oak wood. I believe that despite the white gills, they are the pink-spored Pluteus petasatus. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2012)

File.Name: cc16c_cc15-010.jpg
Notes: 8278896911 This is a pretty little dark-spored Inky Cap (genus Coprinus) mushroom growing along our road - maybe some kind of Woolly Inky Cap (Coprinus lagopus or kin). The cap is covered with universal veil tissue that soon wears away. The caps are thinner than tissue paper and easier to tear. Mushrooms in this genus "deliquesce" - they literally digest themselves into black slime. I have another recent photo of (probably) the same mushroom

File.Name: cc16d_cc15-008.jpg
Notes:
8279861992 This is a group of tiny Pyxie Cup (Cladonia) lichens growing on a sandstone boulder in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2012)

Lichens are composite organisms that are both fungi and algae. This makes reproduction difficult. The lichens in my photo seem to be covered in a greenish powder, which I reckon are soredia reproductive structures - tiny bundles of a few algal cells surrounded by a tangle of fungal hyphae. Wind and rain carry them away to make more lichens. Simple, but it works for them.

File.Name: cc16e_cc10-018.jpg
Notes: 8278733965 This is a different Cladonia lichen growing in a tangle on sandstone in the woods. The fruticose stalks come from a foliose base and look like little Christmas trees. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2012)

File.Name: cc16f_cc14-023.jpg
Notes:
8279729926 I'm not sure what this mushroom is. It's growing on well-rotted oak wood in the canyon. It has the smooth cap and reddish-brown colors of Tubaria furfuracea (see this photo), but it appears to have free gills and lacks the striations on the cap. It's umbonate - like a sombrero hat! (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2012)

You will never know all the mushrooms you find. (But if you recognize this one, please let me know!)
Locality: But if you recognize this one

File.Name: cc16g_cc13-022.jpg
Notes: 8279602352 This mushroom is definitely an Agaricus - dark-chocolate-brown spores, skirt-like ring on the stem, and no volva. It appears "rufescent" - it seems to stain reddish. (It has been raining, which may unnaturally bring out the colors.) It's growing in the woods, and you can see root-like "rhizomorphs" - those thick white root-like things at the base of the stalk. It's rather small, just a couple of inches across. I've been trying to identify it, but I'm not sure what it is. It may be edible - do you feel lucky, punk? (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2012)

File.Name: cc16h_cc14-013.jpg
Notes:
8279549604 This is a "gelatinous blob" of Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica), a Jelly Fungus (Tremelliales) in the woods. They are said to be edible and tasteless - no surprise, they're mostly water. These odd "mushrooms" appear rain and then dry out into a hard yellow crust as it dries out. This one looks like it was dusted with pepper. I'm not sure what the dark specks are. (They are not springtails.) (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2012)

The other brown fungus in the photo is a resupinate Crust Fungus - some kind of the genus Stereum. My book says that "Most Tremellas are frequently found with Stereum, suggesting some kind of relationship between the two.
CommonNames: Witch's Butter; Jelly Fungus; Crust Fungus

File.Name: cc17a_cc17-012.jpg
Notes:
8283345178 These tiny raindrops have been skewered by immature sporophytes of a native Moss (Bryophyta) in the canyon today. How do you skewer a raindrop? (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2012)

Tonight is another ringer for the last few nights. We have another small storm moving in that probably won't produce much rain, but a drizzle is perfect for slime molds and mushrooms and banana slugs!

File.Name: cc17b_cc17-015.jpg
Notes: 8282263945 These are new leaves on native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family, with raindrops- and reflections and spider webs. These plants are summer deciduous - they lose their leaves in summer and depend on their photosynthetic green stems to make do. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2012)

File.Name: cc17c_cc17-037.jpg
Notes: 8282231763 These are new seedlings of native Fiesta Flower (Pholistoma auritum) in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family growing in the woods. It's fun to try to identify seedlings as they appear. These plants are easy, with those distinctive pinnate leaves. You can see the "seed leaves" or cotyledons that are very different from later leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2012)

File.Name: cc17d_cc17-026.jpg
Notes: 8282168559 This is all that's left of the beautiful Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) plasmodium that I showed a few days ago. See my photo. I believe these are the sorangia and slime of Badhamia utricularis. They are growing on well-rotted wood of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family, with fungi. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2012)
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Badhamia utricularis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Badhamia utricularis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: cc17e_cc17-005.jpg
Notes: 8282136969 This is another beautiful cluster of Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) plasmodia becoming sporangia on fallen leaves and twigs of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They might be genus Physarella or Badhamia. I've found the before, and I believe this photo shows what they become. See this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2012)
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Physarella; Badhamia
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Physarella; Badhamia

File.Name: cc17f_cc17-006.jpg
Notes: 8283140256 I should know what kind of Lichen this (or these) is, but I'm not even sure if they are one or two kinds. They are abundant on tree branches like this fallen twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) on the ground. Strange and beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2012)

File.Name: cc17g_cc17-034.jpg
Notes: 8283068038 These are a different white-spored Mycena mushroom growing from dead wood of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). Maybe it's Mycena galericulata? I don't know what kind of Mycena they are, but I can recognize new green shoots of native California Bedstraw (Galium californicum on the left. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2012)
ScientificName: Mycena; Quercus agrifolia; Mycena galericulata; Mycena; Galium californicum
associatedTaxa: Mycena; Quercus agrifolia; Mycena galericulata; Mycena; Galium californicum

File.Name: cc17h_cc17-008.jpg
Notes: 8281990929 This is a troop of tiny white-spored Mycena mushrooms - the classic "LBM" or "Little Brown Mushroom". They are small and fragile - quite ephemeral. These are growing from fallen leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2012)

File.Name: cc18a_cc18-003.jpg
Notes:
8286563252 These are raindrops on a new leaf of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family, reflecting the woods today. I tried to move that stem on the right, and managed to knock off several important raindrops. The starbursts in the center are an artifact of my lens, not a software trick. Nothing holds raindrops like Lupine leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2012)

(I had a better chance at a "lupine with raindrops" photo yesterday with more raindrops after our misty rain. Damn, I botched it.)

It's been cold and windy today - wind gusts over 40 mph and temps in the 30s, my least favorite weather. It's already frosty.
Locality: I had a better chance at a "lupine with raindrops" photo yesterday with more raindrops after our misty rain.  Damn

File.Name: cc18b_cc18-005.jpg
Notes: 8285441543 This is a small mushroom growing on a moss bank on a rock outcrop in the woods, with lichen behind it. I suspect it is some kind of Galerina in the Cortinariaceae family of mushrooms, but I didn't collect it. It's stately and beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2012)

File.Name: cc18c_cc18-006.jpg
Notes: 8286440164 These are new Boletus chrysenteron or Boletus subtomentosus mushrooms growing under our upstairs porch in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. Like all Boletes, they have pores instead of gills. The stem is coarsely reticulate with pretty colors. They said to be edible, but definitely not choice. These are the first Boletes I've seen this year. I look forward to many more - and more interesting ones! (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2012)

File.Name: cc18d_cc18-007.jpg
Notes: 8285340907 I believe these are "Poison Pie" (Hebeloma crustuliniforme or kin) mushrooms in the woods today. They have viscid caps and brown spores. The gills are ragged, with white outlines. These mushrooms are often covered with tiny Springtail (order Collembola) insects that get stuck in the slime - I consider this a field mark. Like the common name suggests, don't eat them. They cause mild to severe gastrointestinal distress. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2012)

File.Name: cc18e_cc17-016.jpg
Notes:
8285274965 These are small Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants in the canyon - maybe the California Liverwort (Asterella californica), but I don't really know. Those little tongue-like structures with tiny bumps on the leaves are probably the flat asexual buds known as gemmae which splash out in the rain. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2012)

It's interesting that these liverworts have asexual gemmae and plants a few feet away have immature sexual sporophytes. (See this photo.) These are different reproductive strategies. I guess it's always good to have a backup!
ScientificName: Marchantiophyta; Asterella californica; gemmae; gemmae; sporophytes
associatedTaxa: Marchantiophyta; Asterella californica; gemmae; gemmae; sporophytes

File.Name: cc18f_cc17-004.jpg
Notes: 8285243383 These beautiful Lepiota mushrooms have the ragged cap of a Shaggy-Stalked Parasol (Lepiota clypeolaria or kin) mushroom. Maybe it's the form "nabiscodisca" that mushroom author David Arora says he find under oaks? These are growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). Like all Lepiotas, the cap has a darker center breaks into scales as it expands. It looks like a perfectly toasted marshmallow. They get a couple of inches across. It's said to be poisonous. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2012)
ScientificName: Lepiota; Lepiota clypeolaria; nabiscodisca; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Lepiota; Lepiota clypeolaria; nabiscodisca; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: cc18g_cc17-010.jpg
Notes: 8285203387 I believe these are the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) mushroom, though the rings look pretty ragged on these specimens after a rain. It looks delicious, but it has a phenol-like smell and taste that is disgusting. Be very careful collecting woodland agaricus species! (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2012)

File.Name: cc18h_cc17-022.jpg
Notes: 8286227534 This is surely the same "rufescent" (red-staining) Agaricus mushroom that I showed a couple of days ago. (Here is my photo.) It smells mild, and doesn't have the felt-like ring of Agaricus hondensis. They are growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). I still don't know what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2012)

File.Name: cc19a_cc19-012.jpg
Notes:
8289235737 This is another photo of the mushroom I believe is the Meadow Coral (Clavulinopsis corniculata) or kin. It's small - only an inch or so across. It's beautiful. It looks like it belongs in the ocean! It's growing among fallen leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in a place where the sun never shines this close to the winter solstice. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2012)

If you look close, you can make out a small native slug in the lower-left trying to get away!

It's was sunny today and the wind died down, but it's very cold. Water froze solid last night in the saucers under the potted plants on the porch, and never completely thawed today. It's another cold night tonight.

File.Name: cc19b_cc19-014.jpg
Notes: 8289164731 I believe this little coral mushroom is the Meadow Coral (Clavulinopsis corniculata) or kin. It's small - only an inch or so across. It's beautiful. It looks like it belongs in the ocean! It's growing among fallen leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in a place where the sun never shines this close to the winter solstice. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2012)

File.Name: cc19c_cc19-005.jpg
Notes: 8290167848 These are yellow-colored ripe fruiting bodies of the Dark Puffball (Lycoperdon foetidum) mushrooms in the woods. You can see that they have an apical pore. The yellow colors are distinctive. See this photo. Despite the species name, they don't have a bad odor. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2012)

File.Name: cc19d_cc19-016.jpg
Notes: 8289090597 I found these handsome potato-like Dark Puffball (Lycoperdon foetidum) mushrooms in the woods today. They are growing under the fallen leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2012)

File.Name: cc19e_cc19-008.jpg
Notes: 8289068535 I guess these "infundibular" (funnel-shaped) mushrooms in our woods are genus Clitocybe with those decurrent gills growing down the stem. They are growing under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. These are quite pretty with that whitish rim on the caps. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2012)

File.Name: cc19f_cc19-026.jpg
Notes: 8290094624 These are the first new "flowers" of Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family that I've seen this year. The red color is mostly from leafy bracts, but I see a few real flowers mixed in - for example at the upper left. This plant is growing in a place where the sun never shines this close to the winter solstice, so please forgive my frosty white balance. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2012)

File.Name: cc19g_cc19-027.jpg
Notes: 8289014775 These are unusually rosy new flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. The shrubs are quite variable. Most flowers are white, but some are a bit rosy - and these are really rosy! This is the stuff that cultivars are made of. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2012)

File.Name: cc19h_cc19-022.jpg
Notes:
8288956927 This is the "mast" - the accumulation of fallen flowers and fruit - under a flowering shrub of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2012)

There's a really interesting (and readable) article on "The Mystery of Masting in Trees" by Dr. Walt Koenig of the Cornell Lab of Ornithology available here. He talks mostly about oaks, but it's a good read.
Locality: and readable) article on "The Mystery of Masting in Trees" by Dr. Walt Koenig of the Cornell Lab of Ornithology available here. He talks mostly about oaks

File.Name: cc20a_cc20-013.jpg
Notes:
8293232094 This is another mystery mushroom that I can't identify. It's growing in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). It sure looks like a Tricholoma, but I really don't know. It's solid and rather handsome. If you recognize them, please let me know! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2012)

Just four photos tonight - company is coming! All these photos today have something in common - I really don't know what the mushrooms are. Well, you can never identify all the mushrooms you find.

The Winter Solstice happens at 3:12 am PST tonight. It's the "dark night of the year" in the Northern Hemisphere. The days will start getting just a bit longer tomorrow, and the sun will rise just a bit higher in the sky tomorrow. Happy Solstice!

File.Name: cc20b_cc20-002.jpg
Notes: 8292122757 This is another mystery mushroom that I can't identify. It's growing in the garden, but there is Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) nearby. It sure looks like a Tricholoma, but they have brown spores despite the white gills. The caps have a whitish center, suggesting a universal veil. If you recognize them, please let me know! (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2012)

File.Name: cc20c_cc20-005.jpg
Notes:
8293114098 These are "Wood Chipper Mushrooms" (my name) growing on wood chips from a trimming project along our driveway a few years ago. I don't know what they are, but I suspect they come with the wood chipper. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2012)

These mushrooms have brown spores and a partial veil that leaves an evanescent ring. You can see veil fragments on the right. I have no idea what they are or if they are edible. Maybe they are some Agrocybe other than A. praecox? If you recognize them, please let me know!

File.Name: cc20d_cc20-004.jpg
Notes: 8292022487 I'm not sure about this mushroom, but I think it might be Nolanea stricta or kin in the Entolomataceae family of mushrooms. I did a spore print right overnight, and the spores definitely are pinkish. It doesn't have any particular smell. Probably not edible. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2012)

File.Name: cc21a_cc21-014.jpg
Notes:
8296024732 I was chasing a tiny fly, and I noticed this tiny gall on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family today. You can see a perfect exit hole, no doubt made by the tiny Cynipid Gall Wasp (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera) who once called it home. It might be the gall of a Red Cone Gall Wasp (Andricus kingi), though this one seems too small. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2012)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, so I can use my digital camera as an expensive micrometer. The gall is about 550 pixels across on my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of the gall is 23.4x550/4800 = 2.7 mm, only about 1/10 inch. It's really tiny!

We have another small storm moving in tonight, and a larger one due on Sunday. We stand at 10.24 inches of rain for the year, above normal.
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Red Cone Gall Wasp; Andricus kingi
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Red Cone Gall Wasp; Andricus kingi

File.Name: cc21b_cc21-018.jpg
Notes:
8296001860 These are mature galls on leaves of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. I don't know what insect causes them, and they are not listed in my book on "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (Ron Russo, 2006). See my photo from last July. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2012)

Quercus chrysolepis is considered an "Intermediate Oak" - separate from the "Black Oaks" like Coast Live Oak and the "White Oaks" like Scrub Oak. I've found several different galls on them.
CommonNames: Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Field Guide to Plant Galls; Coast Live Oak; Scrub Oak

File.Name: cc21c_cc21-003.jpg
Notes: 8294937307 These are pretty new flowers on staminate catkins of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family. The flowers appear as "strings of pearls". These shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. This shrub is male and staminate. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2012)

File.Name: cc21d_cc21-021.jpg
Notes: 8294898507 This is another large "earth-mover mushroom" - genus Cortinarius under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. It pushed up a gopher-sized mound of dirt. Note the rusty-brown gills and fibers on the top of the cap that are remnants of a "cortina" partial veil. I don't know the species, but I'm sure it is subgenus Bulbopodium, with viscid cap and a bulbous base. There are whitish veil fragments on the cap. It's large and hefty. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2012)

File.Name: cc21e_cc21-023.jpg
Notes:
8294863053 This is a large "earth-mover mushroom" - genus Cortinarius - pushing up a large "mushrump" - that mound of soil and leaves - under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. Note the reddish-brown gills. I don't know the species, but I suspect is subgenus Bulbopodium with that viscid cap. See my [Next] photo. I'll dig it out tomorrow when I have better light. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2012)

The secret to finding mushrooms in the woods is to look for the mushrumps!

File.Name: cc21f_cc20-018.jpg
Notes: 8295873380 These odd Puffball (Lycoperdon) mushrooms have an outer skin the peels away from the inner spore case. Maybe it's the Peeling Puffball (Lycoperdon marginatum), though this one seems to peel of in pieces rather than sheets. It doesn't have a sterile base like the mushroom in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2012)

File.Name: cc21g_cc20-015.jpg
Notes: 8295873380 These are little Puffball (Lycoperdon) mushrooms in the woods. Puffballs are more complicated than I thought! These have a narrow rooting base and small spines on the cap, with an off-center apical pore. I won't try to guess what species they are. (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2012)

File.Name: cc21h_cc19-007.jpg
Notes:
8295724386 This is another strange Earthstar (Astraeus) - maybe Astraeus pteridis with that white checkered pattern inside the star? But it wasn't particularly large, and the inner spore sack is quite white, which sounds more like Astraeus hygrometricus. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2012)

I'm sorry this isn't a better photo. I use my self-timer to trigger the camera on these long exposures. A two-second delay used to be fine, but I seem to need a five-second delay these days. *Sigh* - getting shakier as I get older.

File.Name: cc22a_cc19-018.jpg
Notes:
8297920281 These are brilliant ripe berries of native Christmas Berry aka Toyon or California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. The raw berries are astringent - they dry your mouth out - but they are tasty when cooked and were a native American Chumash staple. I've made a sweet Toyon cider by pouring hot water over the berries and letting it sit, like tea. Delicious! (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2012)

It rained or drizzled all day, so I never got out with my camera. We have about 4/10 inch in the rain gauge - not much, but it's been a good soaker to keep the mushrooms coming. A bigger storm is expected tomorrow - but I hope it's clear in the morning. I've just about used up my recent archive!

I wish you all the best for Solstice, Christmas, Hannukah, etc.
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

8297871931 These are the holly-like leaves of Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. If you see what looks like a scrub oak in the woods with red berries instead of acorns, this is it. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2012)

8297829929 This is another photo of pretty new flowers on staminate catkins of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family - hanging like a Christmas tree ornament. The flowers appear as "strings of pearls". These shrubs are "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. This shrub is male and staminate. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2012)

File.Name: cc22d_cc19-001.jpg
Notes:
8297768855 You might have to blink a few times to get this photo in focus!

These are the light-trails of tiny flies (order Diptera) that dance in the sunbeams after a rain. I got the photo on the edge of our roof, showing a bit of our rain gutter. These little flies are quite invisible unless you stand facing the sun just so. I set my camera to 1 sec @ f/22 @ ISO 100, on a tripod of course. Look close - you can see the steady beating of their wings as little blips in the light trails! (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2012)

In some years, we have massive mating flights of these flies raising like clouds above the oak trees. See this photo (and the two [Previous] photos) from Christmas Day, 2009. I'll keep trying to get a better photo.
Locality: and the two [Previous] photos) from Christmas Day

File.Name: cc22e_cc21-024.jpg
Notes: 8298797400 These Lepiota mushrooms might be the same ones I've been calling the Shaggy-Stalked Parasol (Lepiota clypeolaria or kin) mushroom - but they might be something different. These are growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). Like all Lepiotas, the cap has a darker center breaks into scales as it expands. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2012)

File.Name: cc22f_cc18-002.jpg
Notes: 8298752796 I found this small Lepiota mushroom above the retaining wall on the edge of our garden. It's growing from debris of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. Like all Lepiotas, the cap has a solid center breaks into scales as it expands. This one also has a ring on the stem. Look at the sand grains - this mushroom is less than 1/2 inch across. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2012)

File.Name: cc22g_cc17-036.jpg
Notes: 8297637147 I guess that this is a Crust Mushroom in the Stereaceae family of fungi. These fungi are mostly "resupinate" - they form crust- or parchment-like sheets on dead wood, like this fallen branch of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. This is maybe the same fungus that I often find fruiting with
CommonNames: Crust Mushroom; Coast Live Oak; Witch's Butter; Jelly Fungus
ScientificName: Stereaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Tremella mesenterica; Tremelliales
associatedTaxa: Stereaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Tremella mesenterica; Tremelliales

File.Name: cc22h_cc21-008.jpg
Notes: 8297605843 I believe this lumpy gall is caused by unisexual generations of the Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp (Andricus chrysolepidicola) in the family of Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). They also have alternating sexual generations with completely different solitary galls on leaf buds. These woody galls are "non-detachable" - a permanent part of the stem. You can see a few exit holes on the gall. The galls are very hard. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2012)
CommonNames: Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp; Cynipid Gall Wasps

File.Name: cc23a_cc23-015.jpg
Notes:
8302867444 Here are raindrops on new leaves of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family, reflecting the woods today - like a jeweled Christmas ornament. Nothing holds raindrops like Lupine leaves! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2012)

It was cloudy today, and the drizzle started around 4:00 pm. Now it's really raining. We had 0.43 inches of rain last night, and more is expected tonight.

I wish you all the best for Solstice, Christmas, Hannukah, etc.
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: cc23b_cc23-029.jpg
Notes:
8301747515 By the many arms of Shiva!

These are strange new roots of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family growing in the air! Blackberries grow like a weed in our canyon, so I would never think about propagating them - but the secret to making cuttings is to plant the tips of the stems, not the base. It makes sense - they do make brambles. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2012)

File.Name: cc23c_cc23-036.jpg
Notes:
8302744430 This is the streaming plasmodium of a different Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing from dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) wood in the woods. It seems to be invading the little wood fungus at the bottom - or is it streaming away from the fungus? I don't know what this photo shows - but here it is! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2012)

The slime mold might be Badhamia utricularis - see this photo. And see this photo for more info about slime molds.

File.Name: cc23d_cc23-035.jpg
Notes: 8302700868 These are tiny ripe sporangia of a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on the rotting wood of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree, with raindrops. I believe they are some kind of genus Physarum - see this photo that may show the same creatures. What you see here is mostly the lattice-like capillatum of the slime molds. Left of center is a raindrop that looks full of smoke. Maybe it's really full of spores? See this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2012)

File.Name: cc23e_cc23-039.jpg
Notes: 8301591901 These are pretty little peach-colored mushrooms growing on the decomposing bark of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. I guess they are some kind of white-spored Mycena or Hygrophorus, but I really don't know. If you look close at the bark, you can see the white sporangia of tiny Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria). (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2012)

File.Name: cc23f_cc23-028.jpg
Notes: 8301554943 This is a new cluster of Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Naematoloma fasciculare) mushrooms growing from the base of a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. You can see a natural spore print on the mushroom at the bottom that is just losing it's veil - deep brown. They are beautiful, but poisonous - with a bitter taste to keep you away. I've always had trouble capturing the yellow colors of these mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2012)
CommonNames: Sulfur Tuft; Clustered Woodlover; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: cc23g_cc23-052.jpg
Notes:
8302533812 Maybe these are beautiful Frosted Marasmius (Marasmius plicatulus or kin) mushrooms growing in the woods among the fallen leaves of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree and Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) stems in the woods. The velvety caps and wide-spaced white gills are especially handsome. The stalks are tough and polished, but very brittle - like thin glass. If you have pierced ears, you can wear them! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2012)

I'm not sure that these mushrooms are frosted enough to be the real (Marasmius plicatulus). They might well be something else.
CommonNames: Frosted Marasmius; Coast Live Oak; Wild Blackberry
ScientificName: Marasmius plicatulus; Quercus agrifolia; Rubus ursinus; Marasmius plicatulus

File.Name: cc23h_cc23-045.jpg
Notes: 8302510388 These are the strange antler-like fruiting bodies of Candlesnuff Fungus (Xylaria hypoxylon or kin) - with raindrops. It's no doubt growing from buried wood under the leaves. There are many other species that you need a microscope to distinguish, so this ID is really just a placeholder. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2012)

File.Name: cc24a_cc24-023.jpg
Notes:
8306080302 These are surprisingly beautiful grape-sized galls of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, coated in a layer of rain. They are growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family like Christmas ornaments on a yule tree. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2012)

We had almost an inch of rain last night, and more rain is expected on Christmas night. That's my Christmas present this year!

I wish you all the best for Solstice, Christmas, Hannukah, etc.
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

You see a bit of yellow Rust Fungus on the grass blade behind the raindrops. The rain has been my Christmas present. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2012)

File.Name: cc24c_cc24-009.jpg
Notes:
8304974027 Looking into a Banana Slug's world...

This is rather small Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus - or is it Ariolimax californicus?) prowling through the woods past little Mycena mushrooms. I found this one along East Camino Cielo road today in a place where the sun never shines this close to the solstice. I wonder what it sees with those stalked eyes? (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2012)
