File.Name: cc24d_cc24-015.jpg
Notes:
8305957182 This mushroom has Santa colors - it also has a fungus infection.

I believe these are the Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus or kin) mushrooms in the woods today. The cap is reddish and velvety, the stalk is yellow at the apex and red below, and it stains blue when bruised. Like all Boletes, it has pores instead of gills. The cap on the right has been taken over by the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus, which finally turns them into yellow mush. The infection is spreading to the mushroom on the left. The mushrooms are said to be edible, but the fungus infection is NOT. I almost always find the mushrooms slightly infected in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2012)

File.Name: cc24e_cc23-049.jpg
Notes: 8304885441 This is remarkable! I guess these are the tiny shell-like caps of Marasmiellus candidus growing on a dead stem of Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus) in the Poaceae plant family. The widely-spaced gills are distinctive. They were lined up along the stem like little white parachutes. They do have little off-center stems. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2012)

File.Name: cc24f_cc23-003.jpg
Notes: 8305899242 Here is a micro-world of tiny Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) "mushrooms" growing from Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves above the retaining wall on the edge of our garden. There's an acorn in the photo for scale. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2012)

File.Name: cc24g_cc24-019.jpg
Notes: 8305843564 I believe these are the Pear-shaped Puffball (Lycoperdon pyriforme). The young cap on the left looks quite different from the mature cap on the right. The skin starts white with small spines, becomes cracked and falls away, and ends up smooth and brown with an apical pore to release spores. You can see the olive-brown spores on the right, and white "rhizomorphs" in the lower-right. I believe it's growing from dead wood of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree - at least lignin-rich humus. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2012)

File.Name: cc24h_cc23-042.jpg
Notes: 8304770945 This is the "bleeding mycelium" of some fungus growing on the bark of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. You can see a couple of tiny mushroom "buttons" in the upper-right. The drops look like resin, but they are watery. There are amazing things to be found on any dead wood in the woods! I'm still waiting for salamanders. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2012)

File.Name: cc25a_cc25-042.jpg
Notes:
8309554850 Here is a pool in a sandstone rock with lichens, the fallen leaves at the bottom, and reflected tree branches from above. The rock is a water-filled bedrock mortar hole made by the Chumash people in the old days. I've always admired the "Three Worlds" print by M.C. Escher, and I was trying for that effect. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2012)

I wish you all the best for Solstice, Christmas, Hannukah, etc.
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: cc25d_cc25-027.jpg
Notes: 8309504130 These are the Big Laughing Mushroom (Gymnopilus spectabilis aka G. junonius and G. venticosus) growing from the base of a dying tree of Knobcone Pine (Pinus attenuata). The pines were introduced 100+ years ago - the mushrooms didn't kill them! Our west coast mushrooms seem to lack the inebriating qualities of the Japanese and east coast G. spectabilis, so David Arora's name Jumbo Gym is probably more appropriate. Que l·stima. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2012)

File.Name: cc25e_cc25-007.jpg
Notes: 8308380497 I'm not sure if these are "real" Oyster Mushrooms (Pleurotis ostreatus) or kin, but they are definitely Pleurotid fungi. There were lots of them on this broken trunk of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family today. These look brown, but the younger specimens were quite white. They are growing as shelves on the tree stump, and there were more on the fallen trunk on the ground. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2012)
CommonNames: Oyster Mushrooms; Pleurotid fungi; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: cc25f_cc25-022.jpg
Notes:
8309376562 I got slime molds for Christmas!

These are bright little sporangia of Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on fallen leaves and twigs of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. There's an acorn for scale. They might be genus Physarella or Badhamia. See this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2012)

We took a Christmas walk out to our old cabin on Kinevan Ranch on the pass. I won't show the exact location, except to show the old Kinevan Ranch house.

File.Name: cc26a_cc25-017.jpg
Notes:
8312254843 Just another shack in the woods...

Julie and I used to live in this cabin when it was seeing better days about 35 years ago. Now, it's just another tumble-down shack in the woods - but for me this will always be "The Cabin." Julie and I lived here for 10 years with no electricity or phone back in the 70s and into the 80s. We had artesian spring water, propane refrigerator and hot water, Aladdin kerosene lamps, and a dangerous - but wonderful - wood-fire sauna. We had a large garden, along with goats, pigs, rabbits, ducks, chickens, and geese. I started learning local natural history here, we played music into the night, I built my first computer using wire-wrap and a propane torch to solder, and I started teaching middle school. Our two kids started life here.

So many memories in this tumble-down cabin make it a glow for me now... Maybe every tumble-down cabin in the woods has this glow in memory for someone. It's a good place to visit on Christmas Day. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2012)

We moved a few miles away to our new house in a different canyon in 1983. The cabin has been abandoned for many years except for the deer mice and wood rats. Now it's a "Hanta Hotel" - with a threat of Hanta Virus from rodent droppings just breathing the air inside. Life goes on. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2012)

I'll show where the trail is to get here, not the actual location.

We had 1/4 inch of rain last night. I thought the rain was all over today, but we just had a late downpour that doubled it. More rain is always a blessing.

File.Name: cc26b_cc26-010.jpg
Notes: 8313273764 This is another strange Earthstar (Astraeus) mushroom - like a beast climbing over the oak leaves. Maybe it's Astraeus pteridis, with that checkered white pattern inside the star? But it wasn't particularly large, and the inner spore sack is quite white, which sounds more like Astraeus hygrometricus. There seem to be odd smooth granules inside the spore sack, like the crunchies inside a candy bar. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2012) I gotta post at least one photo from today, even though it was too cold and blustery for photos!

File.Name: cc26c_cc25-032.jpg
Notes: 8312152305 I believe this is a Granulated Slippery Jack (Suillus granulatus) mushroom growing under a Knobcone Pine (Pinus attenuata). It's in the Bolete group of mushrooms, distinguished by the slimy-viscid cap and brown glandular spots on the stem. We've eaten them, but they are not the best. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2012)
CommonNames: Granulated Slippery Jack; Knobcone Pine; Bolete

File.Name: cc26d_cc25-012.jpg
Notes: 8313140228 Here is a beautiful Agaricus mushroom - maybe the Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola) or kin. It has a thin but voluminous "double veil" ring with patches on the underside in a cogwheel pattern. It stains yellow when bruised, has a sweet smell and an enlarged base. The ring isn't thick and doesn't stand out from the stem, and the base of the stem doesn't stain yellow. It may well be edible, but I don't trust any of these agaricus species in the woods. I placed this specimen on a fallen leaf of Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae plant family for my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2012)
ScientificName: Agaricus; Agaricus silvicola; Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae
associatedTaxa: Agaricus; Agaricus silvicola; Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae

The pines were introduced into our woods 100+ years ago, and they are finally dying out. File.Name: cc26f_cc25-008.jpg
Notes: 8313043082 This is a young cap of the same Pleurotid fungus I showed yesterday. Unlike the mushrooms I showed yesterday, this one is almost white. It's growing on the same broken tree of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The gills edges are serrated, which suggests genus Lentinus. On the other hand, there are no scales or hairs on the cap, and I don't see any stalk or veil. I'm not sure what it is, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2012)

It is probably the same species as my photo yesterday - I got the photo a foot away. It might be genus Badhamia or Physarella. See this photo for more info about slime molds. File.Name: cc26h_cc25-043.jpg
Notes: 8311917591 Here are more odd but pretty Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on fallen twigs of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. This photo shows the streaming plasmodium in the process of becoming sporangia. They might be genus Badhamia or Physarella. See this photo for more info about slime molds. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2012)
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Badhamia; Physarella
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Badhamia; Physarella

File.Name: cc27a_cc27-002.jpg
Notes:
8315969237 Insects have been scarce lately, but I found this tiny Fly in the order Diptera perched on a tiny Fairy Hair (Macrotyphula juncea) "mushroom" this morning. (See this photo.) Maybe it's a humpbacked Fungus Gnat in the family Mycetophilidae? I don't see bristle-like hairs on its legs, but I love that name - "mushroom lover"! I had to use ISO 800 for this photo, but I like the painterly background. This fly is tiny - less than 1/8 inch. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2012)

It's cold tonight - already freezing on the upstairs porch.
ScientificName: Diptera; Macrotyphula juncea; Fungus Gnat; Mycetophilidae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Macrotyphula juncea; Fungus Gnat; Mycetophilidae

File.Name: cc27b_cc27-024.jpg
Notes:
8316952642 Don't believe everything you read on mushrooms!

These must be "Satan's Bolete" (Boletus satanas) mushrooms that I found in the woods today. My only hesitation is that they are small, only two inches across instead of the usual six+ inches across. They have red caps and red gills. These Bolete mushroom have a spongy spore-bearing surface instead of gills. They don't look very appetizing - in fact they are quite poisonous. The mushrooms stain blue when you scratch or handle then, but they are not that other notorious blue-staining mushroom. It's fun to leave messages for other mushroom collectors to find! (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2012)

File.Name: cc27c_cc27-010.jpg
Notes: 8316888512 These little Mycena mushrooms are growing under (mostly) Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family, with Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) nearby. They have a beautiful pale lavender color. I think they might be the Lilac Mycena (Mycena pura) or kin - more noticeable in the young caps. It's larger than the usual Mycena. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2012)
CommonNames: Bigberry Manzanita; Coast Live Oak; Lilac Mycena
ScientificName: Mycena; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Mycena pura; Mycena
associatedTaxa: Mycena; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Mycena pura; Mycena

File.Name: cc27d_cc27-017.jpg
Notes: 8315767509 This is another photo of the strange and beautiful little "mystery mushroom" I found in the woods today - with a perfect little ring on the stem. See my [Previous] photo. It's growing under a Manzanita (Arctostaphylos) in the Ericaceae plant family, but I can't remember what kind. It seems to have free gills and no volva, which places it is the "Lepiotoid" group of mushrooms. The gills are dark, and I wonder if it is genus Chamaeota with pink spores? I don't think I've ever found it before. I didn't collect it, but I'll try to get back to it tomorrow to make a spore print. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2012)

File.Name: cc27e_cc27-016.jpg
Notes: 8315673355 This is a strange and beautiful little "mystery mushroom" in the woods today - with a perfect little ring on the stem. I'll post a photo of the underside in my [Next] photo. It's growing under a Manzanita (Arctostaphylos) in the Ericaceae plant family, but I can't remember what kind. It seems to have free gills and no volva, which places it is the "Lepiotoid" group of mushrooms. I wonder if it is genus Chamaeota with pink spores? I don't think I've ever found it before. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2012)

File.Name: cc27f_cc27-027.jpg
Notes: 8316710542 This is another photo of the mushroom I believe is the Meadow Coral (Clavulinopsis corniculata) or kin. It's small - only an inch or so across. It's beautiful. It looks like it belongs in the ocean! It's growing among fallen leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in a place where the sun never shines this close to the winter solstice. I only know it from this one place in our woods. (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2012)

File.Name: cc27g_cc26-001.jpg
Notes:
8316648738 These are more handsome Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Naematoloma fasciculare) mushrooms growing from the base of a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. You can see from the stem of the mushroom I picked that they have yellow gills, but reddish-brown spores. You can often tell spore color from the gills - but not always. They are beautiful, but poisonous - with a bitter taste to keep you away. I've always had trouble capturing the yellow colors of these mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2012)

I didn't notice when I took this photo, but there are tiny pin-head slime molds on the tree above the mushrooms, and little orange cup fungi below. The woods are busy right now!

Darn - I've become so used to taking photos of mushrooms in the shade and gloom that I forget how to get photos in a patch of bright, contrasty sunlight!
CommonNames: Sulfur Tuft; Clustered Woodlover; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: cc27h_cc25-015.jpg
Notes: 8315572271 Here is another photo of beautiful Frosted Marasmius (Marasmius plicatulus or kin) mushrooms growing in the woods among the fallen leaves of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). The velvety caps and wide-spaced white gills are especially handsome. The stalks are tough and polished, but very brittle - like thin glass. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2012)

File.Name: cc28a_cc28-001.jpg
Notes:
8320923046 Here is another photo of the gnarly Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera - maybe the Spiny Assassin Bug (Sinea spinipes, but cp. S. diadema). It has spiny legs and remarkable golden wings. You can see a bit of its mighty proboscis folded under its head. I found this insect today on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). See my [Previous] photo and the photos at BugGuide. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2012)

It was a treat to find an insect to photograph today after so many mushrooms! Taking photos of mushrooms and insects takes a completely different mindset - mushrooms require long exposures, small aperture, and low ISO; insects move, so they require short exposures, larger aperture, and higher ISO. It's a challenge to keep it all straight. Another new insect year has begun!

It's been thinking about rain today on and off, but the NWS still says it's coming. It's another small storm, but they do add up. We now stand at 12.06 inches for the rain year, above normal.
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; Spiny Assassin Bug; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Sinea spinipes; S. diadema; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Sinea spinipes; S. diadema; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: cc28c_cc28-011.jpg
Notes: 8320801832 This is another large "earth-mover mushroom" - genus Cortinarius under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees growing in the woods. It's coming up therough native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. Note the rusty-brown gills and scattered hairs along the stalk, remnants of the original "cortina" that these mushrooms have. This mushroom has pushed up a large "mushrump" - that mound of leaves and woodland detritus. Neither the cap nor stem are viscid. I didn't disturb it, so I don't know if it has a swollen base. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2012)
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: cc28d_cc28-009.jpg
Notes: 8319677553 I found more "mystery mushrooms" in the woods today. They look a bit like Laccaria laccata, but I don't think so. For one thing, it's growing on a well-rotted oak log. I don't know what they are. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2012)

File.Name: cc28e_cc27-013.jpg
Notes:
8320692570 This is a beautiful mushroom that I found in the woods yesterday. I know our local mushrooms pretty well, and I don't have a clue what this is! It has great texture on the cap and stem.

My first thought was Lactarius, but I sliced the gills and there is no latex. I flipped through a few of my mushroom books tonight - maybe it's the Plums and Custard (Tricholomopsis rutilans) mushroom? (Great name!) But that one is said to have yellow gills, and the gills of this one are quite white. I'm glad there are still surprises in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2012)

File.Name: cc28f_cc28-016.jpg
Notes: 8320652540 I wonder if these are more tiny shell-like caps of Marasmiellus candidus on a bank of moss in the woods. Compare this photo. They both have widely spaced white gills. These little mushrooms seem to be having a negative effect on the moss. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2012)

File.Name: cc28g_cc27-007.jpg
Notes: 8319532215 I believe these are the large Yellow-Staining Agaricus (Agaricus xanthodermus) mushrooms. They look beautiful and edible, but they taste terrible - with a strong phenolic odor and taste, like an old fashioned doctor's office. Don't eat it! All parts of the mushroom stain bright yellow as you handle it, especially the very base of the stem. They also have a thick skirt-like ring on the stalk, with patches of tissue on the underside. Be very careful collecting woodland agaricus species for the table! (San Marcos Pass, 27 December 2012)

File.Name: cc28h_cc25-001.jpg
Notes:
8320490248 This is a Bolete mushroom, with pores instead of gills, in the woods - but what kind? Probably it's the "Boring Brown Bolete" (Boletus subtomentosus) with that brown cap and large pores. I'm confused by those reddish colors on the stem, but that might be a fungus infection. I guess the Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus) is also possible, though it has a brown cap without a trace of red, and the stem is not red to the base. I don't think so. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2012)

The "fungus infection" is the "Bolete Eater" (Hypomyces chrysospermus) fungus, which starts as a white cottony growth and ends up as the bright yellow material you can see at the bottom of my photo. It's said to be edible but not very good - IF you get to it before the "Bolete Eater"!
CommonNames: Bolete; Boring Brown Bolete; Oak-Loving Bolete; Bolete Eater; Bolete Eater
ScientificName: Boletus subtomentosus; Boletus dryophilus; Hypomyces chrysospermus
associatedTaxa: Boletus subtomentosus; Boletus dryophilus; Hypomyces chrysospermus

File.Name: cc29a_cc29-017.jpg
Notes:
8324433338 Here are more new flower clusters of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family in the woods today. It takes some rain to bring out the delicate colors of these flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2012)

We had some interesting weather today. We finally got 1/4 inch of rain overnight, and today we had - drizzle all day, a bit of snow up at Lizard Mouth, some sleet on the porch, and thunder and lightning that was too close to home! We ended up with about 4/10 inch of rain. Not a lot, but these small storms add up, and there have been lots of them lately.

File.Name: cc29b_cc29-009.jpg
Notes: 8324417304 These are new flower clusters of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family in the woods today. It takes some rain to bring out the delicate colors of these flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2012)

File.Name: cc29c_cc29-012.jpg
Notes: 8323319335 These are red flower buds on native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family - with raindrops. The buds wait on the plant for months to flower, until next April or so. On some plants the buds are pale. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2012)

File.Name: cc29d_cc29-011.jpg
Notes: 8323294455 This is a new plant of native Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family - with raindrops. Just beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2012)
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas

File.Name: cc29e_cc29-004.jpg
Notes:
8324310108 These are edible and delicious Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) mushrooms in the woods today. Be careful though - the mushroom caps start blue like the upended cap on the right, but they soon lose their color and become harder to recognize. We also have an inedible blue Cortinarius mushroom in the woods that looks very similar. See this photo. Caveat emptor! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2012)

Of course you should never eat ANY mushroom just because someone on Flicker says that it (or something that looks like it) is edible. Know what you're eating. That applies to food from the market as well!

File.Name: cc29f_cc29-005.jpg
Notes:
8323221701 This is looking under the cap of an edible and delicious Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) mushroom in the woods. Be careful though - the mushroom caps start lavender or blue like this, but they soon fade to gray or tan and become harder to recognize. See my [Next] photo. We also have an inedible blue Cortinarius mushroom in the woods that looks very similar. See this photo. Caveat emptor! (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2012)

Of course you shouldn't eat ANY mushroom just because someone on Flicker says that it (or something that looks like it) is edible. Know what you're eating. That applies to food from the market as well!

File.Name: cc29g_cc29-001.jpg
Notes:
8324228438 I believe these are the Rufous Candy Cap (Lactarius rufulus) mushrooms on the edge of our garden. They don't seen to have any latex when you slit the gills. (My book says the latex is "white or watery white, unchanging, often scanty or even absent.") They don't have the maple-syrup smell of Lactarius fragilis, though they are still said to be good to eat. I haven't tried them. They look especially handsome on a rainy day. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2012)

These mushrooms certainly are long-lasting. The mushroom on top is the same one I posted on December 5!

File.Name: cc29h_cc26-002.jpg
Notes: 8324200996 Here is another photo of the beautiful new cluster of Sulfur Tuft aka Clustered Woodlover (Naematoloma fasciculare aka Hypholoma fasciculare) mushrooms growing from the base of a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. They have yellow gills, but a deep brown spore color. They are beautiful, but poisonous - with a bitter taste to keep you away. I always have trouble capturing the yellow colors of these mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2012)
CommonNames: Sulfur Tuft; Clustered Woodlover; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Naematoloma fasciculare; Hypholoma fasciculare; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Naematoloma fasciculare; Hypholoma fasciculare; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: cc30a_cc30-010.jpg
Notes:
8329087946 This is the pretty new button of what I believe is a new Coccora (Amanita calyptrata) mushroom in the woods today - as I found it. It's growing in a nest of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaves and grass-like leaves of Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family . It's the first amanita mushroom I've found in our woods this year. The thick volval cap is distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2012)

This mushroom is said to be edible, but I generally avoid eating all Amanita mushrooms because some are deadly poison. My book describes it as having a "fishy" taste, which isn't encouraging.

It's been very cold today. The porch was icy this morning, and it never got out of the 30s today.
CommonNames: Coccora; Coast Live Oak; Roundfruit Sedge
ScientificName: Amanita calyptrata; Quercus agrifolia; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae; Amanita
associatedTaxa: Amanita calyptrata; Quercus agrifolia; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae; Amanita

File.Name: cc30b_cc30-013.jpg
Notes: 8329040484 This is the pretty new button of what I believe is a new Coccora (Amanita calyptrata) mushroom in the woods today. It's the first amanita mushroom I've found in our woods this year. You can see the crumbly outer layer of the volva that leaves the patch on the cap. See my [Next] and [Previous] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2012)

File.Name: cc30c_cc30-015.jpg
Notes:
8328957722 This is the sliced button of what I believe is a new Coccora (Amanita calyptrata) mushroom in the woods today. It's the first amanita mushroom I've found in our woods this year. You can see a bit of the crumbly white volva on the cap, the tissue that will become the ring on the stem, and the tissue that will become the rest of the volva at the base. See my [Next] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2012)

I wouldn't normally slice an amanita button - I'd rather see it mature! But we're heading off to Oregon on Tuesday, and I'll be busy with errands tomorrow, so I won't be able to get back to it.

File.Name: cc30d_cc30-002.jpg
Notes:
8328898188 Here is another gnarly Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera - maybe the Spiny Assassin Bug (Sinea spinipes, but cp. S. diadema). It has spiny legs, a dinosaur-like head, and a mighty proboscis folded under its head. This one was perched on a leaf of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2012)

I've found them on the flowers of this tree before - see this photo.

Notes:
8327644257 Hah - I guess this is a fresh pile of Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus - or is it Ariolimax californicus?) poo in the woods today! It's quite small. See this photo and this one. Just try not to think about sausage!

The turd looks bird-like, with separate white and dark parts - but no bird has a coiled load like this! I guess the dark is from defecation and white is uric acid from excretion. See this informative Web page on "

File.Name: cc30f_cc30-005.jpg
Notes: 8328666888 These are new leaves of Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus) in the Poaceae plant family. The woods are coming alive again after our recent good rains. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2012)

File.Name: cc30g_cc30-008.jpg
Notes:
8327503313 These are more white-gilled "Tricholoma-like" mushrooms growing in the woods - probably the same mushrooms I showed in this photo. I did go back to that previous mushroom to do a spore print, and it turned out to be rusty-brown.

I guess these white-gilled mushrooms are really Cortinarius in the subgenus Telamonia. They do have a basal bulb, and that one on the left seems to have a rusty-spore colored deposit at the base. They are handsome mushrooms, but I won't try to guess what species they are. (San Marcos Pass, 30 December 2012)

File.Name: cc30h_cc24-020.jpg
Notes:
8328479866 I still guess these are the Pear-shaped Puffball (Lycoperdon pyriforme) - but they are growing on lignen-rich humus instead of wood. You can see a progression here from the small white cap in the lower-right , counter-clockwise to the large cap on the left that is turning brown. Compare this photo of the same mushrooms close by. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2012)

I posted this photo to answer

File.Name: cc31a_cc31-004.jpg
Notes:
8332124138 These are new leaves of native perennial California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. They are growing in a place that burned a decade or more ago. It's a "fire follower" plant that lingers. It's a beautiful perennial plant that comes up mushroom-like with early flowers. The foliage is as handsome as the flowers. The stems look like some exotic celery. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 31 December 2012)

Julie and I are taking the train up to Portland, Oregon tomorrow to visit our kids and grandkids, so this will be my last post for a couple of weeks. I love traveling, but I always get jittery with the pre-trip stuff. I had an errand in Santa Ynez today, so I did a quick stop by Lake Cachuma to check on the woods and get a few photos. By the time I get back, the woods will be different and these plants may have flowers!

File.Name: cc31b_cc31-001.jpg
Notes: 8332100886 These are new mushroom-like stems of native perennial California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. The new stems are just emerging from a dry cluster of last year's stems in the chaparral near Lake Cachuma, in a place that burned a decade or more ago. They will need a few weeks to make flowers.
Locality: Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. The new stems are just emerging from a dry cluster of last year's stems in the chaparral near Lake Cachuma

File.Name: cc31c_cc31-010.jpg
Notes: 8330986113 I believe these are the odd staminate "flowers" of Oak Mistletoe (Phoradendron villosum) in the Viscaceae plant family growing as a parasite on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 31 December 2012)

associatedTaxa: Phoradendron villosum; Viscaceae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: cc31d_cc31-007.jpg
Notes:
8331969108 I guess this beautiful lichen is the "fruticose" Gold Eye Lichen (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus or kin) growing on a living branch of Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. I assume the "gold eyes" are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. They can't reproduce the lichen - only the fungus. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 31 December 2012)

Darn - I didn't notice that annoying fiber on the left until I got home.
ScientificName: Teloschistes chrysophthalmus; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; apothecia

File.Name: b101a_ac31-033.jpg
Notes:
66164074 "Strange fruit hanging from the eucalyptus trees..."

Look close! These strange fruit are many Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) hanging from non-native Eucalyptus trees at the Ellwood Main Monarch Grove, part of the Coronado Butterfly Preserve. (Ellwood Mesa, 31 December 2010)

These aren't the horrible "strange fruit" that Billie Holiday sang of - here is something wonderful! The monarch butterflies from all the western states on our side of the Rocky Mountains congregate on the West Coast every year in the same trees. (East Coast Monarchs head to Mexico.) Here are some of them resting for the winter. This is one of the world's great migrations.

This was a difficult photo. I had to shoot into the sun on a cloudy day, three stops over-exposed to bring out some color in the butterflies. The butterflies look very dark when you see them - almost like bunches of dead leaves. It's good camouflage.

It's interesting that they seem to depend on non-native Eucalyptus trees, but I've also found then on native Black Cottonwood (Populus balsamifera ssp. trichocarpa) trees. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/57875013

There's more info about the Coronado Butterfly Preserve here:
   http://www.sblandtrust.org/coronado.html

I'm still waiting for rain to appear, but there's a 100% chance for tomorrow!
Locality:
Populus balsamifera ssp. trichocarpa) trees. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/57875013

There's more info about the Coronado Butterfly Preserve here:
   http://www.sblandtrust.org/coronado.html

I'm still waiting for rain to appear

Notes:
66163693 There aren't enough pixels in a Fotolog image to show all the spots on the wings of all these Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) at the Ellwood Main Monarch Grove, part of the Coronado Butterfly Preserve! (Ellwood Mesa, 31 December 2010)

Thousands of Monarch butterflies from all the western states congregate here every year in the same non-native Eucalyptus trees. It's one of the world's great migrations. Fortunately these trees are protected - by a rope at least.

This was a difficult photo. I had to shoot into the sun on a cloudy day, three stops over-exposed to bring out some color in the butterflies.

File.Name: b101c_ac31-041.jpg
Notes:
66163427 The real challenge in photographing all the Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) is to capture just one up close! (See my [Next] photos.) This butterfly seems to be wearing long underwear against the cold. (Ellwood Mesa, 31 December 2010)

I got this photo at the Ellwood Main Monarch Grove, part of the Coronado Butterfly Preserve. Thousands of Monarch butterflies from all the western states congregate here every year in the same non-native Eucalyptus trees. It's one of the world's great migrations. Fortunately these trees are protected - by a rope at least.

File.Name: b102a_ac28-004.jpg
Notes:
66175301 I found the first new wildflower of the new year a few days ago!

This is native [b]Milk Maids[/b] aka [b]Toothwort[/b] ([i]Cardamine californica[/i]) in the [i]Brassicaceae[/i] plant family. It has an edible root (the "tooth"), but I don't really care to dig it up. Like my [Previous] photo, these flowers are growing in a place where the sun never shines, and I had to fiddle with the white balance to get it (about) right. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2010)

"Big wheel keep on turning..."
It's another new year in the woods!

We've had about an inch of rain so far today - and it's cold enough to snow tonight if it continues. The temperature has been in the 30's all day.

File.Name: b102b_ac31-024.jpg
Notes: 66175013 These are the well-named native of Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii) wildflowers in the Primulaceae plant family. They are the first I've seen this year. Oddly, they are growing in a place where the winter sun never shines. I had to fiddle with the white balance, and it's still not what I saw. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2010)

File.Name: b102c_ac31-022.jpg
Notes: 66174911 These are new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. They've actually been flowering for awhile. They remind me of those candy "Easter eggs" with a scene hidden inside. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2010)

File.Name: b103a_b103-013.jpg
Notes:
66187767 It was cloudy at home in the mountains today, so I went to the woods by Lake Cachuma to find some sun. I found this fish skeleton - a Largemouth Bass (Micropterus salmoides)? - some ways from the water. This is just as I found it, with the green seedling coming up through its empty eye socket. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 January 2011)

Now look closer - are those tiny mushrooms (or slime molds) growing on its head like little spines? I didn't think about them when I got the photo, but I want to go back and find out!

We got about 1-1/4 inch of rain from the storm yesterday, bringing our total for the rain year to 25.22 inches. Not bad for an expected drought year!
Locality: or slime molds) growing on its head like little spines?  I didn't think about them when I got the photo

File.Name: b103b_b103-007.jpg
Notes:
66187231 The forests of Lilliput...

This is a peek into a tiny forest of Moss (Bryophyta) plants and sporophytes growing on fallen wood near Lake Cachuma today. Mosses are commonplace, but they have a strange lifecycle with alternating haploid and diploid forms that look very different. I guess we do too, but we don't usually think of it that way! Open your botany textbook to see how strange they really are. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 January 2011)

File.Name: b103c_b103-017.jpg
Notes:
66187016 This is a clump of a "Witch's Butter" (Tremella mesenterica or kin) mushroom growing on a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods by Lake Cachuma today. Look close and you can see a fat Springtail (order Collembola) insect in the center of the photo. There are a few more sausage-shaped springtails in the lower left. Maybe all the dark spots are springtails! These are the main insects I've been seeing lately. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 January 2011)

My book says the mushrooms are "harmless but flavorless" - so why bother? These gelatinous "mushrooms" are mostly water. They are bright yellow-orange when fresh, but fade clear as they age. They shrivel up to almost nothing when dry, but can revive quickly after a rain.

Wikipedia reports that in China, the fungus is used by vegetarians to prepare "an immunomodulating cooling soup with lotus seed, lily bulbs, jujube, etc." (Whatever that means!)
CommonNames: Witch's Butter; Coast Live Oak; Springtail

File.Name: b104a_b103-015.jpg
Notes:
66202025 This is looking under the skirts of a handsome Agaricus mushroom in the woods. I believe this is the Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silvicola or kin) mushroom growing in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. The edge of the cap and the ring turn yellow with age or handling as you can see - but not the very base of the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2011)

Like all Agaricus mushrooms, they have chocolate-brown spores that will color the gills, though these are still pink. They have a skirtlike ring on the stem, with no "death cup" volva at the base. They have a pleasant sweet odor and taste, like anise or almond extract.

They are said to be edible, but be careful! Some people are allergic, and there are other yellow-staining agarics with a nasty phenol-disinfectant taste. I'm wary of all woodland Agaricus species. "Once bitten, twice shy.

File.Name: b104b_b101-003.jpg
Notes: 66201826 This is looking under the skirts of a beautiful Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus or kin) mushroom growing under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods. You can see the last of the "cortina" hairs pulling free from the edge of the cap. These are the "partial veil" that protect the immature gills. You can also see some of the distinctive snot-like slime on the stem, a remnant of the "universal veil" that once covered the whole mushroom. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2011)

File.Name: b104c_b101-008.jpg
Notes: 66201663 I'm pretty sure these graceful mushrooms are part of the dark-spored Psathyrella longipes group. They grow solitary or with a friend, but not a cluster. I found these deep in the woods where the sun never shines. The caps are "hygrophanous" - changing color as they dry out. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2011)

File.Name: b105a_b105-007.jpg
Notes:
66214502 Under the sea on a coral reef?

Nah, these are some amazing Lichens on a twig of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. What a pretty little world this is! How many "species" do you count? (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 January 2011)

Actually the whole concept of a species gets a little strange when we consider lichens, since the lichen is a partnership between a fungus (of a particular species) and an algae (of a different species). So is the lichen a third species?

I think of lichens as simple epiphytes that grow where they can find space - so why do they prefer one shrub over another? These Chamise twigs are loaded with lichens, much more than any other shrub in this place. There must be more to the story.

I went back to school today after a fine winter vacation with lots of rain and mushrooms! I stopped near Lake Cachuma on my way to school this morning for a few photos.
Locality:

File.Name: b105b_b105-011.jpg
Notes:
66214100 Here are blue flowers of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti var. hoffmannii) in the Solanaceae plant family growing in the woods near Lake Cachuma today. These flowers are "shakers" - the pollen-bearing stamens clasp the female style, and pollen falls out as wind and critters jiggle the flowers - but where does the pollen go? (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 January 2011)

These plants are listed as a different variety because they are nearly glabrous - without hairs. Compare the hairs on this photo of the species plant near home:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/46780004

Little structural differences like this mean a lot to the botanists who make the floras we depend on!

File.Name: b105c_b105-006.jpg
Notes: 66213799 This pretty little mushroom is pushing up through the glandular new leaves of Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I'm not sure what the mushroom is. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 January 2011)

File.Name: b106a_b106-007.jpg
Notes:
66228653 These are new flowers on the native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plants in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. This early-flowering perennial native has red and green leaves. It's a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in this place. It must have a fungal connection with its host plant. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2011)

The flower heads of these Indian Warrior plants are sold on the Internet "legal high" market. The effects are described on one web site as "tranquilizers, muscle relaxants, powerful aphrodesiacs, and sedatives", and they sell for quite a bit.

I'd rather enjoy the flowers. This "wildcrafting" of native plants - especially these interesting root-parasites that can't be cultivated - bothers me!

File.Name: b106b_b106-002.jpg
Notes: 66228392 I call this the "brain mushroom" when I'm hiking in the woods with kids. It's a strange Black Elfin Saddle (Helvella lacunosa) mushroom in the woods today. It's said to be edible, but I'm not tempted. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2011)

File.Name: b106c_b105-003.jpg
Notes:
66228344 There are new flower buds of California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. These beautiful native perennials come from the earth like mushrooms and flower soon after sprouting. I found these plants in the woods near Lake Cachuma. I guess they are slow fire-followers. This is a place that burned at least 10 years ago. (Santa Ynez Valley, 5 January 2011)

The perennial roots were once collected for export to China as a medicinal herb. I'd rather find the flowers.

File.Name: b107a_b106-062.jpg
Notes:
66240010 Kids in trees - where they should be!

I took my after-school Hike Club kids up Birabent Creek to the base of Grass Mountain yesterday. This is looking up into a grand old Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It's my favorite climbing tree. The kids are inching along the branch to get to the next tree. See my [Next] photo. (Santa Ynez Valley, 6 January 2011)

I figure Hike Club has been "grandfathered" into our school traditions - I've been taking kids into the woods every Thursday afternoon for 28 years so far!

(*But hush - don't tell the school insurance company!*)

File.Name: b107b_b106-070.jpg
Notes:
66239895 Kids in trees - where they should be!

I took my after-school Hike Club kids up Birabent Creek to the base of Grass Mountain yesterday. This is looking up into a grand old Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family.

The challenge is to crawl along the branches of the background tree and transfer to the different tree that the girls are sitting on. These boys have almost made it!

Trees to climb, mushrooms to find, ladybugs galore, the creek is running, the sun is *almost* shining - what fun! (Santa Ynez Valley, 6 January 2011)

(*Hush - don't tell the school insurance company!*)

File.Name: b107c_b106-028.jpg
Notes:
66239763 Here are two of my after-school Hike Club kids running across a field of dead grass. But look at all the new green grass coming up through the old! (Santa Ynez Valley, 6 January 2011)

I like the texture of this photo - almost like an Andrew Wyeth watercolor. This little Fotolog image doesn't do it justice.

File.Name: b108a_b108-023.jpg
Notes:
66249877 Here are one of nature's most extraordinary creatures - Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on the wood of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. I think these are my favorite organisms, though I don't find them very often - they are tiny, and they don't last long. You have to be in the right place and the right time to get a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2011)

Slime Molds are remarkable critters that seem to be part fungus and part animal. They are Mycetozoa - which literally means "fungus-animals". They are really animals, not fungi.

Mycetozoa live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in the decaying wood. When the weather is right, they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" without cell membranes.

Look at the top of my photo and you can see them streaming up around tiny stems. They end up as tiny mushroom-like structures that release spores that disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one bit of decaying wood to another.

It's especially interesting because it seems to show biological "altruism" on a small scale. This is interesting to philosophers as well as biologists. Some of those Mycetozoa will end up as part of the stem, and not reproduce. Do they act for "the greater good" of the rest of the cellular mass?

File.Name: b108b_b108-006.jpg
Notes:
66249395 This is a beautiful Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom in the woods today. It's growing under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. It's pushing up through the native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens) in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2011)

This mushroom is handsome, but quite poisonous! The white warts on the cap are fragments of the universal veil that once covered the whole mushroom and form a volva at the base of the stalk. It contains the same interesting toxins as A. muscaria, but in higher concentrations. This mushroom should not be used for recreational purposes! My book says it's one of the most common causes of mushroom poisoning in the Pacific Northwest.
CommonNames: Panther Amanita; Coast Live Oak; Thin Grass
ScientificName: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Agrostis pallens; A. muscaria
associatedTaxa: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Agrostis pallens; A. muscaria

File.Name: b108c_b108-020.jpg
Notes: 66249171 All arthropods (insects, spiders, etc.) have been scarce in the woods lately. It's been cold! But I found this little Wolf Spider (Lycosidae) in the fallen oak leaves near the creek today. Little by little, the days are getting longer and the sun is getting higher after the winter solstice. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2011)

File.Name: b109a_b109-018.jpg
Notes:
66261076 These are shockingly beautiful Witch's Hat (Hygrocybe conica aka Hygrophorus conicus) mushrooms catching a beam of sunshine in the woods today. The brilliantly colored long stems turn black as you handle them. They are growing under California Bay (Umbellularia californica) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2011)

It's a stumper. Why are mushrooms so darn beautiful? Why do they share the colors of spring wildflowers if their only job is to release spores to propagate more fungi? I don't know the answer, unless nature puts a premium on beauty. (I admit, I'm a Platonist on these things!)
ScientificName: Hygrocybe conica; Hygrophorus conicus; Umbellularia californica
associatedTaxa: Hygrocybe conica; Hygrophorus conicus; Umbellularia californica

File.Name: b109b_b109-008.jpg
Notes:
66260353 This is a hefty Manzanita Bolete (Leccinum manzanitae) mushroom growing under a Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree - it's a manzanita relative in the Ericaceae plant family. You can see the distinctive dark scales ("scabers") on the stem. Like all Boletes, it has pores instead of gills under the cap. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2011)

This mushroom is incredibly dense - it must weigh a pound or more. It's edible, but not the best. I'll post another photo of the whole mushroom tomorrow.

File.Name: b109c_b108-026.jpg
Notes:
66260159 These look like little puffballs, but they are a different Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on the wood of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. I believe they are Lycogala epidendrum. The ball of my pen is 0.7 mm, but it's buried. These slime molds are about 1/4 inch (6 mm) across. There's a white balance problem with my photo in deep shade, but the sky reflected on my pen looks right! (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2011)

See my [Previous] photo for more info on slime molds.
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Lycogala epidendrum
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Lycogala epidendrum

File.Name: b110a_b108-011.jpg
Notes:
66273526 These are very beautiful Cortinarius mushrooms growing under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. I believe they are part of the Cortinarius cylindripes clan. Both cap and stem are slimy, which places it in the subgenus Myxacium. They are almost too slimy to pick up! (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2011)

Most all Cortinarius mushrooms end up dingy-brown, but the fresh ones are beautiful! It's best not to eat any of them. The genus contains several poisonous species, and the species are very hard to tell apart.
ScientificName: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Cortinarius cylindripes; Myxacium
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius; Quercus agrifolia; Cortinarius cylindripes; Myxacium

File.Name: b110b_b109-014.jpg
Notes:
66273358 These are strange Candlesnuff Fungi (Xylaria hypoxylon or kin) growing in the woods. They are very tough - like the rotting wood they grow from, only more so. The ripe fruiting bodies are about an inch tall. They look like tiny deer or moose antlers! (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2011)

I don't think these qualify as "mushrooms" at all - they are the fruiting bodies of fungi, but they are not by any stretch of the imagination fleshy!

These fungi are fruiting with new foliage of perennial California Bedstraw (Galium californicum) in the Rubiaceae plant family.

File.Name: b110c_b109-006.jpg
Notes: 66273160 This is the same hefty Manzanita Bolete (Leccinum manzanitae) mushroom that I showed two photos [Previous] growing under a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree. This photo shows that it has pores instead of gills under the cap, and it shows the impressive hidden stalk of the mushroom. This mushroom weighs a pound or more! My book says it's edible, but not the best. I rarely find them. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2011)

File.Name: b111a_b109-001.jpg
Notes:
66287103 Here is a beautiful new flower of the glandular native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. Like all true sages, it has two stamens and a two-forked pistil. The light seems to be splitting on this pistil! (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2011)

For some reason, many of our first winter blooms have red, tubular flowers that the hummingbirds like.

We actually had a few drops of rain tonight - but not enough to measure. I'm afraid we're finally settling into our "La NiÒa" drought weather pattern, though the 25+ inches of rain we've had so far this winter helps mitigate it!

File.Name: b111b_b109-027.jpg
Notes: 66286912 These are beautiful new flower heads of native Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family growing in the mountains near home. This paintbrush is the first to flower. It has woolly-white leaves, unlike other local species. The showy red "petals" are really leafy bracts. The real flowers are the greenish tubes that stick out from the red-tipped bracts and sepals. You can see a few in my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2011)

File.Name: b111c_b106-016.jpg
Notes:
66286849 Here's the whole plant - with flowers - of a native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plants in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. It's a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in this place. It must have a fungal connection with its host plant. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2011)

For more info about this interesting plant, see my other photo from January 6:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66228653

File.Name: b112a_b112-005.jpg
Notes:
66299263 This is the first open flower that I've found of California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. These beautiful native perennials come from the earth like mushrooms and flower soon after sprouting. The flowers usually face the Earth - I propped this one up with a stick. I found it in the woods today near Lake Cachuma. (Santa Ynez Valley, 12 January 2011)

You can see the new stamens and the 3 greenish stigmas on the thick-walled pistils that will become follicles. Sure it looks sexual - that's the point!

File.Name: b112b_b112-011.jpg
Notes:
66299160 These are well-named native Shooting Stars (Dodecatheon clevelandii) wildflowers in the Primulaceae plant family growing in the woods by Lake Cachuma. These are the first of the new flowers that I've seen here. (Santa Ynez Valley, 12 January 2011)

It could be a beautiful spring with all of our December rain, though I'm worried that "La NiÒa" will kick in with a dry Spring. We'll see what happens.

File.Name: b112c_b105-008.jpg
Notes:
66299092 Here is a little native Common Peppergrass (Lepidium nitidum) in the Brassicaceae plant family. And I do mean little! The whole plant is only about 1/2 inch high so far, though it gets larger. The notched seed pods are much larger than the flowers. True to it's name, the seeds have a pleasant spicy taste. (Santa Ynez Valley, 12 January 2011)

I got this photo in the woods near Lake Cachuma at a place that burned at least 10 years ago. I've become interested in the tiny plants that grow on the bare ground between the chaparral shrubs.

File.Name: b113a_b113-002.jpg
Notes:
66311306 Here is a tiny mushroom growing in the moss on the open ground between chaparral shrubs. I have no idea what kind of mushroom it is. I put my pen in the photo for scale. The ball of my pen is 0.7 mm - so the little mushroom is a few millimeters across. (Santa Ynez Valley, 13 January 2011)

I went back to my favorite place to stop on the way to school today - the woodland and chaparral near Lake Cachuma. I've become interested in the tiny plants (and mushrooms) that grow on the mostly bare ground between the chaparral shrubs.

File.Name: b113b_b112-002.jpg
Notes:
66311252 These tiny succulent plants are Sand Pygmyweed (Crassula connata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. I put my pen in the photo so you can see just how tiny they are. The ball of my pen is 0.7 mm - about the size of a leaf! My flora says they are "common, when looked for" - maybe so. This is the smallest native flowering plant I know. (Santa Ynez Valley, 13 January 2011)

Below the plants, there is a black crust that I believe is a Collema lichen. They are commonly associated with cryptobiotic soil crusts that involve cyanobacteria. It's bad to walk on them. I'll try to learn more about them - but not tonight!
ScientificName: Crassula connata; Crassulaceae; Collema; cryptobiotic; cyanobacteria
associatedTaxa: Crassula connata; Crassulaceae; Collema; cryptobiotic; cyanobacteria

File.Name: b113c_b112-004.jpg
Notes:
66310958 Here are two different Bryophytes in one photo - Mosses (Bryophyta) and an interesting Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) growing on bare ground between the chaparral shrubs near Lake Cachuma. I put my pen in the photo for scale. The ball of the pen is 0.7 mm. (Santa Ynez Valley, 12 January 2011)

I've become interested in the tiny plants that grow on the bare ground between the chaparral shrubs.

File.Name: b114a_b114-008.jpg
Notes: 66321589 These are beautiful little sporophytes of a Moss (Bryophyta) plant growing on a sandstone outcrop in the mountains. They look like little long-billed wading birds! (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2011)

File.Name: b114b_b114-004.jpg
Notes:
66321519 Here are sporophytes of tiny Liverworts (Marchantiophyta, genus Marchantia or Asterella?). They are related to the mosses, growing on ground still damp from recent rains. In drier years, I never see the fruiting bodies. These plants are growing on thin soil over sandstone rock. (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2011)

Now look closer! In the upper left, you can see a couple of tiny succulent Sand Pygmyweed (Crassula connata) plants in the Crassulaceae plant family. There are Mosses (Bryophyta) in the center of the photo, and a larger moss-like seedling on the right. (That is probably Turkish Rugging (Chorizantha staticoides) in the Polygonaceae plant family.)
Locality: Bryophyta) in the center of the photo
CommonNames: Liverworts; Sand Pygmyweed; Mosses; Turkish Rugging
