File.Name: b114c_b114-012.jpg
Notes: 66321243 The bare sandstone rock is orangish - so this black crust on the rock must be something else! I believe it is a crust of Collema lichen - which is interesting because it is a lichen composed of a fungus and a cyanobacterium rather than an algae. These soil-building lichens are pioneer plants that build the materials needed for life. You can see a little forest of lichens and mosses growing in the crack below the rock! (San Marcos Pass, 14 January 2011)

File.Name: b115a_b115-028.jpg
Notes:
66331551 This is the first butterfly of the new year!

This is a Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly. My book says that it is "One of the first non-hibernating butterflies to fly in the spring. Beginning Feb. in southern California..." It's time to update the record to mid-January! (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2011)

It's a Blue (subfamily Polyommatinae) butterfly, though it doesn't look very blue underneath. It really is blue on it's upper wings, though it rarely perches with wings open.

Look down at this butterfly's feet - it's taking moisture from "raindrops" on the grass, only it hasn't rained for two weeks. The water drops are the result of guttation. Look it up!

Actually, it's time to update the record to 12 December 2010 for the first butterfly of the new rain year (Sep-Aug). See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65928293

File.Name: b115b_b115-033.jpg
Notes:
66331234 Liverworts, mosses, seedlings, and even worm castings - oh my!

This is a little forest of the sporophytes of tiny Liverworts (Marchantiophyta, maybe Asterella californica?). They are related to the mosses, growing on ground still damp from recent rains, but now it's drying out. There are also a few mosses, a few seedlings, and even a bundle of worm castings in the lower-left. (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2011)

Heavy rains followed by a dry spell may be the key to getting these liverworts to fruit. They are having a good year. These liverworts are growing on real soil, not rock.

File.Name: b115c_b115-041.jpg
Notes:
66330957 It's a jungle out there!

Here's a little mushroom (Mycena?) growing in a jungle of mosses with a few sporophytes. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 15 January 2011)

File.Name: b116a_b115-017.jpg
Notes:
66342843 Brilliant Russula emetica (or kin) mushrooms in the woods growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2011)

I recently heard this mushroom called "The Sickener", and it checks out on Wikipedia - though it's not that bad! I like to nibble on it - it's spicy hot, like wasabi. Sure, don't eat a plate of them, but handling them (and even nibbling) won't hurt you at all. It's one of our most beautiful creatures!

It's remarkable that I can post photos of a new insect, a new wildflower, and a new mushroom all on the same day. I love living in the Santa Barbara mountains!
Locality:
and even nibbling) won't hurt you at all. It's one of our most beautiful creatures!

It's remarkable that I can post photos of a new insect

File.Name: b116b_b116-044.jpg
Notes:
66342640 The beautiful Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) shrubs in the Grossulariaceae plant family are starting to flower. Both foliage and flowers of this native shrub are gorgeous. It has "double spines" - big spines with smaller spines between them. It's a favorite hummingbird flower. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2011)

It's another early red tubular flower. See my photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66287103
and [Previous] to that.

File.Name: b116c_b115-009.jpg
Notes:
66342314 Put your hands together for this... Syrphid fly!

I believe this is a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) resting on a dry oak leaf in a sunny place by our bridge. If you know something about wing venation, it has a spurious vein and closed R5 and M2 cells. I think that nails the ID. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2011)

The weather is warming up, and insects are coming alive. I'm afraid the La NiÒa weather pattern is kicking in, with high pressure dominating our weather. Fortunately we've already had 25+ inches of rain this year - not quite normal, but not bad. But if we don't get any more rain, it will be a sorry summer.

File.Name: b117a_b116-012.jpg
Notes:
66355218 Here's a pretty little "true bug" in the order Hemiptera "snug as a bug" in a furry white leaf of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family.

I don't know what kind of bug it is, but I have some clues. It has 4-segmented antennae and 2-segmented tarsi on its feet. If you look close, it has two cute ocelli (simple eyes), like little red jewels on its head. It's not family Miridae or family Lygaeidae or family Pentatomidae - it must one of those other groups. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2011)

It's been unseasonably warm this weekend, and windy - highs in the 70s and lows in the 60s! It's drying out the woods after our perfect December rains. It's so beautiful that it's hard to complain, but it will be an interesting spring.
ScientificName: Hemiptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Miridae; Lygaeidae; Pentatomidae
associatedTaxa: Hemiptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Miridae; Lygaeidae; Pentatomidae

File.Name: b117b_b116-007.jpg
Notes:
66354538 These are tiny new flowers of BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The flowers look like little origami boxes. Look very close - the flowers are full of tiny Thrips (insect order Thysanoptera)! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2011)

The flowers are tiny but there are zillions of them on every shrub. Usually we get a mass flowering of Ceanothus in February. Some years they cover the mountains like snow that you can see from the beach! It remains to be seen if this will be one of those years.

File.Name: b117c_b116-048.jpg
Notes:
66354227 This is a curious "rosette gall" on flower buds of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I wonder if that is the little critter that made the galls in the lower right of the photo? It looks about the right size - about 1 mm, the size of the exit holes. There were lots of them on the plant. It's definitely a Wasp (Hymenoptera), though I not sure if it is in the family Cynipidae, and it may be a parasite or an inquiline. Galls are complicated! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2011)

I don't believe this gall is listed in Ron Russo's fine book "Field Guide to Plant Galls of California and Other Western States". I have another photo of the galls at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/65585736

Update 2 February 2011:
I just received email from Bob Zuparko, Curatorial Assistant for the Department of Entomology, California Academy of Sciences, that this wasp is a parasitic wasp from the family Platygastridae. He adds that "many platygastrids are known to be parasitoids of Cecidomyiidae (gall-forming flies)." Thank you, Bob!

I take it that's a vote for these galls being caused by gall midges (Cecidomyiidae) and not gall wasps. Most of our other galls formed on Coyote Bush are also made by gall midges, so it rings true.
Locality: Cecidomyiidae) and not gall wasps. Most of our other galls formed on Coyote Bush are also made by gall midges
CommonNames: Coyote Bush; Wasp; gall midges; gall wasps
ScientificName: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Hymenoptera; Cynipidae; Update 2 February 2011:; Platygastridae; Cecidomyiidae
associatedTaxa: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Hymenoptera; Cynipidae; Update 2 February 2011:; Platygastridae; Cecidomyiidae

File.Name: b118a_b117-011.jpg
Notes: 66372048 I believe these little puffballs are Dark Puffballs (Lycoperdon foetidum). They are almost black when young. I believe that is an older specimen of the same mushroom in the back. They develop a apical pore when mature. Despite the species name, they have no particular smell, and are probably edible when young and firm. These are growing under Madrone and Oak trees. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2011)

File.Name: b118b_b117-016.jpg
Notes: 66371924 These are sheaves of dry Moss (Bryophyta) sporophytes growing in a wet place in the mountains. For some reason, they look bundled like sheaves of grain. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2011)

File.Name: b118c_b116-027.jpg
Notes: 66371832 This interesting Fly in the order Diptera is perched on a flower of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. You can see entrance holes where bees (?) have entered the flowers before they opened. This fly looks a little like a Blow Fly (family Calliphoridae) but the long hairs show that it is something else. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2011)

File.Name: b119a_b116-013.jpg
Notes:
66383723 The beautiful new flowers of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family are finally open in the canyon. They are related to avocado. The wide stamens remind me of those fat matches that some restaurants used to give out. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2011)

I've had a run of busy nights. I'll be glad when this week is over!

File.Name: b119b_b116-036.jpg
Notes: 66383660 Urn-like flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. They remind me of those candy "Easter eggs" with a scene hidden inside. (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2011)

File.Name: b119c_b117-007.jpg
Notes: 66383617 This plant will become a treat next summer. These are new leaves of the uncommon native Chaparral Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family. By the time they flower in July, the handsome leaves will be shrivelled up. (San Marcos Pass, 17 January 2011)

File.Name: b120a_b116-039.jpg
Notes:
66401140 I heard there was going to be a rocket launch from nearby Vandenberg Air Force Base last Sunday, so I drove up to the high place between San Marcos Pass and Arroyo Burro Pass.

The missile launch was postponed until Thursday, but I got this beautiful photo of The Channel Islands. That's Santa Cruz Island on the left, and Santa Rosa Island on the right, and the Goleta Coast at the bottom. I could also see San Miguel Island, Anacapa Island, Santa Barbara Island, and Catalina Island all at once! (San Marcos Pass, 16 January 2011)

That straight line on the west end of Santa Cruz that looks like a jetty is Fraser Point. It's a natural feature. I've hiked there looking for succulents.

File.Name: b120b_b120-042.jpg
Notes: 66400862 I took my after-school Hike Club kids to Refugio Beach on Thursday for the -1.2 low tide which happened to peak at 4:30 P.M. Perfect! (The kids are behind me.) That green plant in the foreground is interesting Surf-Grass (family Zosteraceae). It's a real flowering plant that lives in the ocean - the only one that I know of. (Refugio Beach, Gaviota Coast, 20 January 2011)

File.Name: b121a_b119-004.jpg
Notes:
66401649 These are interesting non-native Mexican Tree Daisy (Montanoa bipinnatifida) flowers in the Asteraceae) family. They are interesting mostly because they're flowering here on San Marcos Pass in January! We got this plant as a cutting from my Dad a few years ago, and it's never flowered before because it always gets cut down by frost. But here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 19 January 2011)

It's been an odd weather year - aren't they all! We had very heavy rain in December, but hardly a drop in January. It felt cold before this week, but this plant is proof that it's actually been warmer than usual. We've had enough frost to kill the tomatoes and chiles, but not enough to kill this plant. Is this what global warming looks like locally?

File.Name: b121b_b120-004.jpg
Notes:
66401430 This is the launch of the "big bird" Delta IV Heavy Launch Vehicle as seen at my school in Los Olivos yesterday. It was said to be the largest rocket ever to launch from the West Coast of the United States, but I've heard louder. The windows hardly rattled. (Santa Ynez Valley, 20 January 2011)

The arrow points to the actual missile, if you can see it after Fotolog resamples the image. It was odd that the "contrail" didn't start until the rocket was already quite high. I reckon the contrail stopped when the huge booster rockets had done their job.

File.Name: b122a_b122-020.jpg
Notes:
66410695 We've been having lizard weather lately. This is a yearling Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) on the prowl on a sandstone outcrop. Sure, it's a tight crop. The mature lizards appear later. I suspect they just take longer for them to warm up with greater body mass. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2011)

Draw a line in your mind between its eyes, and draw another line from the front of its face and up over its back... behind where they cross, you can see the mysterious "third eye" of these lizards!

Our unseasonably warm and dry weather continues. Will it ever rain again?

File.Name: b122b_b122-030.jpg
Notes:
66410355 I found this beautiful mushroom today in the drying out woods. It almost looks like a Cortinarius mushroom with those orange streaks on the stem, but I'm sure it is a spring "Blusher" (Amanita rubescens) mushroom. You can see a natural white spore print on the leaf under the cap. This amanita is said to be edible when cooked, but it's easily confused with poisonous species. No thanks! (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2011)

The woods are getting dry with the unseasonable warm weather we've been having, but there are still a few mushrooms.

File.Name: b122c_b122-012.jpg
Notes:
66410125 Here are beautiful native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) flowers in the Brassicaceae plant family. Like all mustards, they have 4 long stamens and 2 short ones. The immature flowers have violet tones, but the open flowers are pure white. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2011)

This is the first plant I've found catching some sun. They have an edible root (the "tooth") which tastes like a radish, but I don't care to dig them up.
Locality: the "tooth") which tastes like a radish

File.Name: b123a_b123-003.jpg
Notes:
66420106 I finally got a photo of a beautiful furry Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) in the canyon today. Look - you can see the real fly underneath its thick hairs! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2011)

It looks like it backed into a spider web.

They're like roosters in the hen yard - ready to jump on anything that comes along, including my tripod! I found them a week ago, but I couldn't get a photo. I went back early this morning when it was still cold, and got this photo of a fly resting on a blade of grass. I believe it is genus Bombylius.

File.Name: b123b_b123-018.jpg
Notes:
66419787 I found this cute little Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) today at the site of an old homestead in the mountains. There were three of these little frogs in the woods, and I had to chase them for a while before got the photo. This frog went up a tree-like Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia), and I got it! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2011)

The frog has serious climbing chops. Look how its hanging on to the bark! I've seen rock climbers do something similar when they "make a move".

This frog seems unusually tan. These frogs are color-shifters from green to tan, but they always have a dark eye-stripe.

It was odd to find them here, because there's no water nearby that I know of. Maybe there's an old cistern at the site that I don't know about?
ScientificName: Pseudacris regilla; Hyla regilla; Heteromeles arbutifolia
associatedTaxa: Pseudacris regilla; Hyla regilla; Heteromeles arbutifolia

File.Name: b123c_b122-037.jpg
Notes:
66419349 I believe this beautiful grass-like plant is a native Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family. It's an unusual sedge, because it's a dry-land plant that grows under the oaks, and even in the chaparral on the south-facing side of our canyon.

In my photo, you can see the male stamens on top and the female seed-producing pistils down lower on the stem. How do they help being self-pollinated?
Locality: Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family. It's an unusual sedge

File.Name: b124a_b123-035.jpg
Notes:
66432738 I found this unfortunate Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) hanging from the end of a strand of spider silk. The flowers are native color-shifting Chaparral Lotus (Lotus grandiflorus) in the Fabaceae) plant family. They are fire followers in a place that burned a few years ago in the Gap Fire. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2011)

I first thought that the unlucky bee was a victim of something like a "ghost net" that keeps on killing fish after it is lost - a bit of forgotten spider web. But look very close at the pink flower on the right, and you can see two tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) legs sticking out from the flower! Crab spiders don't make webs, or do they?
Locality: Thomisidae) legs sticking out from the flower! Crab spiders don't make webs
CommonNames: Honey Bee; Chaparral Lotus; Crab Spider
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Lotus grandiflorus; Fabaceae; Thomisidae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Lotus grandiflorus; Fabaceae; Thomisidae

File.Name: b124b_b124-004.jpg
Notes:
66432443 I should know by now what this pretty little "diurnal" (daytime) Moth in the order Lepidoptera is. I see it every spring, but I still don't know what it is. It's small - not much larger than a pencil eraser. I reckon it's too small to be a true Underwing Moth (genus Catocalain the [i]Noctuidae[/i] family).

These moths are fairly common - meaning I've seen them several times this spring. This one is resting on a branch of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family. They are quite camera shy! (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2011)

(Update 25 Jan. 2011) - Santa Barbara naturalist and photographer Lynn Watson just gave me an ID for this little moth. It's Pyrausta dapalis in the Crambid Snout Moth family (Crambidae). That's not exactly a common name! You can see Lynn's photo at BugGuide:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/371706
CommonNames: Moth; Underwing Moth; Chamise; Crambid Snout Moth
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Catocala; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Pyrausta dapalis; Crambidae
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Catocala; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Pyrausta dapalis; Crambidae

File.Name: b124c_b124-016.jpg
Notes: 66431792 This little Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly has its proboscis out to take moisture from a sandstone outcrop in our canyon. The butterfly keeps its wings folded up when it perches, but it's deep blue on top. You can see a bit of the color in my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2011)

File.Name: b125a_b123-006.jpg
Notes:
66444164 Here are beautiful little sporophytes of a Moss (Bryophyta) plant growing on a sandstone outcrop in the mountains. They are catching the early morning light just right. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2011)

Today's photos are more in my "life on the ground" series. Our unusual weather combination of a very wet December followed by a very dry January seems to helping.

File.Name: b125b_b114-016.jpg
Notes:
66444001 I'm not sure what this photo shows. Here are lichens growing in a bed of mosses. The lichens seem decorated like little Christmas trees! (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2011)

"Lichens are composite organisms consisting of a symbiotic association of a fungus (the mycobiont) with a photosynthetic partner (the photobiont)." (Wikipedia, more or less.) That means they have a problem reproducing, since both fungi and algae (the usual photobiont) must reproduce together. Spore dispersal from the fungi isn't good enough.

I suspect the "Christmas ornaments" on the lichens are isidia - little bits of the lichen that will break off for dispersal.

File.Name: b125c_b124-016.jpg
Notes: 66443724 I don't know what these interesting Liverworts (Marchantiophyta) are growing on the bare ground between the chaparral shrubs near Lake Cachuma. The entire cluster is only an inch (2.5 cm) across. I have no idea what species they are. They are quite different from our "mountain liverworts". I suspect a careless step will destroy the colony. (Santa Ynez Valley, 24 January 2011)

File.Name: b126a_b126-001.jpg
Notes:
66454718 This is a remarkable native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plant with flowers in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. It's a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in this place. It must have a fungal connection with its host plant. I reckon it's a hummingbird plant, though I haven't actually seen hummers visiting them. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2011)

For more info about this interesting plant, see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66228653

File.Name: b126b_b124-011.jpg
Notes: 66454617 The spent flowers of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family are really more interesting than the just open flowers. Here the stamens are falling away, revealing the large pistils that will bear seeds. Those strange orange "candy corn" things around the base of the flower are part of the nectary disk. I got this photo in the woods near Lake Cachuma in a place that burned at least 10 years ago. It's a "fire follower" plant that lingers. (Santa Ynez Valley, 24 January 2011)

File.Name: b126c_b123-037.jpg
Notes:
66454375 These are new "umbel flowers" of native Tauschia arguta in the Apiaceae plant family. The plant is sometimes known as the Southern Tauschia or Southern Umbrellawort, but it deserves a better proper name. They have handsome foliage, but it's hard to show flowers and leaves in the same photo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2011)

There an interesting insect that I "found" in the photo - look close at the stamen on top of the central cluster of flowers. It has plumose antennae, so I guess it's a tiny fly (Diptera), though it looks more wasp-like. (Nano - do any Hymenoptera have plumose antennae?)

File.Name: b127a_b127-010.jpg
Notes: 66466772 I found this little Wasp (Hymenoptera) ovipositing on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family this morning. This wasp isn't "wasp-waisted" so I reckon it's a "Symphyta" wasp - the group of sawflies, horntails>, and kin. Something is seriously wrong with its left rear leg. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2011)

File.Name: b127b_b127-017.jpg
Notes:
66466648 Well blow me down - I believe this is a tattered Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly that I found in the woods this morning. I've never seen them in January before - in fact I've never seen before June or after November. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2011)

However this butterfly got here, it looks like he has stories to tell!

File.Name: b127c_b127-081.jpg
Notes:
66466371 A frog in the hand is worth... what?

I took my after-school Hike Club kids to our old cabin on Kinevan Ranch today, and we visited the frog pond. One of my students is holding this frog. The frogs are doing fine despite the warm weather. I hope this vernal pond lasts for the tadpoles! (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2011)

This is the green form of the Pacific Tree Frogs (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla). I have a recent photo of the tan form here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66419787

I believe they are the same kind of frogs. These frogs are color-shifters from green to tan, but they always have a dark eye-stripe.
Locality:
Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla). I have a recent photo of the tan form here:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66419787

I believe they are the same kind of frogs. These frogs are color-shifters from green to tan

File.Name: b128a_b123-024.jpg
Notes:
66476123 This is the perfect "amanita egg" of a Coccora (Amanita calyptrata) mushroom - the Italian name means cocoon - growing in the woods. It's about the size of a real chicken egg, and it feels soft but quite firm. Inside of the egg, a new mushroom is developing. I'll post another photo of the "egg" cut in half tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2011)

The top of the egg will become the white patch on the cap, and the bottom will become the white "volva" at the base of the mushroom. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66144370

This egg is growing in a mixed woodland of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees - as well as a non-native Fir tree (Abies) by an old homestead in the mountains. I won't try to guess which tree it's associated with.
CommonNames: amanita egg; Coccora; Coast Live Oak; Madrone; Fir
ScientificName: Amanita calyptrata; Quercus agrifolia; Arbutus menziesii; Abies
associatedTaxa: Amanita calyptrata; Quercus agrifolia; Arbutus menziesii; Abies

File.Name: b128b_b122-006.jpg
Notes: 66475887 This is looking under the cap at the gills of an Entoloma ferruginans mushroom in the E. nidorosum group. You can see the "pink" spores piled up on the gills and stem. It has a surprising smell of bleach, like a swiming pool. I wouldn't want to eat it. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2011)

File.Name: b128c_b123-014.jpg
Notes:
66475708 I don't often find Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms in this condition - too many locals collect and devour them! But this has been a great year for the chanterelles, and there are more than even I can pick. Even dried out like this, they might be ok to eat. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2011)

The good news is that we have a 50% chance of rain on Sunday night. We've been dry in our wet season since the beginning of the month, so any rain is welcome.

File.Name: b129a_b129-016.jpg
Notes:
66485098 I found this beautiful but deadly poisonous Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today. It's remarkable to hold this mushroom knowing that it is deadly poison, but it is not dangerous to hold or photograph it. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2011)

You can see the "death cup" at the base of the stem, the "ring" on the stem, and the pure white cap. It has an "amanita aura". It's one of our most elegant mushrooms. Enjoy it, but don't eat it!

I found several of these amanitas in the woods today - all small, no doubt because of the recent dry weather. But the temperature is falling tonight, and there a good chance of real rain tomorrow! We'll build our first fire in weeks tonight.

File.Name: b129b_b129-023.jpg
Notes:
66484888 Here are new mushrooms of the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) growing under a Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family in the canyon. They have pretty yellow caps, obscured by a white patch from their original "amanita egg". See my [Previous] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2011)

I don't like the contrasty lighting - the sun must finally be getting higher!

I used to think these mushrooms were the yellow form of Amanita muscaria, but they are all wrong. For one thing, the volva is collarlike with a free rim rather than scaly with concentric rings.
ScientificName: Amanita gemmata; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Amanita muscaria
associatedTaxa: Amanita gemmata; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae; Amanita muscaria

File.Name: b129c_b123-028.jpg
Notes:
66484612 This is the same perfect "amanita egg" of a Coccora (Amanita calyptrata) mushroom shown in my [Previous] photos. I was hoping for more structural detail, but you can at least see the gills of the the mushroom to be. It's about the size of a real chicken egg, and it feels soft but quite firm. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2011)

The top of the egg will become the white patch on the cap, and the bottom will become the white "volva" at the base of the mushroom. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66144370

After I got my photo, I put the two halves back together and put them back in the ground in the same place I got it. I always do that with mushrooms I pick for photos. Often the mushrooms continue to grow - I reckon they reconnect with their mycelium. I'll try to get back to this mushroom tomorrow (after a week) to see what's happening.

File.Name: b130a_b130-022.jpg
Notes:
66495081 A ring of giant mushrooms guarding the entrance to the underworld?

Nah... I believe these little mushrooms are Omphalina pyxidata in the Tricholomataceae mushroom family. They are only guarding an old acorn cap above the retaining wall by our parking area, though it looks more ominous in my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2011)

The acorn cups and oak leaves have been eroding a tiny badlands in this place. The sandy soil under the acorn is fine, but everything else is washed away!

We had about 1/4 inch of rain today. Not much, but it was good soaking rain that got the ground wet again, just what the mushrooms and wildflower seedlings needed to keep going.

File.Name: b130b_b130-030.jpg
Notes: 66494802 Here are raindrops on a new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family growing in the canyon. The flowers remind me of ballet dancers "en pointe" with tight ballet shoes and petticoats in the air. Look close - you can see the woods reflected in the raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2011)

File.Name: b130c_b130-002.jpg
Notes:
66494564 This is the same kind of Coccora (Amanita calyptrata) mushroom that I showed as an "amanita egg" yesterday and before that. The cap and volva aren't very fluffy because it's been so dry, though we had some rain today. Look at the spider webs with raindrops in the lower part of my photo! (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2011)

It's edible, but I generally avoid eating all Amanita mushrooms because some are deadly poison. My book describes them as having a "fishy" taste, which isn't too encouraging.

Despite the rain today, I went back to the same place I found the "amanita egg". See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66476123

I placed the two halves back together and put them back in the ground in the same place I got it, hoping they would reconnect with their mycelium. My experiment didn't work - they were just mushy.

File.Name: b131a_b129-001.jpg
Notes:
66506810 Is this how to "mic" a snail?

Hah - This is the shell of a native Land Snail (Gastropoda) in the woods. I picked it up and placed it among little sporophytes of Moss (Bryophyta) plants for a photo. They look like little microphones! (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2011)

This snail has a flat shell that turns clockwise. It is NOT one of the nasty introduced Garden Snails (Helix aspersa) that were apparently brought to California over 100 years ago as a food source. Introduced snails have a vertical shell. Fortunately we don't have those snails here in the mountains.

I have no idea what species this snail is. A web site I found lists 28 taxa of gastropods known just for Ventura County to the south of us, and there is a list of 279 species in California! Maybe this is genus Helminthoglypta? The web site is:
   http://www.magney.org/photofiles/VenturaCountySnails1.htm

I rarely notice the live native snails in the woods, though I do find their shells - maybe they are nocturnal? See my one photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/9497119/
Locality:
Helix aspersa) that were apparently brought to California over 100 years ago as a food source. Introduced snails have a vertical shell. Fortunately we don't have those snails here in the mountains.

I have no idea what species this snail is. A web site I found lists 28 taxa of gastropods known just for Ventura County to the south of us

File.Name: b131b_b130-015.jpg
Notes:
66506483 These are beautiful new flowers of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti var. xanti) in the Solanaceae plant family growing in a place that burned a few years ago in the Gap Fire. You can tell by the hairy stems that this is the species, not the variety hoffmannii. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2011)

Look very close - there's a tiny beetle-like Thrip (insect order Thysanoptera) to the left of the stamens of the uppermost flower.
ScientificName: Solanum xanti var. xanti; Solanaceae; hoffmannii; Thysanoptera
associatedTaxa: Solanum xanti var. xanti; Solanaceae; hoffmannii; Thysanoptera

File.Name: b131c_b129-053.jpg
Notes:
66506252 I can't resist... This is one of the tiny Omphalina pyxidata mushrooms in my [Previous] photo, with my pen for scale. The pen has a 0.7 mm ball, which makes the mushroom cap about 2 mm across. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2011)

It seems to be growing right from the sandstone rock. A relative of this mushroom (Omphalina ericetorum) is known as the "Lichen Agaric" because it is always associated with a lichen, but I don't see any lichen here.
Locality: Omphalina ericetorum) is known as the "Lichen Agaric" because it is always associated with a lichen

File.Name: b201a_b127-021.jpg
Notes:
66517237 This is just a little Fly-Fly in the order Diptera resting on a spade-shaped new leaf of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the Portulacaceae) plant family growing in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2011)

Uh - is that little patch of yellow goo behind the fly what I think it is? I'm glad it's not on my sandwich!

I first thought this might be a Cluster fly - genus Pollenia, family Calliphoridae - but the wing venation is wrong. I don't know what kind of fly it is. There are lots of them!
ScientificName: Diptera; Claytonia perfoliata; Portulacaceae; Pollenia; Calliphoridae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Claytonia perfoliata; Portulacaceae; Pollenia; Calliphoridae

File.Name: b201b_b129-049.jpg
Notes: 66516932 This is a strange fungus engulfing last year's acorns in the sandy soil above the retaining wall by our parking area. I guess it is a fungus mycelium, but beyond that, I don't have a clue what it is. I'm glad it's not growing between my toes! (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2011)

File.Name: b201c_b129-058.jpg
Notes:
66516704 Yum - dried out chanterelles taste just fine!

We've had company for a few days, so we tried out the dry Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms in the woods. These mushrooms have been in the ground for almost a month, but they tasted fine. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2011)

This has been a great year for the chanterelles. Even dried out, they are good to eat. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66475708

We usually "dry sautÈ" these fine mushrooms to concentrate the flavor. Clean them with a knife instead of water - they're like sponges. Then slice them into a hot dry pan, and let them stew in their own juices to concentrate the flavor until almost dry. Add a bit of fresh pepper and garlic and a splash of olive oil. Delicious!

File.Name: b202a_b131-008.jpg
Notes:
66528375 There are new flowers in the woods!

This is a gorgeous native Larkspur (genus Delphinium in the Ranunculaceae plant family. I'm still not sure what species this is, but I'm pretty sure it's either D. parryi or D. patens ssp. hepaticoideum. It might actually be a hybrid between the two. The key difference is whether the stem base is very narrow and not well attached - and I hate to break a flowering stem. (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2011)

The Ranunculaceae flowers are usually considered "primitive" because they have many stamens and carpels, but there's nothing primitive about this flower.
ScientificName: Delphinium; Ranunculaceae; D. parryi; D. patens; hepaticoideum; Ranunculaceae
associatedTaxa: Delphinium; Ranunculaceae; D. parryi; D. patens; hepaticoideum; Ranunculaceae

File.Name: b202b_b123-045.jpg
Notes: 66527824 This is a pretty little spider (Arachnida) in the woods posing with dramatic lighting. Since it's not on its web, I won't try to guess what kind of spider it is. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2011)

File.Name: b202c_b130-019.jpg
Notes:
66527749 It's always fun to try to photograph the smallest mushroom.

This is a really small disk-like Cup Fungus in the family Pezizaceae or kin. My pen has has a 0.7 mm ball, which makes the mushroom cap just a bit over 1 mm across. Now that's tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2011)

File.Name: b203a_b203-009.jpg
Notes:
66539797 There is no blue like the blue of Baby Blue-Eyes (Nemophila menziesii) flowering in the woods near Lake Cachuma. This native flower is part of the difficult Hydrophyllaceae plant family. Nothing else has a color like this. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 February 2011)

This particular flower is probably the variety integrifolia with smaller flowers and stamen filaments that are longer than the corolla tube.

Today's photos are all from the woods near Lake Cachuma. This is my favorite stop on the way to and from school.

I almost feel like I should apologize for our fair weather to my old friend Lester who lives near Madison, Wisconsin where they have been hammered by winter weather. See Lester's photos at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/lester_hunt/90476054

File.Name: b203b_b203-006.jpg
Notes:
66539487 These are beautiful and mysterious new seeds of native Hairy Lace Pod (Thysanocarpus curvipes) in the Brassicaceae plant family, with the sun behind. With this backlight, the seeds look like fractal designs. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 February 2011)

It's another mustard with tiny flowers and large seeds. You can strip the seeds off with your fingers and eat them as a snack or a salad garnish. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66299092

File.Name: b203c_b203-019.jpg
Notes: 66539384 This is native Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family flowering in the woods near Lake Cachuma this morning. It's not glandular like the Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) that we mostly have at home. (Santa Ynez Valley, 3 February 2011)

File.Name: b204a_b203-047.jpg
Notes:
66553065 I took my after-school Hike Club kids to remote Surf Beach out past the town of Lompoc on Thursday. We found an enormous flock of gulls where the river meets the ocean. Here they are in the air. That's the river in the foreground and the ocean in the background. (Surf Beach, 3 February 2011)

Sorry this is late. We had my school talent show last night and I got home late. Hah - a group of us teachers sang "Wild Thing" and "Twist and Shout" as the 6th graders played guitar!

File.Name: b204b_b203-050.jpg
Notes: 66553014 I took my after-school Hike Club kids to remote Surf Beach out past the town of Lompoc on Thursday. We found an enormous flock of gulls where the river meets the ocean. That's the river in the foreground and the ocean in the background. (Surf Beach, 3 February 2011)

File.Name: b204c_b203-089.jpg
Notes:
66552928 There's something magical about the place where a fresh water river meets the ocean - at least in our dry part of the world where it's something uncommon. During most of the year, there is a sandbar that cuts off the estuary from the ocean, but now it's open.

This is the mouth of the Santa Ynez River where it meets the ocean at Surf Beach out past the town of Lompoc on Thursday. The river is only about two feet deep - we easily waded it - but it's flowing free, with small sand waves. Maybe salmon and steelhead trout are swimming up the river! (Surf Beach, 3 February 2011)

At the top of my photo, you can barely make out the enormous flocks of gulls that were perched along the river. See my [Next] photos.

File.Name: b205a_b205-003.jpg
Notes:
66558255 Look close! This is a female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) sitting on her nest on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) right off our upstairs deck. I just spotted her this morning when I noticed her landing. I got this photo from about 20 feet away with my "big glass" - my Tamron 200-500 mm lens, with low light and 800 ISO. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2011)

Is her nest naturally lichen-encrusted, or did she place all those lichens?

Hah - she has a surprise that I didn't notice until I got my photos on the computer screen. I'll have another photo tomorrow!

I recommend the "Democracy Now" feed from Cairo, Egypt. There's a two-hour special today. The Friday show was gripping:
   http://www.democracynow.org/shows/2011/2/4

File.Name: b205b_b205-021.jpg
Notes: 66558037 I believe this is a beautiful grass-like native Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family. It's an unusual sedge, because it's a dry-land plant that grows under the oaks, and even in the chaparral on the south-facing side of our canyon where I got this photo. It's easy to over-expose these flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2011)

File.Name: b205c_b203-082.jpg
Notes:
66557945 "To see the world in a grain of sand,
   heaven in a wild flower,
to hold infinity in the palm of your hand
   and eternity in an hour."
   - William Blake

There were rows of these strange critters on Surf Beach where the largest waves at low tide left them. One of my Hike Club kids has one on his finger. (Surf Beach, 3 February 2011) They are the size of marbles, and gelatinous. I don't really know marine invertebrates. I guess they are phylum Ctenophora - maybe "Sea Gooseberries" (genus Pleurobrachia) without the long arms?

File.Name: b206a_b206-023.jpg
Notes:
66567522 These are new flowers of a native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plant in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. It's a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family in this place. It must have a fungal connection with its host plant. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2011)

I got this photo with the sun behind to illuminate the flowers. You can see that they have 2 stamens and a single exserted pistil. Beautiful!

We just got "off the line" from a Skype video call with our grandkids for (almost) their second birthday. I haven't used Skype before - pretty cool!
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Scrophulariaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Scrophulariaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: b206b_b206-009.jpg
Notes: 66567398 This is looking at the "inner circle" web of a small "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa,family Araneidae). It has an abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd! You can see the vertical "stabilimentum" - the thick white band of silk through the center of the web. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2011)

File.Name: b206c_b205-006.jpg
Notes:
66566613 Here is the mama Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) feeding her babies on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) right off our upstairs deck. See my [Previous] photo. Look close - you can see her baby's beak! (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2011)

I got this photo from about 20 feet away on our upstairs deck with my "big glass" - my Tamron 200-500 mm lens, with low light and 800 ISO. Our upstairs deck is like a springboard. I take the photo with my 2 second delay, and try to be very still. It's not the best photo, but it works.

It's wonderful that she's still here. We've had recent BBQ's on the deck, and we've been talking out there. All this time she was just 20 feet away!

File.Name: b207a_b205-024.jpg
Notes:
66578185 I wouldn't want to meet this bug in a dark alley!

This tough-looking insect is an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. You can see its mighty proboscis folded under its head. I found it this weekend prowling on a leaf of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) plant in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2011)

Judging from the photos at BugGuide, I believe it may be genus Sinea. See photos at:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/4347/bgimage
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Sinea
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae; Sinea

File.Name: b207b_b205-025.jpg
Notes: 66578004 This is a beautiful long-legged "Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the order Hemiptera resting on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. It has five-segmented antennae. They do produce odors - like cilantro! (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2011)

File.Name: b207c_b207-001.jpg
Notes: 66577899 Judging from its haphazard web, I believe this is a little Cobweb Spider aka Combfooted Spider (Theridiidae) on it's web in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods this morning. I can't make out the comb on its fourth leg, but I can see its little sclerotized "dingus" at the base of its abdomen. This "he" is really a "she" - the dingus is the female epigynum. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2011)
CommonNames: Cobweb Spider; Combfooted Spider; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: b208a_b205-017.jpg
Notes:
66588302 This is a perfect Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom in the woods. It's smaller than normal - only an inch or two across, growing in the dry leaves under a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2011)

This mushroom is handsome, but quite poisonous! The white warts on the cap are fragments of the universal veil that once covered the whole mushroom and form a volva at the base of the stalk. It contains the same interesting toxins as A. muscaria, but in higher concentrations. This mushroom should not be used for recreational purposes!

It's interesting that the last mushrooms in the drying out woods are almost all Amanitas - the most structurally perfect of all mushrooms. We had a wet December, but it's been mostly dry in January and February so far. The good news is that we might get some rain next week.
ScientificName: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; A. muscaria; Amanitas
associatedTaxa: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; A. muscaria; Amanitas

File.Name: b208b_b206-006.jpg
Notes:
66588113 These are large hand-sized Coccora (Amanita calyptrata) mushrooms - the Italian name means cocoon - growing in the woods. Despite the Italian name, it is only known from the west coast of the US. Look how thick the "volva" - the cup at the base of the stem - is on the mushroom on the right! It is growing mostly under a non-native Fir tree (Abies) by an old homestead in the mountains - which is puzzling. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2011)

This is the same kind of mushroom with the "amanita egg" I showed a couple of weeks ago:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66476123

It's said to be edible, but I generally avoid eating all Amanita mushrooms because some are deadly poison. But this really is a distinctive mushroom that I should try. My book describes it as having a "fishy" taste, which isn't too encouraging.

File.Name: b208c_b205-015.jpg
Notes:
66587942 It's interesting that the last mushrooms in the drying out woods are almost all Amanitas. I believe this is a spring "Blusher" (Amanita rubescens) mushroom. Look close, and you can see a little Drosophilid Fly - aka Pomace Fly - in the family Drosophilidae of the insect order Diptera perched on the edge of the cap. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2011)

This almost looks like a Cortinarius mushroom with those orange streaks on the stem, but see my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66410355
ScientificName: Amanitas; Amanita rubescens; Drosophilidae; Diptera; Cortinarius
associatedTaxa: Amanitas; Amanita rubescens; Drosophilidae; Diptera; Cortinarius

File.Name: b209a_b206-031.jpg
Notes: 66599454 Here are lots of new flowers on a native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family. This one shrub along the road is really covered with flowers! The shiny leaves are pretty too. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2011)

File.Name: b209b_b207-010.jpg
Notes:
66599371 I wasn't sure I'd see any of these caterpillars of Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterflies this year. They are usually our most common butterflies, but the population seemed to crash last year. I'm happy to see the caterpillars - even if they are munching on the leaves of this native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) shrub in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 7 February 2011)

These caterpillars only feed on "scrophs" - plants in the Scrophulariaceae plant family.
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: b209c_b205-019.jpg
Notes: 66599218 This is the same kind of large hand-sized Coccora (Amanita calyptrata) mushroom - the Italian name means cocoon - that I showed two photos [Previous]. This one is growing in sandy soil under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. It's interesting that this mushroom is almost pure white, while the others have quite yellow caps. Different populations, different host trees - who knows? (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2011)

File.Name: b210a_b209-050.jpg
Notes:
66608357 I found the first shy flowers of a beautiful native Chocolate Lily (Fritillaria biflora) in the Liliaceae plant family, growing on the lower slopes of Figueroa Mountain after school yesterday. The flowers always hang down, so I had to cheat a bit to show the beautiful inside. I propped one open flower on another, and rotated the whole image to also show the crinkly leaf. These beautiful flowers are solid and almost waxy, like tulips. (Figueroa Mountain, 9 February 2011)

I had some time to kill before our Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society board meeting last night, so I drove up Figueroa Mountain to see what I could find.

Last year, I found the first Chocolate Lilies on March 10. In 2009, I found them first on February 12. In 2008, I didn't find them until March 13. In 2007, I saw my first lilies on April 11. They seem early this year, but maybe I'm just getting better at finding them!
ScientificName: Fritillaria biflora; Liliaceae; Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society
associatedTaxa: Fritillaria biflora; Liliaceae; Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society

File.Name: b210b_b209-052.jpg
Notes:
66607960 I believe these pretty native flowers are Common Lomatium (Lomatium utriculatum) in the Apiaceae plant family. The individual flowers look like tiny origami boxes. (Figueroa Mountain, 9 February 2011)

I had some time to kill before our Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society board meeting yesterday, so I drove up Figueroa Mountain to see what I could find. See my [Next] photo!
ScientificName: Lomatium utriculatum; Apiaceae; Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society
associatedTaxa: Lomatium utriculatum; Apiaceae; Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society

File.Name: b210c_b203-004.jpg
Notes:
66607762 I believe these are our smallest native flowering plants. They are tiny succulent Sand Pygmyweed (Crassula connata) plants in the Crassulaceae plant family. I put my pen in the photo for scale. The ball of my pen is 0.7 mm - so the tiny leaves are just 1-2 mm long! My flora says they are "common, when looked for" - which probably isn't very often! (Santa Ynez Valley, 10 February 2011)

I got this photo in the chaparral by Lake Cachuma at my usual stop on the way to school today.

File.Name: b211a_b211-006.jpg
Notes:
66618850 This pretty little diurnal (daytime) moth is a Pyrausta dapalis in the Crambid Snout Moth family (Crambidae) of the order Lepidoptera. Look close. It's "puddling" like a butterfly - getting moisture or salts from a sandstone outcrop in the canyon today. It's like a tiny underwing moth - only about 1/4 inch across. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2011)

I thank Santa Barbara naturalist and photographer Lynn Watson for identifying this interesting moth for me. You can see Lynn's photo at BugGuide:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/371706

File.Name: b211b_b211-007.jpg
Notes:
66618670 Here is a graceful long-legged Fly in the order Diptera perched on a leaf of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the canyon by the creek today. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2011)

I find these little mosquito-like flies every year on the Willow trees nearby:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/32117011

Maybe it's a Dance Fly in the family Empididae - but it might also be an atypical Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the subfamily Toxophorinae, maybe the genus Geron. See BugGuide at:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/40597

I'll stand by my name - it's a Graceful Long-legged Fly!
CommonNames: Fly; Bitter Gooseberry; Willow; Dance Fly; Bee Fly; Graceful Long-legged Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Ribes amarum; Empididae; Bombyliidae; Toxophorinae; Geron
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Ribes amarum; Empididae; Bombyliidae; Toxophorinae; Geron

File.Name: b211c_b209-048.jpg
Notes: 66618227 Here is the whole plant of a beautiful native Chocolate Lily (Fritillaria biflora) in the Liliaceae plant family, growing on an outcrop of poor serpentine soil on the lower slopes of Figueroa Mountain. The flowers always hang down like this. See my [Previous] photo for an inside look. (Figueroa Mountain, 9 February 2011)

File.Name: b212a_b129-041.jpg
Notes:
66626793 I had to hike far up our canyon to the "big mushroom woods" to find these beautiful but deadly poisonous Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods today. There's a story to tell. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2011)

Our neighbors in the canyon lost their beloved dog this week, just before its 4th birthday. They got the best vet care, but it did no good. The vet said there are three choices about cause of death - "Amanita, Amanita, Amanita". Their dog died of massive liver failure, kidney failure, and a final stroke.

*Sigh* - these mushrooms really are beautiful, but they are deadly poisonous - to dogs as well as humans. I didn't believe it at first, but try a Google search:
   http://www.google.com/search?q=dogs+amanita+poisoning

The woods are getting too dry for mushrooms - even these deadly poisonous ones. Hopefully there's rain coming next week as predicted. We need it!

File.Name: b212b_b212-009.jpg
Notes:
66626346 This is another "Graceful Long-legged Fly" (my name) working a new female pistillate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family growing by the creek. Willow catkins look like they should be wind-pollinated, and maybe they are - but they also have sweet nectar glands under the dark scales at the base of the pistils. They do attract insect pollinators. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2011)

Maybe this is a Dance Fly in the family Empididae - but it might also be an atypical Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the subfamily Toxophorinae, maybe the genus Geron.

It was breezy today - hard to photograph these tiny insects. Hopefully that's the first sign of rain coming next week as predicted. We need it!
CommonNames: Graceful Long-legged Fly; Arroyo Willow; Dance Fly; Bee Fly

File.Name: b212c_b211-009.jpg
Notes:
66626176 This mosquito-size fly (insect order Diptera) looks like a Mosquito (family Culicidae), and may actually be one - though several features aren't right. It's doesn't have scales of the wing veins, and it's holding its front legs in the air instead of its rear legs. It does have plumose antennae like a male mosquito. It had no interest in me. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2011)

It was breezy today - hard to photograph these tiny insects. Hopefully that's the first sign of rain coming next week as predicted. We need it!

File.Name: b213a_b213-005.jpg
Notes:
66636304 This is a crime scene investigation!

I found this little spider - maybe a Cobweb Spider aka Combfooted Spider (Theridiidae) - on it's web in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods this morning. It appears to be quite dead - or at least paralysed. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2011)

The only evidence is an unknown severed insect leg in the lower-right corner of the photo.

What happened to this spider?
(I have no idea!)

It's been cloudy today - hopefully a sign of rain coming this week as predicted. We need it!
CommonNames: Cobweb Spider; Combfooted Spider; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Theridiidae; Quercus agrifolia; I have no idea!
associatedTaxa: Theridiidae; Quercus agrifolia; I have no idea!

File.Name: b213b_b213-003.jpg
Notes:
66635994 I believe these are new flowers of Climbing Bedstraw (Galium porrigens) in the Rubiaceae plant family. The new flower buds are especially interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2011)

All Galium species have leaves in whorls of four, but there are many different kinds in the local mountains. I'm confused by them all. I guess that's evolution in action!

I finally made a plant list for San Marcos Pass, and I'm trying to get photos of all the plants on my list. You can expect more photos of odd plants!

File.Name: b213c_b213-007.jpg
Notes:
66635537 Here is another beautiful but deadly poisonous Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees that I found in a different place in the woods today. It is their time, wet or dry as its been. Don't let your dog play with these! See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2011)

These deadly mushrooms are easy to recognize. Pure white cap - like a styrofoam cup in the woods; ring or "skirt" on the stem; and the characteristic "death cup" at the base of the stem - see my [Previous] photo.

Lester - Amanita virosa is the European form of the Destroying Angel. You probably saw the Death Cap (Amanita phalloides) with a bit more yellow-green in the cap. All of these mushrooms are equally poisonous. It's a thrill to hold death in your hand - but we do it every time we drive cars or cut veggies!

"[i]Nobody owns life, but anyone who can pick up a frying pan owns death.[/i]"
   - William Burroughs
Locality:
Amanita phalloides) with a bit more yellow-green in the cap. All of these mushrooms are equally poisonous. It's a thrill to hold death in your hand - but we do it every time we drive cars or cut veggies!

File.Name: b214a_b214-004.jpg
Notes:
66646575 Here are beautiful new flowers of native California Saxifrage (Saxifraga californica) in the Saxifragaceae plant family. They are growing on a shady bank along the road - with another native saxifrage, see my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2011)

Start with the flower in the middle-left that's in focus, and go counter-clockwise. You can see the female pistils of the flowers growing further apart towards the pink stamens - I guess to insure that pollination happens. I think most flowers have a "backup plan" to self-pollinate.

There were a few drops of rain falling while I took these photos this morning... but that's about all we've had so far. The real rain is expected on Wednesday. We need it!

File.Name: b214b_b214-005.jpg
Notes:
66646324 These are pretty little flowers of native Mission Star (Lithophragma cymbalaria) in the Saxifragaceae plant family. They are growing on a shady bank along the road. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2011)

This Lithophragma cymbalaria has opposite leaves on its flower stalks, but we also have the similar Woodland Star (Lithophragma affine) with alternate leaves on the stem. These little differences help make our local plants interesting year after year!
ScientificName: Lithophragma cymbalaria; Saxifragaceae; Lithophragma cymbalaria; Lithophragma affine
associatedTaxa: Lithophragma cymbalaria; Saxifragaceae; Lithophragma cymbalaria; Lithophragma affine

File.Name: b214c_b212-003.jpg
Notes:
66646166 Apart from the beautiful but deadly Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushrooms, this is the only mushroom I found in the drying-out woods last weekend. I recognize it as a small Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) mushroom hiding under the grass-like native Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2011)

See my photo of a better specimen from a week ago at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66588302

The spots on the cap are indistinct because it's been so dry, but you can still see them. It's also poisonous to people and dogs!
CommonNames: Destroying Angel; Panther Amanita; Roundfruit Sedge
ScientificName: Amanita ocreata; Amanita pantherina; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae
associatedTaxa: Amanita ocreata; Amanita pantherina; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae

File.Name: b215a_b212-006.jpg
Notes:
66657787 This is another "Graceful Long-legged Fly" (my name) perched on a leaf of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the canyon by the creek. It's head is mostly compound eyes! Maybe it's a Dance Fly in the family Empididae - but it might also be an atypical Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the subfamily Toxophorinae, maybe the genus Geron. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2011)

It was a tough weekend for new photos - too dry for most mushrooms and too early for flowers and insects. There's a 100% chance of rain tomorrow - just what we need. The frogs are going at it - a good sign!
CommonNames: Graceful Long-legged Fly; Bitter Gooseberry; Dance Fly; Bee Fly

File.Name: b215b_b213-001.jpg
Notes: 66657305 Here is a relatively small long-legged Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera resting head-down on a potato leaf in the garden. This critter's body is only about 1/2 inch long - much smaller than some of its cousins. Look close - who would guess that they have "sky blue" colors in their wings! (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2011)

File.Name: b215c_b211-003.jpg
Notes: 66657172 This is another beautiful furry Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae in the order Diptera) in the canyon - maybe genus Bombylius. It must weigh almost nothing. Look how gently it's perched on the leaf stem. (San Marcos Pass, 11 February 2011)

File.Name: b216a_b213-015.jpg
Notes:
66667309 Here are sweet-smelling white flowers of native BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family growing along West Camino Cielo road in the mountains. The individual flowers are tiny, but elegant - and there are zillions of them! The delicate purple 3-parted styles are beautiful. They have a shiny "floral disk" at the base of each flower that serves as a nectar gland. In these flowers the disk is rich blue, but in other plants of the same species they are yellow. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2011)

These spring flowers cover the shrubs - and sometimes the whole mountainside - with a white bloom like snow. In good years, you can see them from the beach!

The leaves are mostly alternate - which is unusual for the subgenus Cerastes. But some plants in this area have opposite leaves, which makes them C. megacarpus var. insularis. Actually, you can find alternate and opposite leaves on the same plant! Some plants may also be hybrids with Ceanothus cuneatus.

It's interesting that these are a dominate shrub on the south-facing slopes of the mountains, but they are replaced by Ceanothus cuneatus on the north-facing slopes. They look almost identical in flower, but these plants have larger seedpods.

Today's photos are all different white-flowered Ceanothus shrubs in the subgenus Cerastes. This species diversity keeps the local plants interesting after all these years. We also have blue-flowered species.

We got about 1-1/4 inch of welcome rain today. A larger storm is expected on Friday. It's just what we need.

associatedTaxa: Ceanothus megacarpus; Rhamnaceae; Cerastes; C. megacarpus; insularis; Ceanothus cuneatus; Ceanothus cuneatus; Ceanothus; Cerastes

File.Name: b216b_b213-030.jpg
Notes:
66666959 These are new flowers of Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's another of the white-flowered Ceanothus shrubs that cover our mountains every spring. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2011)

This one is distinctive. It has two-toned leaves that are green on top and white underneath. It also has "corky stipules" - those round dark things that look like little brown coconuts at the base of the opposite leaves. It doesn't grow as low as our canyon, but it does grow about a mile up East Camino Cielo road near the Painted Cave turn-off.

File.Name: b216c_b124-008.jpg
Notes:
66666700 Here are sweet-smelling white flowers of native Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family growing near Lake Cachuma on my way to school. The individual flowers are tiny, but elegant - and there are zillions of them! The delicate purple 3-parted styles are beautiful. They have opposite leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2011)

These spring flowers cover the shrubs - and sometimes whole hillsides - with a white bloom like snow. The individual flowers are remarkable up close. They have a shiny "floral disk" at the base of each flower that serves as a nectar gland. In these flowers the disk is yellow, but in other plants of the same species it is blue.

You can make soap from the flowers - a handful of flowers will make suds when you rub them in water!

It's interesting that these are a dominate shrub on the north-facing slopes of the mountains, but they are replaced by Ceanothus megacarpus on the south-facing slopes. They look almost identical.

File.Name: b217a_b206-020.jpg
Notes:
66677997 These are remarkable new flowers of a native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plant in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. It may look like I used flash, but this is natural light during a brief moment of sunshine. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2011)

This plant seems to be lacking the usual reddish bracts in the inflorescence. Compare my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66567522

It's a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family in this place. It must have a fungal connection with its host plant.

I've been substitute-teaching at school. It's tough getting up early and going to work once you lose the habit! I'm glad next week is "Mid-Winter Vacation". Real rain is expected tomorrow - 3 to 6 inches!
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Scrophulariaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Scrophulariaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: b217b_b212-001.jpg
Notes:
66677800 These are beautiful flowers of the native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family, related to sassafras and avocado. The flowers are remarkably intricate! (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2011)

Have you ever accidentally eaten a "bay leaf" in a stew? *Yuck* Our native bay trees are like that only more so!

File.Name: b217c_b213-011.jpg
Notes:
66677632 Here is another native Ceanothus shrub starting to flower in our mountains - Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2011)

This shrub is subgenus Ceanothus rather than subgenus Cerastes like my [Previous] few photos. The plant looks very different, but the individual flowers are similar. Ceanothus oliganthus is another shrub with deeper blue flowers that isn't quite flowering yet.
ScientificName: Ceanothus; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus; Cerastes; Ceanothus oliganthus

File.Name: b218a_b210-013.jpg
Notes:
66687071 Here is a well-named native Shooting Star (Dodecatheon clevelandii) wildflower in the Primulaceae plant family growing in the woods by Lake Cachuma. They are really beautiful, especially that crinkly tissue at the base of the anthers that look like little bird beaks. (San Marcos Pass, 10 February 2011)

We're getting hammered by the wind and rain tonight! I'm feeling better about the coming dry summer.

File.Name: b218b_b206-021.jpg
Notes: 66686978 These are the almost ripe berries of a native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The berries turn blue when they are fully ripe. They taste bitter until they are ripe, then bland. The birds like them. (San Marcos Pass, 12 February 2011)

File.Name: b218c_b217-005.jpg
Notes:
66686800 Blues for a rainy day...

We had a bit of rain yesterday - enough to cancel my after-school hike club, so I stopped by Paradise Store on the way home. There was a nice little music jam on the front porch. That's Kwan on guitar, Bob Hart on keys and harp, and a sax player I don't know. Kwan teaches music at my school.

Bob Hart is a local character who plays beautiful rambling ballads on 12 string slide guitar and keyboards. You can hear him on this video, which also has some nice aerial shots of my country:
   http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=zoZkrE3ocM8

File.Name: b219a_b219-021.jpg
Notes:
66695785 This isn't a flower, but the split cap of a little black-spored Inky Cap (Coprinus) mushroom reflecting the woods from rain caught in its upturned cap today. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2011)

Our storm last night came in with great fanfare, but it fizzled out and mostly went west of us. For once, Lompoc got more rain than San Marcos Pass! We didn't get the 3-6 inches expected last night, but we've still had over 3 inches for the week, so I'm not complaining.

File.Name: b219b_b219-003.jpg
Notes:
66695600 What do little nesting birds do in the rain and snow?

This is a mama Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) on her nest this morning - before the snow today. I guess they just sit the storm out under a canopy of oak leaves like little umbrellas. They get cold and wet and... wait until it gets better. Like we all do. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2011)

I wonder how such tiny birds can hold their body heat for a night and even longer. Tiny critters means faster heat loss. Somehow it works!

Compare my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66558255

I think the lichens on her nest have grown!

File.Name: b219c_b219-039.jpg
Notes:
66695436 We had some slushy snow today! This is snow falling around a small Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family, taken from our front porch. It didn't quite stick. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2011)

It's odd - while it snowed, the temperature was at 36∞ Fahrenheit. Then the temperature fell below freezing and the snow turned to rain. Now it's about 28∞ and we're getting corn snow. Confusing.

We've got the wood fire (the only heat in our house) going - let it snow! But not too much snow - it breaks the oak trees.

File.Name: b220a_b219-017.jpg
Notes:
66704291 These are new "fiddleheads" of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the woods. They will become new fern fronds. They are edible - or are they? There are reports of cattle and horse poisoning. Caveat Emptor! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2011)

We're flying to Portland, Oregon tomorrow to visit our kids and grandkids! We'll be back in a week. I saved a few new mushroom photos for when we get home. How do the mushrooms come up so fast after the rain?

File.Name: b220b_b220-010.jpg
Notes:
66704091 Did you notice the little yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) in the lower-left?

Neither did this Fly (order Diptera). This little drama took place today on a flowering Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2011)

File.Name: b220c_b219-005.jpg
Notes:
66703908 Don't you hate it when you botch a perfect photo!

This is our mama Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) about to feed her babies in her nest. I got this photo from our upstairs porch, which is like a natural springboard. I used my self-timer with two second delay as always - I push the button and don't breath. I must have moved, or maybe Julie walked across the living room. It's that sensitive! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2011)

It's still a pretty cool photo! The babies are growing fast.

File.Name: b221a_b221-038.jpg
Notes:
66714686 I've been calling this the "Graceful Long-legged Fly" (my name). Its head is mostly compound eyes! Maybe it's a Dance Fly in the family Empididae - but it might also be an atypical Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the subfamily Toxophorinae, maybe the genus Geron. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2011)

This "Graceful Long-legged Fly" is working a female pistillate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family growing by the creek. Willow catkins look like they should be wind-pollinated, and maybe they are - but they also have sweet nectar glands under the dark scales at the base of the pistils. They do attract insect pollinators.

We originally planned to drive to LA today to catch our Tuesday flight, but it didn't happen. Just as well - there's new life in the woods after our rain this week!

We're really flying to Portland, Oregon tomorrow to visit our kids and grandkids! We'll be back in a week.
Locality: Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family growing by the creek. Willow catkins look like they should be wind-pollinated

File.Name: b221b_b221-005.jpg
Notes:
66714390 This is a beautiful spring "Blusher" (Amanita rubescens) mushroom that I found in the woods this morning. The "partial veil" is just tearing free from the rim of the cap. You can see that it won't leave much of a ring on the stem. This mushroom also has no obvious "volva" from the "universal veil". It gets its name from the reddish colors it develops as it ages. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2011)

This amanita is said to be edible when cooked, but it's easily confused with poisonous species. No thanks!

File.Name: b221c_b221-014.jpg
Notes:
66714189 This is a picture-perfect Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom that I found in the woods this morning. Usually the "death cup" volva is buried deep in the ground, but in this specimen it was easy to see once I cleared a bit. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2011)

These deadly mushrooms are easy to recognize. Pure white cap - like a styrofoam cup in the woods; ring or "skirt" on the stem; and the characteristic "death cup" volva at the base of the stem. They are beautiful, but deadly - don't eat them!

File.Name: b221d_b221-023.jpg
Notes: 66714049 This is a beautiful Amanita mushroom that I found in the woods today. It's growing under a bower of native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens) and California Bedstraw (Galium californicum). I'm pretty sure this is another Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina), though most of the scales have been washed off the cap by the rain. It's beautiful, but quite poisonous. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2011)
CommonNames: Thin Grass; California Bedstraw; Panther Amanita
ScientificName: Amanita; Agrostis pallens; Galium californicum; Amanita pantherina
associatedTaxa: Amanita; Agrostis pallens; Galium californicum; Amanita pantherina

File.Name: b221e_b219-027.jpg
Notes: 66713722 There are still new Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms coming up in the woods. What a bumper crop we've had this year. Julie made a thick chanterelle gravy for our chicken last night. Delicious! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2011)

File.Name: b221f_b219-008.jpg
Notes:
66713537 I found the same mushrooms last year, and I thought they might be the Big Laughing Mushroom (Gymnopilus spectabilis aka G. junonius) or maybe the Spring Agrocybe (Agrocybe praecox). I'm not happy with either choice.

They have dull brown spores and an evanescent partial veil. There are large clusters of them on the hillside. They are growing on wood chips from tree work done two years ago. I guess they were introduced by the wood chipper. I don't know what they are! (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2011)
ScientificName: Gymnopilus spectabilis; G. junonius; Agrocybe praecox
