File.Name: b227a_b226-029.jpg
Notes:
66779295 This is my son Ewan with Roz reading Dr. Seuss to our twin grandkids - Kelvin on the left and Otto to the right. The grandkids were feeling poor for this visit - little Kelvin was sick with a stomach virus, and Otto needed attention. No problem - the unusual Portland snow made it a memorable visit! I like the Chiaroscuro lighting. (Portland, Oregon, 26 February 2011)

Sorry Lester, no great "pictures of Oregon sights" this trip - nothing but babies!

File.Name: b227b_b226-042.jpg
Notes:
66779135 Here is my son Ewan playing mandolin with his son (my grandson) Kelvin on his knee. Look at Kelvin's double-exposed fingers - he's beating out the rhythm! (Portland, Oregon, 26 February 2011)

Ewan has a classic "The Gibson" mandolin with a beautiful spruce top. I think it was made before 1920. It still has beautiful sound.

File.Name: b227c_b226-039.jpg
Notes: 66778810 It's probably hard to see here, but this is my grandson Otto "blowing harp" - playing harmonica - with his Dad Ewan on mandolin and our friend Collen on guitar! Once the big people started playing blues in G, it sounded pretty good! Kelvin is laid back, still feeling *blah*, but live music does have charms. (Portland, Oregon, 26 February 2011)

File.Name: b227d_b226-016.jpg
Notes: 66778711 Here is Grandma Julie and our grandchild Otto playing in the snow in Portland, Oregon last week. Julie's face says it all! (Portland, Oregon, 24 February 2011)

File.Name: b227e_b226-022.jpg
Notes: 66778651 Here is Mama Roz and Otto walking in the fresh snow by their home. We got an inch or so of snow twice - on Wednesday night and Thursday afternoon. It didn't stick to the roads, but it was cold. The thermometer said 18 ∞F on this morning! (Portland, Oregon, 24 February 2011)

File.Name: b227f_b226-002.jpg
Notes:
66778450 Mama Roz and our twin grandkids - warm and snuggly inside, but looking out the window at fresh snow on the Rhododendrons. I like the Chiaroscuro lighting. (Portland, Oregon, 24 February 2011)

The grandkids were feeling poor for this visit - little Kelvin was sick with a stomach virus, and Otto needed attention. No problem - the unusual Portland snow made it a memorable visit.

File.Name: b228a_b228-026.jpg
Notes: 66780444 Here is another beautiful furry Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera in the canyon - maybe genus Bombylius. Look - you can see the real fly underneath its thick hairs. It needs that fur today - it's been cold! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2011)

File.Name: b228b_b228-014.jpg
Notes: 66780362 This is looking at the "inner circle" web of a small long-legged "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) in the family Araneidae. Look how delicately she holds her web under tension, waiting for the slightest tug! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2011)

File.Name: b228c_b228-017.jpg
Notes: 66780271 This fungus isn't beautiful, but it is interesting! This is a strange Coral Mushroom (genus Ramaria) growing in our woods, in a place where our neighbors cut and chipped the chaparral down to the ground. I'm not sure what kind of coral mushroom it is - maybe Ramaria botrytis or kin? (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2011)

File.Name: b301a_b228-012.jpg
Notes:
66789867 I believe this is a Gilled Polypore (Lenzites betulina) mushroom growing from a fallen oak branch in the woods. It appears to be a gilled mushroom, but it's actually a polypore in the fungal order Polyporales. It looks like a 3D Maze! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2011)

Maybe this is an example of convergent evolution - a fungus that developed its gills independently of the rest of the Agaricales mushrooms?

More rain is expected late tonight and tomorrow. We've had 31.26 inches so far this rain year - still above normal. I'm happy, because the life in our woods during the long, dry summer depends on the rain we get now. It's looking good, especially for an expected-dry La NiÒa year!

File.Name: b301b_b221-011.jpg
Notes: 66789691 This is a little Fairy Tub mushroom in the woods, maybe Peziza sylvestris or kin. They look like little fairy bath tubs! It's about the size of a walnut. These cup fungi don't have any stalk, unlike the mushroom in my [Previous] photo. They have crystalline warts like a puffball. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2011)

File.Name: b301c_b228-008.jpg
Notes: 66789447 This is an odd "Elfin Cup" fungus in the woods yesterday. Notice the well-developed stem below the cup. The ribs don't extend on to the underside of the cap, so maybe this is the Ribbed Elfin Saucer (Helvella queletii)? Or maybe it's just another Brown Ribbed Elfin Cup (Helvella acetabulum) mushroom. Either way it's strange and beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2011)
CommonNames: Ribbed Elfin Saucer; Brown Ribbed Elfin Cup

File.Name: b302a_b228-001.jpg
Notes:
66799627 This is a different "composite organism". (See my [Previous] photo.)

This is a little Cynipid Wasp Gall made by tiny wasps in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family). I have no idea which wasp they are. For scale, the buds of the oak leaves are about 1/8 inch long. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2011)

We have many different insect galls in our woods. I reckon this is a bisexual "spring gall" made by female wasps (with no help from males). Male and female wasps will emerge from these galls in early summer to mate and produce another crop of the more distinctive summer galls on the oak branches that will produce only female wasps that will produce more spring galls. And the cycle repeats.

I'm doing my lecture and slide show on insect galls for the Cheadle Center for Biodiversity and Ecological Restoration (CCBER) at UCSB in two months. Expect more gall photos!

We've had about 1/4 inch of rain today. Not much, but its been drizzling all day - a good soaker - and every bit helps bring a good summer!
Locality:

File.Name: b302b_b221-019.jpg
Notes:
6799303 Liverworts, mosses, and lichens - oh my!

Here is a pretty scene on the rocks near the creek. There are tiny flat-leaved Liverworts (Marchantiophyta), little shrub-like Mosses (Bryophyta), and a large whitish lichen in the center. I believe that the dark "craters" are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. I won't try to guess which lichen it is. (San Marcos Pass, 28 February 2011)

Lichens pose an issue for taxonomy because they are composite organisms consisting of a symbiotic association of a fungus and an algae. So how are they named? "Both the lichen and the fungus partner bear the same scientific name, and the lichens are being integrated into the classification schemes for fungi. The alga bears its own scientific name, which bears no relationship to that of the lichen or fungi." (Wikipedia)

The problem is that all "higher organisms" may well be collections of simpler organisms - even you and me! If we reject the idea that "all living organisms are autonomous", where do we stop?

File.Name: b302c_b221-009.jpg
Notes:
66798981 Here are two different Amanita mushrooms from our woods - a spring "Blusher" (Amanita rubescens) on the left, and the beautiful but deadly Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom on the right. (San Marcos Pass, 21 February 2011)

They share a family resemblance - both have swollen bases and partial veil "rings" on the stem, but you can see the differences too. The Blusher developes red stains with handling that the pure white Destroying Angel lacks. The Blusher will also lose most of that veil under her gills and all of the universal veil at the base of her stem, but the Destroying Angel keeps the ring and the "death cup" volva.

Beautiful as they are, neither mushroom should be eaten.

File.Name: b303a_b303-001.jpg
Notes:
66808794 This is a male March Fly (family Bibionidae), order Diptera - and it is March! It's feeling *blah* on a drizzly morning, resting on a leaf of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family in the garden. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2011)

I believe this is genus Bibio because of the sharp spurs on the front tibia. See my [Previous] photo for the female. The abdomen of this male is smaller, but the eyes are much larger - to find the ladies?

I've been seeing lots of females today, but this was the only male I found.

File.Name: b303b_b303-033.jpg
Notes:
66808615 These are female March Flies (family Bibionidae), order Diptera. It's March!

There were a dozen and more of these females perched on new leaves of Bowlesia incana in the Apiaceae plant family. I have no idea why they picked this plant. I believe they are the genus Bibio because of the sharp spurs on the front tibia. They look like beetles.

The males look very different. See my [Next] photo.

File.Name: b303c_b303-010.jpg
Notes:
66808244 These are muscular limbs of a beautiful Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family, wet from last night's rain. You can see the distinctive shredding bark hanging from the limbs. I did no color correction on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2011)

Sure, it's sexy!

File.Name: b304a_b303-009.jpg
Notes:
66821469 These are perfect rain drops on a small Petty Spurge (Euphorbia peplus) in the Euphorbiaceae plant family. There's a flower half-in the big drop in the center. This odd little plant isn't native, but it persists as a weed at the site of an old homestead on the pass. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2011)

We had a terrific "Astronomy Night" at my school last night, so I'm posting these late.

File.Name: b304b_b303-023.jpg
Notes: 66821373 Here are rain drops of a mass of the sporophytes of Moss (Bryophyta). They look like decorations of pure glass - like a free-form Dale Chihuly installation! (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2011)

File.Name: b304c_b303-030.jpg
Notes: 66821322 Here is one more female March Fly (family Bibionidae), order Diptera perched on a leaf of soon-to-flower Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. This isn't the sharpest photo, but it shows the sharp spurs on "kneecaps" of the front tibia that place it in the genus Bibio. See my [Previous] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2011)

File.Name: b305a_b305-017.jpg
Notes:
66825331 Dancing with flowers...

Here is a furry Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera - I think genus Bombylius - working a new flower of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the Portulacaceae) plant family growing in the canyon. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2011)

Please look at all my photos today - they're all gold!

Despite the calendar, today feels like the first day of Spring - temps in the 70s and butterflies in the air! But there are altocumulus "mackerel sky" clouds moving in and there's a good chance of rain tomorrow night. The frogs are going at it again tonight. Bring it on!

File.Name: b305b_b305-022.jpg
Notes:
66824988 Today feels like the first day of Spring - temps in the 70s and butterflies in the air!

This is a beautiful Mylitta Crescent (Phyciodes mylitta) butterfly resting on a leaf of weedy Bur Clover (Medicago polymorpha) along the road in the canyon. I had to chase it for a while to wear it down! (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2011)

I also got a glimpse of Sara Orangetip (Anthocharis sara) and Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterflies in the air. And most surprising, I saw California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterflies today. I usually see them at the end of summer. I'll post a photo-proof tomorrow!

File.Name: b305c_b305-009.jpg
Notes:
66824794 This is a tiny "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa,family Araneidae) - it has an abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd! You can see it's inner web - the sanctum sanctorum for these little spiders. It has a even-tinier fly for prey, but I can't make it out. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2011)

There was wind in forecast today, but it didn't happen. You need absolutely still conditions to get a photo like this!

File.Name: b306a_b305-029.jpg
Notes:
66834106 This is a new female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly resting on a native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. I watched it flutter around the bush for a while, and then it perched for good, and let me get close. I saw three male butterflies in the air while I was waiting. Was she waiting for a pick-up? (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2011)

I'm surprised because I usually find these butterflies in July to October. I wonder what they are doing out so early this year?

It's been breezy and drizzly all day. There's no rain to speak of yet, but the frogs are going at it. I've been (finally!) working on my school "Science Fair Project" about fire-follower plants in our mountains, due on March 18. I'm the semi-retired science teacher - but I still want to do my own project!
Locality: finally!) working on my school "Science Fair Project" about fire-follower plants in our mountains

File.Name: b306b_b305-036.jpg
Notes:
66833977 The only new mushrooms I found yesterday are these little dark-spored Inky Cap (Coprinus) mushrooms growing on old wood chips along our road. It's odd because the ground is still wet from recent rains, but I guess their time is done. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2011)

Now it's time for flowers and insects - though the drizzle today is putting that on hold.

File.Name: b306c_b305-005.jpg
Notes:
66833934 This little Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) seems to have captured this March Fly (genus Bibio, family Bibionidae, order Diptera) - but the little fly made one last move and got away. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2011)

I got this photo yesterday when it felt like the first day of Spring, but everything is different today. It's been breezy and drizzly all day. There's no rain to speak of yet, but the frogs are going at it.

File.Name: b307a_b305-011.jpg
Notes:
66841321 This tough-looking insect is an Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. You can see its mighty proboscis folded under its head. It's perched on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2011)

Judging from the photos at BugGuide, I believe it may be genus Sinea. See photos at:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/4347/bgimage

Busy, busy, busy - I'm working on my science fair project about fire-follower wildflowers, due on March 18. I'm the semi-retired science teacher - but I still want to do my own project!

File.Name: b307b_b305-023.jpg
Notes: 66841189 This is looking up at brown "Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the order Hemiptera perched on a blade of grass in the woods. Look how it tucks its long proboscis down between its legs! (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2011)

File.Name: b307c_b305-014.jpg
Notes: 66841088 This little Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) resting in a Coast Live Oak tree looks sad. That two-humped abdomen looks familiar, but I don't have time to check my archives right now. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2011)

File.Name: b308a_b305-003.jpg
Notes: 66849504 Pretty little flowers and erect seed pods of Bitter Cress (Cardamine oligosperma) in the Brassicaceae plant family. I believe this tiny mustard is native, but it's growing like a weed near a pile of garden trimmings at home. I first noticed this plant a few years ago, and it's spreading like a weed in our canyon, even though it is native to California. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2011)

File.Name: b308b_305-034.jpg
Notes:
66849378 These are female pistillate catkins of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family growing by the creek. They are catching the low winter sun just right. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2011)

Willow catkins look like they should be wind-pollinated, and maybe they are - but they also have sweet nectar glands under the dark scales at the base of the pistils. They do attract insect pollinators. If you look very close, you can see a "Graceful Long-legged Fly" (my name) on the leftmost catkin.

For a closer look at the fly, see my recent photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66714686
Locality:
my name) on the leftmost catkin.

For a closer look at the fly

File.Name: b308c_b305-015.jpg
Notes:
66849246 This is a pretty little Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) resting in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree a few away from the spider I posted yesterday at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66841088

This spider is smaller and more colorful, but they have the same long sad faces and double-humped abdomens. I wonder if they are male and female of the same species? (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2011)

File.Name: b309a_b309-033.jpg
Notes:
66859816 This is a beautiful - but small - native Chocolate Lily (Fritillaria biflora) in the Liliaceae plant family, growing on an outcrop of poor serpentine soil on the lower slopes of Figueroa Mountain. (Figueroa Mountain, 9 March 2011)

The flowers are the same size as usual, but the plants are unusually small this year - and hard to find. That small California Plantain (Plantago erecta) in the upper right is standing larger!

I guess it's been the odd weather this year - very wet December followed a month and a half of no rain at all - and then more rain. These Chocolate Lilies are stunted. It doesn't bode well for wildflowers this year, but things look better at home across the Santa Ynez Valley.
Locality:

File.Name: b309b_b309-039.jpg
Notes: 66859776 These are pretty flowers of a very small Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. fremontii in the Liliaceae plant family. The plants seem stunted this year - only a few inches tall. See my [Next] and [Previous] photos. (Figueroa Mountain, 9 March 2011)
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas

File.Name: b309c_b309-041.jpg
Notes:
66859718 This is the whole plant of a small Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. fremontii in the Liliaceae plant family. I found this pretty plant on the middle slopes of Figueroa Mountain today when I had a break between school and a board meeting. (Figueroa Mountain, 9 March 2011)

I guess it's been the odd weather this year - very wet December followed a month and a half of no rain at all - and then more rain. This plant seems stunted - it's only a few inches high. The [i]Zigadenus fremontii[/i] var. [i]inezianus[/i] in our mountains (with the flower glands greenish) are much larger!
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas

File.Name: b310a_b310-033.jpg
Notes:
66867774 Here is a early new Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2011)

I got this photo along the trail to "The Playground" today. This butterfly was struggling among the weedy Bromus grasses along the trail. I guess it's freshly emerged, but I didn't see any plant in the Scrophulariaceae plant family (their usual favorite) nearby.

File.Name: b310b_b310-005.jpg
Notes:
66867473 Here is a perfect yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. It seems odd that it's on a leaf rather than a flower, but there was a new flower just below the leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2011)

Dpn't over-expose spiders and their webs! I got this photo at -1.67 stops.

File.Name: b310c_b310-021.jpg
Notes:
66867240 Here is a pretty Popcorn Flower (genus Cryptantha) in the Boraginaceae plant family. You need a microscope to tell these flowers apart, and I really don't care. It's growing as a fire-follower on the Gap Wildfire burn a few years ago. You have to remember not to over-expose these white flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2011)

My DMS science fair project on "Fire-follower Flowers on San Marcos Pass" is coming together, but I'm still getting new photos. It's due next Friday!

File.Name: b311a_b311-006.jpg
Notes:
66876927 These interesting native Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) are working a leaf bud of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. I've seen this before. I wonder what the ants get from the oaks? (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2011)

I believe these ants are genus Crematogaster in the subfamily Myrmicinae. Look close at the ant on the right, and you can see the spines on its thorax and the interesting attachment to its abdomen.

It was a crummy day for new photos - foggy and breezy all day - but I did OK!
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae

File.Name: b311b_b311-003.jpg
Notes: 66876731 I found this Cicada (family Cicadidae in the order Homoptera) today hanging from a branch of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. Our summer cicadas "click" in the woods, but I don't think these early cicadas make any sound. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2011)

File.Name: b311c_b311-019.jpg
Notes:
66876425 Here is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a flower head of English Plantain (Plantago lanceolata) weed in the Plantaginaceae) plant family growing along the road in the canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2011)

The spider was originally pointing down towards the stamens instead of up with the pistils, but I spooked it trying to get my photo. Sure, the white balance is a little off with this photo in the shade, but it's still pretty cool!

File.Name: b312a_b311-022.jpg
Notes:
66885510 This is the most obscure wildflower I know in our mountains! Those really are the flowers. I took me years to figure this out. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2011)

This is Bowlesia incana in the Apiaceae plant family. The Apiaceae were once known as the Umbelliferae - because the flowers usually come in umbrella-like "umbels" with lots of flowers, but this is an umbel of one. I think of it as a limit thing - like a circle is a limit of an ellipse, so this flower is the limit of an umbel!

I've been tied to the computer all day sorting photos for my science fair project on "Fire-follower Plants on San Marcos Pass". My deadline is next Friday!

File.Name: b312b_b310-024.jpg
Notes:
66885272 Here is a little plant you don't want to encounter while crawling through the chaparral!

This is the small native Western Nettle (Hesperocnidae tenella) in the UrticaceaIt's growing along the trail to "The Playground". I guess it is another fire-follower plant.

File.Name: b312c_b311-017.jpg
Notes: 66885105 This is a new dry-land native Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family flowering in the canyon. It's really a remarkable plant - related to plants that grow in water, but here it is flowering under the dry chaparral. The male stamens are on top, and the female pistils are below. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2011)

File.Name: b313a_b310-004.jpg
Notes:
66893751 This is a laid-back little spider perched on the inner circle of its web. It's some kind of Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae). (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2011)

I've been tied to my computer all weekend working on my science fair project about "Fire-follower Plants on San Marcos Pass". (I'm the semi-retired science teacher - but I still want to do my own project!) So far I have a slideshow of 528 photos organized by genus and species of the 50+ fire-follower plants that I have found on San Marcos Pass. I have some writing to do.

In our mountains, every plant is a really fire-follower since all the mountains have burned sometime in the past. But I'm interested in those plants that appear from nowhere after a fire and then disappear after a few years to wait in the seed bank until the next wildfire.

My deadline is next Friday. Expect a few photos of... fire-follower plants until then!

File.Name: b313b_b310-003.jpg
Notes: 66893529 These new flowers of a native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family are starting to open. You wouldn't make bouquets of these flowers, but they do the job for this plant. The flowers will be followed by bright red berries. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2011)

File.Name: b313c_b311-018.jpg
Notes:
66893442 The only new "mushroom" that I found in the woods this weekend is this odd Brown Ribbed Elfin Cup (Helvella acetabulum). (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2011)

There's still moisture in the ground, but I guess the mushrooms' time is done. It's been cold. The insects and flowers are mostly not quite ready. It's a brief quiet time for new photos.

File.Name: b314a_b305-027.jpg
Notes:
66903194 Here is a remarkable walnut-sized gall of a Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. I found this gall on the ground under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the Fagaceae plant family. It feels hard and solid, but there are immature wasps inside. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2011)

My science fair work is going well. I have all my photos printed, but now that I get to the easy stuff, my printer is complaining that it's out of ink. No problem - I have until Friday to get it done!

File.Name: b314b_b310-017.jpg
Notes: 66903138 I believe this is native Cleveland's Malacothrix (Malacothrix clevelandii) in the Asteraceae plant family. Not all of our fire-follower plants are beautiful. This one looks and grows weedy, like a dandelion with branched flower stems. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2011)

File.Name: b314c_b310-026.jpg
Notes:
66903032 Here are lush new fire-follower native grasses of Small-flowered Melic Mellica imperfecta in the Poaceae plant family. These native grasses are still doing well on the site of the Gap Fire a few years ago. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2011)

I know the flowers, but I'm not so good with the grasses in our mountains. This is one I can recognize!

File.Name: b315a_a312-143.jpg
Notes: 66911669 This is a mass of native flowers of Large-flowered Phacelia (Phacelia grandiflora) in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. This is another of the fire-follower plants that flower in great profusion for a few years after a wildfire, and then disappear into the seedbank to wait for the next wildfire. (12 March 2010) My science fair project this year happens to be a photo collection of these "Fire-follower Plants on San Marcos Pass". (I'm the semi-retired science teacher - but I still want to do my own project!) Check the dates on today's photos. I've been pulling good photos from my archives for my slide show. Sorry, no time for new photos this week. When science fair is over, I'll have a new lecture and slide show all ready to go for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society.
