File.Name: b315b_a624-081.jpg
Notes: 66911553 This is a remarkable display of native Sticky Snapdragon (Antirrhinum multiflorum) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. It's another fire-follower plant in our mountains, though this one is growing in a place that was "masticated" - mechanically cleared for a fire break - a few years ago. For some of these fire-followers, it's enough to clear the ground. (24 June 2010)
Locality: Antirrhinum multiflorum) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. It's another fire-follower plant in our mountains

File.Name: b315c_a605-007.jpg
Notes:
66911515 Here is a mass of the native fire-follower Cream-Flowered Eardrops (Dicentra ochroleuca) in the Papaveraceae plant family. The plants are growing in a place that burned several years ago in the Gap Fire. (5 June 2010)

I got this photo last year, and I think they may be all done this year. That's what the fire-followers do. They flower in great profusion for a few years after a wildfire, and then disappear to wait for the next wildfire. It's a specialized adaptation, and the subject of my science fair project that I'm still working on - due Friday!
Locality:
5 June 2010)

I got this photo last year

File.Name: b316a_a404-008.jpg
Notes:
66919710 This is an uncommon Slender-pod Jewelflower (Caulanthus heterophyllus var. pseudosimulans) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's a fancy mustard that I found in the mountains last year. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2010)

This plant is growing on the site of the Gap Wildfire from 2007. I've been looking for it this year in the same place, but I haven't found it yet. That's the way with these native fire-follower plants - they soon disappear.

That's the subject of my DMS science fair project on "Fire-follower Plants on San Marcos Pass". I'm the semi-retired science teacher - but I still want to do my own project. This is my 28th annual science fair project!

My project is almost done. Science fair happens on Friday night. On Saturday, I can look for new photos - if it isn't raining as predicted!

File.Name: b316b_8427-097.jpg
Notes:
66919471 This is native San Luis Blazing Star aka Small-flowered Stickleaf (Mentzelia micrantha) in the Loasaceae plant family. The leaves are instantly recognizable because they feel rough like sandpaper. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2008)

This plant is uncommon, but there were lots of them a few years ago in a place that was "masticated" - mechanically cleared - for a fire break on a north-facing slope. I haven't seen it since then. That's the way with these native fire-follower plants.
CommonNames: San Luis Blazing Star; Small-flowered Stickleaf

File.Name: b316c_8625-142.jpg
Notes:
66919341 I guess these pretty Golden Stars (Bloomeria crocea) in the Liliaceae plant family are fire-follower plants in our mountains. I haven't seen them since June 2008 after a small wildfire along East Camino Cielo road in our mountains. That's the way with our fire-follower plants - they soon disappear to wait for the next wildfire. (San Marcos Pass, 25 June 2008)

I've been digging in my photo archives to find photos of fire-followers I haven't used before. The DMS science fair happens on Friday. I'm almost done with my project on these interesting native flowers.

File.Name: b317a_8622-031.jpg
Notes:
66927225 This is one of our strangest fire-follower plants in the mountains - Cream-Flowered Eardrops (Dicentra ochroleuca) in the Papaveraceae plant family. These flowers always remind me of Holy Rollers having a meeting in the woods with arms raised for glory! (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2008)

They are another one of those curious plants that sprout after wildfires and flower for a few years, and then disappear into the seedbank - not to be seen until the next wildfire. They might be done this year.

Today's photos are more of the fire-follower plants that I've been pulling from the archives for my DMS science fair project - due tomorrow! I don't think I've shown any of these before. My project is just about done. The poster looks great, thanks to Julie. I'll have a photo of it tomorrow.

File.Name: b317b_9413-253.jpg
Notes:
66926973 Here is a beautiful flower head of (I believe) the Hairy Suncup (Camissonia hirtella) in the Onagraceae plant family. The stems keep producing new flowers from the front as they continue to grow. (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2009)

This is another one of those curious plants that sprout after wildfires and flower for a few years, and then disappear into the seedbank - not to be seen until the next wildfire. They are the subject of my DMS science fair project - due tomorrow!

File.Name: b317c_8622-045.jpg
Notes:
66926811 This is a wild mustard, but it's not one of those pesky (but showy) introduced mustards that have taken over California.

This is a native fire-follower mustard -the California Mustard (Thelypodium lasiophyllum) in the Brassicaceae plant family. The plant gets taller than I am. They have tiny flowers, but the mature stems look shaggy, decorated with pendent long seed pods. It's not common. (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2008)

This is another one of those curious plants that sprout after wildfires and flower for a few years, and then disappear into the seedbank - not to be seen until the next wildfire. They are the subject of my DMS science fair project - due tomorrow!

File.Name: b318a_b318-003.jpg
Notes:
66936292 Here is my Science Fair poster hanging in the usual place at Dunn Middle School. Thanks for the help, Julie!

I did my project this year on "Fire-Follower Plants on San Marcos Pass, Santa Barbara County, California". Along with this poster, I had a slide show with 518 photos of interesting fire-follower plants I've taken over the years - most are my fotolog shots. It was a pretty fabulous show. Work done! (Santa Ynez Valley, 18 March 2011)

I admit my project is more display than experiment. I have a beautiful poster and a slide show with hundreds of my photos. I don't really have a question apart from the obvious "What kinds of fire-followers do we have?" - but cataloging is the basis of all natural history, and natural history is surely the basis of all biology. It's good enough!

I've been doing these photo collections for the last 6 years:
* Butterflies - http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/13005722
* Summer Wildflowers - http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/19617957
* Mushrooms - http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/33688224
* Insect Galls - http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/48653938
* Interesting Flies - http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/60450212

Of course I did other projects before - this is my 28th DMS science fair, and I've always done my own project! It's not wasted work. Many of these recent SciFair projects have become my lecture topics for the Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society.

I've been busy, so I look forward to being free this weekend to get out in the woods for new photos! Right now, the weather forecast is not good: a bit of rain tonight, and major rain on Sunday. *Sigh* But late-season rain is always good in this summer-dry part of California, and there's always a chance for a new photo!

File.Name: b319a_b319-019.jpg
Notes:
66942657 It's small world!

These are tiny wasp galls on a catkin of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I'm not sure what kind of wasp lives in these galls, but I'm sure they are in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. The galls are only a few mm long. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2011)

Science Fair is over, but it was a crummy day for new photos - cold, dark, and drizzly most of the day. We had 1/2 inch of rain last night, and real rain is expected tomorrow - the forecast is 5 to 9 inches of rain! That will do some damage. We'll see what happens.

File.Name: b319b_b319-002.jpg
Notes: 66942379 This is a little white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. It seems like it should be on a white flower, but here it is out in the open. Maybe it's waiting for the blackberry flowers to appear? They are late this year. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2011)

File.Name: b319c_b319-006.jpg
Notes:
66942256 Here is a pretty little green caterpillar (Lepidoptera) on a leaf of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family this morning - with raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2011)

My first thought was the illusive Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) caterpillar, but a quick web search proved me wrong. I'm not sure what it is.
ScientificName: Lepidoptera; Symphoricarpos mollis) in the Caprifoliaceae; Hemaris diffinis
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera; Symphoricarpos mollis) in the Caprifoliaceae; Hemaris diffinis

File.Name: b320a_b320-008.jpg
Notes:
66950684 This is the creek that flows by Cold Springs Tavern, swollen with rain today. The trees in the creek are deciduous White Alder (Alnus rhombifolia) in the Betulaceae plant family. I hope they don't get washed away! (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2011)

We've been hammered by an unusual late-season rainstorm today, 9.6 inches (24+ cm) of rain so far! It's another "March Miracle" rain for the First Day of Spring.

Hah - three of us foolish friends wheeled our raft down Angastura Pass road to Gibraltor Dam and rafted the Santa Ynez River down to the first crossing after the "March Miracle" rains of 1991 twenty years ago, when we got almost 20 inches of rain in one week. The river was cranking at least 15,000 cfs. We got to the end after dark to be greeted by the Search and Rescue people that Julie had called, who were wondering what to do about us. All I can say is YAHOO! (And NEVER AGAIN!)

It's still raining, though it seems to be easing off now. Our annual rainfall is now over 40 inches. It's a great start to the dry summer - native plants and butterflies will be happy!

File.Name: b320b_b320-002.jpg
Notes: 66950257 The waterfall along our driveway rarely flows, but here it is! It falls about 20 feet total, though this photo only shows part of it. It's a small drainage and it takes a lot of heavy rain to get it working. We've had 9+ inches (23+ cm) of rain so far from this late-season storm! (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2011)

File.Name: b320c_b320-014.jpg
Notes:
66950171 Our only immediate damage from the heavy rains is this small mudslide along our road in the canyon, though the creek at one point came across our bridge and almost buried our water pump. I'll be digging mud tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2011)

The downside is that this slide took out a colony of native Larkspur (Delphinium patens?) in the Ranunculaceae plant family.

That little Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family above the slide is hanging on. It seems to be flowering.
ScientificName: Delphinium patens; Ranunculaceae; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Delphinium patens; Ranunculaceae; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae

File.Name: b320d_b320-WCIR.jpg
Notes:
66949911 We've been hammered today by an unusual late-season rainstorm, 9+ inches (23+ cm) of rain so far!

This is the NOAA weather satellite image at 1:00 PM today. You can see the storm center sitting off the coast of San Francisco pulling moist air counter-clockwise right to us at the bump in California. Our winter storms come from the North, but they pull up moisture from the South - and this storm has a deep tail that extends almost out to Hawaii! That gives us good "orographic enhancement" as the rain meets our east-west mountains head-on. It's still raining hard. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2011)

File.Name: b321a_b319-001.jpg
Notes:
66959944 With all the rain, I almost forgot that yesterday was the vernal equinox - the first day of Spring!

These are new umbrella-like leaves of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the Portulacaceae) plant family in the garden - with raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2011)

File.Name: b321b_b321-037.jpg
Notes:
66959785 It was almost dark tonight, when I noticed this cool reflection in my almost full rain gauge. I grabbed my camera, but I had to do serious white balance correction. The exposure was 30 seconds @ f/11 @ ISO 200! (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2011)

This is my home "weather station" - a thermometer and a rain guage. I've always used a coffee can for my rain guage, but Julie got me this fancy one for Christmas, and it is pretty nice. It's almost full, with the 10.8 inches (27+ cm) of rain we've had over the weekend. Imagine all that rain falling at once!

File.Name: b321c_b321-028.jpg
Notes:
66959339 We have a river again!

This is the Santa Ynez River down in the valley to the north of us, on the one-lane road near Manzanita Lane at Paradise. It crested last night at 23,000 cfs (that's "cubic feet per second"), but the river drops fast when the rain stops. It looked raftable - but watch out for sweepers and strainers! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 21 March 2011)

We were hammered by an unusual late-season rainstorm yesterday, with 10.8 inches (27+ cm) of rain over the weekend. I tried to get to the river yesterday, but I got turned around at the first campground. Road washed out? Nah, it was just a creek flowing over the road, no big deal.
Locality: 27+ cm) of rain over the weekend.  I tried to get to the river yesterday

File.Name: b322a_b322-006.jpg
Notes:
66967713 This isn't a fungus or a stalked insect egg - it's another wasp gall growing on a new leaf of deciduous Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii) in the Fagaceae plant family growing near Lake Cachuma. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 22 March 2011)

I believe this is the strange gall of the "Hair Stalk Gall Wasp" (Dros pedicellatum) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera.

This means that the structure is actually part of the oak tree, not something attached to the tree. Look close and you can see that the gall stem is the extension of a leaf vein.

My book by Ron Russo on Plant Galls of California and other Western States (2006) suggests that this is "the bisexual generation of an as yet undescribed unisexual, summer-fall generation with it's own distinct gall." Galls are complicated like that!
Locality: 2006) suggests that this is "the bisexual generation of an as yet undescribed unisexual
CommonNames: wasp gall; Blue Oak; Hair Stalk Gall Wasp; Plant Galls of California and other Western States
ScientificName: Quercus douglasii; Fagaceae; Dros pedicellatum; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus douglasii; Fagaceae; Dros pedicellatum; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: b322b_b322-013.jpg
Notes:
66967449 These sure look like Western Box Elder Bugs (family Rhopalidae in the Hemiptera), but if so they're lost! The closest Box Elder (Acer negundo var. californicum) trees are about 10 miles west of here, along the river beyond the town of Buellton.

These bugs are going at it on a new leaf of deciduous Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii) in the Fagaceae plant family growing near Lake Cachuma. I guess that's the female on top with her larger abdomen full of eggs. Look how her hind legs are blurred - she is stroking her partner with those legs. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 22 March 2011)
CommonNames: Western Box Elder Bugs; Box Elder; Blue Oak
ScientificName: Rhopalidae; Hemiptera; Acer negundo; californicum; Quercus douglasii; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Rhopalidae; Hemiptera; Acer negundo; californicum; Quercus douglasii; Fagaceae

File.Name: b322c_b322-003.jpg
Notes:
66967139 I believe these are our smallest native flowering plants getting ready to flower. They are tiny succulent Sand Pygmyweed (Crassula connata) plants in the Crassulaceae plant family. I put my pen in the photo for scale. The ball of my pen is 0.7 mm - so the flowers are about that size! My flora says they are "common, when looked for" - which probably isn't very often! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 22 March 2011)

I got this photo in the chaparral by Lake Cachuma at my usual stop on the way to school today.

File.Name: b323a_b319-012.jpg
Notes:
66975275 These are new flowers of the Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) in the Fabaceae family growing in the canyon. The common name is a mystery to me, but the species name makes sense, because most leaves are "truncate" - square on the ends. I prefer the name "Blunt-leaved Lupine". (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2011)

Today's photos are all lupines. They all have pea-flowers and hand-shaped "palmate" leaves - like holding your hand open with fingers extended. We have many different species that are starting to flower.

We had another 1/2 inch of cold rain today. It was just above freezing when I went off to school this morning, but snow didn't happen. Even I am ready to say we've had enough rain! But more rain is in the forecast through the weekend.

File.Name: b323b_b319-016.jpg
Notes:
66974951 Here are beautiful hairy lupine leaves holding raindrops.

Ah, but what kind of lupine? I thought I knew all our local lupines, but this one has me stumped. It's small, growing along the road in our canyon. It almost looks like the Bajada Lupine (Lupinus concinnus) in the Fabaceae plant family with those hairy leaves, but that would be surprising here by the coast. It's not flowering yet, so I'll get back to it soon. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2011)

File.Name: b323c_b319-017.jpg
Notes: 66974720 These are the first new flowers of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family growing in our canyon. Like all lupines, they have hand-shaped "palmate" leaves - like holding your hand open with fingers extended. We have many different species. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2011)

File.Name: b324a_b319-029.jpg
Notes:
66983661 This is an impressive spider nest under the eaves of our house. I'm not sure if the fine webbing of the nest will come through in this re-sampled 500 pixel copy. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2011)

I'm sure it's a bottlecap-size Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae). I have a photo of the beast at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/57903308

We've had another day of cold rain - about 1/2 inch so far, and its still raining. The temperature was mostly in the 30s (Fahrenheit) today. The new flowers have taken a beating from the rain and cold lately, and so has my archive of new photos! *Sigh* - more rain is expected on Saturday.
Locality: Fahrenheit) today.  The new flowers have taken a beating from the rain and cold lately

File.Name: b324b_b322-004.jpg
Notes:
66983409 These are beautiful "umbels" of Sanicle flowers in the Apiaceae plant family. Judging from the leaves, I guess it's another Pacific Sanicle (Sanicula crassicaulis), but we have many similar species of this genus. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 22 March 2011)

You have to remember not to over-expose these yellow flower clusters. I got this photo at -2/3 stop.

File.Name: b324c_b319-018.jpg
Notes:
66983243 Here are beautiful "flowers" - really staminate catkins - of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. Sometimes the catkins are yellow, other times they have more red. The combination of the colors in the woods is gorgeous! (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2011)

Does anyone else remember the "catkin scene" in Ken Russell's "Women in Love" (1969) movie?

File.Name: b325a_b325-082.jpg
Notes:
66991154 This is a handsome little Weevil beetle (Curculionidae) in the order Coleoptera on a furry leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae) plant family. The beetle is only 2 or 3 mm long. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2011)

When you read that "almost 25% of all known life-forms" are beetles (Wikipedia), most of them are tiny, especially the largest taxonomic family which is the Curculionidae.

We had 2.3 inches of rain yesterday, bringing our rain-year total to 45+ inches. Not bad for an expected dry "La NiÒa" year! We have one more small storm expected tomorrow, then the weather should turn warmer and dry. That's good timing because I'll be off on Monday with the Dunn Middle School 7th grade kids to the Pinnacles National Monument for an adventure!
CommonNames: Weevil; Wild Sweetpea; Pinnacles National Monument
ScientificName: Curculionidae; Coleoptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Curculionidae
associatedTaxa: Curculionidae; Coleoptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Curculionidae

File.Name: b325b_b325-011.jpg
Notes:
66990767 This is a new moth-like Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly perched on the weedy Brome grasses (genus Bromus) that they seems to prefer. It will be well camouflaged in the summer when the grasses turn brown, but it stands out now. It's a real butterfly, with knobby antennae and soccer-ball eyes. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 25 March 2011)

Soon the introduced annual grasses will start to release their stickery seeds that ruin my socks and take the joy out of being in the woods. That's my definition of the beginning of summer.

File.Name: b325c_b325-001.jpg
Notes:
66990557 These are beautiful Old-Fashioned Bleeding-Heart (Dicentra spectabilis aka Lamprocapnos spectabilis) flowers in the garden this morning - with raindrops. They are in the Fumariaceae plant family, or the subfamily Fumarioideae of the family Papaveraceae depending on your source. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2011)

The gophers love them! We're trying to grow them in pots this year. So far, so good.
ScientificName: Dicentra spectabilis; Lamprocapnos spectabilis; Fumariaceae; Fumarioideae; Papaveraceae
associatedTaxa: Dicentra spectabilis; Lamprocapnos spectabilis; Fumariaceae; Fumarioideae; Papaveraceae

File.Name: b326a_b326-023.jpg
Notes:
66998031 Here is a male March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera) spread-eagled on leaves of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae) plant family today. I believe this is genus Bibio because of the sharp spurs on the front tibia. It was seeking shelter from the drizzle today. I dig those large hairy eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2011)

More rain today. Only 1/10 inch so far, but it's been dark and drizzly all day - unlike the 10+ inches we got a week ago! The weather is forecast to turn warm and dry next week, just in time for my trip with Dunn Middle School kids to the Pinnacles National Monument!
CommonNames: March Fly; Wild Sweetpea; Pinnacles National Monument
ScientificName: Bibionidae; Diptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Bibio
associatedTaxa: Bibionidae; Diptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Bibio

File.Name: b326b_b326-010.jpg
Notes:
66997849 This is an impressive long-legged Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae) perched on a leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods this morning before it started raining. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2011)

Judging from its web, this is a "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa), but it has unusually large chelicerae (jaws). It also has swollen pedipalps - it must be thinking about mating. (Aren't we all!)
CommonNames: Orb-Weaver Spider; Coast Live Oak; Garbage Line Spider
ScientificName: Araneidae; Quercus agrifolia; Cyclosa; chelicerae; pedipalps
associatedTaxa: Araneidae; Quercus agrifolia; Cyclosa; chelicerae; pedipalps

File.Name: b326c_b326-028.jpg
Notes:
66997636 The native Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) are herding the Aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) on the almost-flowering Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae) plant family in front of our house. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2011)

It was dark and breezy today as more rain moves in, so I had to bump up my ISO to 800 for this photo. It sure looks like that ant on top is communicating with the ant on the right. Heh - I think that ant on the right has a bubble of aphid-nectar in its mouth!
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: b327a_b327-015.jpg
Notes:
67006446 Here is a beautiful golden Beetle (order Coleoptera) that I noticed today on a leaf of Bedstraw (Galium aparine) on the wild side of our garden.

It looked soft-bodied, and my first thought was Cantharidae, but looking at the photos at BugGuide, it may be an unusual Meloidae (in the genus Nemognatha?) See:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/219885

I'm heading off on adventures for a week! First I'm off tomorrow with the Dunn Middle School 7th grade to the Pinnacles National Monument. I'll leave that trip a bit early (on Wednesday) to go with Julie on a "Death Valley Adventure" with her "Channel Islands Restoration" non-profit with geologist Tanya Atwater and botanist Steve Junak as our guides! See:
   http://www.cirweb.org/dv/

I'll be back in a week and a few days. We might be delayed by desert charms!
Locality:
Galium aparine) on the wild side of our garden.

It looked soft-bodied
ScientificName: Coleoptera; Galium aparine; Cantharidae; Meloidae; Nemognatha
associatedTaxa: Coleoptera; Galium aparine; Cantharidae; Meloidae; Nemognatha

File.Name: b327b_b326-021.jpg
Notes:
67006054 This is a humble Wood Louse - suborder Oniscidea of the order Isopoda of the subphylum Crustacea. It's one of our few terrestrial crustaceans. Usually they hide during the day, but this one ventured out onto a stalk of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae) plant family on a gloomy day. It looks (and acts) like a little armored vehicle - but it can't roll up into a pill! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2011)

The lumpy dark eyes of this critter look just like the eyes of a millipede in the subphylum Myriapoda. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/57940057
ScientificName: Wood Louse; Oniscidea; Isopoda; Crustacea; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Myriapoda
associatedTaxa: Wood Louse; Oniscidea; Isopoda; Crustacea; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Myriapoda

File.Name: b327c-b326_019.jpg
Notes: 67005688 This is a beautiful "fiddlehead" of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta) in the woods near home. It looks like it's still being unwrapped. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2011)

File.Name: b327d_b325-013.jpg
Notes:
67005544 This is a shy Owl's Clover (Castilleja) flower in the Scrophulariaceae plant family hiding in the grasses near Lake Cachuma. We have several species, but I think this white flower might be Castilleja attenuata. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 25 March 2011)

The flower has a perfect "Alice the Goon" face from E.C. Segar's classic Popeye comics!

File.Name: b327e_b327-008.jpg
Notes: 67005237 These native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plants in the Scrophulariaceae plant family are still going strong, in fact they're just starting to flower along West Camino Cielo road across the mountains. It's a fire-follower - one of those curious plants that flowers for a few years after a wildfire, and then disappears. It's also a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family in this place. It must have a fungal connection with its host plant. Beautiful! (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2011)
ScientificName: Pedicularis densiflora; Scrophulariaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Pedicularis densiflora; Scrophulariaceae; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae

File.Name: b327f-b326_008.jpg
Notes: 67005093 This is "The Blusher" - a well-named blushing Amanita rubescens mushroom in the woods. This one is a little the worse for wear after our heavy rains this week. It was just about the only mushroom I found in the woods, apart from "weedy" species like Psathyrella and Coprinus. Despite the saturated wet soil, I guess the mushrooms are done for the year. How do they know? (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2011)

File.Name: b406a_b405-023.jpg
Notes:
67082153 This perfect Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is perched on a flower of some desert Phacelia species - maybe Phacelia crenulata? Look close - the spider is sucking its Diptera prey, but it also has a stash of old prey! (Trona Pinnacles, 5 April 2011)

It's been a busy week with trips to the Pinnacles National Monument and Death Valley National Park. I have hundreds of new photos.

After 100 degree days in Death Valley, it was refreshing to return home to the coastal gloom. There's a chance of rain tomorrow!
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Pinnacles National Monument; Death Valley National Park

File.Name: b406b_b405-013.jpg
Notes: 67082055 This is a tiny Western Pygmy Blue (Brephidium exile) butterfly resting on a male-flowering branch of Desert Holly (Atriplex hymenelytra) in the Chenopodiaceae plant family. It's the smallest butterfly in North America, barely the size of my thumbnail. (Trona Pinnacles, 5 April 2011)

File.Name: b406c_b404-106.jpg
Notes:
67081974 We camped at the Trona Pinnacles on our way to and from Death Valley National Park. These are tufa spires made of porous calcium carbonate rock formed as a deposit from springs when a lake covered the now-dry bed of the Searles Lake basin. The towers are as high as 140 feet (43 m). It's a strange and beautiful landscape. (Trona Pinnacles, 4 April 2011)

Those are mostly Desert Dandelion (Malacothrix glabrata) and Desert Gold (Geraea canescens) flowers in the Asteraceae plant family in the foreground.
CommonNames: Trona Pinnacles; Desert Dandelion; Desert Gold
ScientificName: tufa; Malacothrix glabrata; Geraea canescens; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: tufa; Malacothrix glabrata; Geraea canescens; Asteraceae

File.Name: b406d_b402-066.jpg
Notes: 67081481 This is a young Chuckawalla (Sauromalus obesus) lizard. It's shedding its skin and feeling cranky. They lose their spots and striped tail as they mature. The species name is appropriate. These potbellied lizards like to crawl into cracks in the rocks and inflate so you can't pull them out. (Titus Canyon, Death Valley, 2 April 2011)

File.Name: b406e_b402-024.jpg
Notes: 67081401 I guess this is a pretty Common Side-blotched Lizard (Uta stansburiana) in the Death Valley desert. They are not so pretty back home on the coast! (Titus Canyon, Death Valley, 2 April 2011)

File.Name: b406f_b401-077.jpg
Notes:
67081224 Here are Treebeard and entwife Julie standing on Aguereberry Point high in the Panamint Mountains with Death Valley behind us. (Aguereberry Point, Death Valley, 1 April 2011)

It's been a busy week with trips to the Pinnacles National Monument and Death Valley National Park. I have hundreds of new photos. So what did I do when I got home? I took a walk in the woods and got a hundred new photos!
CommonNames: Pinnacles National Monument; Death Valley National Park

File.Name: b407a_b404-046.jpg
Notes:
67088362 Here is a beautiful new flower of native Beavertail Cactus (Opuntia basilaris) in the Cactaceae plant family. The beetles sure like the flower, and so do I! (Trona-Wildrose road, 4 April 2011)

We did get some cold rain and hail at home today. The temperature is near freezing, and we might actually get some snow tonight or tomorrow - in April!

File.Name: b407b_b402-085.jpg
Notes: 67088086 This is a ghost-like Rock Nettle (Eucnide urens) flower in the Loasaceae plant family. It's a native Blazing Star bush with stinging hairs on its sandpaper-like leaves and large almost-white pale flowers. The beetles like it! (Titus Canyon, Death Valley, 2 April 2011)

File.Name: b407c_b401-063.jpg
Notes:
67087902 Here is the youngest member of our group posing next to a remarkable flowering native Panamint Daisy (Enceliopsis covillei) in the Asteraceae plant family. This plant is considered a "class II endemic" - with a majority of its range in Death Valley National Park. It's growing in the dry wash along the Wildrose Road from the Panamint Valley into the mountains. (Wildrose Canyon wash, Death Valley, 1 April 2011)

I give my thanks to our botanist Steve Junak for spotting this uncommon wildflower as we drove by! The flowers are as much as 6 inches across. They are long-stemmed, like wild Gerbera daisies.

File.Name: b407d_b405-071.jpg
Notes:
67087505 No, these aren't the poppy fields of Oz - but the Antelope Valley California Poppy State Reserve must be the next best thing! (along Highway 138, 5 April 2011)

This display of wildflowers is fantastic, though there really isn't much diversity. There are mostly orange California Poppies (Eschscholzia californica) and yellow Goldfields (Lasthenia californica), along with a scattering of Purple Owl's Clover (Castilleja exserta) and small Lupines (Lupinus bicolor).

We didn't actually visit the preserve. We just pulled off onto a dirt easement road under some power lines. It was very windy. The flowers combine like the surprising brush strokes of color in an impressionist painting. There aren't enough pixels in this Fotolog photo to show them all!
CommonNames: California Poppies; Goldfields; Purple Owl's Clover; Lupines
ScientificName: Antelope Valley California Poppy State Reserve; Eschscholzia californica; Lasthenia californica; Castilleja exserta; Lupinus bicolor
associatedTaxa: Antelope Valley California Poppy State Reserve; Eschscholzia californica; Lasthenia californica; Castilleja exserta; Lupinus bicolor

File.Name: b407e_b403-060.jpg
Notes:
67087115 A few of us went hiking into the badlands below Zabriskie Point in Death Valley. We hiked a few miles to our pickup at the mouth of Golden Canyon, and of course I lugged along my tripod. That striking peak in the background is Manley Beacon. (Zabriskie Point, Death Valley, 3 April 2011)

From Wikipedia, I learn that the philosopher Michel Foucault called his 1975 acid trip at Zabriskie Point the greatest experience of his life. I can see why! And there is a famous scene in the obscure movie "Zabriskie Point" by Michelangelo Antonioni (1970).

File.Name: b407f_b404-127.jpg
Notes: 67086843 Here is the first sliver of the new moon setting just behind the sun as it got dark, with one of the Trona pinnacles in the foreground. The exposure was 2 seconds @ f/14 @ ISO 200. (Trona Pinnacles, 4 April 2011)

File.Name: b408a_b405-035.jpg
Notes:
67094747 This is a fantastic flower of the Gravel Ghost (Atrichoseris playphylla) in the Asteraceae plant family. The leaves are roundish in shape, and lay flat on the ground. The naked flowering stems rise 1-3 feet above the leaves with white flowers which seem unconnected to the leaves - hence the name. That filagree of violet on the inner florets is wonderful! (Trona Pinnacles, 5 April 2011)

Today's photos are more of the wildflowers I found on our recent Death Valley trip. I have been trying to get new photos at home on San Marcos Pass, but we had a bit of real corn snow today! It's been unseasonably cold for April, and local wildflowers and insects are mostly on hold. I hope it warms up soon.

File.Name: b408b_b402-089.jpg
Notes: 67094573 This is a wonderful flower of Desert Five-Spot (Eremalche rotundifolia) in the Malvaceae plant family. It is such a beautiful flower on such a beat-up looking plant! This plant is only an inch or two high, growing on a rocky alluvial plain. (Titus Canyon, Death Valley, 2 April 2011)

File.Name: b408c_b402-035.jpg
Notes: 67094419 These are tiny flowers of the Little Gold Poppy (Eschscholzia minutiflora) in the Papaveraceae plant family. It looks just like a California Poppy (Eschscholzia californica), but the flowers are tiny - the petals are only about 1/4 inch long. It's another "belly plant", since you have to get down on your belly to appreciate it! (Titus Canyon, Death Valley, 2 April 2011)

File.Name: b408d_b403-032.jpg
Notes: 67094263 This tiny Purple Mat (Nama demissum) plant with flowers in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family is barely an inch high! It's growing in the gravel at the high Dante's View overlook of Death Valley. It's a "belly plant" for sure, since you have to get down on your belly to appreciate it! (Dante's View, Death Valley, 3 April 2011)

File.Name: b408e_b402-070.jpg
Notes: 67094142 These are pretty yellow flowers of the Lesser Mohavea (Mohavea breviflora) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. I don't know anything about it, except that it grows in rocky canyons. If you look very close, you might see a little Thread-legged Bug(?) peering out from the center! (Titus Canyon, Death Valley, 2 April 2011)

File.Name: b408f_b404_038.jpg
Notes: 67094018 I believe these are flowers of the Mojave Indigo Bush (Psorothamnus arborescens) in the Fabaceae) plant family - but cp. Psorothamnus fremontii. One of my books says the plants were a source of dyes for desert-dwelling Native Americans. With that intense color, I believe it. (Trona-Wildrose road, 4 April 2011)

File.Name: b409a_b405-015.jpg
Notes: 67099176 This is another "belly flower" since you have to get down on your belly to appreciate it! I believe it's a Desert Star (Monopilon bellioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. The pretty little flowers are white with a yellow center, but the hairy leaves are just as beautiful. The flower on the right has a bright red mite that's visible. (Trona Pinnacles, 5 April 2011)

File.Name: b409b_b402-061.jpg
Notes: 67099142 We drive past endless miles and miles of dull Creosote Bush (Larrea tridentata) in the unusual Zygophyllaceae plant family on our way to Death Valley, but the flowers and fuzzy seed pods are really quite beautiful. This humble plant is the staff of life for many desert critters. (Furnace Creek campground, Death Valley, 4 April 2011)

File.Name: b409c_b402-080.jpg
Notes:
67099109 This is a different white composite in the desert - Desert Chicory (Rafinesquia neomexicana) in the Asteraceae plant family. (Trona Pinnacles, 5 April 2011)

The flowers are similar to the California Chicory (Rafinesquia californica) back at home. See my photo from 5 June 2008:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/36182871

File.Name: b410a_b404-082.jpg
Notes:
67099383 Here is a little moth hiding in a newly opened flower of (I think) Snake's Head (Malacothrix coulteri) in the Asteraceae plant family. I might be wrong about the flower - maybe it's the Desert Dandelion (Malacothrix glabrata). It's hard to tell these flowers apart without seeing the phyllaries. (Trona Pinnacles, 5 April 2011)

I'm glad Fotolog is back in action after two days down. I still have 100s of Death Valley photos and I haven't even started on my Pinnacles photos! On the other hand, it's finally starting to warm up after a bit of snow on Friday, and I've been seeing some new insects at home. What a strange rain year this has been - with rainy "bookends" in October and December and March, and dry weather in between.

File.Name: b410b_b404-002.jpg
Notes: 67099292 Here is a beautiful Desert Mallow aka Apricot Mallow (Spaeralcea ambigua) in the Malvaceae) plant family growing in a desert canyon. The color of this flower is spectacular! (Titus Canyon, Death Valley, 2 April 2011)

File.Name: b410c_b405-006.jpg
Notes: 67099260 This is beautiful Death Valley Sage (Salvia funerea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. The fuzzy leaves are holly-like, and have a pleasant fragrance when crushed. It's considered a "class II endemic" - with a majority of its range within Death Valley National Park. (Titus Canyon, Death Valley, 2 April 2011)

File.Name: b411a_b411-008.jpg
Notes:
67103005 This is a beautiful newly-emerged Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working new flowers of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. The flowers have a deep-throat and seem made for these butterflies. You can see some pollen on its proboscis. Thus pollination happens. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2011)

It feels like Spring is finally settling in. It's about time - we had a bit of snow a few days ago! I'll save the rest of my Death Valley and Pinnacles photos for the busy times ahead when I need a break.

File.Name: b411b_b411-012.jpg
Notes:
67102863 I believe this is a Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly showing her beautiful upper wings for a change. I reckon it's a lady because of the brown on her wings, but I might be wrong. She's perched on a twig of Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum), but she was really more interested in the still-flowering Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa) in the Ericaceae plant family growing nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2011)

I have photos showing the plain underside of the wings. See:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66331551
ScientificName: Celastrina ladon; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Celastrina ladon; Adenostoma fasciculatum; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; Ericaceae

File.Name: b411c_b406-010.jpg
Notes: 67102597 These are a pair of little Moths (Lepidoptera) mating on a blade of grass in the woods a few days ago. I reckon that's the female on top with the large abdomen full of eggs. They appear quite dead, but maybe it's just post-coital depression aka la petite mort? I have no idea what kind of moths they are. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2011)

File.Name: b412a_b410-035.jpg
Notes:
67111121 Here is a little pencil-erasor sized Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the edge of our garden. For some reason, they like this plant. If you look close, you can see the remains of its tiny breakfast fly-prey between its furry pedipalps. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2011)

When she saw this photo, Julie said - "Oh, it's so cute and fuzzy!" Even more remarkable, they make eye contact with their large front eyes. It's strange to make personal eye contact with any critter this small, but I'm sure the spider was looking right at me and was aware of my presence.

File.Name: b412b_b410-027.jpg
Notes:
67110945 This is the business end of a slender Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera in the insect order Odonata) perched on a sandstone rock in the canyon. Look close and you can see the remains of its tiny breakfast fly-prey below it's whiskered face. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2011)

There are more remains to the right of it's front-right leg. It's true that they come back to the same places. To get the photo, focus on where it's been and wait, and it will probably come back to the same place!

File.Name: b412c_b408-009.jpg
Notes:
67110746 I've been busy at BugGuide.com, and I believe this is a Western Striped Cucumber Beetle (Acalymma trivittatum) on (of course) Wild Cucumber (Marah fabaceus) in the Cucurbitaceae plant family. It's in the family of Leaf Beetles (Chrysomelidae) of the order Coleoptera. See the BugGuide.com page at:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/334245

They are pretty beetles. Maybe they can do some damage to domestic plants, but they are no problem as long as they stick to the wild plants. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2011)
CommonNames: Western Striped Cucumber Beetle; Wild Cucumber; Leaf Beetles
ScientificName: Acalymma trivittatum; Marah fabaceus; Cucurbitaceae; Chrysomelidae; Coleoptera
associatedTaxa: Acalymma trivittatum; Marah fabaceus; Cucurbitaceae; Chrysomelidae; Coleoptera

File.Name: b413a_b411-015.jpg
Notes: 67118777 This is a furry Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera - I think genus Bombylius - perched on decomposing sandstone soil. No it's not sticking its tongue out at me. That black thing is its proboscis for sucking nectar from flowers, and it's not retractable. This photo has desert colors, but I took it near home, not in Death Valley. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2011)

File.Name: b413b_b411-021.jpg
Notes:
67118650 Here is a mass of aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) on a gone-to-flower Kale (Brassica oleracea) plant in the garden. I believe they may be the Mealy Cabbage Aphid (Brevicoryne brassicae). See the info at BugGuide.com:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/343017

It's always fun to look for critters that aren't aphids in the aphid masses! I believe that is the larva of a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) in the upper-center of my photo. Notice that cleared patch on the stem below the critter - it's not an accident! If you look very close, you can see the outline of an aphid that once stood there, like a chalk outline at a police homicide scene. (San Marcos Pass, 11 April 2011)
CommonNames: aphids; Kale; Mealy Cabbage Aphid; Syrphid Flower Fly
ScientificName: Aphididae; Homoptera; Brassica oleracea; Brevicoryne brassicae; aren't; Syrphidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Aphididae; Homoptera; Brassica oleracea; Brevicoryne brassicae; aren't; Syrphidae; Diptera

File.Name: b413c_b406-002.jpg
Notes: 67118543 Here is a Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) trying to get warm on a cold day. It didn't want to budge, so it let me get close with my camera. It's showing its wonderful mating colors. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2011)

File.Name: b414a_b330-023.jpg
Notes:
67124856 This is a pretty flower cluster of Fremont's Star Lily (Zigadenus fremontii var. fremontii) in the Liliaceae plant family.

Now look closer at the left-most petal on the lower right flower. That's a tiny Tick (superfamily Ixodoidea of the Arachnida) just waiting for some critter like you or me to walk by and brush against it, so it can attach itself and suck some blood!

I got this photo at Pinnacles National Monument on our Dunn Middle School 7th grade camping trip two week ago. Great trip. I got "ticked" twice on this trip. It's no big deal - just pull them out ASAP. I have many photos which I'll post some day. (Pinnacles National Monument, 30 March 2011)
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Tick; Pinnacles National Monument
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; fremontii; Liliaceae; Ixodoidea; Arachnida
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; fremontii; Liliaceae; Ixodoidea; Arachnida

File.Name: b414b_b410-014.jpg
Notes:
67124646 This is the beautiful caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in the woods. It's munching on a leaf of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) shrub in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. As far as I know, they only feed on these scrophs. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2011)

The population of these once-common butterflies crashed last year. I hope they recover soon, but I've seen very few caterpillars this year - especially on the Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) shrubs that they usually prefer. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/21985505
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Bush Monkey Flower; Climbing Penstemon
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; scrophs; Keckiella cordifolia
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae; scrophs; Keckiella cordifolia

File.Name: b414c_b410-010.jpg
Notes: 67124347 I don't know what this shy caterpillar will become, but it's definitely a larval Lepidoptera hiding on a leaf cluster of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae) plant family. This shrub is usually the domain of the Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly caterpillars (see my [Next] photo), but they are in short supply this year. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2011)
ScientificName: Lepidoptera hiding on a leaf cluster of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona
associatedTaxa: Lepidoptera hiding on a leaf cluster of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia; Scrophulariaceae; Euphydryas chalcedona

File.Name: b415a_b415-003.jpg
Notes: 67131532 Here is another perfect Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly working a new flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family on the wild edge of our garden this morning. It must be newly-emerged. It's so beautiful with those blue spots like stars against the dark night of its wings! (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2011)

File.Name: b415b_b406-003.jpg
Notes:
67131431 There are still a few mushrooms in the woods, even though it's finally drying out for summer. These are small dark-spored Inky Cap (Coprinus) mushrooms growing on old wood chips along our road. They are too thin to think about eating. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2011)

These are what I think of as "ephemeral mushrooms" - the little decomposers that come and go. The more interesting mushrooms are mostly mycorrhizal - the mushrooms like Amanita and Boletus that grow in association with forest trees. They seem to be done for the year.

File.Name: b415c_b412-006.jpg
Notes:
67131238 Here is another Fremont's Star Lily (Zigadenus fremontii), but it looks subtly different from the lily in my [Previous] photo. It's taller, and the glands on the petals are greenish rather than yellowish. (And it doesn't have a Tick!)

I believe this is Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus, even though I found it in the chaparral near Lake Cachuma and not on top of the mountains where it belongs. I've also found the species - Z. fremontii var. fremontii here. These little differences keep botany interesting, and they may be a sign of evolution in action. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 12 April 2011)
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Z. fremontii; fremontii
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Z. fremontii; fremontii

File.Name: b416a_b416-010.jpg
Notes:
67137360 Here is a furry Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera - I think genus Bombylius - tiptoeing over a new flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family on the wild edge of our garden this morning. Its wings are beating so fast that you can't see the wing closest to my camera! (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2011)

It was breezy today, but beautiful! Sunny and clear. Maybe it's really spring now?
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Bombylius; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Bombylius; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: b416b_b416-036.jpg
Notes:
67137238 This is an interesting predator Fly (order Diptera) with its prey. It's perched on a a flower head of English Plantain (Plantago lanceolata) in the Plantaginaceae plant family. I guess this is a Dance Fly in the family Empididae. It is a predator fly near the creek. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2011)

Now look at my photo from 15 February 2011:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66657787

Are these the same fly? It sure seems like the fly in my new photo is dusted with pollen. I guess they are the same - both predators and plant feeders.
ScientificName: Diptera; Plantago lanceolata; Plantaginaceae; Empididae; and
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Plantago lanceolata; Plantaginaceae; Empididae; and

File.Name: b416c_b416-032.jpg
Notes:
67137059 My new butterfly today is this little Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus). It's a hairstreak butterfly without tails, though it still rubs its hind wings together like any other hairstreak. It's trying to hide in the green annual weeds along the road by our creek. It looks brown from a distance, but up close it looks more like a pointillist painting by Georges Seurat - many color swatches (including violet) that combine to make an eye-catching brown. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2011)

Now look closer. It has a grayish patch on the lower part of its hind wing. Maybe it's a Moss' Elfin (Callophyrs mossii)? There are Stonecrops (plant family Crassulaceae) nearby.

I saw many other new butterflies today, but couldn't get the photos: Sara Orange Tip, Common Buckeye, Lorquin's Admiral - even a California Dogface!
Locality:
plant family Crassulaceae) nearby.

I saw many other new butterflies today
ScientificName: Callophrys augustinus; pointillist; Callophyrs mossii; Crassulaceae
associatedTaxa: Callophrys augustinus; pointillist; Callophyrs mossii; Crassulaceae

File.Name: b417a_b417-037.jpg
Notes:
67143769 What can I say? This is a newly-emerged Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly working newly opened flowers of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. Beautiful. This butterfly has clown colors! (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2011)

This butterfly has soccor ball eyes! I'm sure this is an optical effect, not a pigment. I reckon the spots are a diffraction pattern of light reflecting from the thousands of six-sided ommatidia or "simple eyes" that make up the butterfly's compound eyes like a retina.

I found several new butterflies today, but the one I most wanted to capture eluded me - the brilliant green Bramble Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetorum) ovipositing on Deerweed (Lotus scoparius). Time after time, I got camera and tripod ready and... she flew away. I'll try again tomorrow.
CommonNames: Variable Checkerspot; Sugar Bush; Bramble Hairstreak; Deerweed
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; ommatidia; Callophrys dumetorum; Lotus scoparius
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Rhus ovata; Anacardiaceae; ommatidia; Callophrys dumetorum; Lotus scoparius

File.Name: b417b_b417-048.jpg
Notes:
67143584 This is a female Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly (order Lepidoptera) working a flower of Wild Mustard - probably non-native Black Mustard (Brassica nigra) in the Brassicaceae plant family. You can see a bit of the blue on her upper wing. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2011)

She might be faking it to draw me away. A few minutes before, she was ovipositing (laying eggs) on a nearby Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family.
CommonNames: Silvery Blue; Wild Mustard; Black Mustard; Deerweed
ScientificName: Glaucopsyche lygdamus; Lepidoptera; Brassica nigra; Brassicaceae; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae

File.Name: b417c_b417-035.jpg
Notes: 67143440 I believe this is a Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly perched on foliage of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae) plant family. It's definitely a butterfly - look at those knobby antennae. It is very similar to the Funereal Duskywing (Erynnis funeralis) butterfly with those white fringes, but it's not so black. I love these sad names, both Latin and common! (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2011)
CommonNames: Mournful Duskywing; Climbing Penstemon; Funereal Duskywing

File.Name: b418a_b418-013.jpg
Notes:
67157293 I was walking in the woods, and a little Dark-eyed Junco (Junco hyemalis oreganus) aka "Oregon Junco" bird jumped out right in front of me. I know that signal! I looked on the ground and found her nest with 4(!) eggs.

I was careful not to touch anything as I got my photo, and I waited for a while and saw her return to the nest. As common as birds are in the woods, I don't stumble on their nests very often. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2011)

File.Name: b418b_b417-007.jpg
Notes: 67157079 This is a beautiful little iridescent Beetle (Coleoptera) on a still-flowering shrub of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I'm not sure what kind of beetle it is, but what amazing colors! (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2011)

File.Name: b418c_b417-008.jpg
Notes:
67157010 This sure looks like a Carpet Beetle (family Dermestidae in the order Coleoptera), maybe genus Anthrenus - keep it away from my Indian rugs and old collections! But this beetle in the woods is far from anything to damage. It has beautiful appressed scales that remind me of butterflies. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2011)

This little beetle is working a still-flowering shrub of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family.
ScientificName: Dermestidae; Coleoptera; Anthrenus; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Dermestidae; Coleoptera; Anthrenus; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: b419a_b417-038.jpg
Notes:
67157737 This is an interesting local spider. It's about 1/2 inch long. It makes a little cup-like silken nest and sits in it. I found several of these in the chaparral on Sunday. My photo is looking almost straight down.

I think this may be an unusual Coneweb Spider aka Desertshrub Spider in the family Diguetidae. I believe it has 6 eyes rather than the usual eight. (I'll post another photo tomorrow to make my case.) Wikipedia says "They fashion a cone-like central retreat in which they hide and lay eggs" - which sounds right.

The photos at BugGuide aren't the best, but look at this one:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/389125

I apologize. Our Internet connection went down yesterday so I couldn't post. We called our local ISP last night - RAIN not Verizon - and everything was working when I got home from school today. Support your local Internet Service Provider!
Locality:
I'll post another photo tomorrow to make my case.) Wikipedia says "They fashion a cone-like central retreat in which they hide and lay eggs" - which sounds right.

File.Name: b419b_b418-005.jpg
Notes:
67157563 This is another little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) munching its tiny breakfast fly-prey on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) on the edge of our garden - next to the blackberries. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2011)

It was breezy when I got this photo, and it got downright windy as the day progressed. I do what I can.

File.Name: b419c_b415-013.jpg
Notes:
67157445 This is a pretty little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family on the wild edge of our garden. I've been getting photos in the blackberries every morning lately. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2011)

Look close and you will see that it is missing one of its front "grabbing" legs. It's a common disability with these spiders - who try to grab a prey bigger than they can hold! I still wonder if it will regenerate that missing leg on its next molt.

File.Name: b420a_b419-024.jpg
Notes:
67164067 This is a muscular little Robber Fly (family Asilidae in the order Diptera) resting on a dead stem in the woods. They are predators who catch their prey on the wing. I think they are our most impressive flies. To make an analogy with mammals, they are more like leopards than rabbits! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 19 April 2011)

I got this photo yesterday at my common stop by Lake Cachuma on my way to school in the morning.

File.Name: b420b_b417-001.jpg
Notes:
67163874 Here is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) caught in the air approaching a new flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family on the wild edge of our garden. If you look close, you can see it has full pollen baskets - of white pollen. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2011)

On the cool foggy mornings we've been having, the bumblebees are the first pollinators out, but the honey bees are a close second.

File.Name: b420c_b417-017.jpg
Notes:
67163788 Here's another shot of the same kind of spider as my [Previous] photo. If you look close, it sure looks like it has 6 eyes (rather than the usual 8) arranged like this:
". :: ."

These spiders make a little upturned cup-like silken nest and sit in it. This one is in a Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) plant in the Fabaceae plant family. I believe they may be an unusual Coneweb Spider aka Desertshrub Spider in the family Diguetidae. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2011)

File.Name: b421a_b415-011.jpg
Notes: 67170427 Maybe this is a little Sphecid Wasp in the family Sphecidae of order Hymenoptera. It's working a new flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family on the wild edge of our garden. If you look close, you can make out the raised "collar" (the "pronotum") around the rim of its thorax that (I think) distinguishes it from other wasps. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2011)

File.Name: b421b_b419-022.jpg
Notes: 67170229 Like an ogre in the woods, this spider nest is decorated with the body parts of its recent kills. I'm not sure what kind of spider it is, but it's pretty creepy. I wouldn't want to vist this nest! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 19 April 2011)

File.Name: b421c_b418_003.jpg
Notes: 67170123 This is an interesting Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera that I found trying to get warm on a branch of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family on a drizzly morning. I'm not sure what genus or species it is. I don't believe I've seen it before, but you have to get very close to see how unique it is, so it's easy not to notice. It has a strong proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2011)

File.Name: b422a_b422-062.jpg
Notes:
67177303 I got home from school today late, and noticed this tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a "Pentel Sharplet-2 0.7" pen sticking out over the upstairs deck railing. I went down to my truck for my tripod, changed lenses and settings and ... got the photo! That bit of fluff on the pen is probably an oak catkin - they're everywhere. The pen tip is 0.7 inch, so this spider's head is only about 1.5 mm across! (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2011)

Darned if we didn't get some unexpected (and un-forecast) rain tonight! This was real rain, not the usual morning fog drip we've been having every day lately. Every little bit helps as we approach our summer dry season.
Locality: and un-forecast) rain tonight!  This was real rain

File.Name: b422b_b418-024.jpg
Notes:
67177161 Here is a pretty flower of Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family. Did you notice the tiny Fly (order Diptera) working the green nectar glands in the lower-left? (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2011)

This is the robust inezianus variety of this plant, which is fitting in the Santa Ynez Mountains. It's large, with greenish glands rather than yellow like the species, usually restricted to our local mountains. There are many plants this year, but this is the only one I've found actually flowering. That's the way with these native fire-follower plants - they flower for a few years after a wildfire, like the Gap Wildfire in 2007, and then they disappear to wait for the next fire.
CommonNames: Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas; Fly
ScientificName: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Diptera; inezianus
associatedTaxa: Zigadenus fremontii; inezianus; Liliaceae; Diptera; inezianus

File.Name: b422c_b416-012.jpg
Notes:
67176983 Here is a Bumble Bee (family Bombus, order Hymenoptera) snuggling into a new flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family on the wild edge of our garden. The bumble bees are always the first insects to appear on the flowers on the cold drippy mornings we been having this week. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2011)

I admit that I don't much enjoy photographing bumble bees - they are too big and furry to show the kind of structural detail I like in my insect photos.

File.Name: b423a_b423-030.jpg
Notes:
67182875 These are mating Bee Flies (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera on a dying branch of Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) in the Asteraceae plant family. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2011)

Cole Porter of course:

   "Birds do it, bees do it
   Even educated fleas do it
   Let's do it, let's fall in love...

File.Name: b423b_b423-006.jpg
Notes:
67182769 Life and death among the aphids...

I believe this worm is the larva of a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) sucking the life from a Mealy Cabbage Aphid (Brevicoryne brassicae) on a Kale (Brassica oleracea) plant in the garden. It seems to be attached like a remora on a shark. If aphids could scream, I imagine they would sound like elephants trumpeting! (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2011)

Are all those aphid-husks the result of this worm or are they just the aphids molting?

File.Name: b423c_b422-064.jpg
Notes:
67182669 Here's another photo of the tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) I found on our upstairs porch last night. I got this photo at a better angle, and it shows why I had trouble getting both eyes and palps in focus - they are some distance apart! The light was fading when I got this shot. I figure the spider's head is only about 1.5 or 2 mm across. See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2011)

I actually saw this spider again today on the porch. It was hiding over the side, so I tried to nudge it back, and it ... fell down to the ground. I'm sure it wasn't hurt, but it's gone.

File.Name: b424a_b423-003.jpg
Notes:
67188935 This is a beautiful female Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) perched on an immature flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the edge of our garden. These little flies have such amazing eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2011)

I know it's a female, because I watched it lay eggs under the flower head. These critters may be the cause of the galled flower heads on this shrub. Tephritid flies lay their eggs inside plant tissue, and they are known to cause galls in other plants. See my photos at:
http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/48320206
http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/60719971
http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/61255076

It looks a lot like the Sunflower Seed Maggot (Neotephritis finalis), though I haven't found anything specific on the web about gall formation on Venegasia flowers.
CommonNames: Fruit Fly; Canyon Sunflower; Sunflower Seed Maggot
ScientificName: Tephritidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Neotephritis finalis; Venegasia
associatedTaxa: Tephritidae; Diptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; Neotephritis finalis; Venegasia

File.Name: b424b_b423-037.jpg
Notes:
67188458 I'm pretty sure this is a predacious Dance Fly in the family Empididae near the creek. It's not much larger than a mosquito. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2011)

I've been puzzling over the relation between this fly and my "Graceful Long-legged Fly". Both flies have wrap-around eyes. See my photos at:
http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66626346
http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66657787
http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66714686

Now I see some differences:

* This fly has a shorter proboscis, flattened from the sides;
* The abdomens are shaped differently - this fly looks blunt and linear, not pointed;
* This fly has widely spaced antennae and the others don't, but this might be a gender thing;
* This fly has black rather than orange legs;
* This fly has a more muscular thorax and legs - more like a dragonfly.

I still don't know if these differences add up to a family difference. Either way, both are pretty interesting flies!

File.Name: b424c_b424-005.jpg
Notes:
67188189 I thought our mushrooms were all done for the year, but stand corrected. This is "The Blusher" - a well-named blushing Amanita rubescens mushroom that I found in the woods this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2011)

It gets its name from the reddish colors it develops as it ages. This amanita is said to be edible when cooked, but it's easily confused with poisonous species. No thanks!

It was a crummy day for photos - drippy morning, then very windy. But I guess the fog drip and light rain adds up enough moisture to bring out a few new 'shrooms!

File.Name: b425a_b423-023.jpg
Notes:
67195748 This is a beautiful Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly perched on a blade of grass in the woods. It's showing both the brilliant blue upper wings that are its namesake and the dull grey (with distinctive dots) lower wings. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2011)

*Sigh* Another windy day - too windy for new photos. I hope it ends soon.

File.Name: b425b_b423-038.jpg
Notes:
67195534 I can't resist one more photo of (I think) a predacious Dance Fly in the family Empididae near the creek - hanging from a grass blade, with its prey. Judging by the patterned wings, I guess the prey is a little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae). (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2011)

*Gulp* If you look close, you can see a bit of fly-gore hanging from the tip of its proboscis. On the other hand, isn't it neat how it holds its prey by cradling it in the bend of its bristly hind legs - and pinning it down with it's middle legs?

For some discussion about these impressive flies, see:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67188458

File.Name: b425c_b423-002.jpg
Notes: 67195382 Here's another photo of the same female Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) as my [Previous] photo, still perched on an immature flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the edge of our garden. This photo shows her impressive ovipositor. These flies may be the cause of the galled flower heads on this shrub. See my [Previous] photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2011)

File.Name: b426a_b410-002.jpg
Notes:
67202869 This is the beautiful fasciated stem of a Kale (Brassica oleracea) plant in the garden. The normally round stem has become a thick ribbon, with flower buds all over it. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2011)

Wikipedia says that "Fasciation can be caused by a mutation in the meristematic cells, bacterial infection, mite or insect attack, or chemical or mechanical damage. Some plants may inherit the trait." It occurs in many species. Whatever the cause, it's beautiful and strange!

It's been windy again today - where does all that air go? But it's just as well. I'm doing another talk and slide show about "Insect Galls on San Marcos Pass" next Monday, May 2 - and fasciated stems are a kind of plant gall, though not caused by insects. I've been going through my recent photos.

This time I'm talking to a University crowd at the Cheadle Center for Biodiversity and Ecological Restoration (CCBER) at UCSB. It's a honor. Of course I'm nervous!
ScientificName: fasciated; Brassica oleracea; Cheadle Center for Biodiversity and Ecological Restoration
associatedTaxa: fasciated; Brassica oleracea; Cheadle Center for Biodiversity and Ecological Restoration

File.Name: b426b_b423-009.jpg
Notes:
67202538 Here are a circle of "alate" aphids (with wings) waiting for takeoff on the leaf of a Kale (Brassica oleracea) plant in the garden. I believe these are the not-very-mealy adult form of the Mealy Cabbage Aphid (Brevicoryne brassicae) - but California has over 450 species of aphids! Bon voyage, little aphids! (San Marcos Pass, 23 April 2011)

It's interesting that this kale plant is right next to the fasciated kale plant in my [Next] photo. This one is covered with aphids, but the fasciated plant has none. The plants are even touching, but the aphids don't cross over. Is this something the plant breeders should check out?

File.Name: b426c_b424-012.jpg
Notes: 67202300 These are strange late-season "mushrooms" - at least they are the fleshy fruiting body of a fungus - that I found in the woods last weekend. I guess they are some kind of "Fairy Tub" cup fungus, family Pezizaceae or kin. What a strange texture they have. They look like they belong in the ocean! (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2011)

File.Name: b427a_b427-016.jpg
Notes:
67209350 Insects are lined up on these new flowers of native Chamise (Adenostoma fasciculatum) in the Rosaceae plant family!

Looking up from the lower-right, there is the large Beetle (Coleoptera - maybe family Chrysomelidae?); then a smaller beetle (maybe family Dermestidae - is that a face on it's butt?); then a tiny Wasp (Hymenoptera) on the upper-left flower. It's curious - all the insects are pretty sharp, but no single flower is! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 27 April 2011)

The wind let up a bit this morning, so I stopped in the woods by Lake Cachuma for a few photos. Then it got windy again.
ScientificName: Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Coleoptera; Chrysomelidae; Dermestidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Adenostoma fasciculatum; Rosaceae; Coleoptera; Chrysomelidae; Dermestidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: b427b_b427-002.jpg
Notes: 67209063 I found this handsome Snakefly (genus Agulla, family Raphidiidae, order Neuroptera) insect today on new flower buds of native Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 27 April 2011)
ScientificName: Agulla; Raphidiidae; Neuroptera; Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Agulla; Raphidiidae; Neuroptera; Lonicera subspicata; denudata; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: b427c_b427-005.jpg
Notes:
67208763 This is a tiny flower of Canyon Clarkia (Clarkia epilobioides) in the Onagraceae plant family. It's the first of our "Farewell-to-Spring" wildflowers that I've seen this year. The nodding buds behind are diagnostic of the species. As a photographer, you have to be careful not to overexpose the stamens and 4-lobed stigma of these small white flowers. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 27 April 2011)

I reckon it's really "Farewell-to-Spring" when the annual grass seeds get loose. I've ruined many a pair of socks from those damned foxtail and ripgut grasses and their kin! This hasn't happened yet, but their time is coming soon.

File.Name: b428a_b428-010.jpg
Notes:
67215992 Here are the lower wings of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly resting on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides). It must be freshly emerged to have such colors. I'll post another photo showing the upper wings tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2011)

I actually saw this butterfly from the upstairs porch. I loaded a memory chip, changed lenses, went downstairs and got my tripod from the car, and ... got the photo! Sometimes it happens like that.

File.Name: b428b_b423-040.jpg
Notes: 67215848 I found this odd spider with a bottle-gourd shaped abdomen resting on a leaf of a Sanicle plant in the Apiaceae plant family. It's quite large - about 1/2 inch long, and there was no web nearby. My first thought was "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa of the Araneidae), but it's much larger than our usual spiders in that group. I don't know what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2011)

File.Name: b428c_b427-008.jpg
Notes: 67215726 This is how a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) plays hide-and-go-seek - not very well! It's trying to hide behind a branch of Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) that's just starting to flower. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 27 April 2011)

File.Name: b429a_b429-205.jpg
Notes:
67221861 Walking on water? Well, there is water there, but it's mostly green algae slime.

This is a Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) that I found in our little outdoor bathtub frog pond today after school today. Was it eating tadpoles? (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2011)

Doesn't that "left hand" in front look strange? But it's right!

We didn't realize that the recirculating pump got clogged, so the little pond quickly got choked with algae. We're on it.

File.Name: b429b_b429-207.jpg
Notes:
67221678 This isn't the best photo, but it's strange enough that I want to post it.

It's a tiny Lace Bug in the family Tingidae of the order Hemiptera on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. I think of them as plant-suckers, but this one is sitting at the bottom of a line of tiny dead Flies (order Diptera). Maybe coicidence? (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2011)

I got home from school today after our "Greek Festival" - I took 350 photos of the kids doing their stuff! I felt a bit sun-sick and dehydrated, but I went looking for interesting photos on a windy afternoon and got this strange one. The photo is taken under the leaf with the sun behind.

File.Name: b429c_b428_007.jpg
Notes: 67221524 Here is another photo of the same California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly in my [Previous] photo, showing a bit of the upper wings. The butterfly is resting on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides). It must be freshly emerged to have such colors. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2011)

File.Name: b430a_b430-041.jpg
Notes:
67227949 This is a beautiful Jumping Spider (Salticidae - maybe genus Phidippus?) that I found on a leaf of Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. It's much larger than the other jumping spiders I've been posting lately - maybe 1/2 inch long. You can see a bit of its green chelicerae (jaws). (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2011)

My book says these spiders can change the color of their eyes, but this one changed from ruby to green in just the second or two between this photo and the next! I'm sure it's iridescence, like hummingbird colors.

At least the wind which has deviled us all week eased up today. It was breezy, but not gusty. The wind and low humidity have taken a toll. We had a bit of rain a week ago, but now it feels dry, like summer.

I took my usual photo walk this morning, but I've been preoccupied with the lecture/slide show on insect galls I'm doing at UCSB on Monday evening. I've been putting off my work, and tomorrow is catch up day!

File.Name: b430b_b430-026.jpg
Notes:
67227692 Yellow on yellow...

This is a pretty yellow "true bug" in the insect order Hemiptera perched on a yellow flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. I did no saturation enhancement on this photo, but yellow is always funky with my camera. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2011)

Maybe it's a Scentless Plant Bug (family Rhopalidae) - perhaps genus Harmostes? Compare the BugGuide photo at:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/241314/

I've photographed this critter before. See my photos from last year at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/62963938
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/63132424

File.Name: b430c_b429-212.jpg
Notes:
67227432 Me and my shadow...

I found this large Darkling Beetle (family Tenebrionidae, order Coleoptera - probably genus Eleodes) climbing down our concrete block retaining wall last night. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2011)

The kids call these "Stink Beetles", but I prefer the old name "Pinacate Beetle". When threatened or attacked, these beetles stand on their head and release a brew of noxious chemicals from the posterior of their abdomen. It's the beetle equivalent of a skunk. From Wikipedia: "The name pinacate is Mexican Spanish, derived from the Nahuatl (Aztec) name for the insect, pinacatl, which translates as 'black beetle'.

File.Name: b501a_b501-018.jpg
Notes:
67234239 I woke up to the the pitter-patter of ... Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) falling on our bed!

This photo is INSIDE the bedroom window. Ants walking on the ceiling were falling in our bed like rain. In a couple of hours they were all gone. The little ants are vicious - they bite and sting. (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2011)

My slideshow for my talk on Insect Galls tomorrow night is finally done - but it took three tries to get it (sort of) right. I don't like PowerPoint!

File.Name: b501b_b501-009.jpg
Notes: 67234201 We were invaded by these native Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) this morning! Here are winged ants and the fierce little ants that kept me from getting the photo I wanted. They bite and sting! (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2011)

File.Name: b501c_b501-002.jpg
Notes:
67234167 We were invaded by native Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) this morning - but not as bad as this little Cricket (family Gryllidae in the order Orthoptera). (San Marcos Pass, 1 May 2011)

The damn ants bite and sting! It was a personal sacrifice to get this photo!

File.Name: b502a_b416-004.jpg
Notes:
67241927 I believe these are the pretty little galls of a Round Gall Wasp (Besbicus conspicuus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. They are growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2011)

I've been preoccupied with insect galls lately. My lecture/slide show at UCSB last night went well, but I'm glad it's over so I can get back to my usual photos!.
