File.Name: b502b_b430-017.jpg
Notes: 67241885 I guess this is a kind of gall, though it is not an insect gall. These are deformed leaves of a native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family which has been invaded by a rust fungus (Uredinales, Basidiomycotina). The plants completely change when they are infected - the leaflets are thin and widely spaced, and the stems have no thorns. I find this every year on a few plants in the canyon. It doesn't seem to be spreading. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2011)

File.Name: b502c_b430-032.jpg
Notes:
67241850 This is an insect gall on native Fleabane Daisy (Erigeron foliosus) in the Asteraceae plant family. The plant is not common in our mountains, but in this place there are quite a few and almost all of them are galled. This gall is not listed in my book, but I suspect it is caused by a Gall Midge (family Cecidomyiidae) in the order Diptera. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2011)

I've been preoccupied with insect galls lately. My lecture/slide show at UCSB last night went well.

File.Name: b503a_b503-015.jpg
Notes:
67246815 Here is a beautiful metallic-colored Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working a new flower of native Checker Bloom (Sidalcea malviflora) in the Malvaceae plant family. (Tequepis Canyon, Santa Ynez Valley, 3 May 2011)

It seems distinctive, but I'm not even sure what family this iridescent golden-green bee is. We have Metallic Leafcutter Bees, Metallic Sweat Bees, and Cuckoo Bees that all have metallic green colors. What is it?
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Checker Bloom; Metallic Leafcutter Bees; Metallic Sweat Bees; Cuckoo Bees

File.Name: b503b_b503-018.jpg
Notes: 67246717 This is a beautiful Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly - the first I've seen this year. It's perched on a non-native grass in the woods near Lake Cachuma. I chased this butterfly for almost a half-hour back and forth along the trail as it chased away every flying critter that came by. I finally got it! (Tequepis Canyon, Santa Ynez Valley, 3 May 2011)

File.Name: b503c_3.jpg
Notes:
67246663 This is a small Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) struggling in a spider web. I didn't see any spider on the web, so I guess the unlucky bee was a victim of something like a "ghost net" that keeps on killing fish after it is lost - a bit of forgotten spider web in the woods. (Tequepis Canyon, Santa Ynez Valley, 3 May 2011)

Of course I set the bee free - after I got the photo! It's not quite sharp, but this is an action photo.

File.Name: b504a_b504-003.jpg
Notes:
67252585 This is a tiny Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) munching the blue-tipped stamens of (I think) Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. This flower has blue pollen! (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2011)

This bee was mostly collecting the pollen on its butt - you can see what it has collected. Because of that, maybe it's a Megachilidae bee?
ScientificName: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Hydrophyllaceae; Megachilidae
associatedTaxa: Apoidea; Hymenoptera; Phacelia cicutaria; hispida; Hydrophyllaceae; Megachilidae

File.Name: b504b_b504-020.jpg
Notes:
67252416 Here is an elegant new flower of Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus). It's kind of an upside-down Mariposa Lily in the Liliaceae plant family.

It's a "tender trap" for pollinators - easy for insects to crawl into, but hard to get out past those hairs without carrying a load of pollen to pass on to other flowers. This is the first one I've seen this year at home. (San Marcos Pass, 4 May 2011)

File.Name: b504c_b503-009.jpg
Notes:
67252252 This is a pretty flower head of native Golden Stars (Bloomeria crocea) in the Liliaceae plant family. I think of this as a fire-follower, but I found this one yesterday along the road in Tequepis Canyon where it hasn't burned for many years. I reckon annual fire clearing along the road is enough to set this plant off. (Tequepis Canyon, Santa Ynez Valley, 3 May 2011)

My usual stop on the way to school is the chaparral by the Bradbury Dam overlook near Lake Cachuma. It's been closed this week. I'll bet it's a National Security thing after the recent murder of bin Laden. (Don't get me started...)

File.Name: b505a_b505-013.jpg
Notes: 67258627 I believe this is a cute little Treehopper (family Membracidae) in the order Homoptera. It's perched on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family on the wild edge of our garden. It's beautiful and strange! (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2011)

File.Name: b505b_b505-006.jpg
Notes: 67258478 This is a small Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera that I found working flowers of native Fiesta Flower (Pholistoma auritum) in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family this morning. It was working the stamens and the pistils - I'll post another photo tomorrow to prove it. (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2011)

File.Name: b505c_b505-019.jpg
Notes: 67258397 These are green aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) sucking a stem of Bedstraw (Galium aparine) in the Rubiaceae plant family. This plant with whorled leaves is so ubiquitous in the woods that it may as well be considered native. Do the introduced plants bring their own aphids? (San Marcos Pass, 5 May 2011)

File.Name: b506a_b506-014.jpg
Notes:
67264415 "Look at those chompers!"

This is a pretty bee-sized Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) trying to dry off on a garden flower of Snapdragon (Antirrhinum majus?) this morning. I think this is the yellow-jacket-wasp-type bee that I usually find hovering near the flowers. It has impressive mandibles! (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2011)

File.Name: b506b_b506-007.jpg
Notes: 67264243 Here is a common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) in the order Hymenoptera snuggling into a flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. Look at its extended right-rear leg - it has a good load of pollen collected in its basket! (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2011)

File.Name: b506c_b506-004.jpg
Notes:
67264117 This is another photo of the cute little Treehopper (family Membracidae) in the order Homoptera that I showed in my [Previous] photo - only this photo is today, not yesterday. I guess they don't move around much. It's perched on a leaflet of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family on the wild edge of our garden. It's beautiful and strange! (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2011)

Those skinny wings can't be useful for flying, but maybe they help it glide when it hops?

File.Name: b507a_b507-027.jpg
Notes:
67269847 Here is another ferocious-looking Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) near the throat of a native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) flower in the Polemoniaceae plant family. In the blink of my eye it flew away. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2011)

It was foggy and drippy all morning, then windy when it finally cleared today. Given the choice, I prefer gloom to wind every time!

File.Name: b507b_b507-032.jpg
Notes:
67269737 I've said it before - for the scant week or three that it flowers, we have no finer plant in our mountains than this native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family! The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2011)

It used to be called [i]Styrax officinalis[/i] var. [i]redivivus[/i] - but it's so strange that a species native to southern Europe and the middle east is also a native shrub of Southern California. The name has been put right, but it's still an interesting shrub.

This is an example of the "Mediterranean-American disjunction", and there are more examples - like maybe native [b]Yerba Buena[/b] ([i]Satureja douglasii[/i]) in the [i]Lamiaceae[/i] plant family which is related to the Mediterranean summer and winter savory plants, but also grows in our canyon.

There's a web page about this by Arthur C. Gibson (director of the UCLA Mildred E. Mathias Botanical Garden?):
  http://www.botgard.ucla.edu/html/membgnewsletter/Volume5number2/Styraxofficinalis.html

Gibson concludes that: "The only reasonable conclusion is that this genus was once widely distributed across the Northern Hemisphere and became restricted to certain smaller zone."

This plant also has a curious local distribution. It's very common on San Marcos Pass in both the woodland and chaparral, but where else do you find it in Santa Barbara County?

File.Name: b507c_b507-009.jpg
Notes:
67269578 It was foggy and drippy this morning, but that's good for saturated photo colors.

These are beautiful new flowers of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. That flower on the left has done its stuff to ensure pollination, but the others are just beginning. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2011)

File.Name: b508a_b508-008.jpg
Notes:
67275787 It might take a moment to get this photo - just count the wings and remember that flies have two wings...

These are mating Tephritid Fruit Flies (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) on a flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the edge of our garden this morning. They have such beautiful eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2011)

Happy Mother's Day!

File.Name: b508b_b508-020.jpg
Notes: 67275698 This is a large Bumble Bee (family Bombus, order Hymenoptera) snuggling into new flowers of native Tomcat Clover (Trifolium willdenovii) in the Fabaceae plant family. I got this photo on the hillside by our mailbox. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2011)

File.Name: b508c_b508-035.jpg
Notes:
67275559 This is a remarkable ant-mimic "true bug" in the insect order Hemiptera. You can see its sucking proboscis barely visible under its head. It has jointed antennae like an ant, and it even moves quick and jerky like an ant. This little bug is tiny, about 2 mm long. It's working a native plant of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2011)

Ant mimicry is called "myrmecomorphy". Ants are everywhere, so it's not surprising that there are look-alikes. There are several families of Hemiptera that have ant-mimicking members. This bug may well be in the family Alydidae, but there are also ant-mimics in the family Miridae and probably more.

It was windy today. This is the best shot I could get.
ScientificName: Hemiptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; myrmecomorphy; Alydidae; Miridae
associatedTaxa: Hemiptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; myrmecomorphy; Alydidae; Miridae

File.Name: b509a_b507-036.jpg
Notes:
67282607 This is a nursery of tiny spiderlings on a leaf of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family down by our creek. They were all over the web an hour ago, but now it's starting cool down and they are all collected together in a dogpile of spiders. I'm not sure what kind of spiders they are, but the web look pretty disorganized. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2011)

It's been unseasonably cool and windy lately, with high temperatures barely in the 60s. I'll be glad when it warms up again.

File.Name: b509b_b508-032.jpg
Notes:
67282384 Here is a tiny Lace Bug in the family Tingidae of the order Hemiptera on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2011)

They have a remarkable net-like lattice-work on their body that reminds me of shogun-era samurai armor, but underneath all that armor is a skinny little bug. *Sigh* I'll bet we all feel like that some days!

File.Name: b509c_b508-012.jpg
Notes:
67282274 This is a handsome little Snipe Fly (family Rhagionidae, order Diptera) - maybe genus Rhagio trying to warm up on a leaf in the woods. Fortunately, it's not one of those biting snipe flies (genus Symphoromyia) that are just starting to appear. I swatted the first one on my arm this weekend. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2011)

One good thing about the cool drippy weather we've been having is that the insects are slowed way down.

File.Name: b510a_b507-020.jpg
Notes: 67288828 This is a beautiful Bumble Bee (family Bombus, order Hymenoptera) trying to warm up on a leaf of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) shrub in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. Another leaf is in the way, but no problem. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2011)

File.Name: b510b_b510-003.jpg
Notes: 67288754 Here are tiny Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) working a single flower of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family on the dry side of our garden this morning. These ants are only 1-2 mm long. These are NOT the invading Argentine Ants (Linepithema humile), but they're just as tiny. (San Marcos Pass, 10 May 2011)
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Linepithema humile
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Linepithema humile

File.Name: b510c_b508-042.jpg
Notes:
67288571 We have many different non-native Brome grasses (genus Bromus in the Poaceae plant family) - like Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus) and Red Brome (Bromus rubens) and the rest of their kin that are trying to take over the woodland and grassland. Most are stickery, foxtail-like grasses that I don't like.

But this grass is different. This is native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass in the garden. We've been encouraging it, and it's having a good year. (San Marcos Pass, 8 May 2011)

You have to know something about grasses to recognize it. Most grasses look the same when young, but this grass has short awns on its glumes that makes it recognizable.

You have to be careful not to over-expose grasses. This photo is -1.3 stops from what my meter recommended.
CommonNames: Brome; Ripgut Grass; Red Brome; California Brome
ScientificName: Bromus; Poaceae; Bromus diandrus; Bromus rubens; Bromus carinatus; awns; glumes
associatedTaxa: Bromus; Poaceae; Bromus diandrus; Bromus rubens; Bromus carinatus; awns; glumes

File.Name: b511a_b511-012.jpg
Notes:
67295613 This is a large Bumble Bee (family Bombus, order Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native Rock Phacelia (Phacelia imbricata) in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 May 2011)

This was a lucky shot. I was already focused on a nearby flower when this bee appeared, and I was almost too close to get the photo, and it was windy. As it is, I captured it nearly full-frame.

I had some time to kill between school and a Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society board meeting, so I drove up to the lower slopes of Figueroa Mountain today. It was too windy, but I got a few photos.

File.Name: b511b_b511-001.jpg
Notes: 67295522 These are mating Tephritid Fruit Flies (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the edge of our garden this morning. They have such beautiful eyes. They are tiny. This is a tight crop of a nearly 1:1 magnification photo. (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2011)

File.Name: b511c_b511-003.jpg
Notes: 67295492 I'm not sure what this photo shows. There's a Beetle (Coleoptera) inside this opening flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the native edge of our garden this morning. The beetle might well be a Click Beetle (family Elateridae) because of the points on the rear of its thorax. But is it feeding or is it trapped? (San Marcos Pass, 11 May 2011)

File.Name: b512a_b512-002.jpg
Notes: 67301144 This is a handsome little Weevil beetle (Curculionidae) in the order Coleoptera on a petal of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family this morning. I dig those crazy antennae! (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2011)

File.Name: b512b_b512-004.jpg
Notes:
67301008 Oak trees have easy-to-see staminate (male, pollen-bearing) catkins, but they also have these hard-to-see female pistillate flowers that are pollen-receptive. They look like little acorns.

This is the female pistillate flower of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 12 May 2011)

I'm not sure how these flowers work. The male catkins were out a month ago, and now the female flowers appear? This is an example of protandry - male function comes before female function. Maybe the trees catch and hold the pollen in the stellate hairs (star-like) hairs on their stems?

File.Name: b512c_b506-012.jpg
Notes:
67300793 I believe this is a soggy Wild Bee (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) trying to dry off on a garden flower of Snapdragon (Antirrhinum majus?). I've been browsing at BugGuide, and I think it might be a Cuckoo Bee in the genus Nomada. (San Marcos Pass, 6 May 2011)

Cuckoo Bees resemble wasps and lay their eggs in other bees' burrows. They are fast fliers, and aware - hard to photograph when dry. I'm pretty sure they can fly backwards. See the photos at:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/5211/bgimage

And see my other photo of this same bee at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67264415

File.Name: b513a_b507-016.jpg
Notes:
67306811 This is part of the seed head of a native Large-flowered Dandelion (Agoseris grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. I show the flower in my [Previous] photo. It has these spectacular dandelion-type seed heads that are several inches in diameter. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2011)

We had our annual Dunn Middle School track meet today for over 400 kids mostly from the Santa Ynez Valley. I'm tired and a bit sun-sick, but it was a great day!

I didn't run the warm-up lap this year because I hurt my back last weekend, but see this photo from the vault:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/35279851

File.Name: b513b_b508_024.jpg
Notes:
67306703 Pizza with orange worms or Cheeto's as a topping?

This is a composite flower head of native Large-flowered Dandelion (Agoseris grandiflora) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's more interesting than a typical weedy dandelion. It's a perennial, and the seed heads are special. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2011)

File.Name: b513c_b507_022.jpg
Notes:
67306568 This is a delicate new flower of native Thimbleberry (Rubus parviflorus) in the canyon. This is a different kind of wild blackerry in the Rosaceae plant family. They are common and almost weedy in the Sierras and Oregon, but they are scarce here in the Santa Barbara mountains. They rarely (never?) fruit here. (San Marcos Pass, 7 May 2011)

I found them flowering at the same time last year, almost to the day. See my photo at:


   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/61523814

File.Name: b514a_b514-014.jpg
Notes:
67312889 This is a long-legged ghost fly (order Diptera) in the woods today. I'm not sure what it is, and I won't try to guess. Once I noticed this one land on a stem of Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Labiatae plant family, I started seeing them everywhere. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2011)

Darned if we didn't get a bit of rain this morning, and more is expected tonight and tomorrow!

I've been enlisted to go on the 8th grade graduation trip to San Francisco tomorrow. Linda sent me an email of 28 reasons to join the trip - it was funny and I couldn't refuse. I'll be back on Friday.

File.Name: b514b_b514-029.jpg
Notes:
67312743 I was all set to say that this strange critter is a Mealybug (family Pseudococcidae of the order Homoptera), but I'm glad I checked at BugGuide! It's a Mealybug Destroyer - or at least kin to one. (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2011)

The proper Mealybug Destroyer is a mealybug specialist, and this one is interested in the aphids on this branch of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. See the photos at:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/339256/bgimage

I believe this is a the larvae of a Lady Beetle (Coccinellidae of the order Coleoptera) - maybe a Twice-stabbed Lady Beetle in the genus Chilocorus which I've seen before on this shrub?

Someone at BugGuide noted that "Certain coccinellid larvae develop wax and mimic the mealybugs that are their food source." This sounds right.
CommonNames: Mealybug; Mealybug Destroyer; Mealybug Destroyer; Greenbark Ceanothus; Lady Beetle; Twice-stabbed Lady Beetle
ScientificName: Pseudococcidae; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Chilocorus
associatedTaxa: Pseudococcidae; Homoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Chilocorus

File.Name: b514c_b514-024.jpg
Notes:
67312402 These are the first native flowers of Woolly Blue Curls (Trichostema lanatum) in the Lamiaceae plant family that I've seen this year in the canyon - with raindrops! (San Marcos Pass, 14 May 2011)

Yes, we had a bit of unusual May rain this morning, and there's more expected tonight and tomorrow. Every little bit helps!

File.Name: b520a_b517-084.jpg
Notes:
67345768 "City of light"

This is not Jim Morrison's L.A., but "the city" - San Fransisco and the Golden Gate Bridge - glowing at night from across the bay. (Marin Headlands, 17 May 2011)

We took our 8th grade graduating kids up to a place in the Marin Headlands overlooking the Golden Gate bridge and the city. What a night!

File.Name: b520b_b519-038.jpg
Notes:
67345682 We visited the spectacular "Rainforests of the World" exhibit at the California Academy of Sciences museum in San Francisco. The rainforest exhibit is contained within a spectacular 90-foot diameter glass dome. It feels hot and humid inside.

There are butterflies inside. This is a Heliconiid butterfly - maybe a Tiger Longwing (Heliconius hecale)? It's resting on one of my student's finger. (San Francisco, 19 May 2011)

I don't approve of these captive butterflies - but it's a pretty cool exhibit!

File.Name: b520c_b518-060.jpg
Notes: 67345595 I love this painted building with hanging laundry in Chinatown! (San Francisco, 18 May 2011)

File.Name: b521a_b521-032.jpg
Notes:
67350350 Flies in love!

I'm glad I got this photo, because I would never guess that these two flies are the same species. I guess these are pollen-dusted March Flies (family Bibionidae, order Diptera). There were hundreds of them on the new flowers of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2011)

We got 1-1/4 inches of unusual May rain while I was away on a school trip last week. The ground is still damp, and butterflies are still scarce - but there are new insects in the woods!

File.Name: b521b_b521-063.jpg
Notes: 67350194 This is an impressive "Bee Assassins". It's a true bug in the Reduviidae family of the insect order Hemiptera - probably Apiomerus californicus. It's the size of a quarter. This one is perched in a Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) plant in the Fabaceae plant family, and it's looking right at me. It looks like something you wouldn't want to meet in a dark alley! (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2011)
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Apiomerus californicus; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Apiomerus californicus; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae

File.Name: b521c_b521-024.jpg
Notes:
67350021 Life and death in the mountains...

This is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with its prey on a new flower head of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. I guess the unfortunate fly is a male March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera) - see my photo after the [Next]. Note the cast-off carcass of the other fly. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2011)

I wonder what the world looks like to this spider with all those eyes...
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Golden Yarrow; March Fly
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Bibionidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Bibionidae; Diptera

File.Name: b522a_b520-017.jpg
Notes:
67355567 Here is a beautiful Gabb's Checkerspot (Chlosyne gabbii) butterfly perched on a branch of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa) in the Ericaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2011)

It was cold and cloudy and windy today, but I've been busy preparing photos for the end-of-the-year school slide show. I'm not doing the slide show this year, but I've been taking most of the school photos.

It will all be over in a week. I'm officially retiring this year, after 28 years at Dunn Middle School. I still have to write my graduation speech for Friday, but then I'm done. I'll admit - it's a bittersweet moment.
CommonNames: Gabb's Checkerspot; Eastwood Manzanita; Dunn Middle School

File.Name: b522b_b521-048.jpg
Notes: 67355455 This is a tiny caterpillar (order Lepidoptera) working to "skeletonize" a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. It's eating just the green layer of the leaf, first one side, then the other. This caterpillar is living in a shroud of webbing. I guess it will become a moth, not a butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2011)

File.Name: b522c_b521-078.jpg
Notes:
67355296 These are almost-ripe berries of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family - with one single flower still open. If you look very close, you might see the tiny wasp with its abdomen up in the air on the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2011)

The fruit are edible and sweet/sour at first, but they soon turn bitter. They are a "suck and spit" fruit, a great design for seed propagation!

File.Name: b523a_b520-024.jpg
Notes:
67361629 This is a handsome Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly working a new flower of native Wedge-leaved Horkelia (Horkelia cuneata) in the Rosaceae plant family growing in the canyon. I guess it's a female because of the dull colors. The males have more blue. (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2011)

Look how the butterfly is carefully inserting it's proboscis between the flat stamens of the flower. The flat filaments of the stamens remind me of those wide matches that restaurants used to give out.

File.Name: b523b_b521-005.jpg
Notes:
67361433 This caterpillar has clown colors!

Here are the psychedelic colors and form of a Western Tussock Moth (family Lymantriidae in the order Lepidoptera - maybe Orgyia vetusta or kin?) working a native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2011)

Pretty as the caterpillars are, the adult moths are dull. The females emerge without wings to mate, lay their eggs, and die. What a life!
ScientificName: Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Orgyia vetusta; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Orgyia vetusta; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae

File.Name: b523c_b520-006.jpg
Notes:
67361238 This is a beautiful blue caterpillar (order Lepidoptera), but I'm not sure which butterfly it goes with. I can't find it in my "Caterpillars in the Field and Garden" field guide or on the web. If you know what it is, please let me know!

This caterpillar seems lost on a bridge post by our creek. Maybe it fell from a nearby Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree? (San Marcos Pass, 20 May 2011)

I've seen this caterpillar before. See my photo from 30 May 2009:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/50865068

File.Name: b524a_b521-090.jpg
Notes:
67366777 This is a gorgeous yellow horticultural Onion (genus Allium, family Liliaceae) just starting to flower in our garden. If you look close, you can see a little spider on the outside of the center-left flower. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2011)

I stayed home today and got my commencement speech roughed out for Friday. I'll be officially retired in just 3 days. You're right Lester - congratulations and condolences are both in order. I've been teaching at DMS for 28 years, and it will take some time to get it out of my system.

(Hah- it will take about 2 minutes at the beginning of summer - but I'm sure it will catch up to me later!)

If you don't know author David Foster Wallace's commencement speech from 2005, check it out:
   http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=M5THXa_H_N8
   http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=uSAzbSQqals
Locality:
Hah- it will take about 2 minutes at the beginning of summer - but I'm sure it will catch up to me later!)

If you don't know author David Foster Wallace's commencement speech from 2005

File.Name: b524b_b521-028.jpg
Notes:
67366560 This little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) has its breakfast prey on a new flower head of native Blue Elderberry (Sambucus mexicana) shrub in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2011)

I believe the unfortunate prey is a male March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera). See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67350350

Also see my photo of another crab spider with prey at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67350021

These spiders are masters of camouflage, so it was odd to find the white spider on yellow flowers and the yellow spider on these white flowers. Both spiders appear to be successful.
CommonNames: Crab Spider; Blue Elderberry; March Fly
ScientificName: Thomisidae; Sambucus mexicana; Caprifoliaceae; Bibionidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Thomisidae; Sambucus mexicana; Caprifoliaceae; Bibionidae; Diptera

File.Name: b524c_b521-014.jpg
Notes:
67366410 I found this odd spider (Arachnida) on a Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's odd because it only seems to have 6 eyes while most spiders have 8, though maybe those outer eyes are really double? (I think I see a vertical line dividing them). Its web is pretty disorganized. (San Marcos Pass, 21 May 2011)

It's a handsome spider. You can't see it very well in this photo, but the underside of its abdomen is bright yellow.
CommonNames: spider; Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak

File.Name: b525a_b525-055.jpg
Notes:
67373664 This is a beetle rave happening inside an uncommon Lilac Mariposa Lily (Calochortus splendens) wildflower in the Liliaceae plant family. The flower looks electric! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 May 2011)

Today was "Serendipity Day" at school - do something fun because this is the last week of school! Donna and I did a Hike Club in Paradise. The road is still closed at the first crossing of the river because of high water, so we hiked with DMS kids a couple of miles past the road closure to a good swimming hole.

We had great fun, and I found a few new flowers to photograph. I only had my "kit lens" and my monopod for support, but I did fine on a windy day.

Only two more days! Tomorrow night is the DMS slide show (I took most of the photos) and Friday is graduation and my retirement party. I may not have time to post for a few days.

File.Name: b525b_b525-050.jpg
Notes:
67373531 What's a nice native like you doing in a field of stickery non-native Brome grasses?

These are beautiful new flowers of perennial Scarlet Bugler (Penstemon centranthifolius) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family growing along the road by the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 May 2011)

It's a classic hummingbird flower, and I saw a hummer working the flowers - though I couldn't get the photo.

File.Name: b525c_b525-059.jpg
Notes:
67373449 These are beautiful summer-blue native flowers of Chaparral Penstemon (Penstemon heterophyllus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family growing along the road along the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 May 2011)

It's odd. Most native flowers are late in this cool year, but these summer flowers seem early. Maybe I just haven't hiked hiked this road at this time before. (I'm usually busy now with my school slide show, but this year I gave the work to Mac!)
Locality: I'm usually busy now with my school slide show

File.Name: b526a_b525-014.jpg
Notes:
67383998 Here are interesting new flowers of the Shredding Evening Primrose (Camissonia boothi ssp. decortans) in the Onagraceae plant family. Like the Phacelia in my [Previous] photo, new flowers will keep appearing as the flower stalks unwind. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 May 2011)

I can't recall seeing these flowers before. They are growing in a place that burned a few years ago, so maybe they are fire-followers? Or maybe I've just never hiked along the river this early into summer. (I've usually been busy with my school slide show about now, but this year my former student - now a DMS teacher - Mac did it, and a fine job! Thanks Mac!)
Locality: I've usually been busy with my school slide show about now

File.Name: b526b_b525-069.jpg
Notes: 67383841 Phacelias are a difficult group of native plants, but I believe these are the impressive flower plumes of Phacelia ramosissima var. latifloia in the Hydrophyllaceae) plant family. They keep getting new flowers on the stalks as they unwind. These look like hungry caterpillars! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 May 2011)

File.Name: b526c_b525-002.jpg
Notes:
67383735 This is a different kind of insect collection...

On our hike up the Santa Ynez River on Wednesday, we met a lady fly fisherman who showed us her working collection of hand-tied fishing flies. These seem to be mostly wet flies. I prefer casting dry flies, but I love to see people working the river which is still flowing good enough to keep the cars out. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 May 2011)

There's a rumor of an unsafe bridge. The road might be closed for some time, which suits me just fine - it's a great walk up the river!

File.Name: b527a_b525-033.jpg
Notes:
67384478 News Flash: Treebeard jumps into retirement!

Not quite. That's me jumping off the rock into the swimming hole at the second crossing of Paradise (I love saying that) last Wednesday - but I am now officially retired. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 25 May 2011)

I delivered the commencement speech at the DMS graduation for the class of 2011, and everything went just fine. Nick filmed it. I'll see if I can get it on YouTube. I went out with a bang!

(DMS students & parents - if you got a photo of my "experiment" please email it to me!)

The original plan was to go beer tasting at a local brewery and then have a retirement party at our house in the mountains... what was Linda thinking?! After graduation, we all just wanted to get home. We'll have the party "Real Soon Now"!
Locality:
DMS students & parents - if you got a photo of my "experiment" please email it to me!)

The original plan was to go beer tasting at a local brewery and then have a retirement party at our house in the mountains... what was Linda thinking?! After graduation
CommonNames: News Flash: Treebeard jumps into retirement!

File.Name: b527b_b524-005.jpg
Notes:
67384252 These small ants are herding a colony of tiny aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) on a green stem of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2011)

I've seen these ants before. I think they might be genus Crematogaster in the subfamily Myrmicinae. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66876927
ScientificName: Aphididae; Homoptera; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae
associatedTaxa: Aphididae; Homoptera; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae

File.Name: b527c_b520-028.jpg
Notes: 67384123 This is a strange little Mugwort Weevil - at least that's what it should be called. It's a small weevil beetle in the family Curculionidae of the order Coleoptera that only occurs on leaves of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family. What a strange face! This is the first one I've noticed this year. (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2011)

File.Name: b528a_b528-023.jpg
Notes:
67389507 I believe this is the nymph of a Bush Katydid - genus Scudderia in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera. Its antennae are very long, off the photo. It's perched on a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2011)

These katydid nymphs have remarkable colors before they become large dull-green cattle-like insects that move slowly and munch leaves. This is the first one I've seen this year.

It was very windy today on the first day of the rest of my (newly retired) life! I managed to get a few photos close to the ground.
ScientificName: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: b528b_b528-014.jpg
Notes:
67389311 My first thought was that this is another Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on a leaf of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. But maybe not. I don't see closed cells on the wings or a "spurious vein". I'm not sure what fly this is. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2011)

I think she might be ovipositing. I saw rhythmic contractions in her abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2011)

File.Name: b528c_b528-006.jpg
Notes:
67389136 This is a Syrphid Flower Fly - aka Hover Fly - (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) hovering near a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. My photo is not quite sharp, but not bad! If you look very close, you can see the outline of its wing beats. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2011)

It was very windy today, so I tried photographing these hover flies. They have a built in gyroscope that holds them steady in the air despite the wind.
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Hover Fly; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: b529a_b529-010.jpg
Notes:
67394913 "Oh, baby, baby, it's a wild world..." (Cat Stevens)

These are three fledgling House Wrens (Troglodytes aedon) on the garden path on their first flight from home. It must also be their last flight from home. I don't think they can get back to their nest box on the porch. We'll be sure to keep our doors closed to keep the cat inside. (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2011)

The little bird on the lower-right seems to be the runt of the litter, and it sure looks like its stronger siblings are ganging up on it. I wish it well.

Marge Simpson - "Kids can be so mean."
Bart Simpson - "We can? Thanks mom!"
(Bart then smacks Lisa in the head.)

This photo seems appropriate after the Dunn School graduation for the high school kids today, including 14 of my former middle school students. Fly on little birds!

Update 30 May 2011: The little wren didn't make it. Julie found it in the garden this morning. I'm not surprised.
Locality:
Bart then smacks Lisa in the head.)

This photo seems appropriate after the Dunn School graduation for the high school kids today

File.Name: b529b_b529-003.jpg
Notes:
67394667 This Giant Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera was grounded by the wild winds today. In the original photo, I can make out the simple six-sided ommatidia or "simple eyes" that make up its compound eyes. It's resting of a native plant of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family on the dry side of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 29 May 2011)

According to a neighbor's weather station, we had wind gusts of 50 mph today! I shouldn't have been able to get this photo in the wind, but I timed it just right between the gusts. Where does all that air come from, and where does it all go?

Despite the wind, we had a bit of real rain last night! Here in the Santa Barbara mountains, we have a Mediterranean winter-wet, summer-dry weather pattern. Late-season rain is always a blessing as we enter our dry summer period.
ScientificName: Tipulidae; Diptera; ommatidia; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Tipulidae; Diptera; ommatidia; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae

File.Name: b529c_b528-022.jpg
Notes:
67394521 This is another photo of a remarkable Bush Katydid nymph - genus Scudderia in the order Orthoptera. It is in the False Katydid subfamily Phaneropterinae of the family Tettigoniidae. It's munching a flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 28 May 2011)

These katydid nymphs have remarkable colors before they become large dull-green cattle-like insects that move slowly and munch leaves.
CommonNames: Bush Katydid; False Katydid; Canyon Sunflower
ScientificName: Scudderia; Orthoptera; Phaneropterinae; Tettigoniidae; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Scudderia; Orthoptera; Phaneropterinae; Tettigoniidae; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae

File.Name: b530a_b530-047.jpg
Notes:
67400941 Butterflies in love!

Here are a mating pair of Marine Blue (Leptotes marina) butterflies on an almost-flowering stem of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. What can I say? (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2011)

After gale-force winds yesterday, the winds finally stopped today. It's been weeks of wind! I think it blew all the insects away, though I found a few.

D'oh - I have to report for Jury Duty tomorrow instead of taking photos. We'll see what happens...

File.Name: b530b_b530-054.jpg
Notes:
67400772 This is a handsome Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera that I found near a hole of the Burrowing Bees in the genus Andrena (family Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) in my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2011)

Yipes - is that her egg behind her? I came back 1/2 hour later, and the "egg" was deflated and she had moved over a bit. That's a big egg for a little fly!

My book says that "The larvae are parasitic on a wide variety of insect larvae, usually those that occupy burrows; hosts include solitary bees nesting in the ground...

File.Name: b530c_b530-060.jpg
Notes: 67400626 I heard bees swarming in the woods today. I looked around, and then I looked down and saw hundreds of these Wild Bees (Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) working their holes in the ground. I believe they are Burrowing Bees in the genus Andrena. I was nervous, but they didn't try to sting me. These are solitary bees, but they all nest together to create a defense. It works! (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2011)

File.Name: b531a_b527.jpg
Notes:
67407665 Treebeard goes out with a bang!

This is the action part of my graduation speech at Dunn Middle School last Friday. I lit "Big Bertha" - a large fountain firework - and tried to put it out with a fire extinguisher. It didn't go out. My lesson - for me and the 8th grade graduates - was that when it's your time to go, no force on earth can stop you! (San Marcos Pass, 24 May 2011)

This is a single frame from my friend Nick Vincet's video of my last graduation speech. Nick put the whole thing on YouTube:
   http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oja5UAlkxUM

I have to go back for jury selection tomorrow.
I'd rather be taking photos!

File.Name: b531b_b530-027.jpg
Notes:
67407425 This is a little Snakefly (genus Agulla, family Raphidiidae, order Neuroptera) insect trying to hide from me behind the inner parts of a Coastal Morning-Glory (Calystegia macrostegia ssp. cyclostegia) in the Convulvulaceae plant family that I found on the burn site of the Gap Fire almost three years ago. I got this photo on "The Playground" trail down from West Camino Cielo Road on top of the mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2011)

These little snakeflies seem intelligent and aware!
ScientificName: Agulla; Raphidiidae; Neuroptera; Calystegia macrostegia; cyclostegia; Convulvulaceae
associatedTaxa: Agulla; Raphidiidae; Neuroptera; Calystegia macrostegia; cyclostegia; Convulvulaceae

File.Name: b531c_b530-066.jpg
Notes:
67407293 These tiny ants are herding a colony of tiny dark aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) on a not-yet-flowering stem of California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) shrub in the Polygonaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2011)

I think I've seen these dark aphids before - with different ants on a different plant. See my recent photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67384252

File.Name: b601a_b524-001.jpg
Notes:
67413279 Here is a handsome Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera resting on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. Its legs go way out of my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 30 May 2011)

Darn, I got called into the jury box today. Jury selection is still happening, but every time I've been actually called, I've been selected. We'll see what happens. Happily there's no court tomorrow, so I can get some photos - if it's not windy like today.

Don't miss my final graduation speech at Dunn Middle School, where I've taught science for 28 years and am now retired. My video-friend Nick Vincet put it on YouTube:
   http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oja5UAlkxUM

File.Name: b601b_b531-003.jpg
Notes:
67413065 This is another cute little Treehopper (family Membracidae) in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the insect order Homoptera. This one is perched on a leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. It seems to be missing its right rear leg, which might be grievous for a hopper. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2011)

It has amazing eyes. Maybe it needs them to see where it's hopping?

File.Name: b601c_b531-001.jpg
Notes: 67412911 Here is another beautiful nymph of a Bush Katydid - genus Scudderia in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera. It's resting on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. This photo shows almost the whole insect. It's small, though the long legs and antennae make it look larger. (San Marcos Pass, 31 May 2011)
ScientificName: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: b602a_b602-042.jpg
Notes:
67417449 This is a perfect little Long-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera) perched on a flower of Coastal Morning-Glory (Calystegia macrostegia ssp. cyclostegia) in the Convulvulaceae plant family today. It's still a nymph - it doesn't have wings yet. I'm not sure what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2011)

I'm posting early today because it's windy - and Julie and I are stepping out tonight for dinner and a show by blues singer Taj Mahal!
ScientificName: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Calystegia macrostegia; cyclostegia; Convulvulaceae
associatedTaxa: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Calystegia macrostegia; cyclostegia; Convulvulaceae

File.Name: b602b_b602-022.jpg
Notes:
67417235 Spittle Bugs aka Froghoppers (family Aphrophoridae of the order Homoptera) make these frothy masses on plant stems to protect their larvae - like someone hocked a big loogie on this plant stem of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family.

Tiny Thrips (order Thysanoptera) and a larger fly (order Diptera) have been caught in the froth. The fly might be a male March Fly in the family Bibionidae. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2011)
CommonNames: Spittle Bugs; Froghoppers; Trask's Yerba Santa; Thrips; fly; March Fly
ScientificName: Aphrophoridae; Homoptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Thysanoptera; Diptera; Bibionidae

File.Name: b602c_b521-042.jpg
Notes:
67417030 This isn't the best photo, but it's an interesting insect. It dropped away right after I got this photo today.

This is a native "Timema" insect. It's a chunky member of the Walkingstick clan in the family Timematidae, of the suborder Timematodea, of the unusual order Phasmatodea. This one is perched on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. I don't find them very often, so they are always interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2011)
ScientificName: Timematidae; Timematodea; Phasmatodea; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Timematidae; Timematodea; Phasmatodea; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: b603a_b602-004.jpg
Notes:
67424809 Green on yellow...

Here is a very different nymph of a Katydid (family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera) on new flowers of native composite Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. This might be the Angle-wing Katydid in the genus Microcentrum. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2011)

Today was a perfect day for photos with almost no wind ... but my jury selection is still in progress after three days to fill the last seat. I reckon I'm on. I can't complain. This is one of the few real duties of being a citizen, but I'd rather be out taking pictures!
ScientificName: Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Microcentrum; still
associatedTaxa: Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Eriophyllum confertflorum; Asteraceae; Microcentrum; still

File.Name: b603b_b602-017.jpg
Notes:
67424601 I guess this is the larva of a Beetle (Coleoptera), though it sure looks like a Lepidoptera caterpillar, right down to the faux proleg-bumps along its back. I assumed they were caterpillars when I got the photo.

This larva has beautiful iridescent green-blue colors. I found many of them yesterday on the leaves of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2011)

The Yerba Santa is not looking so good this year. It's a short-lived fire-flowerer shrub, and its time may be just about up at this location that was cleared (but not burned) a few years ago.

File.Name: b603c_b602-013.jpg
Notes:
67424468 "Where have all the butterflies gone?
I guess they're waiting, every one."

This is the beautiful chrysalis of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in the woods. It's food plants are the "scrophs" in the Scrophulariaceae plant family, but they sometimes crawl quite a distance before pupating. This one is hanging on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 2 June 2011)

Other critters have been busy. Note the spider webs and the ant on top. It's a perilous time, but they are tough. It looks ripe.

The butterflies are waiting for warm weather, but it continues to be a cool spring. Clouds are moving in tonight, and more rain is expected this weekend.
ScientificName: Euphydryas chalcedona; Scrophulariaceae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Euphydryas chalcedona; Scrophulariaceae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: b604a_b604-002.jpg
Notes:
67429116 A poor day for a coming-out...

This beautiful California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly must be freshly emerged from its chrysalis to have such perfect colors. It's perched under new flower buds of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family.

I found the butterfly fluttering around on the edge of our road today before the unseasonable rain started, too cold to take wing. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2011)

It's almost summer, but we had some cold rain today - the high temperature barely made 50 ∞F. We have a Mediterranean winter-cold-wet, summer-hot-dry weather pattern. Late-season rain is unusual, but always welcome - I'm ready for some warmth!

File.Name: b604b_b604-005.jpg
Notes:
67428987 I was hoping this was a Firefly beetle - family Lampyridae or Phengodidae - but I guess it's another Soldier Beetle (Cantharidae) in the order Coleoptera. I was surprised to find any insect on such a gloomy day as today was. Note the raindrops on the Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) leaf! (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2011)

This beetle may be Cultellunguis ingenuus or kin. See the info at BugGuide:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/355800
CommonNames: Firefly beetle; Soldier Beetle; Wild Blackberry
ScientificName: Lampyridae; Phengodidae; Cantharidae; Coleoptera; Rubus ursinus; Cultellunguis ingenuus
associatedTaxa: Lampyridae; Phengodidae; Cantharidae; Coleoptera; Rubus ursinus; Cultellunguis ingenuus

File.Name: b604c-WCIR.JPG
Notes:
67428782 This is a NOAA satellite image of the beautiful tightly-curled storm that is sitting over us right now. It almost looks like a "pineapple express" storm, but the moisture just isn't there. We've only had a few hundredths of a inch of rain so far - but our average for June is only 0.11 inch, so we're well on our way. More rain is expected tonight and tomorrow. (NOAA Satellite Image, 2 June 2011)

It was cold and rainy today - the high temperature barely made 50 ∞F. We have a Mediterranean winter-wet, summer-dry weather pattern. Late-season rain is unusual, but always welcome - I'm ready for some warmth!

File.Name: b605a_b605-011.jpg
Notes:
67435591 This is an exposed flower of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family. This flower was brought to the ground by the rain today, and it lost a petal, so you can peek inside. The hairs are arranged to make it easy for an insect to get inside, but hard to get out again. It's a good trick! (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2011)

I got this photo in the rain today, and what a good rain it's been! It's been raining all day. We have a Mediterranean winter-wet, summer-dry weather pattern, so any late-season rain like this is unusual.

We've had 1.5+ inches of rain so far from this storm, beating the old June record of 1.4 inches set in 1991 - and that's for the whole month of June! (Records for San Marcos Pass only began in 1965, but that's still impressive - most years we have NO rain in June.)

I wonder what all the insects make of this rain?

The rain has made it hard to get photos this weekend. That's too bad, because I have jury duty all next week that will use my time. I'll do what I can for new photos.
Locality: Records for San Marcos Pass only began in 1965

File.Name: b605b_b605-008.jpg
Notes:
67435400 These are new flowers of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family - in the rain today. There's nothing like a rainy day to bring out the colors of the natural world. (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2011)

It's been raining all day. The rain let up a bit late in the afternoon, so I went out looking for photos armed with paper towels and a trash bag to protect my camera, but then it started to come down hard again. I got this photo in the rain and ran back home.

File.Name: b605c_b604-003.jpg
Notes:
67435299 These are new flowers of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family on the dry side of our garden - with raindrops! I got this photo yesterday when the rain was just beginning. (San Marcos Pass, 4 June 2011)

These new flowers stand up tight against the flower stalks - only a large insect can force them open. Honeybees can do it, but it's not easy! The dried leaves are widely used for "smudge sticks" - like natural incense. I'm a cynic about these things, but even I have to admit that it's a powerful herb.

File.Name: b606a_b606-014.jpg
Notes:
67441683 This is a strange tumor-like gall on a frond of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta). Even ferns have galls!

I believe this gall is caused by the Wood Fern Sac Fungus (Taphrina californica). A different Taphrina species causes peach leaf curl. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2011)

I was bumped out of the jury box today, by the prosecution. Yahoo! I mean, I'm sorry. But I'm not sorry to be off this case. It involved sexual matters, and it was a long case that interfered with the upcoming Live Oak Music Festival in two weeks. No hard feelings - I'd rather be out taking photos!

We ended up with 1.77 inches of rain from the last storm. That beats the old June record of 1.4 inches set in 1991 - and that's for the whole month of June! (Records for San Marcos Pass only began in 1965, but that's still impressive - most years we have NO rain in June. The average rainfall for June is only 0.11 inches.)

I'm really curious what the rain will do for the native life in the woods. I hope it doesn't bring a new round of weeds and mosquitoes.
Locality: Records for San Marcos Pass only began in 1965
ScientificName: Dryopteris arguta; Wood Fern Sac Fungus; Taphrina californica; Taphrina
associatedTaxa: Dryopteris arguta; Wood Fern Sac Fungus; Taphrina californica; Taphrina

File.Name: b606b_b606-011.jpg
Notes:
67441430 This is a native Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) doing something on a flower cluster of just-starting-to-flower native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or R. tomentella) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It has a single bump on its petiole. I have a new book on identifying ants to genus, but its still beyond me. (San Marcos Pass, 6 June 2011)

I was excused from jury duty late today, but I still wanted to get some new photos after losing the weekend to rain. This will do!
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; something; Rhamnus californica; R. tomentella; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; something; Rhamnus californica; R. tomentella; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: b606c_b605-007.jpg
Notes: 67441190 These are strange Cup Fungi growing from under the firewood pile by the house. Maybe they are Peziza repanda or kin, but I really don't know. I first noticed them in the rain yesterday, but they've no doubt been around for a while. Mushrooms grow fast - but not that fast! (San Marcos Pass, 5 June 2011)

File.Name: b607a_b607-027.jpg
Notes:
67446837 This is an interesting spider wrapping its prey. It seems to only have six eyes, unlike the usual eight eyes for most spiders. Or are the outside-eyes really joined, like this spider at BugGuide?
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/258461

This might be an uncommon Coneweb Spider aka Desertshrub Spider in the family Diguetidae. It has a vertical thimble-shaped home nearby. I'll post another photo tomorrow that shows it. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2011)

File.Name: b607b_b607-009.jpg
Notes: 67446652 This is the only butterfly I found in the woods today - a Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) perched on a grass. The weekend rains seem to have knocked down our insects. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2011)

File.Name: b607c_b607-032.jpg
Notes:
67446526 These are new flowers of California Tea aka Forest Scurfpea (Rupertia physodes aka Psoralea physodes) in the Fabaceae) plant family. It's not common in our mountains, but it has an interesting common name. I've never tried to make tea from this plant, but it does have a fragrance. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2011)

The gland-spotted flowers remind me of Turpentine Broom (Thamnosma montana) in the desert. I have an old photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/9762769

This is one of those native plants that could probably disappear from the woods without a trace and make no difference - though it adds some diversity to the woods.
CommonNames: California Tea; Forest Scurfpea; Turpentine Broom
ScientificName: Rupertia physodes; Psoralea physodes; Fabaceae; Thamnosma montana
associatedTaxa: Rupertia physodes; Psoralea physodes; Fabaceae; Thamnosma montana

File.Name: b608a_b608-004.jpg
Notes:
67453436 This is a tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods this morning. Look close - this spider has caught an even tinier breakfast spider! (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2011)

Lets see - I got this photo (in shade!) at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. It's a 840 pixel crop of my 4288 pixel original, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the spider is 23.6x840/4288 = 4.6 mm. That sounds right - about 3/16 inch across. That's why it's not quite sharp!

File.Name: b608b_b607-018.jpg
Notes: 67453331 Here is a handsome white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched on a flower spike of Wood Mint (Stachys bullata) in the Labiatae plant family. The spider seems to be missing its right front leg. It's a common affliction for these spiders that often grab prey bigger than they are. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2011)

File.Name: b608c_b607-030.jpg
Notes: 67453284 This is another shot of the odd spider in my [Previous] photo. It's odd because it only seems to have six eyes, unlike the usual eight eyes for most spiders. This one is sitting at the opening of it's "home" - a vertical silk tube an inch or two long that it decorates with the husks of its victims. I think this might be an uncommon Coneweb Spider aka Desertshrub Spider in the family Diguetidae. (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2011)

File.Name: b609a_b607-017.jpg
Notes:
67458715 This is a cute little Treehopper (family Membracidae in the superfamily Fulgoroidea) of the insect order Homoptera. This one is perched on a leaf of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It has amazing eyes. Maybe it needs them to see where it's hopping? (San Marcos Pass, 7 June 2011)

This little critter is about the same size as my Jumping Spider yesterday. It was about a 1000 pixel crop rather than an 840 pixel crop. You can do the math.
ScientificName: Membracidae; Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Membracidae; Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: b609b_b607-002.jpg
Notes: 67458643 Here are beautiful flowers of native Speckled Clarkia (Clarkia cylindrica) in the Onagraceae plant family. They also go by the name Farewell-to-Spring because of when they flower - though in this cool, wet spring they just might go on and on! Note the little Beetle (Coleoptera) munching a stamen on the left. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2011)

File.Name: b609c_b608-026.jpg
Notes:
67458493 This is the odd cocoon of a Ribbed Casemaker Moth (genus Bucculatrix, family Bucculatricidae) in the order Lepidoptera. The larvae are host-plant specific, and this is on the upper side of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf, so it may well be a Live Oak Ribbed Casemaker (Bucculatrix albertiella) moth. (San Marcos Pass, 8 June 2011)

These are definitely "microlepidoptera" - tiny little critters. The tiny moth larvae "skeletonize" oak leaves by eating the soft tissue between the top and bottom surfaces of the leaves. That gives you a sense of scale.

Note the odd hairs surrounding the pupa. These should be standing up like a picket fence, but I guess the weekend rain knocked them all down. I don't know what they are for, but there they are.
CommonNames: Ribbed Casemaker Moth; Coast Live Oak; Live Oak Ribbed Casemaker
ScientificName: Bucculatrix; Bucculatricidae; Lepidoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Bucculatrix albertiella
associatedTaxa: Bucculatrix; Bucculatricidae; Lepidoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Bucculatrix albertiella

File.Name: b610a_b610-031.jpg
Notes:
67463655 It looks like an ant, and it moves like an ant - but it's not an ant!

This is another remarkable ant-mimic "true bug" in the insect order Hemiptera. You can see the sucking proboscis under its head. It has jointed antennae, and it even moves quick and jerky like an ant. Most remarkable - it even has a false "nodal spine" on it's back to mimic our most common native ant. This insect is perched on a leaf of native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2011)

This bug may well be Closterocoris amoenus in the Miridae family. See the images at BugGuide:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/153885
ScientificName: Hemiptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae
associatedTaxa: Hemiptera; Eriodictyon traskiae; Hydrophyllaceae; Closterocoris amoenus; Miridae

File.Name: b610b_b610-013.jpg
Notes: 67463389 These are beautiful flowers of native California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. Now look at the top of my photo. There is a beautiful nymph of a Bush Katydid - genus Scudderia in the family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2011)
ScientificName: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera
associatedTaxa: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Scudderia; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera

File.Name: b610c_b610-019.jpg
Notes:
67463295 The Fog Monster is totally in control!

This is looking west across San Marcos Pass today, along the east-west axis of the Santa Ynez Mountains. That's a small ridge near Painted Cave in front, and Broadcast Peak with radio antennas in the back - the ocean to the left, and the Santa Ynez Valley to the right. Our home is right in the middle, under the clouds. (San Marcos Pass, 10 June 2011)

"Fog" isn't the right word when it's 2800 feet thick! This is our typical "June gloom" weather pattern. It usually clears before noon, and then is followed by "fog winds" in the disturbed atmosphere. It was a bit late to clear today!

File.Name: b611a_b611-011.jpg
Notes: 67469476 Here is the real thing - a handsome native Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica or R. tomentella) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. You can see that it has a single bump on its petiole - something the ant-mimics in my [Previous] photos take pains to duplicate. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2011)
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Rhamnus californica; R. tomentella; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Rhamnus californica; R. tomentella; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: b611b_b611-003.jpg
Notes:
67469454 I have ant-mimics on my mind!

This is a different ant-mimic "true bug" in the insect order Hemiptera. You can see the sucking proboscis under its head. It has jointed antennae, and it even moves like an ant. It also has a false "nodal spine" on it's back - actually two, side by side - to mimic our most common native ants. It looks like a tiny Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) with those spiny front legs.

I got better photos of it, but this is the only one that shows both the proboscis and the spines. It's perched on a flower head of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2011)

I suppose I should apologize for the photo, but it was overcast when I got the photo, and this critter is really tiny. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. The bug is 270 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the insect is 23.6x270/4288 = 1.5 mm - about 1/16 inch across. That's why it's not quite sharp!

Now that I know what to look for, I might be able to get a better photo.
CommonNames: true bug; Assassin Bug; Canyon Sunflower
ScientificName: Hemiptera; Reduviidae; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; really
associatedTaxa: Hemiptera; Reduviidae; Venegasia carpesioides; Asteraceae; really

File.Name: b611c_b611-023.jpg
Notes:
67469361 This may well be the adult form of the ant-mimic "true bug" in my [Previous] photo. It looks less like an ant as an adult, but it still moves in an ant-like way. If I'm right, this is Closterocoris amoenus in the family Miridae of the order Hemiptera. It's perched in the throat of a flower of native Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2011)

See the images of species Closterocoris amoenus at BugGuide:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/153885

File.Name: b612a_b612-011.jpg
Notes:
67474528 This is something remarkable!

This is the streaming stage of a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) on the wood of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. First they seem to make the white bubbles. Then they grow the stems which absorb the bubbles, and they form sausage-like tops. It's a remarkable transformation. They might transform further to become the Stemonitis axifera in my [Previous] photo. I'm not sure.

I think these are my favorite organisms, though I don't find them very often - they are tiny, and they don't last long. You have to be in the right place and the right time to get a photo. But what are they doing out in June? (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2011)

Slime Molds are remarkable critters that seem to be part fungus and part animal. They are Mycetozoa - which literally means "fungus-animals". They are really animals, not fungi.

Mycetozoa live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in the decaying wood. When the weather is right, they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" without cell membranes.

Look at my photo and you can see them streaming up around the tiny stems. They end up as tiny mushroom-like structures that release spores that disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one bit of decaying wood to another.

I don't know what they're doing in our woods in June! We have a Mediterranean winter-wet, summer-dry weather pattern and this should be our summer-dry time, but it was gloomy almost all day today, and we had 1-3/4 inches of rain a week ago. The woods are wet. What a strange weather year this has been!
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Stemonitis axifera; Mycetozoa
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Stemonitis axifera; Mycetozoa

The fruiting bodies look like vacuum cleaner fluff, and they have a well-defined stalk. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2011)

I believe they are the species Lycogala epidendrum. These slime molds are about 1/4 inch (6 mm) across. (San Marcos Pass, 11 June 2011)

File.Name: b612d_b612-016.jpg
Notes:
67473976 This is "The Blusher" - a well-named blushing Amanita rubescens mushroom that I found in the woods this morning - in June! (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2011)

It gets its name from the reddish colors it develops as it ages. This amanita is said to be edible when cooked, but it's easily confused with poisonous species. No thanks!

We never get mushrooms in June in the Santa Barbara mountains - but here it is. It was was gloomy almost all day today, and we had 1-3/4 inches of rain a week ago - there are mushrooms in the woods again. What a strange weather year this has been!

File.Name: b612e_b612-020.jpg
Notes:
67473909 I don't know what this cute little mushroom is. I went into the woods today looking for salamanders and slime molds. I turned a rotting branch over, and there it was. The branch and the mushroom were originally flat. Of course I "left it like I found it". That's a good rule for ranch gates - and life in the woods! (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2011)

Note the pale little cup fungi in the background.

File.Name: b612f_b612-024.jpg
Notes:
67473799 This is an impressive mass of Brown Witch's Butter (Tremella foliacea) mushroom growing from a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) branch in the woods today. You can see a bit of the usual yellow Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica in the lower-left corner of my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2011)

The other fungus in the photo is a Crust Fungus - some kind of the genus Stereum. My book says that "Most Tremellas are frequently found with Stereum, suggesting some kind of relationship between the two." I'll leave it to the mycologists to figure that out!

It sure looks like kelp in the ocean. It's said to be edible and tasteless - no surprise, it's mostly water.
CommonNames: Brown Witch's Butter; Coast Live Oak; Witch's Butter; Crust Fungus
ScientificName: Tremella foliacea; Quercus agrifolia; Tremella mesenterica; Stereum

File.Name: b613a_b613-003.jpg
Notes:
67480135 They look bioluminescent, but this is natural light!

These tiny Mycena acicula (or kin) mushrooms are growing on a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) branch in the woods today. I did do one trick. I turned the branch over into a sun beam and shot down into the dark - and then flipped my photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2011)

It's a wonder that there are any new mushrooms in our woods in June! This should be our dry season, but we had almost 2 inches of rain a week ago. The woods are still wet. What a strange weather year this has been!

File.Name: b613b_b612-015.jpg
Notes:
67479954 I can see accumulations of "pink" spores on the gill of this little mushroom, which places it in the pink-spored Entolomataceae family of mushrooms - maybe Leptonia? (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2011)

The real question is what it's doing in our woods in June! This should be our dry season, but we had almost 2 inches of rain a week ago. The woods are still wet. What a strange weather year this has been!

File.Name: b613c_b612-031.jpg
Notes:
67479842 I believe these are dark-spored Psathyrella mushrooms in the woods. They are growing from a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) branch. The caps are "hygrophanous" - changing color as they dry out. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2011)

It's a wonder that there are any new mushrooms in our woods in June! This should be our dry season, but we had almost 2 inches of rain a week ago. The woods are still wet. What a strange weather year this has been!

File.Name: b613d_b612-018.jpg
Notes:
67479680 This strange Stemonitis Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) sure looks different from the mature slime molds I show at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67474332

They live up to the name Chocolate Tube Slime that they sometimes get. I found them growing under the bark of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) branch rather than on the surface like my previous photo. Maybe it's just an environmental difference? (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2011)

If you look close, you can see the individual stalks exchanging genetic material. Like I said, they are really strange creatures!
