File.Name: b613e_b613-007.jpg
Notes:
67479343 This is the "hot dog on a stick" phase of the growth of a Slime Mold (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on the rotted wood of a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. I guess it is genus Stemonitis. There appears to be new material streaming up the stalks. See my [Previous] photos for more info about these remarkable critters. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2011)

These interesting critters seem to show biological "altruism" on a small scale. This is interesting to philosophers as well as biologists. Some of these Mycetozoa will end up as part of the stem, and not reproduce. Do they act for "the greater good" of the rest of the cellular mass?
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Stemonitis; Mycetozoa
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Quercus agrifolia; Stemonitis; Mycetozoa

File.Name: b613f_b613-001.jpg
Notes:
67479218 I'm learning about Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) - from the woods!

The slime molds seem to start as these egg-like balls. Then a stem grows - but I don't know if it grows up from the ground, or down from the "eggs"- I suspect the latter. As the stems grow, they lift the "egg mass" up into the air - see my [Previous] photo. The eggs and new material from the ground then merge to form the characteristic "hot dog on a stick" structures that characterize this Stemonitis species - see my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2011)

Yesterday I called this critter Stemonitis axifera, but I spoke too soon. There are many species of genus Stemonitis that can only be distinguished microscopically.

For more info about these remarkable critters, see my [Previous] photos starting at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/67474528
ScientificName: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Stemonitis; Stemonitis axifera; Stemonitis
associatedTaxa: Myxomycetes; Myxogastria; Stemonitis; Stemonitis axifera; Stemonitis

File.Name: b614a_b614-004.jpg
Notes:
67485741 This is a tiny Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a single stamen of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family on the dry side of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2011)

I did get some photos today, but I was preoccupied with the Live Oak Music Festival is coming up this weekend! For more info about Live Oak see here:
   http://www.liveoakfest.org/
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; White Sage; Live Oak

File.Name: b614b_b613-014.jpg
Notes:
67485626 This beautiful California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly is "puddling" - getting moisture or salts from the garden soil. Sometimes you catch more butterflies on bare ground than with flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 13 June 2011)

There are a few butterflies in the woods, but not many with our cool weather this year. I'm still waiting.

File.Name: b614c_b614-018.jpg
Notes:
67485538 The Convergent Lady Beetles - aka "ladybugs" - Hippodamia convergens in the family Coccinellidae, order Coleoptera - are converging in the woods today down by the creek!

I didn't find masses of them at rest, but there are hundreds - maybe thousands - in the air! I guess they are converging for a mating rave. That's our pump house in the background. To get a photo like this, shoot into the sun with a dark background and the insects in the light. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2011)

I set my camera (on a tripod of course) to be as un-sensitive as possible - ISO 50, f/32, with a shutter speed of 1/6 second. Every wing beat shows as a separate blip on each ladybug's trace. I figure these ladybugs beat their wings at about 14/0.17 = 82 beats/second!

Like these ladybugs, many of our friends are converging for the Live Oak Music Festival this weekend! The line-up to get in and claim a camping space happens Thursday at 3:00 pm. We're getting stuff ready!
Locality: on a tripod of course) to be as un-sensitive as possible - ISO 50

File.Name: b614d_b614-024.jpg
Notes: 67485228 This is a surprising - maybe a late-season Oak-Loving Bolete (Boletus dryophilus or kin) mushroom? It has a smooth tan cap and pores like a sponge instead of gills. The pores stain blue when bruised, but they are not that other notorious mushroom. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2011)

File.Name: b614e_b608-010.jpg
Notes: 67485043 These are little dark-spored Inky Cap (Coprinus) mushrooms growing on old wood chips along our road. They are too thin to think about eating. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2011)

File.Name: b614f_b612-003.jpg
Notes:
67485010 These are the featureless mushrooms growing on woodchips from tree work a couple of years ago. They remind me of the pod-creatures from "Invasion of the Bodysnatchers" - not fully-formed.

They might be Spring Agrocybe (Agrocybe praecox) or they might be something totally different introduced by the chipper of the work crew. They have a ring and brown spores. I have no idea if they are edible. (San Marcos Pass, 12 June 2011)

File.Name: b615a_b610-045.jpg
Notes:
67491762 This beautiful white Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is perched on flowers of some kind of Thyme (genus Thymus) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing in our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2011)

I have no more time for photos. Both of my kids are home and we're getting ready for the Live Oak Music Festival! I'll be back with more photos next week.

The lineup for camping starts tomorrow afternoon, and we'll be there. I love Live Oak most for the chance to see old friends and the campground jams - and I figure the lineup counts as one more night to jam!

To see what you're missing, check out:
   http://www.liveoakfest.org/

File.Name: b615b_b614-015.jpg
Notes: 67491594 This is a winged Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera). This ant has a rather large abdomen - I wonder if it is a queen, large with eggs? A moment after I took this photo, a large Funnel Weaver (Agelenidae) spider ran out and grabbed her and carried her off. So it goes. (San Marcos Pass, 14 June 2011)

File.Name: b615c_b615-002.jpg
Notes:
67491531 These are a pair - probably a mating pair - of Crickets, family Gryllidae in the order Orthoptera. They are resting on foliage of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 15 June 2011)

Both of my kids are home and we're getting ready for the Live Oak Music Festival! I did walk down into the canyon today looking for swarming ladybugs. I didn't find them, but I did find these crickets. They are probably some kind of Camel Cricket, but I don't have time to look them up tonight.

File.Name: b621a_b619-052.jpg
Notes:
67524295 Along about 2:00 in the morning, everything starts looking blurry - it can't be all the beer I've been drinking!

This is the late-night music jam in our Live Oak Music Festival camp. I took this photo at 5/8 second, at f/4, and ISO 1600. I had to take LOTS of photos to get a few sharp ones - but sometimes the unsharp photos capture the moment! (Live Oak Camp, Santa Ynez Valley, 18 June 2011)

These are all people I love. I see most of them just once a year. They are my Live Oak Friends!

To see what you missed, check out:
   http://www.liveoakfest.org/    http://www.oldmanluedecke.ca/

File.Name: b621b_b619-020.jpg
Notes:
67524168 Here are Wil, Ewan, John, and Josh jamming at our Live Oak Music Festival camp this weekend - with extra fiddles, drums, lots of guitars, etc. It was another memorable Live Oak night. (Live Oak Camp, Santa Ynez Valley, 18 June 2011)

You can see "Jigger" sitting front and center capturing the jam on video. (He is remarkably steady!) I'll really try to put it on the Web sometime this year!

File.Name: b621c_b619-007.jpg
Notes:
67524046 This is my son Ewan on mandolin and John on fiddle jamming together in our campground. These two only see each other once a year at the Live Oak Music Festival - it's remarkable how they connect musically! (Live Oak Camp, Santa Ynez Valley, 18 June 2011)

Ewan is playing his "The Gibson" mandolin from 1908 - it still sounds great after more than a century!

File.Name: b621d_b619-010.jpg
Notes: 67523931 This is our Live Oak Music Festival camp with the moon rising over our bright Coleman lantern. There is music happening here - played by people I love! (Live Oak Camp, Santa Ynez Valley, 18 June 2011)

File.Name: b621e_b618-002.jpg
Notes: 67523890 This is our BBQ dinner for Saturday night at the Live Oak Music Festival - chicken, sausages, garlic bread (in foil), and grilled onions, peppers, pineapple, and peaches - all with BBQ sauce. Thanks for thinking of the peaches, Ewan - BBQ peaches with sauce are fabulous! (Live Oak Camp, Santa Ynez Valley, 18 June 2011)

File.Name: b621f_b620-001.jpg
Notes:
67523710 This is our flag at the Live Oak Music Festival - it's a warning for neighbors to find another spot to camp if they want to sleep! But you don't go to a music festival to sleep, do you?? (Live Oak Camp, Santa Ynez Valley, 19 June 2011)

Apparently some people do. We played until 5:30 AM on Friday night. Miraculously, we got no complaints that night - though we did the next morning. We moved to the campfire circle after 2:00 am for the next two nights. Keep it cool!

(The cop who shut us down each night was good about it - he was enjoying the music!)

The flag says "Kicking out the jams since 1995" - I think we've been going to the festival since 1993, but maybe not jamming. That's 19 years of Live Oak!

File.Name: b622a_b621-028.jpg
Notes:
67530237 This is a furry Snowberry Clearwing Moth (Hemaris diffinis) working native flowers of native Coyote Mint aka White-leaf Monardella (Monardella hypoleuca) in the Lamiaceae plant family - caught in the air. They are also known as Bumble Bee Moths. It's a kind of Sphinx Moth (Sphingidae) that flies in the day time. This is the first one I've seen this year. They're cute! (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2011)

The flowers are interesting. This seems too early for Monardella hypoleuca that usually flowers in July. I usually find them scattered in the woods, but these are flowering in great profusion along our road across the canyon where our neighbors cleared last year. I guess they really are fire-follower plants.

Yesterday was the Summer Solstice - the longest day of the year, and the official start of summer. It's also the day I started this fotolog back in 2003. I've posted 8,175 photos over 8 years. My very first photo is still one of my favorites:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/251716

File.Name: b622b_b622-007.jpg
Notes:
67530068 This critter looks like a wasp, but if you look closer you can see hairs on its legs for collecting pollen. It must be some kind of Wild Bee (family Apidae, order Hymenoptera). After browsing at BugGuide, I think it might be a Cuckoo Bee in the subfamily Nomadinae - maybe genus Nomada? (San Marcos Pass, 22 June 2011)

Cuckoo Bees resemble wasps and lay their eggs in other bees' burrows. They are fast fliers, and they seem aware of me - they are hard to photograph!

This one is working a new flower spike of non-native Lamb's Ear (Stachys lanata) in our garden today.

File.Name: b622c_b621-005.jpg
Notes: 67529863 This is a handsome Long-horned Beetle (Cerambycidae) in the order Coleoptera. It's pretty large - an inch or two long, and it's antennae are even wider than that. I believe they are nocturnal. I found this one inside our house in the morning, and placed it on a leaf of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata) in the Labiatae plant family for the photo. (San Marcos Pass, 21 June 2011)

File.Name: b623a_b623-066.jpg
Notes:
67535615 This is a beautiful Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in the woods today, working new flowers of endemic native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. I love its soccer ball eyes and mohawk! (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2011)

This butterfly took off a moment after I got this photo. A few years ago, they were our most common butterfly, but no longer. In fact, all butterflies are scarce this year. I hope they're just late. It has been an unusually cool and rainy Spring.

67535456 This amazing Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera is dancing above flowers of native endemic Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. Note the pollen on its long proboscis - thus pollination happens. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2011)

I got sharper photos of these little flies today, but this is still my favorite.

File.Name: b623c_b610-038.jpg

That's my name, because I only find them on the furry foliage of endemic native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family growing in the mountains near home. I don't know anything else about them. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2011)

Notes:
67535281 Here are the red oh-so-red flowers of native Scarlet Larkspur (Delphinium cardinale) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. They are all the more remarkable because this is our summer-dry season when the ground is dry. I did no color correction on this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2011)

This uncommon native flower only grows in a few places in our mountains - along the Knapp's Castle trail (where I got this photo) and near Arroyo Burro on East Camino Cielo road. Take note, Lynn!

Notes: 67535186 These are beautiful new flowers of Elegant Clarkia (Clarkia unguiculata) in the Onagraceae plant family. The petals look like they've been cut with scissors. (San Marcos Pass, 23 June 2011)

67535053 What a great color combo...

File.Name: b624a_b624-030.jpg
Notes:
67540874 There are thousands of these tiny Pacific Tree Frogs (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) by the frog pond near our old cabin on Kinevan Ranch. They are not much bigger than your smallest fingernail. I captured this one perched on a blade of grass. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2011)

We used to live near this frog pond in the old "Chinaman's Cabin". I used to take our 2-3 year old kids out to the pond and say "shush". We'd be quiet for a few minutes, and then the frogs would erupt in a chorus of sound! Great memories.

I had a gripe yesterday about advertisements on my photo page, and they disappeared before morning. Thanks fotolog!

File.Name: b624b_b624-066.jpg
Notes:
67540731 "They call me MISTER rattlesnake!"
"Yes sir, I mean no harm, just let me go on my way..."

My son Ryan almost stepped on this venomous Southern Pacific Rattlesnake (Crotalus viridis helleri) - "la serpiente de cascabel" - near our old cabin at Kinevan Ranch today. I got a few photos before it went away. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2011)

I'm not particularly afraid of rattlesnakes, but I do worry about stepping on them. When I inadvertently step of our cat, she hisses - but if you step on a rattlesnake, they might strike! Really, the snakes do everything they can to avoid contact. Respect their wishes.

ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: b624d_b624-055.jpg
Notes:
67540505 Here is a beautiful new flower of Harvest Brodiaea (species Brodiaea) in the Liliaceae plant family. The plant gets its name because it flowers late when the spring grasses are dry, at harvest time. (San Marcos Pass, 24 June 2011)

Ah, but which Brodiaea is it? The Clifton Smith "Flora of the Santa Barbara Region" (my bible) calls it Brodiaea jolonensis, but it may well be Brodiaea terrestris ssp. kernensis instead. See the interesting webpages by Wayne Armstong, starting with:
   http://waynesword.palomar.edu/figueroa1.htm

(I've been in touch with Wayne, and he believes these are B. terrestris. I have to admit that I don't really care!)

The plants are uncommon. I only know them from this one place in the old Kinevan apple orchard where we used to live 30 years ago.
Locality: I've been in touch with Wayne
